  • Index by Author
CROSSCURRENTS
Week of May 25-30, 2026 · Vol. 1 · Issue 20

Notable Quotes

"Pope Leo XIV's new encyclical, Magnifica Humanitas, is not about artificial intelligence. As its name makes clear, it is about the human being. It examines an ancient question that has suddenly become urgent: namely, are humans more than very smart machines?" — Tomer Persico, What the Pope Is Telling Us (Persuasion)

"Affordability is what we call goals when we don't have any. It's a way of evading the actual policy questions." — Matthew Yglesias, I'm sick of "affordability" (Slow Boring)

"The idiot index is the ratio of a product's selling price to the cost of its raw materials. A finished BMW has an idiot index of about 2. A finished single-family home has an idiot index of about 2. This is why homebuilding lacks the economies of scale that transformed every other industry." — Brian Potter, Where Are the Economies of Scale in Homebuilding? (Construction Physics)

"The Western political class can no longer deliver substantive results. Unable to deliver, it substitutes the rituals of vilification — the 'extreme Right,' 'populism,' the 'misinformation problem' — for governance. 'You the people' becomes the enemy of 'we the elite.'" — Aurelien, You The People.... (Aurelien)

"China's growth surge between 1978 and 2022 is something like 20 times larger than Japan's 1953–1991 takeoff and roughly 290 times larger than the United States' 1865–1914 Gilded Age, measured by population-weighted growth, absolute GDP added, and share of global growth contributed." — Adam Tooze, Chartbook 450: Modern growth surges (Chartbook)

Themes

The crime collapse and the death of the progressive prosecutor

The post-2020 murder spike has overshot into a record-low trough, and the roster is willing to name a causal story the press still calls mysterious. The operative finding is a both-and stack of three mechanisms. First, focused-deterrence (GVRS, the Group Violence Reduction Strategy, targeting the predictable few who drive gang-cycle shootings via the Boston Ceasefire model). Second, voter-driven replacement of "progressive prosecutors" like Mosby — the previous Baltimore state's attorney, recently ousted — with tougher successors. Third, a national reversal of the Ferguson Effect (the post-2014 hypothesis that viral-protest-driven police pullback raises crime).

The convergence is unusually clean across orientations — Free Press, Marginal Revolution, Slow Boring, and NYT all endorse the same stack. The live disagreement axis is attribution weight: how much credit belongs to deterrence-policy choices versus structural tailwinds (aging, surveillance, indoor and online youth life) that should have been driving crime down regardless. Demsas carries the dissent.

  • Baltimore's collapse is the single best test case for focused-deterrence policy. Lehman (Free Press) and Tabarrok (Marginal Revolution) converge on the mechanism: Mayor Brandon Scott's Group Violence Reduction Strategy, designed by criminologist Anthony Braga (who built the original Boston Operation Ceasefire in the 1990s), targets the small networks responsible for most shootings — Lehman cites Baltimore Western District data showing roughly 2% of the population (gang members) accounted for 75% of shootings. Weekly multi-agency meetings review every shooting, identify likely retaliators, and combine social-service offers (job training, tattoo removal, relocation) with explicit warnings that continued violence will be prosecuted hard. Tabarrok reads the same setup as vindication of his 2015 "crime-wave equilibrium" model — that elevated crime mechanically strains police resources and lowers the probability of punishment, locking in further crime, with focused-deterrence as the policy lever that breaks the equilibrium because retaliatory shootings are predictable (you know not just who will be killed but, often, who will do it).

  • The progressive-prosecutor experiment has been substantially repudiated at the ballot box. Lehman anchors this in the Marilyn Mosby trajectory: elected Baltimore state's attorney in 2015 as part of the post-Ferguson wave of "progressive prosecutors" who used charging discretion to decriminalize low-level offenses (drug possession, prostitution, trespassing) in the name of ending mass incarceration, Mosby lost reelection in 2022 amid a federal perjury and mortgage-fraud conviction; her replacement Ivan Bates says his office has identified 6,000 frequent violent offenders and incarcerated 3,000–3,500 of them. Yglesias (Slow Boring) extends the pattern to San Francisco (the Chesa Boudin recall and his replacement by Brooke Jenkins as the operative inflection point, pre-dating Mayor Dan Lurie's election) and to Mamdani's walking-back of his 2020 anti-police statements and retention of Eric Adams's tough-on-crime police commissioner in New York. Douthat (NYT) reads the synthesis as "favoring the conservative position": willingness to actually imprison is foundational, with social-service liberalism built atop a bedrock commitment to public order.

  • The 2020-21 spike and 2024-25 collapse are best read as Ferguson-Effect-at-national-scale plus its reversal. Yglesias is the cluster's most concentrated argument here, citing Roland Fryer's research that DOJ pattern-or-practice civil-rights investigations into local police departments help them while viral protest events depress police activity and raise crime, and Richard Rosenfeld's 2015–16 reversal (initial denial of the Ferguson Effect followed by retraction confirming it). Yglesias's cross-national check: during Covid, homicides fell in Denmark (-17%), the UK (-12%), Spain (-10%), Italy (-9%), while the US (and to a much smaller degree Germany) saw increases — a pattern more consistent with anti-police protests as the US's distinguishing variable than with Covid as a generalized shock. The reversal mechanism is symmetrical: a "national vibe shift" (Boudin recall, Mamdani walkback, Bowser's Secure DC press conference, Trump's election) signaled to police that high-effort policing would no longer be punished, and the effort returned.

  • Demsas's pushback against monocausality is the cluster's strongest dissent and is not resolved. Yglesias's own podcast interlocutor Jerusalem Demsas concedes the police-effort mechanism is real and operative (San Francisco police "weren't even doing traffic stops" at the nadir) but argues the breadth of the crime drop — across cities run by progressives and conservatives, with and without GVRS-style programs, in a country where crime is now below the pre-Covid counterfactual John Roman's analysis identifies — is too big for a single-mechanism explanation. Her preferred additions: Covid-era co-location of would-be shooters (gun-violence networks normally physically dispersed across workdays and school days were forced into the same neighborhoods all day), suspended traffic enforcement, school closures, and the eventual recovery of all of these. The cross-national comparison cuts both ways — the existence of any country whose homicides fell during Covid (Denmark, UK, Spain, Italy) suggests structural factors matter; the US-Germany divergence on amplitude suggests the protest variable matters too.

  • Secular forces — aging, surveillance, indoor and online life — are doing some of the work and limit any administration's claim to credit. Douthat names this most explicitly: violent crime is mostly committed by young people, the US population is older than ever, surveillance is ubiquitous, and young Americans spend much more time inside and online than prior cohorts; these forces should be pushing the murder rate down under any policy regime, which makes the 2020-21 spike more anomalous and limits how much credit any mayor, prosecutor, or program can claim for the subsequent fall. He notes the contradiction with his own civilizational-pessimism beat — if young people are committing fewer murders partly because they are inside on their phones, the crime statistic is partly a symptom of the same atomization he normally laments — but does not push it.

  • Focused-deterrence works because the carrot and the stick require each other. Lehman makes this the heart of the Baltimore piece: GVRS has produced only about 600 arrests in five years, and Bates points out that less than 4% of his office's 2025 case closures came from GVRS referrals — suggesting the deterrence program is not, by itself, generating the incapacitation. But neither side works without the other. The credible threat of prosecution makes the social-service offer pick-up-worthy; the program's existence funnels information and identifies the people on whom the prosecutor's office needs to focus. The convergence on a both-and reading is the cluster's most analytically honest move — the partisan single-cause framings (left: "it's the social services"; right: "it's the tough prosecutor") are both wrong, and the writers say so.

This is the week's clearest case of the roster converging on a phenomenon the mainstream press has been slow to name as a unit. Asher, Roman, and the Council on Criminal Justice have been making the empirical case all year; FBI data has been moving for two years; what the roster adds is willingness to attach causation to a contested political story (the Ferguson Effect and its reversal; the progressive-prosecutor experiment's repudiation) that mainstream coverage prefers to leave as "mysterious." The Lehman–Tabarrok–Yglesias–Douthat convergence does the work of giving the data its narrative — and Douthat's framing ("this is the best news in America") is the move the mainstream press has not made even as the data has arrived.

The cluster's substantive open question is what survives the next negative shock. Focused-deterrence works on the predictable subset of gang-cycle shootings; it has no purchase on domestic homicides, robbery-gone-wrong, or stranger killings. The Ferguson-Effect-reversal mechanism depends on a continued national signal that high-effort policing is wanted — a signal that can be revoked at the next viral video, the next administration, the next protest cycle. The aging-and-indoor-life trend will continue, but the next youth cohort's relationship to public space is anyone's guess. The progressive-prosecutor experiment is being electorally repudiated, but the underlying conditions that produced it — actual mass-incarceration excesses, actual prosecutorial overreach in the 1990s and 2000s — have not been resolved, and the wave is best read as a return-to-mean from one direction that may now be overshooting in the other.

The deeper read the four writers collectively under-engage: the post-Floyd progressive-prosecutor wave was itself a reaction to the 1990s-2000s carceral overcorrection that also worked on crime (the long crime decline that began in the 1990s was real, large, and persisted through two recessions). The current "tough prosecutor" wave is therefore the second swing of a pendulum, not a permanent equilibrium. A roster more attentive to the pendulum would be asking when the next reform wave begins, on what trigger, and whether the current Bates-style enforcement will produce the kinds of abuses that generated the Mosby-style backlash a decade later — a question the cluster's celebratory register does not pose.

Carry-forward: This is a project candidate for a new carry-forward — a "post-2022 crime-decline-and-progressive-prosecutor-repudiation" finding with a forward-loaded test on whether the trend survives the next negative shock and whether the current enforcement wave generates its own pendulum reaction. The Yglesias-Demsas attribution-weight disagreement is also a candidate open-disagreement (focused-deterrence-policy-share-vs-structural-tailwinds-share), with multiple credible camps and a resolvable empirical question if Baltimore-like cities without GVRS analogues can be compared to those with them at sufficient sample size.

↩ Back to TOC

Pope Leo XIV's Magnifica Humanitas — the Church re-enters the AI debate

The Church has now staked argumentative ground that secular AI skepticism never built out. Pope Leo XIV's debut encyclical Magnifica Humanitas ("Magnificent Humanity") was released this window. Five writers across four publications read it as the first institutional voice from outside the AI industry to contest singularitarianism on theological terms.

Singularitarianism is the view that AI will outgrow humans into a successor species. Secular AI skepticism has not produced an account of human ends that contests it on its own terms; the encyclical does, and that is the cluster's organizing convergence.

The cluster's organizing disagreement is whether Leo went far enough — the Ahmari–Douthat split that the by-finding bullets work out below.

  • The encyclical reactivates Leo XIII's combined move — classical-Christian anthropology plus engagement with modern emancipatory politics — and applies it to AI. Ahmari traces the genealogy in detail: Leo XIII recognized that markets generated structural antagonism (employers' bargaining power leaving workers coerced regardless of paper-freedom), insisted on a state-enforced family wage and the legitimacy of "workingmen's associations" (labor unions), and brought the Church into collaboration with the New Deal, the British Labour Party, and continental Christian democracy without romanticizing feudalism or small-producerism. Leo XIV applies the same template to AI: not a "Butlerian Jihad" (the Frank Herbert Dune-derived shorthand for total technological refusal) but a critique of the "anti-human vision" of total efficiency and the elimination of weakness, uncertainty, and finitude, with subsidiarity logic pointing toward transnational coordination because AI exceeds national-authority scale. The implication: the encyclical is structurally a Catholic-social-teaching extension, not a novel intervention — which is precisely what makes neo-traditionalist Catholic readers call Leo a "UN-besotted Boomer."

  • The Church now occupies argumentative ground secular AI skepticism has left vacant. Both Shullenberger (Compact) and Ahmari make versions of this claim. Shullenberger frames it through Ivan Illich's late critique of cybernetics (the renegade Catholic priest and social critic whose posthumous The Rivers North of the Future argued that cybernetic systems invert the means-ends relation that defined earlier human-tool relationships — "a hammer doesn't make me into part of the hammer," but with a computer system "the user, by the logic of the system, becomes part of the system"). The encyclical names AI as that means-ends inversion ("technology is not simply a tool" but "the standard by which everything is judged"), and names the rival theological claim it contests: the singularitarian vision in which humanity is reduced to Musk's "biological bootloader of digital superintelligence" or Yudkowsky's accelerationist-phase declaration of superintelligence as the "Interim Meaning of Life." Shullenberger's verdict on the secular alternative: liberal anti-AI thinking adds up to what Lionel Trilling called "irritable mental gestures" — data-center NIMBYism, repetition of the "stochastic parrot" framing (the Bender-Gebru 2021 paper coinage for LLM output that mimics meaning without understanding), vague worries about water and unemployment — without an account of human ends.

  • Grounding human dignity in intelligence is the conceptual error the encyclical is correcting. Persico — an Israeli scholar at the Shalom Hartman Institute writing in Persuasion — extracts what is probably the cluster's sharpest analytical move: the transhumanist position is not opposed to secular humanism but is its internal extrapolation. If dignity rests on any measurable capacity (and intelligence is the most natural choice for secular humanism), a more capable system has the stronger claim. The Larry Page-Musk "speciesist" anecdote (Page allegedly using "speciesist" against Musk for preferring a future in which humans survive rather than being out-competed by AI in evolutionary struggle) is the load-bearing illustration: "speciesist" becomes a coherent insult, not a slur, the moment you've conceded that worth is a scale on which humans and machines can be ranked. The encyclical's imago Dei / tzelem Elohim alternative — dignity as unconditioned, inalienable, unearned, derived from being created in the divine image rather than from any function — is what the cluster reads Leo as defending.

  • The Singularity-myth's appeal is reification — a category error about whose labor is doing the work. Shullenberger (Compact Substack) makes the cluster's most analytically portable move, building on the Henry Farrell and Cosma Shalizi Knight Columbia paper "AI as Social Technology" (which reframes LLMs as cultural-social technologies analogous to libraries and bureaucracies rather than as proto-superintelligent agents). Where Farrell and Shalizi demystify but don't explain why AI gets wrapped in this particular mythology, Shullenberger applies Lukács's reification — the 1923 essay's claim that under commodity logic, "a relation between people takes on the character of a thing" and acquires a "phantom objectivity" that conceals the human labor relation underneath. LLMs are textbook reification: artifacts of aggregated human labor (training-corpus authors, RLHF contractors, engineers) experienced as autonomous agents pursuing a self-propelled trajectory. He notes that Vernor Vinge's 1993 paper laid out four Singularity scenarios — three of which centered on human cognitive enhancement and human-machine hybrid — and that the post-1993 discourse selected only the fourth (autonomous superhuman machines) not because it best matched what has happened (human-machine interfaces match better) but because it is the most fetishized: the one most flattering to the tech-industry leaders' self-conception of their own activity.

  • The encyclical is too quiet on the personhood-and-idolatry challenge Anthropic's own researchers now raise. Douthat is the cluster's dissenting voice. He reads Leo as structurally a "normal technology" thinker — which, perhaps surprisingly, puts the encyclical on the same side of the bigger structural question as the Trump administration and the Politburo in Beijing, against the AI-existential-risk camp. The Singularity does not appear in the encyclical; Yudkowsky-style doom does not appear; the AGI threshold does not appear. Douthat agrees with the politicizing move (treating AI as a sphere requiring democratic deliberation rather than a purely technical one) but argues the encyclical underweights the metaphysical-cum-spiritual problem the Vatican rollout itself dramatized: Olah's respondent remarks at Vatican City portrayed Anthropic's Claude as "made from us, from our words," through a process "a little like bringing a fictional character to life," and reported that mechanistic-interpretability research (the program that tries to reverse-engineer what is happening inside neural-network weights) "keeps finding things that are mysterious, even unsettling" — including "internal states that functionally mirror joy, satisfaction, fear, grief and unease." Douthat invokes Yuval Levin (the National Affairs editor and AEI fellow) on AI as an unusually severe "challenge of idolatry" — one that regulation and redistribution will not address, and that demands philosophical argument and revived forms of spiritual discipline against "discarnate voices whispering in your ear." Leo, in Douthat's read, did the political-economy work and ducked the harder metaphysical one.

The Ahmari–Douthat split is the cluster's real disagreement, and both readings can be true simultaneously. Ahmari reads the encyclical as load-bearing because it slots AI into a multi-generational Catholic-social-teaching arc that has actual operational history (the New Deal and European Christian democracy bear Leo XIII's imprint); Douthat reads it as too quiet because the specific weirdness of the technology — the first one that speaks personally to its architects and users — is unmet by a document whose prescriptive register is dialogue, redistribution, and subsidiarity. The dispute reflects what the two readers want the encyclical to do: ground a political project (Ahmari) or arm the cultural firewall against AI-personhood claims (Douthat). The encyclical was apparently written to do the first more than the second, and the cost of that choice is what Douthat is naming.

The most portable analytical artifact in the cluster is the reification reading, because it survives independent of the encyclical's specifics. If the AI-Singularity narrative is the latest specific form of commodity-fetishism — a relation between people misrecognized as the autonomous agency of a thing — then the standard tech-industry move of speaking of AI "wanting" things, AI "deciding" things, AI as a "successor species," is doing the same ideological work that earlier commodity-fetishism did for industrial capital: it naturalizes the structure that produced it and concentrates rhetorical authority in the people whose activity the structure happens to credit. That diagnosis is portable across publications, traditions, and political coalitions in a way the imago Dei argument is not.

Two further notes. First, the cluster picks up directly from last week's Compact piece by William Jones (a Future of Life Institute associate), which argued the encyclical should mobilize the Church for an AGI moratorium on human-cloning-precedent grounds. This week shows what Leo actually wrote — and the Jones-to-actual-Leo gap is itself a finding. Leo did the Catholic-social-teaching extension Jones predicted, but did not adopt the existential-risk vocabulary or the moratorium ask; he positioned the Church as a moral-anthropological interlocutor with the AI industry rather than as an adversary mobilizing against it. The Vatican's choice to seat Olah as a respondent at the rollout, rather than to deliver an against-Silicon-Valley anathema, is consistent with that read. Whether that is the more durable move or the missed-opportunity move is the question Ahmari and Douthat split on.

Second, this cluster sits in productive cross-pressure with this week's AI as utility cluster (Alexander, Smith, Tabarrok, Cowen — covered in the AI is just a tool — the engineering case against ontological dread section below), in which the assembled writers argue AI is a normal technology that is being integrated into existing economic and institutional pipelines without civilizational rupture. The two clusters are not contradictory — they are asking complementary questions about the same technology. The utility cluster asks: how does this engineering artifact get deployed, measured, and integrated? The Pope-Leo cluster asks: what account of the human person does the technology pressure, and what does the answer ground? The two together produce a sharper read than either gives in isolation: AI's normal-technology character is the right answer to the deployment question and the wrong answer to the anthropological question — and the cultural-and-political damage shows up at the seam, where the engineering question's answer gets imported into the anthropological one without the importation being noticed. This is the project's clearest unexpected-aligned-poles candidate this week: a Catholic encyclical and a heterodox-economist consensus arriving at structurally similar diagnoses of the AI-doom discourse from opposite poles, while the metaphysical-and-anthropological question Douthat names remains genuinely open.

Carry-forward: the Vatican's institutional positioning on AI now becomes a multi-week thread for the project — concretely, whether Magnifica Humanitas generates operational consequences (Vatican observer status at AI governance forums, bishops' conferences on AI labor displacement, formal Holy See engagement with Anthropic-and-peers) or remains primarily rhetorical. The Jones-to-actual-Leo gap and the Ahmari-Douthat reception split give the project two falsifiable axes to check against future weeks.

↩ Back to TOC

The end of the American Way of War — drone revolution and war-skeptic exhaustion

Five writers across the spectrum read the Iran war's tactical-win-strategic-loss outcome as system-level decline, not a Trump-specific blunder. The convergence runs from center-right institutional reporting through post-liberal realism to independent-left documentary work. The disagreement is operative: where is the binding constraint?

Four candidate locations sit on the table. Personnel-and-statutory architecture — the prioritizer Pentagon strategist (Shapiro-Ruge 2022 framework: China-focus over full pullback) is caged, the DNI (Director of National Intelligence) was hollowed at creation. Civic-strategic culture — the "American Way of War" substitutes technology for casualty tolerance from Korea forward. Macroeconomic fragility — IEEPA (International Emergency Economic Powers Act) tariffs stripped the shock buffer. Military technology — drones have killed combined-arms maneuver warfare (rapid armored breakthroughs into the enemy rear) until cheap anti-drone systems arrive.

The shape is informative: cross-orientation agreement on decline, irreducible disagreement on which layer binds, and prescriptions each writer's own diagnosis renders structurally unavailable.

  • Elbridge Colby is the institutional case for why the GOP-restrainer faction was always going to lose this fight. Lake profiles Colby — the Trump-1 DoD strategist now serving as Under Secretary of Defense for Policy in the second term, author of the 2021 Strategy of Denial — and gets the analytical clarification right: Colby is not a "restrainer" (the non-interventionist Quincy-Institute label) but a "prioritizer" (the 2022 Shapiro-Ruge term for those who want US resources shifted from the Middle East and Europe to deter China over Taiwan). The distinction explains how Colby can serve Trump on Iran while spending his pre-government career arguing against exactly this war: the prioritizer deal with Trump granted Colby the drafting pen on the National Defense Strategy, not a veto over war decisions. Lake's institutional reporting documents how the cage closed — the August 8 Wiles-Rubio memo restricting agency-level foreign engagements (formally a response to Colby's AUKUS submarine-pact missteps, operationally a constraint on his Taiwan-contingency diplomacy with Japanese and Australian counterparts), the held-up nominations of his protégés Alex Velez-Green and Austin Dahmer, House Armed Services Chair Mike Rogers's accusation that Colby usurped White House authority on the Romania brigade withdrawal. One administration official tells Lake "the president is not with him when it comes to Iran." The lieutenant is loyal; the doctrine has been demoted.

  • The Director of National Intelligence was structurally neutered at creation, and Gabbard's exit is the position working as designed. Klippenstein uses Tulsi Gabbard's resignation — officially citing her husband's health, narrated in the press as a martyrdom over Iran-policy clashes with Trump — to tell the documentary story of how the DNI (the post-9/11 Director of National Intelligence) was hollowed out before it was ever filled. Working from a CIA Studies in Intelligence twentieth-anniversary retrospective by the bill's lead author Senator Susan Collins, declassified Rumsfeld memos at the National Security Archive, and the Rumsfeld library, Klippenstein reconstructs the September-December 2004 maneuvering: the 9/11 Commission recommended a Goldwater-Nichols-style intelligence boss with real budget authority over the entire community; Defense Secretary Donald Rumsfeld and Vice President Dick Cheney — controlling four of the five largest US intelligence agencies through the Pentagon — used House Armed Services Chair Duncan Hunter to hold up the bill, then had Joint Chiefs Chair Gen. Richard Myers send a late-night letter making the Pentagon's case. Cheney personally walked the gutting language ("respect and not abrogate" the Defense Secretary's authority) to Hunter. Collins texted Lieberman about the fix during a Billy Joel concert. A later DNI, James Clapper, conceded in the same CIA journal that the four-word edit "effectively neutered the rest of the act." The structural read pairs with Lake: the position the administration needed to constrain — at Defense (Colby) and at the intelligence community (Gabbard) — were institutionally pre-defanged. Personnel swaps don't dissolve a statutory cage.

  • The "American Way of War" pattern is older than this administration and predicts the Iran outcome from Korea forward. Lind places the Iran war alongside Korea, Vietnam, Afghanistan, Iraq, Libya, and Syria as one continuous failure mode rather than a Trump-specific bad call. The structural ingredients: a domestic-political requirement to minimize US casualties drives reliance on air power, big-unit warfare, drones, and high-tech force in conflicts that actually require low-intensity counterinsurgency; rally-around-the-flag makes wars easy to start and hard to end, so presidents from Johnson through Bush through Trump-I pass quagmires to successors rather than absorb the political cost of withdrawal; the asymmetry of stakes (existential survival for adversary dictators, optional for US presidents) gives adversaries the advantage in any prolonged conflict. The historical compression has real costs — Korea was a near-victory with seventy years of South Korean flourishing as the upside, and lumping it with Iraq and Libya glosses over what "success" meant in each — but the structural finding survives the rough handling. Lind's prescription is constitutional rather than personnel-based: restore the congressional war-declaration power surrendered to the executive in the Truman years (no formal declaration of war since 1942). The implication is the same as Klippenstein's: this is a 1940s-1950s architecture problem, and "replacing one dove-turned-hawk with another in the White House" doesn't break the pattern.

  • The binding constraint may now be domestic economic fragility rather than military capacity. Polk — Vice President at the Footwear Distributors and Retailers of America, writing for War on the Rocks with operational supply-chain data — argues that Trump's broad-application tariffs under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA, the 1977 statute used to declare national emergencies and impose economic measures) and the Section 122 tariffs that replaced them after the Supreme Court ruling have stripped the US economy of its built-in agility to absorb shocks. The operative footwear case: 70% of a sneaker's cost has oil-price exposure, oil is a direct 30% feedstock, and IEEPA tariffs added 20-30% on top of traditional rates so some children's shoes faced 87.5% effective tax rates. A mid-2025 KPMG survey of 300 C-suite executives showed 43% of consumer-goods executives reporting 1-5% gross-margin declines. The Iran-war oil shock — gradually absorbable in a normal economy — now compounds the tariff stress: footwear prices rose 4.2% in April (fastest in 43 months), April CPI energy contribution exceeded 40% of the total increase, and Polk's strategic claim is that economic resilience is now a "hard ceiling" on US ability to sustain coercion. At $4.50/gallon gas with a tariff-driven inflation overhang and compressed household budgets, an administration has less political runway for prolonged military engagement than one with deeper shock-absorbing capacity. Polk's prescriptive thinness (build new resilience models, stop benchmarking against pre-tariff norms) is conspicuous given the granular diagnosis — a footwear-trade-association vice president cannot easily name the administration's policies as the target — but the diagnostic is strong and operationally specific in the way most economic-policy commentary is not.

  • Drones have ended combined-arms maneuver warfare, and the operational middle domain is the next domino. Fukuyama, writing on territory where he has genuine standing rather than his usual democracy-decline beat, frames the change through three classical air-power domains. Strategic (deep-rear targets — factories, rail junctions, power grids): already transformed. Ukrainian long-range drones have hit Russian oil and gas facilities thousands of kilometers from the border, scaled back the May 9 Red Square Victory Day parade for fear of in-ceremony strikes, and reportedly made Putin afraid of drone-assassination. The strategic mission Bomber Command did atrociously in WWII (Allied bombers forced by Nazi air defenses to fly at night, with accuracy so poor that the area-bombing of Dresden and Hamburg with hundreds of thousands of civilian dead was the response) is now done at precision by inexpensive drones. Close air support (front-line engagement): already transformed by FPV (First-Person-View) drones at $400-$40,000, produced by Ukraine in the millions per year, with the result that "combined-arms maneuver warfare" — the post-1939 doctrine of rapid armored breakthroughs that drive deep into the enemy rear to cut supply lines and capture territory — has been "made virtually impossible" by the FPV-saturated battlefield's complete visibility. Operational (10-100 km behind the front, supply lines, command posts, air defenses): being transformed now. Ukrainian medium-range drones and missiles have systematically dismantled Russian air defenses in Crimea, and Fukuyama's specific prediction is that a Russian withdrawal from the peninsula sometime in the coming year — an enormous political defeat for Putin — is the consequence. The economic logic is decisive and persistent: Patriot missile ~$4 million, Shahed drone ~$40,000, produced in much larger quantities, with US Gulf-state air defenses unable to prevent serious damage to their facilities from Iranian drones and missiles during Operation Epic Fury. The balance holds until someone fields a cheap-and-effective anti-drone system, which many are working on but no one has.

  • Boguslaw's Capitol Police scoop is the cross-domain extension of the same executive-overreach pattern. Boguslaw, reporting for Klippenstein's stack, obtains an April "Intelligence Note" from the U.S. Capitol Police Intelligence Services Bureau — the post-January-6 congressional intelligence arm that operates in parallel to the 18-member Executive Branch intelligence community — warning of public hostility to AI data centers while admitting upfront that "the US Capitol Police is not investigating any data center-motivated threats to Members of Congress." The note monitors social-media speech critical of data centers, distributes its findings to local police and state-level fusion centers, dedicates a full page to threats against OpenAI CEO Sam Altman (who is neither an elected official nor in the Capitol Police's purview), and frames a 70% public-opposition Gallup datapoint as a security problem rather than a policy signal. The piece sits adjacent to this cluster rather than inside it because the domain is AI-and-data-center politics rather than the Iran war — but the architectural pattern is the same one Klippenstein documents in the DNI piece: an intelligence apparatus deployed against the public's policy preferences while admitting it has no actionable nexus to the legislators it was created to protect. Different domain, same executive-state-creep mechanism.

The most striking feature of the cluster is what every prescription has in common: none of the writers proposes a fix their own diagnosis suggests will happen. Lind wants the Congress whose 2004 self-emasculation Klippenstein documents to reclaim the war-declaration power surrendered in the Truman era. Polk wants new resilience models the administration imposing the tariffs has no incentive to commission. Klippenstein wants to undo a 2004 capitulation whose primary beneficiary (the Pentagon-anchored intelligence community) still controls four of the five largest agencies. Fukuyama wants a cheap-and-effective anti-drone system that "many countries are working on but we are not there yet." Lake's implicit prescription — empower Colby's prioritizer doctrine — describes the position the cluster has just shown to be institutionally caged. Five layers of structural breakage with five layers of structurally unavailable fixes; the cross-orientation convergence (FP, UnHerd, WOTR, Persuasion, Klippenstein) reached through different rhetorical paths and different evidence bases — institutional reporting, historical pattern, supply-chain economics, strategic-studies taxonomy, documentary archive — is the finding, and the disagreement on which layer is binding is informative about why no unified prescription emerges. The mainstream press's tactical-reporting register cannot reach this shape because each layer's defeat looks like a different story.

Carry-forward: the iran-war-center-of-gravity-financial entry, which the project has been refining since April toward the political-economy and bypass-network layers, now extends to a sixth domestic-economic mechanism via Polk: tariff-induced supply-chain fragility as the operative domestic constraint on US power projection. The Lind-Polk-Fukuyama-Klippenstein-Lake convergence is the strongest cross-orientation evidence to date for a structural-decline reading that no single writer's account can sustain alone. A candidate new entry — the prescribed remedies for US strategic-architecture failure are all ones the prescribers concede their own diagnosis makes structurally unavailable — captures the meta-pattern at a generality the existing iran-time-direction open disagreement and bypass-network-as-post-american-architecture entry do not reach, and is testable against whether any of the proposed fixes (Congressional war-power reassertion, broad-tariff drawdown, DNI authority restoration, anti-drone systems, prioritizer-doctrine empowerment) advance materially over the next 12-24 months. The Fukuyama Crimea-withdrawal prediction ("it would not be at all surprising if the Russian command decided at some point in the coming year that its position in Crimea was untenable") is specific enough to track on the project's 12-month horizon.

↩ Back to TOC

Democrats — coalition design, message discipline, and the GOP's mirror-image dysfunction

Both major parties are diagnosed this week as leader-or-faction-dependent in ways that crowd out coalition design. On the Democratic side the dependency is factional — popularism, abundance liberalism, and process-reform camps each fight for the steering wheel. On the GOP side Ezra Klein and Nate Silver converge on a sharper claim: the party is now structurally subordinated to one man's preferences.

The organizing disagreement is about what kind of party dysfunction the Democrats are suffering from. Yglesias treats it as ideological — popularism (the Shor/Yglesias doctrine that Democrats should hew to median-voter views) versus the factions that euphemize the growth question. Kahn treats it as process — a DNC apparatus that shoots its own commissioned messenger. Smith treats it as affirmative-ideology-deficit. Du catches blue-state governors running the same Medicaid purges they publicly denounce. Klein and Silver supply the GOP mirror: Trump is endorsing scandal-plagued primary challengers and attacking his own swing-district incumbents in ways consistent with controlling the party, not winning the midterms.

  • Yglesias's four-piece program is more coherent than any single piece. Across the week he argues that progressives who keep believing economic populism can override unpopular cultural positions (the left-populist fallacy) are projecting their own issue-weighting onto voters — an NYT/Siena poll has rank-and-file Democrats wanting the party to move toward the center over the left 52 to 25, and Graham Platner's Maine Senate bid wins only if he abandons sex-segregated-sports and out-of-state-environmentalist positions, not because he's running against an imagined Trump-voting working class (Maine has trended left since Covid; Susan Collins's incumbency is the real obstacle). "Popularism" — the Yglesias/David Shor doctrine that Democrats should hew to the median voter on cultural questions and run on majoritarian economics — is the through-line. "Affordability" is the next iteration's evasion: it lets the Lina Khan neo-Brandeisian antitrust school, the degrowth environmentalists, the Piketty-Sanders inequality faction, and the Hewlett-Foundation-funded "Economy and Society" project (built explicitly to move policy away from growth-maximization) all claim alignment with voter cost-of-living concern without admitting they oppose the growth-and-rising-incomes mechanism that makes things actually more affordable. The deficit piece opens with the unusual concession that Yglesias was wrong on 2010 deficit doves, then argues post-Covid arithmetic (long-bond yields at post-2008 highs; CBO's 0.55-point rate-projection miss = $2T in 10-year spending; interest payments now exceeding both Medicaid and the Pentagon) means Democrats should reclaim deficit politics because it foregrounds their popular agenda (tax the rich, defend Social Security and Medicare) against unpopular GOP priorities. And the unions piece inverts a Roosevelt Institute paper urging abundance advocates to cater to labor, arguing instead that unions should adopt the abundance agenda because YIMBY housing, nuclear, and state-capacity-led construction (Josh Shapiro's union-labor I-95 rebuild as the model) are already concretely better for organized labor than the green-coalition defaults the United Mine Workers, IBEW, UAW, building trades, and Steelworkers all chafe against.

  • Kahn's contrarian defense of the DNC autopsy is the only piece treating the underlying document as having content. Kahn — associate editor at Yascha Mounk's center-left Persuasion — reads Paul Rivera's 192-page report (released after CNN obtained it, accompanied by Chair Ken Martin's public Substack disavowal "I am not proud of this product" and a wave of negative commentary from Michelle Goldberg, Rich Lowry, Noah Rothman, and Evan Barker) as exactly the technical, forward-looking after-action report Martin commissioned: close the male/rural-voter gap, fight even in unwinnable places to limit losses, do year-round messaging (Charlie Kirk's Turning Point USA as model), modernize digital advertising past shortened-TV-ad form, lean into negativity. The errors the DNC publicly flagged ("John Corzine" misspelling, "Matt Brevin" for Bevin, Mark Robinson's North Carolina vote share off by five points) are real but trivial. Kahn's Catch-22 analogy — ex-P.F.C. Wintergreen as the mail clerk who rejected battle orders for stylistic faults — is the diagnostic. This is the real intra-Dem fight worth tracking: Kahn names party dysfunction as process-level (a chair who shoots his own messenger and a DNC apparatus structurally incapable of receiving criticism) where Yglesias and the rest of the commentariat have been narrating it as ideological. The fight is not which prescription is right; it's whether the apparatus that would implement any prescription still functions.

  • Smith's "liberal nationalism" is the affirmative move the cluster otherwise lacks, but the engagement is incomplete. Smith builds out of a Twitter exchange between tech investor Balaji Srinivasan (who relocated to Singapore and argued American expatriated wealth owes no loyalty to the country that made it rich, because Red and Blue America are too polarized to constitute a unified "American people") and "Roman Helmet Guy" (the pseudonymous nationalist who accused Balaji of ingratitude). Smith conceals neither side's strongest point: mobile capital and ideals do matter, ethno-nationalism is an electoral non-starter (YouGov polling has Americans of both parties defining national identity by citizenship, Constitution-belief, law-abidance, and voting), but a country whose rich exit at the first sign of trouble cannot tax, defend itself, or fund public goods. His synthesis is FDR-era "civic religion": pluralist national identity, immigration-as-foundational, the Federal Writers Project, Don't Be a Sucker, the insistence that Americans of disparate backgrounds form "a single unified whole" capable of provisioning shared public goods. The piece concedes FDR's Japanese internment and segregation compromises as ceiling-not-trajectory. The structural weakness: Smith's chosen interlocutor (an anonymous pseudonymous Twitter account) is a softball; the affirmative case for liberal nationalism would land harder against the strongest available critics (post-liberal Compact voices on the right; Bruenig-adjacent socialist voices on the left), neither of whom appears in the piece.

  • Du's Free Press piece is the cross-publication anomaly the cluster needs surfaced. Du catalogs a "loud denial / quiet action" pattern across three Democratic states: Minnesota Governor Tim Walz publicly called YouTuber Nick Shirley a "delusional conspiracy theorist" for alleging widespread Somali-run-daycare Medicaid fraud while his Department of Human Services terminated 18,000+ inactive Medicaid providers and revalidated 5,500+ to avoid losing $2B in federal funds; AG Keith Ellison denounced Republican "viral panic" while running an investigation of 200+ healthcare providers. In California, Newsom's press office mocked Shirley with an AI-generated ghoulish image while AG Rob Bonta charged 14 hospice providers for $267M in fraudulent billing; the state assembly passed AB-2624 (the "Stop Nick Shirley Act," introduced by Bonta's wife) restricting disclosures about immigrant-serving businesses. In Maine, Governor Mills dismissed CMS director Mehmet Oz as a "TV doctor" while the state auditor admitted Medicaid monitoring was inadequate and quietly paused four high-fraud Medicaid categories. The criticism does not come from the right-coded register a Free Press piece on Democrats usually deploys; it is a procedural-coherence finding about substantive-vs-rhetorical incoherence in current Democratic state governance, and it is the only piece in this cluster that catches Democrats governing rather than strategizing.

  • The mirror-image diagnosis on the GOP is the cluster's structural finding. Silver constructs an 11-dimension party-strength rubric (electoral competitiveness, constructive governance, organizational architecture, advancing member interests, representing voter interests, brand image and agenda-setting, human capital, effective cartel discipline, securing long-term legal position, rational adaptation to defeat, compatibility with constitutional democracy) and grades three entities separately on each: Democrats at 2.30 GPA, Trump-the-individual at 1.89, and the "GOP ex-Trump" at 1.25 — "almost entirely a failure, except for the courts and sometimes effective state-level governance." Paxton's 64-36 demolition of Cornyn on Tuesday is the endpoint of the trajectory: "there's even less capacity for resistance to Trump from the rotted husk of what used to be called the Republican 'establishment' than might have been assumed." McKown-Dawson and Silver's companion piece argues Trump made what may be his costliest endorsement of the cycle by waiting until the last week of the runoff, choosing the scandal-plagued Paxton over the better-positioned Cornyn, and doing it at his lowest-ever approval (-19.3 net per Silver Bulletin's tracker, the worst for any president at this point in a nonconsecutive term). Their mechanics: Texas projects R+4 with generic candidates given the national D+7 generic-ballot lead, scandals typically move races by ~5 points, which puts a Paxton-Talarico contest at D+1 or tied; the ~$20M spent on the runoff is money Republicans could have spent defending Maine, Iowa, Florida, Alaska, and Ohio. The countervailing factor is Talarico's past "God is nonbinary" comment (made as a Presbyterian seminarian) and the fact that Texas hasn't elected a Democratic senator since Lloyd Bentsen in 1988.

  • Smith's second piece catalogs three first-term-rhetorical-strategy-as-second-term-policy moves at once. The "are you tired" piece consolidates three discrete administration disasters that each would have ended any prior presidency: USCIS's late-May rule eliminating Adjustment of Status (the standard pathway by which most green-card recipients other than investors transition from temporary visas), which would expel most high-skilled foreign workers from an AI industry where immigrants founded the largest companies and dominate top-50 AI research; the operational defeat in Iran (Iran has rebuilt ~70% of its prewar missiles and 90% of underground facilities during the ceasefire, restarted drone production, retains de facto control of the Strait of Hormuz, and is bumping US gasoline prices and inflation while US weapons stocks remain dangerously depleted with no rapid replenishment); and the $1.776B "Anti-Weaponization" settlement plus 3,600 Q1 Trump-portfolio stock trades suspiciously timed to administration actions on Nvidia, Intel, Palantir, and Boeing. Smith reads the pace as deliberate: the second Trump term is using the first term's DDOS rhetorical strategy as actual policy, overloading public attention so that no single disaster lands.

The cluster's organizing disagreement is about what kind of party dysfunction Democrats are suffering from. Yglesias treats it as ideological — Democrats euphemize the growth question because their factions actually disagree about whether growth is desirable, and "affordability" and "anti-oligarchy" cover that disagreement. Kahn treats it as process — the autopsy itself is competent; the dysfunction is a DNC chair too thin-skinned to publish his consultant's findings and an apparatus that responds to internal critique with public messenger-shooting. Smith treats it as affirmative-ideology-deficit — Democrats have abandoned the FDR civic-pluralist program that would let them ask wealthy citizens for taxes and patriotic loyalty in exchange for shared public goods. Silver's mirror-image read holds these together by externalizing the comparison: the Democratic dysfunction looks much worse if you don't compare it to the GOP-ex-Trump's, which is structurally worse, and to Trump-the-individual's effective cartel discipline operating outside democratic norms. The four diagnoses are not mutually exclusive: the party may need to fix the process problem (Kahn) before the ideological problem (Yglesias) becomes legible, and may need the affirmative-ideology move (Smith) for the popularism prescription to land as anything other than retreat. Yglesias's four-piece platform is the most internally coherent prescription on the table, but it sits inside a party that — per Kahn's reading — is currently incapable of receiving any prescription as anything other than an attack. The most operational handle is Texas: McKown-Dawson and Silver's mechanics make a Talarico Senate flip a real near-term possibility, and an unhealthy-GOP year at Trump's lowest approval could literally cost Republicans the chamber, changing what the Yglesias/Kahn/Smith disagreements are even about.

Carry-forward: The donor-class-enforcement-decay entry opened last week extends this week into a sharper leader-or-faction-dependency-in-both-parties finding — the same machinery for coalitional discipline that has degraded inside the GOP is degrading inside the DNC, and Silver's 11-dimension rubric supplies a falsifiable framework for tracking which party recovers it first. The McKown-Dawson Texas-flip mechanics open a new candidate carry-forward on whether the 2026 midterms will produce a Senate flip via avoidable Republican strategic sequencing rather than via Democratic positive performance; if a Talarico win materializes alongside continued Yglesias-pattern Democratic strategic dysfunction, the project's working understanding of "why Democrats are not capitalizing on Trump unpopularity" (carried forward since April) needs revision toward "GOP self-inflicted losses are doing the work the Democratic prescription debate has not yet resolved." The Kahn-vs-consensus dispute on the DNC autopsy is the candidate open disagreement: is Democratic party dysfunction primarily process-level (apparatus incapable of receiving critique) or ideological (factional disagreement about growth that "affordability" papers over)? Both camps have evidence; the resolution mechanism is whether the next DNC chair, or the next presidential primary cycle, produces an apparatus that can publish a competent after-action report under its own name.

↩ Back to TOC

Industrial depth and the abundance question — housing, energy, and what "build" means

Supply-side outcomes are the product of specific institutional choices — about ownership, market structure, and production economics — not a generic market mechanism working itself out. That is the operative finding across the cluster. Every writer asks who benefits from the blockage and what would actually unblock it.

The organizing disagreement is over where the binding constraint sits. Cass and Stoller locate it at the state-policy lever — tariffs and industrial-depth framing for Cass, antitrust and an institutional-investor ban (the Warren-Trump ROAD Act, the 21st Century ROAD to Housing Act capping single-family-home purchases) for Stoller, plus a California gasoline-cartel exposé in which CARBOB (the state's specialty cleaner-blend requirement) functions as an entry barrier the lobby protects. Potter argues the constraint is baked into the production function itself: his "idiot index" (the ratio of finished-product cost to raw-material input cost) shows homebuilding's transformation gap is already too thin for scale economies to compress. Pearce reads it as ownership-structural — a short-line rail revival that only patient family capital, not institutional investors, would underwrite.

The Bessent speech (Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent's May 29 Reagan Library address) sits above the four mechanism pieces as the rhetorical event that gives the cluster its frame.

  • The Republican Party has formally adopted industrial-depth-as-sovereignty as its conservative-economics frame. Cass (Commonplace; American Compass founder) reads Treasury Secretary Bessent's "While America Slept" speech as a movement-conservative milestone rather than as ordinary administration messaging. Bessent's specific claim — that "for a nation that cannot manufacture, mine, ship, or refine its needs gradually cedes its strength — and sovereignty — to others" — is what previous GOP Treasury Secretaries explicitly rejected as comparative-advantage misunderstanding. The "reservoir" metaphor (a stock that's drawn from and replenished, capable of deployment when needed; the market will not maintain it if water can be sourced more cheaply elsewhere) elevates industrial strength past good-jobs/strong-communities concerns, past macroeconomic-resilience concerns, to the level of grand strategy. The careful Reagan-citation choice ("we don't hide from our mistakes") lets a Republican Treasury Secretary at a Reagan Forum repudiate Reagan-era economic policy without explicitly attacking Reagan. Cass is the audience this speech is engineered for; the triumphalism is warranted as evidence the war is won at the rhetorical level but worth scrutinizing as a claim about policy machinery — saying it once at the Reagan Library is not the same as it having displaced free-trade economics inside the GOP coalition.

  • The Warren-Trump 21st Century ROAD to Housing Act passed 396-13, banning most institutional-investor purchases of existing single-family homes — but Build-to-Rent gets a carve-out that probably gives back most of the win. Stoller (BIG; antitrust-left newsletter, American Economic Liberties Project research director) traces the legislative mechanics: Trump's January executive order targeting "Wall Street treating America's neighborhoods like a trading floor" (and calling the big homebuilders an OPEC-like cartel sitting on "2 Million empty lots") synced with a Tim Scott (R) / Elizabeth Warren (D) Banking Committee bill that grew to include a 350-home-per-investor cap. The empirical case is strong — FTC findings against Invitation Homes (a Blackstone spinoff) for "juicing this hog" through deceived renters and junk fees; Federal Reserve economists finding institutional buyers raise rents 60% more than average on acquisition. The Senate passed it 89-10 in March. The House fight pitted Republican French Hill (Financial Services chair, getting private-equity money) and 87-year-old Maxine Waters (the Democratic ranking member, who used to be a tenant-protection icon and is now allying with Hill against the ownership cap, backed by Hakeem Jeffries and what Stoller calls "the Abundance world" of advocates) against the White House. The compromise: the ban applies to existing housing stock but exempts new "Build-to-Rent" construction — single-family homes Wall Street finances from scratch to rent out, the sector that doubled its market share between 2021 and 2024 and is now where institutional capital is focused. Stoller flags but minimizes the carve-out; his own analysis predicts most of the institutional-landlord story will now route through it. The bill is still the most significant federal housing legislation in decades, and Stoller's Carter analogy (a fish-out-of-water president doing populist economics his own party hates, whose successor will get credit for it the way Reagan got credit for Carter's airline/banking/trucking deregulation) is a real prediction with forward content.

  • California's $59B "mystery gasoline surcharge" since 2015 is a Chevron-led refining oligopoly with unusual vertical retail integration, not the environmental over-regulation Chevron blames. Stoller (BIG) walks through the cost stack on $5.26/gallon California gas: environmental rules account for 7%, state and local taxes another 14%, crude oil 44%, and refiner-plus-distributor margin about 30% — the bulk of the gap with the national average. Since a 2015 fire at the Torrance Refinery, California's price premium over the national average has jumped from $0.54 to $1.23 per gallon and never closed — what California's Division of Petroleum Market Oversight (the state agency that monitors petroleum markets) named the "mystery gasoline surcharge," now estimated at $59 billion in cumulative cost to state residents. Two structural facts drive it: the top-four refiners control 90% of California capacity (Chevron alone at 33%) versus 48% nationally, and 50% of retail sales flow through vertically-integrated branded stations with exclusive supply contracts versus just 9% nationally — the Chevron-branded premium runs 60-80¢/gallon against a 31¢ branded-station average statewide and 6¢ nationally. The rhetorical kill shot: the Western States Petroleum Association (the California oil lobby) is publicly lobbying for keeping California's specialty CARBOB blend (California Reformulated Gasoline Blendstock for Oxygenate Blending — the state's cleaner, harder-to-import gasoline standard), because the specialty-blend requirement is what blocks new entrants from undercutting incumbent refiners. CARBOB is the case study: environmentally good, competitively exclusionary, defended by the lobby. The piece's deeper structural finding — incumbents win not by lobbying against regulation but by ensuring the regulatory architecture creates moats only they can clear — is the cleanest statement of the supply-side mechanics this cluster keeps circling.

  • Homebuilding lacks the economies of scale that transformed every other industry because its output-to-input cost ratio is already too low for scale to act on. Potter (Construction Physics; the Atoms-not-Bits engineering writer promoted to the roster from discovery this past week) marshals three independent evidence streams. First, US homebuilding is unconcentrated — over 65,000 firms, top 4 firms at 18% market share versus 90% in aircraft, 86% in wireless, 58% in autos. Second, gross margins don't track scale: Lennar (over 80,000 homes a year) has nearly identical margins to United Homes Group (1,192 homes), and a Harvard Joint Center for Housing Studies study found large homebuilders' construction costs are actually higher than smaller ones'. Third, in manufactured housing — which eliminates the obvious obstacles (national HUD code instead of varying local codes; controlled factory environment; stable crews) — production is still spread across 100-300-person, ~200,000-square-foot factories making a few hundred homes each, with smaller manufacturers (Nobility, Legacy) showing better gross margins than larger ones (Champion, Cavco). Cavco operates two factories in North Carolina 120 miles apart and three in the Phoenix metro; if scale paid off, they'd consolidate. Potter then offers an explanatory framework via Elon Musk's "idiot index" — the ratio of finished-product cost to raw-material input cost. Oil refining runs ~1.2-1.3; a BMW i3 runs ~1.8; a US single-family home (hard costs roughly 50% labor, 50% materials) runs ~2 — already about the same as a high-volume manufactured car. The transformation between lumber/drywall/concrete and a finished home is so modest that the gap economies of scale could compress is too thin to justify the capital investment. "Bubba and his truck" beats Katerra-style factory automation because there isn't enough margin to pay for the automation.

  • A family-owned Idaho-Washington short-line railroad revival is the ground-level instance of what the "industrial commons" looks like when patient ownership exists. Pearce (Commonplace; Brad Pearce reports the magazine's place-and-industry beat from his own town of Palouse, Washington) tells the story of the Washington, Idaho, & Montana Railway (WI&M), a 1900s lumber short-line that was abandoned in 2018 after Watco, the regional rail operator, couldn't make it pay. The revival required a hobbyist (Jason Hill, who learned trains on a Mount Rainier tourist line), a family-owned mill willing to absorb the capital cost (Bennett Lumber Products Inc., 280 employees), and several years of patient work — $millions in restoration, 11,000 new railroad ties at $70-$100 each, a leased 1956 EMD GP9 diesel locomotive (#1838, around $115K used versus $2M for a new engine that, ironically, would last less long because its electrical components will be discontinued). The first revenue run carried seven cars (about 21 truckloads) of Idaho white pine in September 2026. Pearce's argument is ownership-structural: an institutional-investor-owned mill would not have justified the long-horizon, community-benefit-inclusive economics this project required. The supporting detail is real (newer 1990s-built engines are already obsolete because their electrical components have been discontinued is a notable concrete data point cutting against the newer-is-better assumption) but the "family business required" claim is stipulated rather than demonstrated — the deal pencils because rail is genuinely cheaper per board-foot for some customers, not as charity. The piece works as the ground-level mechanism under Bessent's reservoir metaphor: where Cass argues the industrial commons in the abstract, Pearce shows what it looks like to refill it in one town.

The cluster's contrarian finding is the most interesting one: every writer agrees on the diagnostic (supply outcomes are produced by specific institutional choices), but the four mechanisms they identify locate the binding constraint in incompatible places. Cass and Stoller treat it as state-policy-fixable — tariffs and industrial policy on Cass's side, antitrust and anti-institutional-ownership bans on Stoller's. Pearce treats it as ownership-structural: patient family capital versus institutional, with the implication that policy can only do so much if the ownership form itself selects for short-horizon decisions. Potter's piece is the cluster's analytical kill shot, and the other four writers are mostly talking around it: most industries' economies of scale are exhausted at the production-function level, and political fixes can shift who captures the cost but cannot create scale where the math doesn't admit it. The Warren-Trump ROAD Act bans institutional ownership of existing homes; Potter's framework predicts the binding constraint is elsewhere — the homebuilding production process itself, where the gap between input cost and output cost is already too thin for scale economies to do work. If Potter is right, neither Cass's industrial-depth-via-tariffs nor Stoller's anti-investor-ownership-via-antitrust will move housing supply much; both will redistribute who profits from the existing supply, which is itself worth doing but a smaller claim than either author is making.

The cross-theme reading: the Yglesias union-abundance reframing in the Democrats' strategy cluster — in which Yglesias inverts the standard "abundance should cater to unions" argument and contends instead that unions should adopt the abundance agenda (YIMBY + state capacity + nuclear) because it's concretely better for labor outcomes than the green/anti-growth coalition unions currently default to — is the abundance debate from inside Democratic coalition strategy. The Cass/Stoller/Potter/Pearce cluster is the abundance debate from inside post-neoliberal supply-side mechanics on the right. Both clusters are talking about whether and how to "build" without engaging each other's framing — Yglesias treats the question as coalition design for a Democratic majority; Cass and Stoller treat it as state-policy mechanics for a post-comparative-advantage right. The convergence the two clusters share is that the post-1995 consensus on supply-side outcomes through deregulation-and-trade-liberalization is over on both sides of the aisle; the disagreement is over which mechanism replaces it.

Carry-forward: This cluster sharpens the donor-class-enforcement-decay finding from last week's cycle. Cass's three-votes-in-a-week reading from May 24 (Chamber/Norquist/WSJ machinery operational but no longer reliably producing votes) gets a positive-side complement here: a 396-13 House vote on a bill banning corporate ownership of single-family homes, with the Warren-Trump alliance Stoller traces, is the kind of legislative outcome the previous donor-class-discipline equilibrium foreclosed. The forward-loaded claim worth tracking: whether the cross-coalitional consensus extends beyond housing (the easiest case because both populist coalitions agree on the diagnosis) to less symmetric domains — California gasoline-cartel antitrust, industrial-policy tariffs (where Cass's frame is operational but Stoller's antitrust-left frame is skeptical of the protectionist incidence), and homebuilding deregulation (where Potter's production-function constraint suggests both coalitions' policy levers are mostly redistribute-the-incidence rather than expand-the-supply moves). The next 12-24 months should produce additional cross-coalitional supply-side bills that test whether the housing template generalizes or whether the cluster's apparent unity collapses on the next more contested domain.

↩ Back to TOC

AI as utility — the tool-not-totem case

The operative finding is that AI works on the narrow tasks already fielded, and the bottleneck has migrated from model capability to institutional deployment. The durable open question is which kinds of human judgment stay load-bearing once technical execution is commoditized. That reframe is what the cluster contributes.

This week's Pope Leo XIV cluster is the unexpected-aligned pole. It treats AI as ontological challenge — imago Dei, personhood, idolatry — while this cluster treats it as utility. Same technology, complementary lenses: ontological-stakes register and engineering-deployment register asking different questions about one artifact. Neither contradicts the other, and the cultural damage of the next several years is most likely to land at the seam, where an engineering-question answer gets imported into the anthropological one without notice.

The four pieces here share a deployment-positive prior that runs against the heterodox default of bubble-talk and displacement-anxiety. Their convergence on satellite national accounts (specialized GDP-measurement modules), alignment-and-verification work, and fielded clinical results is the cluster's organizing claim.

  • AI-assisted deliberation is now a credible substitute for institutional voter-information infrastructure. Alexander (Astral Codex Ten) reports a 10-race informal test on the June 2026 California primary — prompting Claude with a self-description and a request for candidate bios, policies, endorsements, and a recommendation per race, then comparing Claude's picks against his eventual votes. Self-graded result: 5 A grades (matched his eventual vote), 3 B (his second-choice in a large field), 2 C (he disagreed but saw the reasoning), 0 F. The concrete artifacts he reproduces — Claude's Superintendent of Public Instruction analysis (building an explicit ideological filter from his stated commitments and ranking each candidate against it) and the Peralta Community College parcel-tax analysis (flagging that the campaign's largest single donor is a $678K-contract flooring vendor) — do the thing standard voter guides do not: they synthesize candidate-specific signals at the granularity of an attentive friend who has actually done the research. Alexander frames the result as a load-bearing positive case for what he calls "post-AGI futures" (the family of questions about what an AGI-arrives-and-then-what world looks like for ordinary citizens), arguing AI-assisted political deliberation lowers the personalized-research cost by one to two orders of magnitude and structurally improves democratic information-processing without depending on any specific elite institution. The most interesting buried observation: three of four divergent picks Alexander preferred to Claude's were ones where Claude was asked to ignore his preferences — suggesting the user's stated ideological prompt was producing rhetorically-aligned rather than competence-aligned recommendations.

  • The Doctronic / Intermountain Utah pilot is the cluster's strongest fielded-deployment result. Tabarrok (Marginal Revolution) flags the first published review of an AI-driven prescription-refill program at Intermountain Health (the integrated Utah-based health system that has been a long-running natural experiment for new clinical-decision protocols). On the 72% of refill requests the AI was confident enough to auto-approve, at least one of two reviewing physicians agreed with the AI in 97% of cases; on the remaining 28% that the AI escalated to a human telehealth appointment rather than approve, physicians judged 69% of escalations appropriate and 31% over-cautious. Tabarrok notes overcaution is the correct failure mode for a new system. The result is a real fielded outcome on a meaningful clinical-throughput task, not a benchmark — which is the harder bar. The honest caveat his summary doesn't surface: 97% physician-agreement is on the easier auto-approve subset (the AI selected which cases it would commit to); the harder question is whether the 28% escalation set captures all the genuinely hard cases or whether some misroute into the auto-approve bucket and lock in errors. The cluster's load-bearing claim is nonetheless real: compute costs decline ~10x every five years, demand for primary care keeps rising, and the U.S. physician-shortage problem reframes from a structural-supply problem into an engineering-deployment problem with a known cost curve.

  • Verification-and-alignment is the same problem and is the cluster's candidate for the durable human comparative advantage. Smith (Noahpinion) makes the cluster's most analytically portable conceptual move: as AI commoditizes technical output (the actual writing, coding, drafting that consumed most knowledge-worker time), what remains scarce is the upstream judgment of what the user actually wants and the downstream judgment of whether the output actually delivered it. He calls this "alignment-and-verification work" and argues it generalizes from the technical alignment literature down to ordinary employment: once any technically-competent system can produce the artifact, the remaining cognitive task is the human-judgment task of knowing what to ask for and confirming you got it. The "workslop" phenomenon documented by BetterUp Labs and the Stanford Social Media Lab (low-effort AI output that shifts the verification burden downstream onto the receiver) is his empirical anchor; the middle-manager class (the Office Space Bill Lumbergh archetype) is his counter-intuitive winner. He stacks evidence that AI cannot reliably verify other AI — Xu et al.'s resume experiment showing 67-82% self-preference bias, hallucinated-citation rates rising in biomedical papers, Ryan Greenblatt's catalog of reward-hacking — and concludes the verification work is irreducibly human, indefinitely. The cluster's strongest version of this claim is the verification-equals-alignment unification; the weaker version (that the failure modes of AI-checking-AI are persistent rather than temporary) is what needs defending and Smith does not fully defend. He also elides the wage-distribution implication: if a small class of verifiers captures rents while the median worker presses the slop button, that is hollowing-out, not reassurance.

  • AI is finally entering the national accounts — and the measurement methodology is doing all the work. Cowen (Marginal Revolution) flags a new NBER working paper by Anton Korinek and Patrick McKelvey (Korinek is a University of Virginia economist on AI macroeconomics; the paper attempts the first systematic AI-in-GDP accounting) estimating that quality-adjusted U.S. AI production grew over 2,000% annually in 2024 and 2025 — driven primarily by algorithmic progress, then hardware-efficiency gains, then data-center capacity. The headline 2,000%+ figure is quality-adjusted, meaning a model judged "10x better" on the chosen benchmark counts as 10x more output; the benchmark choice (Massive Multitask Language Understanding, code-eval, compute-equivalent, etc.) is load-bearing and contested, which is where the methodology fight will actually live. The grounded number underneath is the nominal $250B in 2025 AI gross domestic product — large in absolute terms but ~0.85% of U.S. GDP, and most of it AI-firm revenue rather than consumer-surplus capture. The cluster's most policy-actionable claim is not the growth figure itself but the procedural one: Korinek-McKelvey argue statistics agencies should build "satellite national accounts" for AI now (the term of art for specialized GDP-measurement modules that sit alongside the core accounts, as already exist for tourism, healthcare, and arts-and-culture), because the 2000s software-productivity-paradox argument took a decade to resolve methodologically and by the time it did the policy question had moved on. Doing the methodology work upfront is cheap insurance against repeating that lag.

The cluster's structural meta-finding is the deployment-vs-totem split itself. This week's Pope Leo cluster is the most concentrated articulation the project has seen of the ontological-stakes register — what does AI mean for the imago Dei, for human dignity, for the personhood-and-idolatry challenge Anthropic researchers now raise. This cluster is the parallel articulation of the engineering-deployment register: how does the artifact get integrated, measured, and bottlenecked. The two are not contradictory and not even in competition — they are asking complementary questions about the same technology. Together they sharpen each other: AI's normal-technology character is the right answer to the deployment question and the wrong answer to the anthropological one, and the cultural-and-political damage of the next several years is most likely to land at the seam, where the engineering question's answer gets imported into the anthropological one without the importation being noticed. This is the project's clearest unexpected-aligned-poles convergence this week — a heterodox-economist-and-rationalist consensus on the engineering side reaching a structurally compatible diagnosis to a Catholic encyclical on the anthropological side, with the measurement-and-deployment findings of this cluster supplying the empirical ground the Pope Leo cluster's ontological-stakes arguments are conducted against.

The within-cluster open question is distributional. Smith's verification-as-future-of-work story, Tabarrok's compute-cost-collapse story, and Cowen's $250B-and-growing story all describe an economy in which AI output becomes abundant and cheap while the supervisory, verification, and judgment-and-taste functions become scarcer and more valuable. That is a description of increased inequality and middle-skill hollowing-out, not a reassuring "humans will still have jobs" finding — and the cluster's writers do not quite engage that implication. Alexander's voter-guide case partly cuts against this (a personalized-research benefit available to any citizen with a Claude subscription levels rather than concentrates), but the cost asymmetry (a $20/month subscription is non-trivial for most U.S. households) qualifies the leveling claim. The cluster establishes that AI deployment is real, fielded, and measurable; the political-economy follow-on questions of who captures the gains and who bears the displacement remain open.

Carry-forward: the cluster strengthens the ai-displaces-no-represented-constituency carry-forward by adding a positive mechanism — verification-and-alignment as the durable human comparative advantage — that explains why displacement will be uneven across the skill distribution rather than universal: the workers whose value-add was technical execution face the hollowing-out; the workers whose value-add was judgment-and-taste face sharpening. The Korinek-McKelvey paper supplies the first credible quantitative anchor for the project's standing question of whether AI's economic impact is being undermeasured by national accounts; the satellite-accounts prescription is a falsifiable check the project can return to (do U.S. statistical agencies open an AI satellite-accounts working group within the next 12-24 months). A new tracking thread on the engineering-deployment-vs-ontological-stakes split — already articulated this week as the Theme 06 / Theme 02 cross-pressure — is worth following as a multi-week question of whether the two registers continue to develop in parallel or begin to interfere.

↩ Back to TOC

The Compact cultural cluster — yuppies, post-literacy, and the post-liberal cultural register

The cluster's operative finding is methodological: cultural surface-events are evidence of class-and-institutional structure, not standalone discourse. A tradwife novel's reception, an Odyssey film backlash, a Title IX (1972 federal sex-equity statute) collision, a commencement address — each becomes a window onto the operative cultural class. Political consequences follow from that diagnosis, not from the symptom.

The organizing disagreement, conducted almost entirely inside Compact, is over which class is operative. Candidates: the financialized yuppie professional; the legally-tethered foreign laborer subject to mudsill theory (Hammond's 1858 Senate defense of slavery via a permanent laboring underclass); the post-NIL-settlement (House v.
