The piece does the rare and useful work of taking community voices seriously without treating them as decoration — Brown-Lane in Cherry Hill and McKown in Patterson Park are quoted at length with specific operational claims about what changed, not just affect. That texture is what makes the article more credible than a typical "academic confirms our priors" crime piece.

↩ Back to TOC

Why are Murders Down in Baltimore?

Alex Tabarrok · 2026-05-29 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Charles Fain Lehman's Free Press piece on Baltimore's homicide collapse, plus Tabarrok's own 2015 prediction (and 2019 "crime wave" paper) that the post-Freddie Gray arrest collapse would tip Baltimore into a high-crime equilibrium that strained-police-resources would self-sustain.

April 2026 saw four homicides — the lowest single-month total since at least 1970 — and the city is on pace for under 100 murders this year vs. 323 in 2022. Tabarrok argues two things are happening: (1) a national post-George-Floyd-excess restoration of policing is providing tailwind, and (2) Baltimore-specific work under a new mayor and prosecutor — Group Violence Reduction Strategy (GVRS), modeled on Operation Ceasefire — is intervening before retaliatory shootings cycle, because gang-adjacent murders are highly predictable (same associates, predictable timing). Carrots (job training, tattoo removal, relocation offers to known associates of victims) plus sticks (a personal letter from Mayor Scott saying the city is watching) appear to be working.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is the same-week third major piece on the crime drop, alongside Lehman (Free Press) and Yglesias (Slow Boring long-form). Tabarrok's angle is distinct from both: he's claiming the data vindicates a specific theoretical move (his crime-wave equilibrium model, where elevated crime mechanically reduces conviction probability and locks in further crime) and a specific policy instrument (focused deterrence on the small predictable subset of murders). The Lehman summary Tabarrok cites does the empirical heavy lift; Tabarrok's contribution is to graft it onto his prior theoretical framework — a legitimate move and one of MR's strengths as a venue.

The piece's weakest spot is the relative weight on the two mechanisms. Tabarrok concedes "national trends confound" — but does not work through how much of Baltimore's drop is national-tailwind versus GVRS-specific, which matters for the policy export claim. If Baltimore is dropping at the same rate as Detroit and Memphis (where GVRS is not the active intervention), the focused-deterrence story is doing less work than the post is suggesting. The honest version of "Carrots, sticks, and a little Pre-Cog" is that the program is plausibly contributing on top of a national tide — not that it explains the collapse. (Yglesias's same-week piece is more careful about this decomposition; reading the two together is the right move.)

The "Pre-Cog" framing — retaliation is predictable, so target the predictable subset — is a real insight, but it's worth flagging that this only addresses a fraction of murders (gang-cycle ones). Domestic homicides, robbery-gone-wrong, and stranger murders are not in the focused-deterrence theater, and a city whose murder rate is dominated by them won't see Baltimore-like gains.

↩ Back to TOC

The amazing — somewhat mysterious — crime drop

Matthew Yglesias · 2026-05-28 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Jeff Asher's projection that 2026 will produce America's lowest homicide rate on record; the Council on Criminal Justice's annual report; John Roman's Substack analysis showing crime is below pre-Covid counterfactual; James Comey's 2015 "Ferguson Effect" claim; Richard Rosenfeld's reversal-published 2016 paper confirming Ferguson Effect; Roland Fryer's research on DOJ pattern-or-practice investigations vs. viral protest events; the 2020 Hopkins study showing protest demographics were more cross-ideologically representative than the 2020 electorate; Gordon Dahl and Stefano DellaVigna's 2008 paper "Does Movie Violence Increase Violent Crime?" (incapacitation via cinema attendance); Lindo/Swenson/Waddell on The Ultimate Fighter TV show reducing crime through early exposure; the Brooke Pinto-Bowser Secure DC Act and the DC police-chase-and-pardon case; the Boudin recall and Brooke Jenkins replacement in SF; the Mamdani 2020-stance walkback after election.

This piece is a transcript of The Argument podcast, with Jerusalem Demsas (Atlantic / Slow Boring) as Yglesias's interlocutor; she pushes back throughout. Yglesias's central claim is that the 2020-21 crime spike was primarily caused by reduced police effort in response to the George Floyd protests (citing Roland Fryer's research that DOJ pattern-or-practice investigations help departments but viral protest events depress police activity and raise crime), and that the 2024-25 record decline is the same mechanism in reverse — a "national vibe shift" after Trump's election and after progressive prosecutor recalls (Boudin in SF replaced by Brooke Jenkins; Mamdani walking back his 2020 anti-police statements; Mayor Bowser's DC press conference on Secure DC marking the inflection point in DC trends). Demsas concedes the police-effort mechanism is real and operative in places like SF (where police "weren't even doing traffic stops"), but argues against monocausality — Covid co-location of would-be shooters, school closures, suspended traffic enforcement, the cross-national pattern (Denmark -17% homicides, UK -12%, Germany +modest) all matter; she also defends the 2020 protests on their own merits, citing the Hopkins study that protest demographics were more cross-representative than the 2020 electorate. Yglesias concedes some of this but holds that the US's unique anti-police protest is what drove the US-Germany divergence; cross-national co-Covid conditions weren't sufficient. The conversation pivots to root causes (the structural problem is recruiting: liberal cities run by Democrats produce few liberal applicants for police jobs; current cops are aging out, Gen X, mostly Republican; academy classes don't fill even with increased funding) and to a "Police for America" proposal modeled on Teach for America — a TFA-style program that would create a generation of college-educated people with operational policing credibility for criminal-justice policymaking. Demsas argues for police-union reform (work-rule bargaining specifically) as the highest-leverage intervention. The "peer review" segment closes with the Dahl-DellaVigna movie-violence-as-incapacitation paper, and Yglesias's broader theory that "things young men like to do" function as crime-reduction mechanisms by absorbing the rolling-downhill propensity to violence rather than addressing distal "root causes."

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is a long-form podcast transcript published as an article, and the genre matters: it's not Yglesias presenting a polished argument but Yglesias in real-time dialogue with Demsas, who is doing the work an editor would otherwise do — pushing back, conceding strategically, and forcing him to name his actual mechanism. The piece is more honest about uncertainty than a Yglesias column would be ("you are obviously correct that things were more deadly in the United States" is not Yglesias's solo register; that's Demsas pushing him), and it gains analytical credibility from that.

The core empirical claim — that the 2020-21 crime spike was primarily caused by the police-effort retreat following protests — is supported by the cross-national divergence (Western countries with COVID-but-no-anti-police-protests saw crime fall; the US and Germany are the major outliers, and Germany's increase was much smaller despite similar pandemic conditions) and by the city-level pattern (cities where there was a visible "signal of changed mood" — the Boudin recall in SF, the Secure DC press conference, Mamdani's walkback — see inflection points that don't correspond cleanly to legislative changes). Yglesias's reading is plausible, and Demsas concedes the police-effort mechanism is real. The disagreement is about weight: is it the predominant cause (Yglesias) or one of several (Demsas)?

The strongest counter to Yglesias's monocausal frame, which the piece does not adequately address, is Demsas's co-location-of-would-be-shooters point. The research she gestures at — that gun-violence networks involve people who are normally physically dispersed during the workday/school day but were co-located during Covid lockdowns — is a genuine alternative mechanism that doesn't require any reference to police effort, and it predicts exactly the 2020-21 spike. The cross-national comparison Yglesias relies on doesn't cleanly distinguish: Denmark and UK have very different gun-violence baselines and different lockdown patterns; "no anti-police protests + crime fell" doesn't disprove the co-location story for the US, where gun-violence-network dynamics are unusually concentrated. A careful synthesis would say: police effort matters AND co-location matters AND they're not independent (cops doing fewer traffic stops + everyone co-located in the same neighborhood compounds the effect). Yglesias resists this synthesis because it weakens his single-cause framing, which his anti-progressive-activist polemic depends on.

The piece's recruiting-crisis subsection is the most underrated analytical move in it, and it's where Yglesias's framework actually has more bite than the headline argument. The claim that big-city Democratic mayors face a structural recruiting problem — liberals don't want to be cops, cops are mostly Republican and aging out, academy classes don't fill even with raised funding, the existing officer corps lives in distant exurbs or "weird cop neighborhoods" — is a genuine fact about contemporary US municipal governance that gets very little airtime. It also undercuts both progressive ("defund and replace with social workers") and conservative ("just hire more cops") proposals, because the operative constraint is on the supply side of policing labor, not the demand side. The "Police for America" proposal is a reasonable response to this — and Demsas's quick "I think Detect for America is a good idea" concession is interesting, because it represents a genuine cross-faction agreement on a politically risky idea that neither side has yet championed.

Yglesias's argument has a structural blind spot worth flagging: he treats progressive activist preferences as the explanatory variable for both the protest movement and the subsequent police retreat, but he doesn't engage Demsas's point that "anti-police affect" was authentically generated by the Floyd video (visceral, unambiguous, 9-minute close-up) rather than by activist messaging. If the protest movement was itself a response to a viral, visceral fact pattern that doesn't repeat — which is Demsas's reading, and Yglesias's "where are the Michael Brown-Freddie Gray protests now?" question doesn't dispose of it — then the policy lesson isn't "progressives should exert emotional self-discipline about police" but "the contingent pattern of viral fact-patterns explains both the upswing and the downswing, and policy advice is harder." Yglesias's pivot to a strategic-Democrat critique ("progressives should project police positivity") presupposes that progressive messaging is the operative cause, which the empirical record doesn't quite support.

The movie-violence-as-incapacitation segment, while presented as light "peer review," is doing real argumentative work. Yglesias is reaching for a theory of crime that doesn't require addressing root causes (poverty, mental health, family structure) — "things young men like to do" function as crime-reduction mechanisms by absorbing the rolling-downhill propensity to violence. This is consistent with his broader abundance-liberal commitment to operational solutions over structural transformation. It's also a closer-to-honest answer than "more police" — what Yglesias actually believes, the piece suggests, is that crime is best addressed by a mix of high-effort policing AND keeping young men occupied AND not having protest-induced vibe shifts. Demsas's "RoboCop" joke and "white-collar jobs going away, people will be cops instead" exchange shows both interlocutors recognize the supply-side recruiting problem might be temporarily solved by AI displacement, which is a darkly funny but probably load-bearing observation about where the next decade of US policing labor markets is heading.

The piece's main analytical weakness is unevenness of evidence-handling. Yglesias deploys cross-national data carefully when it supports his case (Denmark, UK), then waves at it when it doesn't (Germany's increase, similar amplitude, without anti-police protests). The honest version would say "the cross-national pattern is consistent with police-effort mattering but doesn't pin down the magnitude." Demsas catches this informally; the formal version of the critique would be even sharper.

↩ Back to TOC

The Best News in America

Ross Douthat · 2026-05-30 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Charles Fain Lehman's recent Free Press essay attributing Baltimore's crime drop to a combination of focused-deterrence programs and a less anti-carceral prosecutor, plus crime analyst Jeff Asher's forecast that 2026 may produce the lowest U.S. homicide rate ever recorded in FBI statistics.

Douthat uses his pre-Great-Recession stint in gentrifying Baltimore as the lead anecdote (a friend's prediction that "this can't last" came true after the 2008 crash and then again after the 2015 Freddie Gray-era murder spike), then pivots to the present: Baltimore's homicide count fell from 348 in 2019 to 133 in 2025, an eight-times-the-national-average city now beating its own pre-decline baseline. He attributes this to two specific Baltimore programs Lehman identifies — focused-deterrence interventions targeting violence-prone young men (sticks plus social-service carrots) and a new city prosecutor who reversed the "Peak Woke" anti-carceral approach — and reads the synthesis as favoring the conservative position: "the willingness to actually imprison people seems foundational, with the effectiveness of social service liberalism built atop a bedrock commitment to public order." He then caveats hard: secular forces (older population, ubiquitous surveillance, more time indoors and online) should be pushing murder rates down regardless of who governs, which both makes the 2020-21 spike more anomalous and limits any administration's claim to credit. Closes by noting that low-crime America undercuts both the doom-loop framing inside the U.S. and the anti-American "Hobbesian nightmare" framing from Europe and China.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

The piece is a textbook example of a Douthat move: take a single data point on the left's terrain (urban crime, a topic the post-2020 progressive coalition mostly stopped wanting to discuss) and use it to credit the post-Woke turn in policy while pre-empting the rebuttal. The 234 (2008) → 348 (2019) → 133 (2025) arc is the column's spine, and it's the right arc to pick — most readers, including most NYT readers, will not have updated their priors against the 2020-era "cities are ruined" frame even though FBI data has been moving in this direction for two years. Jeff Asher (the analyst Douthat cites) is the canonical source on this beat and has been making the case all year; Douthat is amplifying not coining.

The Lehman synthesis claim is where the column does its analytical work, and it's worth scrutinizing. Lehman, a Free Press / Manhattan Institute writer, identifies two interventions: focused-deterrence (a well-evidenced program model — David Kennedy's Boston "Operation Ceasefire," replicated successfully in Oakland and a handful of other cities) and a turn away from the "anti-carceral" approach of Marilyn Mosby (the prior progressive prosecutor whose tenure ended in federal perjury and mortgage-fraud convictions). Douthat names neither Kennedy nor Mosby — neither name appears in the column — but the implicit reference is to Mosby's replacement Ivan Bates and to the focused-deterrence research literature. A reader who doesn't already know this background will read "targeted program" as generic and miss that Baltimore is replicating a model with decades of academic backing. The summary glosses this; a stronger column would have anchored it.

The "favors the conservative position" claim is the column's most contestable. Focused-deterrence is structurally a both-and intervention (the credible carrot is part of what makes the credible stick work — see the Kennedy-school evaluation literature), so saying the prosecutorial half is "foundational" and the social-service half is "built atop" is a framing choice rather than an empirically settled one. Douthat acknowledges the synthesis, then asserts a hierarchy within it; the assertion isn't defended, and progressive readers will notice. That said, the prosecutorial-turn claim probably is correct in the specific Mosby-to-Bates case, since Mosby's office had become functionally incapable of prosecuting violent crime by the end of her tenure — so the column lands on a defensible point even when the broader generalization is shakier.

The secular-forces caveat (aging, surveillance, indoor/online life) is the column's most honest move and is doing more work than it first appears. It cuts against any administration's claim to credit — including the Trump administration's, which Douthat references via the "golden age" line. It also implicitly sets up Douthat's broader civilizational-pessimism beat: if young people are committing fewer murders because they're inside on their phones, the crime stat is partly a symptom of the same atomization Douthat normally laments. He gestures at this in the closer ("influenced by aging and digital isolation") but doesn't push it; in a different column he'd run that contradiction harder. Here he leaves it as a hedge.

↩ Back to TOC

Pope Leo XIV's Magnifica Humanitas — the Church re-enters the AI debate

Pope Leo’s unfashionable universalism

** Sohrab Ahmari ** · 2026-05-26 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The publication of Pope Leo XIV's first encyclical Magnifica Humanitas on May 25 (with Anthropic co-founder Christopher Olah present at Vatican City for the release), and the immediate backlash from neo-traditionalist Catholics calling Leo a "Boomer" who isn't anti-modern enough.

Ahmari argues the encyclical's ostensible AI subject is incidental: Leo XIV's actual project is a defense of universal moral reason ("a universal truth about the good, knowable by human reason") against the various irrationalisms — market fundamentalism, technocracy, fundamentalist-identity-nationalist reactions — that have filled the vacuum left by liberalism's, socialism's, and "positive science's" exhaustion. The encyclical's intellectual lineage runs through Leo XIII's 1891 Rerum Novarum, the founding document of modern Catholic Social Teaching: Leo XIII recognized that markets generated structural antagonism (employers' bargaining power leaving workers coerced regardless of paper freedom), insisted workers have natural rights to a family wage enforced by the state, and championed "workingmen's associations" (labor unions). Crucially, Leo XIII was modern — he didn't romanticize feudalism or small-producerism — and brought the Church into collaboration with the US New Deal, the British Labour Party, and continental Christian democracy. Leo XIV applies the same pattern to AI: not a Butlerian Jihad against the technology but a critique of the "anti-human vision" of total efficiency, control, and the elimination of weakness/uncertainty/finitude, with subsidiarity logic pointing toward "transnational institutions" because AI is a problem too large for national authorities — which is precisely why the Catholic Right is calling him a UN-besotted Boomer.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

Ahmari's distinctive contribution to this week's Pope Leo cluster is the post-liberal-Catholic-from-the-left-of-post-liberalism reading. Compared to Compact's Jones (last week, 5/24), who urged Pope Leo's encyclical to mobilize the Church for an AGI moratorium on human-cloning-precedent grounds, Ahmari is more sympathetic to the actual document: he doesn't push Leo toward a hard ban, recognizing the encyclical explicitly praises AI's potential while critiquing its "technocratic paradigm." Compared to Shullenberger's Compact Substack piece last week — which framed the AI industry's blame-French-Theory line as incoherent because generative AI literally implements Barthes's death of the author — Ahmari is operating on the constructive side rather than the diagnostic side: he's narrating what a workable Catholic response to AI looks like, where Shullenberger is dismantling Silicon Valley's culture-war alibi. The continuity with Ahmari's earlier UnHerd piece "The Pope versus the President" (linked from this piece) and his Commonweal essay on "Christian Democracy" is consistent — Ahmari is one of the most coherent contemporary defenders of the Leonine synthesis (classical Christian anthropology + modern emancipatory politics + critique of pure-market liberalism), and Pope Leo XIV's actual position appears to match Ahmari's priors closely enough that the importing-his-priors concern is real but not disqualifying.

The most testable claim Ahmari makes is the genealogical one: that Leo XIV is reactivating Leo XIII's specific combination of moves (classical-Christian anthropology + acceptance-of-modernity + state-and-association enforcement of just outcomes + suspicion of pure market and pure technocratic logics) rather than launching a novel program. The textual evidence Ahmari adduces — the encyclical's "concrete lives of people" formulation echoing Rerum Novarum, the explicit invocation of "structural reforms," the subsidiarity logic pointing toward "transnational institutions" via Pacem in Terris — is consistent with this read. Where Ahmari may be importing his prior is the framing of the encyclical as a defense of "universalism" specifically. The encyclical's actual operative argument — that the human person has divine dignity that must be defended against technocratic logic — could equally be read as a particularist Christian-anthropological intervention rather than as a defense of universal reason as such; Ahmari's framing is closer to a Habermasian reading than the text might support. (Compare George NR last week on Habermas's distinction between morality and ethics as load-bearing for the neutralist project Ahmari is here defending in Catholic dress.) The stronger frame for Ahmari's read is functional: regardless of whether Magnifica Humanitas is best described as universalist, its critics on the Catholic Right read it as one and react accordingly, and the Catholic Right's critique is the dominant pole the encyclical is being absorbed against.

The cluster connection worth flagging: the encyclical's release coincides with Pope Leo XIV personally meeting Anthropic's Chris Olah at Vatican City. This is the same Anthropic that this week's distrust-discourse continues to discuss as one of the more safety-conscious AI labs. The Vatican's signal-of-engagement-rather-than-condemnation is the substantive policy news inside Ahmari's frame; how this plays out in concrete AI-policy advocacy (Vatican observer status at AI governance forums, Catholic bishops' conferences on AI labor displacement) will be the test of whether Leo XIV's "transnational governance" position has operational consequences or remains rhetorical.

↩ Back to TOC

The Thinker Who Foresaw Pope Leo's Critique of AI

Geoff Shullenberger · 2026-05-26 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Pope Leo XIV’s encyclical Magnifica Humanitas (released late May 2026) on artificial intelligence and human dignity, read alongside the late social critic Ivan Illich’s posthumously published The Rivers North of the Future (2005), which argued that cybernetic systems invert the means-ends relation that defined earlier human-tool relationships.

Shullenberger’s argument is that Leo is making three connected moves the secular AI-skeptic discourse has not made. First, he names AI as a means-ends inversion (technology has become "the standard by which everything is judged"), echoing Illich’s distinction between a hammer (which doesn’t make the user part of the hammer) and a computer system (which by its logic makes the user part of the system). Second, he names the rival transcendent vision: the singularitarian/transhumanist position (he cites Musk’s "biological bootloader of digital superintelligence" line, Yudkowsky’s accelerationist phase calling superintelligence the "Interim Meaning of Life") is itself a theological claim that contests the Church’s account of human ends. Third, he positions the Church’s response as an Augustinian one — Leo’s theological lodestar — against transhumanism as a Pelagian heresy (human perfectibility through technique). Shullenberger’s verdict on the secular response: liberal anti-AI thinking amounts to what Lionel Trilling called "irritable mental gestures" — data-center NIMBYism, repetition of the "stochastic parrot" framing, vague concerns about water and unemployment — but no answer to the question of human ends. Illich, he closes, saw modernity itself as an extension of Church history; Magnifica Humanitas’s broad resonance suggests that reading was right.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

Shullenberger is Compact’s managing editor and has been working an Illich-revival beat for several years (his 2023 Outsider Theory essay collection threads Illich, Lasch, and Jacques Ellul through his media criticism), so the Illich frame here is not opportunistic but consistent with his standing intellectual project. Magnifica Humanitas (which the Vatican released this month as Leo XIV’s first major encyclical) gives him the rare opportunity to argue that an institution he’s been advocating for has just adopted his preferred framing of the AI debate.

The strongest move in the piece is the Augustine-Pelagius framing. Leo XIV, born Robert Prevost, is an Augustinian friar (he was prior general of the Order of Saint Augustine before his papal election) and Augustine is his theological lodestar — that’s not Shullenberger projecting; it’s pre-existing fact about Leo’s intellectual formation. Reading transhumanism as Pelagian (the fifth-century British monk Pelagius held that humans could perfect themselves through their own will, against Augustine’s insistence on grace and human limitation) is a genuinely substantive analogy: both positions claim that human limitations can be overcome through human means, and the Church’s historical response to Pelagius is therefore a live precedent for its current response to the singularity. This is the kind of mechanism-level reading that’s rare in coverage of papal encyclicals, which mostly stop at "the Pope is worried about AI."

The weaker move is the dismissal of secular AI skepticism as "irritable mental gestures." Shullenberger lists "data-center NIMBYism, incessant parroting of the phrase ‘stochastic parrot,’ vague worries about water, unemployment, and misinformation" as if these were obviously inadequate. Some of them are inadequate as visions of the human good (and his point that secular skeptics have not articulated what humans should be doing instead is well-taken), but several of these complaints — water consumption, labor displacement, the epistemics of model-generated text — are concrete empirical concerns with track records, not just affective reactions. Emily Bender’s "stochastic parrots" paper (2021, with Timnit Gebru and others) is a specific argument about why scaling laws don’t necessarily produce understanding, not a slogan; the Karen Hao / MIT Technology Review reporting on data-center water use is on-the-ground documentation. The honest version of Shullenberger’s argument is that these complaints, even if individually correct, don’t add up to an account of the human good — but lumping them together as Trillingian gestures concedes the rhetorical point at the expense of the analytical one.

The piece is most interesting as evidence for a thesis that’s been quietly accreting across Compact and First Things over the past year: that the Catholic Church under Leo XIV is positioning itself as the institutional carrier of certain critiques of techno-capitalism that the secular left has either abandoned or never developed. Shullenberger’s closing claim that "this is the most obviously Christian epoch" (quoting Illich) is doing more work than the sentence’s casual presentation suggests — it’s an argument that the AI debate is ultimately a religious debate, and that the Church has an institutional comparative advantage in religious debates. Whether that argument scales beyond a Catholic-aligned readership is the open question; the piece itself does not try to make the case to non-Catholic readers, which is both its honest virtue and its main limit. Pairs naturally with the Kuhner piece on Hollywood-as-canon-by-default in the same Compact issue (both are arguing that secular institutions have abdicated transmission roles the Church or the schools used to fill) and with the running Crosscurrents thread on AI as a worldview-export problem rather than just a technology-deployment problem.

↩ Back to TOC

Reification and the Consciousness of the Tech Bro

Geoff Shullenberger · 2026-05-29 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Henry Farrell and Cosma Shalizi's new Knight Columbia paper "AI as Social Technology," which argues that Large Language Models (LLMs) are better understood as social-cultural technologies (analogous to libraries, languages, markets, bureaucracies) than as proto-superintelligent agents. Shullenberger also engages Vernor Vinge's 1993 "The Coming Technological Singularity" paper (the locus classicus of the modern Singularity myth, which laid out four distinct scenarios, only one of which centered on "awake" superhuman machines), Manuel DeLanda's 1991 War in the Age of Intelligent Machines (which used "Singularity" in the physics/systems-theory sense), and — implicitly — the broader tech-industry discourse in which AI-lab leaders treat the Singularity as imminent inevitability.

Shullenberger accepts Farrell and Shalizi's demystification as "correct and helpful" but identifies what he says is a gap: the authors don't explain why AI is wrapped in this particular "mythic garb." His answer deploys Georg Lukács's concept of reification from "Reification and the Consciousness of the Proletariat" (1923) — defined inline as the way commodity-structure logic makes "a relation between people take on the character of a thing" with a "'phantom objectivity,' an autonomy that seems so strictly rational and all-embracing as to conceal every trace of its fundamental nature: the relation between people." LLMs, Shullenberger argues, are textbook reification: artifacts of human social labor (the training-corpus authors, the engineering teams, the users) perceived as autonomous "agents" pursuing a self-propelled trajectory. The Singularity is "simply the specific narrative form this mystification takes today." He notes that Vinge's four Singularity scenarios included three that centered on human cognitive enhancement and human-machine hybrids, but the post-1993 discourse selected only the fourth (autonomous superhuman machines) — and that selection, Shullenberger argues, was driven not by empirical accuracy (the human-machine-interface scenario more closely matches what has actually happened) but by ideological convenience: the autonomous-machine scenario is the most fetishized, the one most flattering to tech-industry leaders' self-conception of their own activity. The closer notes that the public is broadly unenthusiastic about this "ersatz utopia," linking to a prior Compact piece on AI-skepticism, and suggests political mobilization of that skepticism is the most plausible route to shifting debate from "the countdown to infinity" to "what is actually happening to human society and institutions right now."

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

Shullenberger is Compact magazine's editor and, since the Compact Substack launched, has been writing serially on tech-industry consciousness — the previous oneliners log multiple adjacent pieces this year alone: "Palantir's Cynical Ideology" (Sloterdijk-Žižek cynical ideology applied to Karp's "embrace dominance" manifesto), "Critique of Agentic Reason" ("agentic" Silicon Valley reduces individuals to stimulus-response nodes), "The Agony of the Silicon Presidency" (Trump's Silicon Valley courtship as Obama-mirror), "Artemis Shows Another Tech Is Possible" (manned spaceflight as human-augmenting counterexample), "The Information State Isn't Going Away" (Trump-Musk alliance as information-state iteration), "What the Tech Right Learned from Habermas" (Karp and Yarvin as radicalized discourse-ethics), plus this week in Compact magazine itself a parallel piece on Ivan Illich and Pope Leo's encyclical critique of AI. The Reification essay is the most explicitly Marxist-vocabulary piece in this run; it is best read as the theoretical chapter of a project Shullenberger has been building piece-by-piece across 2026.

The Lukács move is the stress point. Lukács's reification, in the 1923 essay Shullenberger cites, has a specific structural meaning: under capitalism, the commodity-form colonizes domains of human activity that are not themselves commodity-exchange, producing a worker who experiences her own labor as a thing-to-be-sold rather than as her activity, and producing a society that experiences its own collective products (markets, prices, institutional structures) as objective laws external to human agency. Applied to AI, this gives Shullenberger a clean argument: LLMs are literal artifacts of aggregated human labor (the training corpus is the textual output of millions of writers; the RLHF tuning is the output of thousands of contractors); treating their outputs as autonomous machine-cognition rather than as mediated-human-cognition is exactly the kind of "phantom objectivity" Lukács identifies. The move is intellectually defensible — the parallel is real, and the Farrell-Shalizi reframing of LLMs as "social technology" already implies something like it without using the vocabulary. What's worth pressing is whether the Lukács framing adds explanatory power beyond what the Farrell-Shalizi reframing already supplies, or whether it primarily imports Marxist-tradition prestige. Shullenberger's distinctive claim — that the selection of the autonomous-machine Singularity scenario (over Vinge's other three) is itself explained by reification — is the part that does original work; the rest of the essay is closer to a translation of Farrell-Shalizi into Frankfurt-school vocabulary.

A second pressure point: Shullenberger's account treats "the leaders of the tech industry" as the primary believers in the Singularity-as-autonomous-machine, contrasted with a broadly unenthusiastic public. This empirical claim is partially supported (the Compact-Smith Noahpinion-roundup oneliner on Americans rating AI as their 29th most important issue is consistent with it; Cowen's "do Americans really hate AI?" arguments push the other way). But Shullenberger doesn't engage the harder version of the question: if the tech-industry fetishism is reification, what is the corresponding interest it serves? Lukács's original analysis was tightly linked to a class theory — reification served capital by mystifying the labor relation. Shullenberger gestures at "the nature of our political and economic order" but does not specify what interest the AI-Singularity fetishism serves for whom. Without that, the diagnosis remains structural without being predictive: it tells us the myth is doing ideological work but not what the work is for.

For Crosscurrents the piece anchors a growing Shullenberger thread: theoretical sophistication applied to tech-industry self-presentation, drawing on Marxian, Habermasian, Tocquevillean, Girardian, and now Lukácsian vocabularies. The cumulative project is more substantial than any individual piece. Worth tracking as the theoretical wing of the broader heterodox AI-criticism cluster (alongside Conor McGlynn's Arendt-Fanon piece in Compact, the Therieau "Right-Wing Dreamworld" piece in The Drift, and the Cowen-Noahpinion-Smith messaging-pivot exchange) — the question for the synthesis is whether the theoretical apparatus is doing analytical work or supplying intellectual cover for a pre-existing distaste for the Silicon Valley class.

↩ Back to TOC

What the Pope Is Telling Us

Tomer Persico · 2026-05-29 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Pope Leo XIV's first encyclical, Magnifica Humanitas ("Magnificent Humanity"), released May 15, 2026, addressing AI through the tradition of Catholic social teaching that runs from Rerum Novarum (1891) through Centesimus Annus (1991).

Persico — an Israeli scholar of religion at the Shalom Hartman Institute and author of the 2026 NYU Press book In God's Image: How Western Civilization was Shaped by a Revolutionary Idea — reads Leo XIV's encyclical as fundamentally anthropological rather than technological. The encyclical positions itself against two related anthropologies: the transhumanist view that humans are entities to be optimized and surpassed (illustrated by Larry Page allegedly calling Elon Musk a "speciesist" at a birthday party), and the secular-humanist view that grounds dignity in mental capacity (intelligence, rationality, reason). Persico's key move: those two views are not opposed; the transhumanist is drawing out the premise of the secular humanist. If dignity is grounded in mind, then a more intelligent system has the stronger claim to it; "speciesist" stops being an insult and becomes an argument. The encyclical's alternative is the imago Dei / tzelem Elohim tradition: dignity is unconditioned, inalienable, and unearned because it derives from being created in the divine image, not from any measurable capacity. Persico closes by noting that the AI debate is forcing rediscovery of the "theological genealogy of human dignity" that grounded universal humanism, abolition, human rights discourse, and the liberal international order — and that Genesis still describes the problem better than the technological vocabulary does.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is the Jewish-Israeli angle on what is now a converging multi-article cluster on Pope Leo XIV across the heterodox right and center (this week alone: Compact's William Jones reading the encyclical as a mandate to mobilize the Church against superintelligence; Compact Substack's Geoff Shullenberger on AI-industry blame-French-Theory rhetoric; the New Atlantis's "Idols of the Valley" cited approvingly inside Cass's Commonplace round-up; the Pope's namesake-of-Leo-XIII framing across multiple Catholic-conservative venues). Persico's distinctive contribution is the secular-humanism-as-on-ramp-to-transhumanism critique. The argument is genuinely sharp and not reducible to standard religious-conservative anti-tech grievance: if you ground human worth in any measurable capacity (and intelligence is the most natural choice for secular humanism), then a more capable system wins the dignity contest by your own criterion. The Larry Page "speciesist" anecdote is the load-bearing illustration — used here not as gossip but as a demonstration that the transhumanist objection to humanism is internal to humanism's own structure.

Persico writes from the Shalom Hartman Institute, a Jerusalem-based pluralist Jewish think tank, and brings the Hebrew-tradition tzelem Elohim (image of God) framing into the encyclical's imago Dei register — a deliberate move that makes the dignity argument explicitly Abrahamic rather than narrowly Catholic. This both broadens the encyclical's claim and signals to Persuasion's center-left, anti-illiberalism readership (Yascha Mounk's publication, founded around small-d democratic norms and pluralism) that this is a Jewish-Christian-secular argument, not a Catholic-confessional one. The "engines of Western moral history" framing — imago Dei grounding "abolition, human rights discourse, and the liberal international order" — is Persuasion-house argumentation, slotting religious-tradition resources into a defense of liberalism rather than against it.

The piece's weakness is that it never engages the strongest counterargument: that a thick imago Dei anthropology can also ground exclusion (the criterion "created in God's image" was historically used to deny full personhood to non-Christians, women, and others), and that secular humanism's appeal was precisely that it extended dignity beyond any theological criterion. Persico's claim that the dignity-grounded-in-mind premise collapses under AI pressure is one half of the argument; the other half — that an imago Dei premise can be both expansive enough to ground modern human-rights discourse AND restrictive enough to exclude AI from dignity claims — needs more work than the essay does. The "in a society that measures human beings by efficiency, it would not be long before someone raises the question of what is to be done with human beings who are inefficient" passage is the strongest move toward that defense (and is a real worry), but it's gestured at rather than developed.

For the project carry-forward, this is the Persuasion entry in a converging multi-source Pope Leo cluster — Persico's angle (secular humanism as transhumanism's on-ramp) is the most analytically distinctive of the cluster and is worth pulling forward in the synthesis composer's section on cross-orientation convergences around AI's anthropological challenge. The piece does NOT need a fundamental-flaw flag — it's making a careful structural argument the reader should take on its merits.

↩ Back to TOC

Pope Leo Isn't Standing Athwart the Singularity

Ross Douthat · 2026-05-27 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Pope Leo's encyclical on artificial intelligence, "Magnifica Humanitas," released this week, and specifically to Anthropic co-founder Chris Olah's gentle counter-presentation at the Vatican rollout, where Olah argued that interpretability research on Claude reveals "structures that mirror results from human neuroscience" and "internal states that functionally mirror joy, satisfaction, fear, grief and unease."

Douthat frames the encyclical as the latest test case in the "is AI a normal technology" debate. Leo's text emphatically denies AI personhood (no body, no experiences, no moral conscience) and slots most of its argument into familiar Catholic-social-thought registers — dialogue, redistribution, regulation, subsidiarity, criticism of arms races and concentrated technological power. Douthat argues this places the pope, perhaps surprisingly, on the same side of the bigger structural question as the Trump administration and the Politburo in Beijing: AI is a major technology that requires democratic and regulatory management, but not a civilizational rupture on the order of superintelligence or the Singularity (neither of which appears in the encyclical, nor does Eliezer Yudkowsky-style AI-doom). Douthat agrees with the politicizing move (treating AI as a political-deliberative sphere rather than a purely technical one) but argues the encyclical underweights the personhood-and-idolatry problem that Olah's Vatican remarks foregrounded. He invokes Yuval Levin's response — that the "challenge of idolatry" with AI is unusually severe and won't be solved by regulation — and closes by arguing the church's test is whether it becomes a natural place to look for answers about how to live with "discarnate voices whispering in your ear."

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is Douthat doing what he does best: reading a high-status text on its own terms, identifying the move it doesn't make, and arguing that the unmade move is the more important one. The framing of Leo as a "normal technology" thinker — and therefore, structurally, on the same side as Trump and Xi against the AI-existential-risk camp — is the column's most interesting analytical claim, and it's one most secular liberal readers won't have considered. Most NYT readers will have parsed the encyclical (if at all) as "Pope says slow down on AI" and missed that Leo conspicuously avoids the Singularity, the AGI threshold, and the doom-scenario vocabulary entirely. Douthat catches that absence and treats it as a positive choice rather than an oversight, which is fair — encyclicals are not casual documents.

The Olah quote is the column's load-bearing element and deserves attention on its own. Chris Olah is one of the most credentialed people in mechanistic interpretability — the research program that tries to reverse-engineer what's happening inside neural network weights — and his Anthropic-side framing of Claude as "made from us, from our words" through a process "a little like bringing a fictional character to life" is doing real corporate work even setting aside the philosophical claims. (Anthropic's commercial positioning increasingly leans on the "AI as a kind of being" framing — see also their interpretability papers' rhetoric.) Douthat takes the philosophical claim seriously without ratifying it, which is the right move; he's clear that he agrees with the pope that Claude isn't conscious. But he uses Olah to set up the column's payoff: even if Claude isn't conscious, users will experience it as if it were, and the idolatry problem follows from the experience rather than the metaphysics.

The Yuval Levin reference is a tell. Levin is one of the more institutionally-minded conservative writers — editor of National Affairs, AEI fellow, frequent Douthat interlocutor — and his "challenge of idolatry" framing is exactly the move you'd expect from someone trying to translate Catholic social thought into Burkean conservatism. Douthat is using Levin to credentialize what is, structurally, his own argument: that the prescriptive parts of the encyclical (more regulation, more redistribution, more subsidiarity) are inadequate to the actual problem, which is metaphysical-cum-spiritual. A reader less familiar with the Douthat-Levin axis will read the move as "external corroboration," when really it's an in-house argument being made by two members of the same intellectual fellowship. Not a flaw — but worth flagging.

The column's closer, framing the church's test as "how much church comes to seem like a natural place to look for answers" about AI-personhood, is the most Douthat-coded sentence in the piece. It's an apostolic-mission frame disguised as a sociological observation, and the right response to it is to note that most religious traditions have well-developed practices for dealing with "discarnate voices whispering in your ear" (the Eastern Orthodox tradition has unusually rich literature on this from the desert fathers onward, as does Tibetan Buddhism, and various Jewish traditions on demonological discernment). Douthat gestures at this with "revive forms from eras when it felt more natural to worry about discarnate voices" — and a reader who hasn't lived inside religious-discernment literature might want to know which forms specifically. But this is a side-question; the column's main move (Leo is unintentionally aligned with the AI-is-normal camp; the idolatry problem is the unmet challenge) lands.

↩ Back to TOC

The end of the American Way of War — drone revolution and war-skeptic exhaustion

Meet the Top War Skeptic Inside the Trump Pentagon

Eli Lake · 2026-05-28 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Colby's role as Under Secretary of Defense for Policy in Trump's second term and his March 3 Senate Armed Services Committee testimony defending Operation Epic Fury (the second U.S. war against Iran) — a war Colby spent his pre-government career arguing against. The piece also responds to the broader institutional politics around Colby: held-up nominations of his protégés Alex Velez-Green and Austin Dahmer; the August 8 Wiles-Rubio memo restricting agency-level foreign engagements after Colby's AUKUS (Australia-UK-US submarine pact) meetings went badly; House Armed Services Chair Mike Rogers's accusation that Colby usurped White House authority on the Romania brigade withdrawal; the 2025 Ukraine arms-shipment pause Colby was blamed for.

Lake builds the profile from "a dozen interviews with Colby's friends and rivals" plus extended biographical material. The substantive claims: (1) Colby is more accurately a "prioritizer" (a 2022 Shapiro-Ruge term) than a "restrainer" — he supports Trump's $1.5 trillion defense budget but wants resources shifted from Middle East and Europe to deter China over Taiwan, an argument laid out in his 2021 book The Strategy of Denial; (2) he is now executing a war he opposed because Trump approved Pentagon-supplied war plans, and Colby has publicly endorsed the war's success while privately one administration official told Lake "the president is not with him when it comes to Iran"; (3) his actual legacy may be in Europe ("NATO 3.0," pressing allies to defend the continent themselves); (4) congressional Republican hawks attack Colby as a proxy for attacking Trump and Hegseth, whom they "wouldn't dare attack"; (5) Ross Douthat — Colby's Harvard friend and former Washington housemate, now a Crosscurrents-roster NYT columnist — characterizes Colby as a Cold War-mandarin throwback rather than a post-Cold War blob figure. The piece is sympathetic but not hagiographic: it documents Colby's awkward straddle (defending in public the war he warned against in private) and reports rival critiques in their own voices.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

Eli Lake's profile is genuine reporting — twelve interviews, named institutional adversaries (Rogers, Cotton, Kaine, McConnell), a leaked Wiles-Rubio memo obtained by The Free Press, and the most detailed public account to date of how Colby's office actually operates inside the second Trump Pentagon. This is FP in its stronger genre (institutional access journalism), not its weaker one (vibes-column). The framing is broadly sympathetic — Colby is "the loyal lieutenant to a president who has launched a war Colby has spent his career trying to prevent" — but the piece does not protect him from the contradiction it documents. The "prioritizer" framing (vs. "restrainer") is a real analytical clarification: Colby is not a Quincy-Institute non-interventionist; he wants the U.S. to fight the right war (Pacific) rather than the wrong wars (Middle East, Europe), and that distinction explains why he can serve Trump on Iran while believing the Iran war is a resource diversion from China deterrence.

For the Crosscurrents-Iran cluster, this profile is load-bearing. The roundup's Iran reporting this week and prior includes Niall Ferguson (FP) revising his "short war" call and warning Tehran will drag negotiations until U.S. economic pain forces concessions; Aurelien on the platform-to-projectile shift disadvantaging Western military power; the Ross Douthat NYT columns pressing Dubowitz on the preventive-war logic; Klein on Caldwell conceding Trumpism-as-democratic-restoration collapses when the unpopular war exposes unchecked executive power; Klippenstein-style restrainer critiques. Colby is the institutional figure at the center of all this — the named Pentagon official who built the intellectual case the populist-restrainer wing thought was carrying the day, then provided the war plans Trump approved. Lake's piece supplies the political-economy answer to the "what happened to the restrainers?" question this roundup keeps generating: the war went forward because the prioritizer wing's deal with Trump did not include a veto over war decisions; it included drafting the National Defense Strategy (which Lake notes "neither urged nor anticipated" a second Iran war and was promptly overtaken by it).

The piece's most consequential institutional detail is the August 8 Wiles-Rubio memo restricting agency-level foreign engagements — formally a response to Colby's AUKUS missteps, but operationally a discipline mechanism that constrains the under secretary's ability to negotiate directly with Japanese and Australian counterparts on Taiwan-contingency commitments. Combined with the held-up nominations of Velez-Green and Dahmer (Colby's protégés, sent back to the White House in January), the picture is of a strategist with a defined doctrine, a published book, a confirmed Senate-grilled appointment — and an institutional position that the GOP hawks in Congress are systematically caging. The "loyal lieutenant" line Colby keeps repeating is the cost of the cage.

Colby was on the Crosscurrents slow-roster and was archived 2026-04 — too quiet for weekly coverage as a writer. As a profile subject, however, the case for tracking him is now stronger, not weaker. The FP piece does not claim Colby is a hero; it documents a coherent intellectual at the friction point between a doctrinal commitment (China-first) and a presidential decision (Iran war) and shows how Washington institutions actually resolve that kind of conflict (by demoting the doctrine while preserving the official). Worth pairing in synthesis with the broader Iran-restraint cluster as evidence that the GOP-restrainer faction's congressional and bureaucratic position is weaker than its rhetorical prominence suggested.

↩ Back to TOC

How the ‘American Way of War’ failed in Iran

** Michael Lind ** · 2026-05-28 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The winding-down of the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran (resumed limited bombing in the Persian Gulf, the Trump-Khamenei MOU awaiting approval) and the broader debate about why a fast tactical victory has produced an inconclusive strategic outcome.

Lind argues that blaming Trump for the Iran outcome misses the pattern: from Korea (1950-53) through Vietnam, Afghanistan, Iraq, Libya, and Syria, major American wars since 1945 have repeatedly ended with U.S. enemies coming to or remaining in power despite initial tactical successes. The common thread is the "American Way of War" — a domestic-political requirement to minimize U.S. casualties that drives reliance on air power, big-unit warfare, drones, and high-tech force in conflicts that actually require low-intensity counterinsurgency. Domestic politics compounds the problem: it is politically easy to start wars (rally-around-the-flag) but hard to end them, so presidents from Johnson to Bush to Trump-I have passed quagmires to successors rather than absorb the political cost of withdrawal. Geopolitically, the asymmetry of stakes — survival is existential for dictators, optional for U.S. presidents — gives adversaries the advantage in any prolonged conflict. The structural fix Lind proposes is congressional reassertion of the war-declaration power surrendered to the executive in the Truman years (no formal declaration of war since 1942), since "replacing one dove-turned-hawk with another in the White House" doesn't break the pattern.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

Lind's distinctive contribution to the Iran cluster is the long-arc frame — putting the present war alongside Korea, Vietnam, Iraq, Afghanistan, Libya, and Syria as one continuous American failure mode rather than as a Trump-specific outcome. Compared to Polk (WOTR, this week) — who diagnoses U.S. economic-fragility as the new constraint on military action — Lind's diagnosis is older and more political: it's the structure of how Americans tolerate war that produces the failure. Compared to Fukuyama (Persuasion, 5/24), who reads Trump's incompetence as the direct cause of American decline, Lind explicitly denies that frame: Obama-Libya-Syria and the Bush-Iraq pattern produced equivalent outcomes, so the pattern is institutional, not personal. Compared to Colby (FP, in earlier weeks on prioritization), Lind is on adjacent terrain — both argue against open-ended commitments — but Lind's prescription is constitutional (restore the declaration-of-war power) rather than grand-strategic (prioritize the Pacific). The Sohrab Moeini "Dangerous Martyrdom of Khamenei" UnHerd piece linked inside Lind's text indicates the publication is running a coordinated Iran-skeptic cluster, with Lind supplying the structural-American-power frame and Moeini the Iran-side frame.

The piece's main vulnerability is the historical compression: lumping Korea (a near-victory with 72 years of South Korean flourishing as the upside), Vietnam (total defeat), Afghanistan (defeat after twenty years), Iraq (regime change followed by chaos), Libya (overthrow followed by anarchy), Syria (war from outside against both sides), and Iran (a decapitation strike that did not produce regime change) into a single "American Way of War" failure mode glosses over substantial differences in what the U.S. was trying to achieve, what counted as success, and what counterfactuals look like. Lind's own concession that Korea was a "partial success" is the cleanest test of the frame — if the cases that fit the frame least are the ones the U.S. fought most conventionally (Korea), the diagnosis may be over-generalizing from cases of attempted regime change in countries with low U.S. understanding rather than from "the American Way of War" as such. Compared to last week's Lind UnHerd "America is losing Cold War II" (linked from this piece) and his Commonplace tax piece from 5/24 (out of window), Lind's structural-decline frame is consistent: he reads American institutions as no longer fit for what they're being asked to do, and his prescription is reform of those institutions rather than personnel change at the top. This is the default-doomerism reading; the calibrated-reading charitable read is that he is correctly identifying that the Iran war has produced no breakthrough on the underlying Iran question, and that this fits a pattern that calling it a "Trump war" doesn't explain.

The carry-forward connection is to iran-war-center-of-gravity-financial — Lind operates at a different layer (war-launching political architecture) than Polk (economic-buffer-erosion) or Bordoff (energy-physical-shortage), so his piece extends rather than competes with the existing cluster. His argument that the Iran war's rally-around-the-flag effect was missing this time is a specific data point not picked up elsewhere in the roster: if true, it would mean even the first stage of the American Way of War pattern (start with public support, lose it as casualties mount) has weakened, raising the question of whether the pattern itself is changing.

↩ Back to TOC

Glass Jaw? The New Economic Fragility Recasting American Power

Andy Polk · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The compounding of (1) Trump's International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA) and Section 122 broad-application tariffs, (2) the Iran-war-driven oil-price shock (April 2026 CPI energy contribution >40% of total CPI increase), and (3) the May 14-15 Trump-Xi Beijing summit dynamics where Polk argues American economic fragility was internationally visible.

Polk's central claim is that tariff effects are not just additive but structural: broad-application tariffs (IEEPA-then-Section-122) eliminated the agility that let American companies absorb cost shocks through margin compression, supplier renegotiation, product-quality reduction ("shrinkflation"), and price-pass-through delay. The footwear industry data Polk supplies is the operative case: 70% of a sneaker's cost has oil-price exposure, oil is a direct 30% material-cost feedstock, and IEEPA tariffs added 20-30% on top of traditional rates so that some children's shoes faced 87.5% effective tax rates. A mid-2025 KPMG survey of 300 C-suite executives showed 43% of consumer-goods executives reporting 1-5% gross-margin declines — among the steepest across all sectors. The Supreme Court's striking-down of IEEPA tariffs does not restore the buffer because (a) firms hold margin to survive what comes next, (b) refunds have not arrived, and (c) the administration immediately replaced IEEPA with Section 122 of the 1974 Trade Act (10% blanket tariffs as bridge to Section 301 hearings). The Iran-war oil shock — which would have been absorbable gradually in a normal economy — now compounds the tariff stress: footwear prices rose 4.2% in April (fastest in 43 months), CPI at 3.8% YoY, energy prices accounting for >40% of total CPI increase. The strategic claim is that economic resilience is now a "hard ceiling" on US ability to sustain coercion: at $4.50/gallon gas, tariff-driven inflation overhang, and compressed household budgets, the administration has less political runway for prolonged military engagement than one with deeper shock-absorbing capacity. Polk argues this dynamic was visible at the Beijing summit (May 14-15, 2026): Xi's swagger was political-necessity theater (China has property crisis, weak consumer demand, youth unemployment data suppression, clumsy export-dumping) but China exploited a real American weakening. The prescription: stop benchmarking against pre-tariff norms (a full reversal won't restore them), build new resilience models for stacked rather than discrete shocks, and prepare for fragmented (not coordinated) international response to the next crisis, which Polk predicts may be sovereign-debt.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

Polk's distinctive contribution to the project's US-economic-fragility cluster is the defense-economics-policy angle articulated from a trade-association-vice-president position with first-person supply-chain data. The piece is the closest the discovery layer has come to a Klippenstein-equivalent in the economic-policy domain — operational specifics from inside an industry the author is paid to represent, with explicit cross-mapping to national-security constraints. Compared to MR-Cowen pieces on shale-gas, Tooze on China-growth-surges, and Stoller on the gas cartel (all clustered around the same underlying US economic-structural questions), Polk's contribution is the tariff-mechanism-as-fragility-multiplier layer: prior pieces have focused on individual sectoral fragilities (energy, batteries, rare earths), while Polk specifies the cross-sector channel through which the multi-domain shocks compound. The Iran-cluster connection is tight: Polk's "economic-fragility as a hard ceiling on coercive power" is the operational mechanism behind what Lind (UnHerd, this week) describes as the "American Way of War" pattern — but at a different layer (Polk: domestic economic constraint; Lind: war-launching political architecture). Together they triangulate a more complete diagnosis than either alone supplies. The Bordoff (Heatmap, 5/17) energy-physical-shortage finding is the third leg.

Polk's strongest empirical evidence is the supply-chain-mechanics work — the 70%/30% oil exposure split for footwear, the KPMG executive survey, the 87.5% effective tariff rate on children's shoes, the April footwear-price-rise data, the John Deere $1B tariff bill projection, the NAM finding that 56% of US goods imports are domestic manufacturing inputs. This is the kind of granular industry data the project usually has to piece together from multiple sources, and Polk's vice-president-at-FDRA position gives him operational access. The piece is honestly self-aware about author affiliation (FDRA is named explicitly), which is the appropriate response to the conflict-of-interest concern.

The piece's main vulnerability — worth flagging carefully — is the prescriptive ambiguity. Polk diagnoses tariff-induced fragility persuasively but the prescription is unusually thin: "build new resilience models," "stop benchmarking against pre-tariff norms," "policymakers must build a new framework." For a trade-association VP with explicit operational data, the lack of specific policy asks (e.g., a phased tariff drawdown schedule, supply-chain-diversification subsidies, strategic-stockpile expansion) is conspicuous. The most likely reasons: (a) FDRA's institutional positioning constrains him from naming specific Trump-administration policies as the target, and (b) the article's stated purpose is to reframe how policymakers think about tariffs rather than to advocate specific reversals. The reader should hold the diagnosis as strong and the prescription as deliberately under-specified. The Beijing-summit framing (Xi's swagger as political-necessity theater backed by China's domestic weakness) is somewhat in tension with the rest of the project's reads — Foer (Atlantic, 5/17) and Schuman (Atlantic, 5/17) read Xi's posture as evidence of real cross-orientation Chinese strength, not theater; Stoller's efficiency-moat frame puts Chinese AI capability above the demographic-pessimist read. Polk's "China is in worse shape than it looks" frame is the WOTR-house-style mainstream-hawk read and worth taking seriously as a counterpoint, but the project's overall position is more attentive to the Stoller-Foer-Schuman side than to Polk's reassurance.

The carry-forward connection: Polk extends iran-war-center-of-gravity-financial (load-bearing) by naming the specific domestic-mechanism (tariff-induced agility loss) that makes the financial-center-of-gravity finding operative on the US side. The new candidate carry-forward worth opening (if the synthesis composer agrees): us-economic-fragility-as-national-security-ceiling — Polk's specific frame is novel enough to merit its own slug, and the 2028-election political economy implications (an oil-spike-during-tariff-overhang administration faces real political-runway constraints) are testable. The Lind-Polk cross-orientation convergence on US strategic-action constraint — from "American Way of War" structural-political layer and from "tariff-induced fragility" economic layer — is the synthesis's most-prized convergence finding of the week on the US-power-projection question.

↩ Back to TOC

Ukraine and Iran are Changing Warfare

Francis Fukuyama · 2026-05-27 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The shifting Ukrainian battlefield position vs Russia (long-range drone strikes on Russian oil/gas facilities thousands of kilometers from Ukraine; Putin's reported drone-assassination fear; the May 9 Red Square Victory Day parade scaled back over strike fears) and the US Iran war (Operation Epic Fury, the strikes on Iran's Fordow enrichment facility, the failure of Gulf-state air defenses against Iranian drones and missiles).

Fukuyama — the End-of-History political scientist now best known as a writer on democratic decline (currently at Stanford's Olivier Nomellini chair, also publishing the "Frankly Fukuyama" column carried forward from American Purpose at Persuasion) — frames the technology-of-war change through three classical air-power domains: strategic (deep-rear targets like factories and rail junctions); operational (10-100km behind front lines, supply lines, command posts, troop massing); and close air support (front-line engagement). Strategic and close-air-support are already transformed: long-range drones now do the strategic mission Bomber Command did atrociously in WWII (when accuracy forced indiscriminate area bombing of Dresden and Hamburg); FPV (First-Person-View) drones at $400-$40K, produced by Ukraine in the millions per year, now do the close-air-support mission that A-10 Warthogs and AC-130 gunships did at high risk of fratricide and air-defense vulnerability. Combined-arms armored maneuver warfare — rapid breakthroughs by armored vehicles deep into the enemy rear — has been "made virtually impossible" because the FPV-saturated battlefield is now completely visible. The operational middle domain is next: medium-range Ukrainian drones and missiles have been systematically taking out Russian air defenses in Crimea, threatening a Russian withdrawal that would be an enormous political defeat for Putin. The decisive economic logic: Patriot missile ~$4M vs Shahed drone ~$40K, with the Shahed produced in much larger quantities; this balance persists until someone fields a cheap-and-effective anti-drone system, which many countries are working on but no one has.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is Fukuyama writing on a topic where he genuinely has standing — international relations / strategic studies — rather than on his usual democracy-decline beat, and the piece is correspondingly more solid than his average column. The three-domains framing (strategic / operational / close air support) is standard military-strategy taxonomy and Fukuyama uses it well: it lets him be precise about WHICH parts of air-power are obsolescing and which (operational, with caveats) still depend on legacy capabilities. The Crimea analysis is the piece's most interesting concrete prediction — that the operational-distance drone-and-missile saturation of Russian air defenses on the peninsula could force a Russian withdrawal during the next year. That's specific enough to be testable on the project's carry-forward horizon.

The honest question raised in the workflow instructions is whether the "warfare revolution" claim is novel or just conventional drone-discourse repackaged. The answer is: mostly repackaging, but careful repackaging. The headline argument (drones change war; combined arms is obsolete; cheap-vs-expensive economics; FPV-induced battlefield transparency) is by now standard across the defense-policy commentariat — Noah Smith made essentially the same case this week ("all non-drone militaries are obsolete," $400 FPV vs $4K shell vs $5M tank vs $3M laser), and Tooze, the War on the Rocks roster, Bordoff on energy implications, and others have been on it for months. Fukuyama's distinctive contribution is the three-domains structure (which forces him to identify the operational middle as still partly airpower-dependent rather than collapsing the whole picture into "drones win") and the specific operational-then-strategic decoupling claim about Crimea. Both are sharper than the average drone-discourse take. The omission of the Smith-style explicit cost arithmetic for each mission-type (only the Patriot-vs-Shahed comparison appears, and only at the end) is a missed opportunity — Smith's comparison-table approach makes the economic logic more vivid than Fukuyama's mostly verbal treatment.

The piece's most important argumentative move is small and easy to miss: Fukuyama explicitly rejects the strategic-bombing-as-political-coercion theory, citing the Russian failure to break Ukrainian morale through civilian strikes and the WWII Allied area-bombing precedent. This is the project carry-forward connection back to Aurelien's "facsimiles of life" and "upstairs/downstairs" essays this month, both of which argued Western strategic thinking is paralyzed by Cold-War-era abstract models. Fukuyama, who is much closer to the Cold-War strategic-studies establishment than Aurelien is, ends up endorsing a parallel critique: the air-power-coerces-civilian-populations theory ("the armchair strategizing of people like Lindsey Graham") does not work, has not worked, will not work. That is a substantive concession from a writer whose canonical work assumes the West's strategic frameworks are basically correct.

For the project carry-forward, the most testable prediction is the Crimea-withdrawal claim ("it would not be at all surprising if the Russian command decided at some point in the coming year that its position in Crimea was untenable, and began to withdraw its forces there"). That's specific enough to track and consequential enough to matter politically. The piece also slots into the open-disagreement on "anti-drone systems" — Fukuyama believes one is coming but isn't here yet, which is the same position most of the cluster takes, so it's not really a disputed question, but the timing-and-cost specifics are.
