↩ Back to TOC

The Real Reason Tulsi Gabbard Resigned

Ken Klippenstein · 2026-05-26 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: competing post-resignation narratives casting Tulsi Gabbard as martyr (Iran clashes, Trump West Wing freeze-out, intelligence-community frustration) — Klippenstein argues all three cast her as the wronged reformer she wasn't.

Klippenstein uses Gabbard's exit to tell the structural story of why the Director of National Intelligence (DNI) position has been institutionally toothless since its 2004 creation. Drawing on a CIA in-house Studies in Intelligence retrospective by the bill's lead author Senator Susan Collins, plus declassified Rumsfeld memos archived at the National Security Archive and Rumsfeld's own library, Klippenstein reconstructs the September-December 2004 maneuvering. The 9/11 Commission recommended a Goldwater-Nichols-style intelligence boss with real budget authority over the entire community; Defense Secretary Donald Rumsfeld and Vice President Dick Cheney — who controlled four of five largest US intelligence agencies through the Pentagon — instructed House Armed Services Chairman Duncan Hunter to hold up the bill, then had Joint Chiefs Chairman Gen. Richard Myers send a late-night letter making the Pentagon's case. Cheney personally carried the gutting language ("respect and not abrogate" the Defense Secretary's authority) to Hunter. Collins, in her retrospective, cheerfully recounts texting Lieberman about the fix during a Billy Joel concert. Later DNIs (Clapper specifically) called the position "neutered." Klippenstein's read: Gabbard never had a chance because the position's authority was hollowed out twenty years before she got it, and Congress signed off on its own emasculation.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is Klippenstein doing what he does best: working from the documentary record (Rumsfeld memos, the Lieberman-inserted Congressional Record fact sheet, the CIA's own anniversary essay) to puncture a contemporary narrative. The structural argument is sound and well-sourced — the load-bearing Rumsfeld September 11, 2004 memo ("Intelligence 'Reform'" with scare quotes) and the subsequent "Possible Talking Points" memo to Andy Card and Condoleezza Rice are real, archived, and quoted accurately. Clapper's own "neutering" characterization in the same CIA journal is the most damning citation: a former DNI conceding the office was structurally rigged from inception.

The Susan Collins-as-villain framing does some rhetorical work the documents alone don't quite earn. Collins's "sausage-making" opening and the Billy Joel anecdote are read as evidence of obliviousness; they could also be read as a politician's retrospective candor about real institutional constraints (no bill was going to pass over Cheney's personal veto in 2004). Klippenstein's "ball-gag wearing gimp in Pulp Fiction" line about Congress is the kind of flourish that energizes the piece's audience but undercuts the structural argument's analytical credibility with readers outside it. The substance survives the framing — the Pentagon-blocks-reform story is true — but a tighter editorial hand would have let Collins's own words indict her without the meta-narration.

The piece's most analytically interesting move is the closing reframe: the "main actor wasn't a person or an agency. It was 'national security,' the mindset itself, and the religion behind it." This is doing real work — it moves the argument from "Rumsfeld blocked Collins" (a specific 2004 episode) to "the security establishment systematically blocks democratic accountability over intelligence" (a transhistorical claim about institutional culture). Klippenstein leaves the religion-metaphor underdeveloped — it would have been worth one paragraph specifying what practices constitute the "religion" beyond the 2004 Goldwater-Nichols-knockoff fight — but the move is correctly positioned. The Gabbard story functions less as biography than as case study, and the case study is strong.

Worth noting: the piece does not attempt to verify or refute the husband-health resignation reason directly. It simply argues the policy story is more important than the personal one. That's defensible journalism — the structural critique stands or falls on the 2004 documents, not on what Gabbard told Trump in 2026.

↩ Back to TOC

Congress's CIA Eyes AI Data Center Critics

Dan Boguslaw · 2026-05-28 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: a freelancer-sourced internal document from the U.S. Capitol Police Intelligence Services Bureau (ISB), the post-January-6 congressional intelligence arm that operates in parallel to the 18-member Executive Branch intelligence community.

Boguslaw reports — for Klippenstein's stack, with editing by William Arkin — that the ISB issued an internal "Intelligence Note" in April flagging anti-data-center sentiment as a potential threat to Members of Congress, distributed to local police and state-level fusion centers. The note concedes upfront that "the US Capitol Police is not investigating any data center-motivated threats to Members of Congress" and then proceeds to monitor social media posts critical of data centers anyway. The triggering event appears to be an unsolved shooting at the home of Indianapolis city councilman Ron Gibson, a pro-data-center vote, where a "No data centers" note was left at the door. The report also dedicates a full page to threats against OpenAI CEO Sam Altman — who is neither an elected official nor in the Capitol Police's purview. Boguslaw notes a Gallup poll showing 70% of Americans oppose local data-center construction, and frames the document as Congress deploying intelligence capabilities against the public rather than addressing the underlying policy grievance.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is the project's primary-reality fill at its most useful: a previously unreported internal document made public, with the PDF embedded so readers can audit. The reporting is solid on its mechanics — Boguslaw quotes directly from the document, links the politico.com piece explaining the ISB's 2023 creation, and identifies the named triggering event (the Gibson shooting). The journalistic find here is not so much that the ISB exists — that's been reported — but that the ISB is using its new mandate to monitor protected political speech with no actionable nexus to Congress, and is doing so in a document distributed to fusion centers (which laundering bias amplification).

The framing is sharper than the document strictly supports in one place. Boguslaw writes that "Congress, now in the intelligence business, is responding by focusing on the threat of the American public rather than the threat to the American public" — which is rhetorically strong but conflates an internal-threat-assessment note (a relatively low-stakes document type) with an active surveillance program. The piece would be even stronger if it sketched what ISB does with such notes — does it open files on tracked posters, share names with FBI, recommend prosecutions? The document is most damning as evidence that the ISB's intelligence remit has drifted from "threats to legislators" to "policies that draw political opposition," and the Altman page is the cleanest tell of that drift.

The Altman digression is worth pausing on. The note's inclusion of threats against a private-sector AI CEO under a Capitol Police intelligence product, with Altman's own blog quote ("I have underestimated the power of words and narratives") implicitly invoked, suggests the AI industry has begun importing the "AI critics as security threat" frame directly into a congressional intelligence channel. That's a real story buried at the bottom of an 850-word piece, and reads as the load-bearing finding — more so than the headline conclusion about a Congressional intelligence agency monitoring critics. Boguslaw might have led with it.

↩ Back to TOC

Democrats — coalition design, message discipline, and the GOP's mirror-image dysfunction

The left-populist fallacy

Matthew Yglesias · 2026-05-29 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: An NYT/Siena poll showing 52% of rank-and-file Democrats want the party to "move to the center" vs. 25% who want it to move left; The Economist's profile of Maine Senate candidate Graham Platner and his theory that economic populism can override unpopular cultural positions; the leaked DNC 2024 election autopsy; Michael Bennet's 2020 presidential campaign as alternative-to-Biden electability play; the post-Charlie-Kirk First Amendment crackdown and the Larry Bushart case (sheriff who jailed him 37 days over a Facebook post, won at FIRE's Foundation for Individual Rights and Expression); David Sirota's progressive-media interview pattern; Brian Beutler's "resistance" critique of frontline Democrats. Plus reader-question topics on Israel counterfactuals, the Colorado gubernatorial primary, Lindsey Graham as "responsible Republican," and the 1993 film Falling Down.

This is Yglesias's Sunday mailbag, but the lead question on Graham Platner produces the load-bearing argument. Adam asks why progressives keep believing — against repeated electoral evidence — that economic populism can override unpopular cultural positions. Yglesias's answer: it's because progressives themselves weight cultural issues over economic ones (they treat economic disputes as technical, cultural ones as moral), so they project that prioritization onto voters; absent acute economic crisis, voters put more weight on values questions than on contestable tax-policy questions. He concedes the "anti-oligarchy" framing could work if taken seriously — but doing so means abandoning unpopular cultural positions (sex-segregated sports, plastic-straw bans, listening to out-of-state environmentalists over Alaska labor unions on Alaska resource policy). The piece's secondary moves: (1) Platner doesn't have to win over Trump-voting working-class Maine — Maine is Clinton/Biden/Harris blue and has moved left since Covid; the actual challenge is Susan Collins's incumbency and avoiding factionalism that creates Biden/Collins crossover permission structures. (2) Michael Bennet would have been a better 2020 nominee than Biden — same electability profile, better policy substance (energy, expanded CTC focused not layered, K-12 takes), no age issue. (3) On Lindsey Graham and Marco Rubio as "responsible Republicans": Graham is too messy and Rubio too clearly self-interested to qualify, but Todd Young (Indiana), Shelley Moore Capito (West Virginia) and similar "quietly reasonable" red-state Republicans are a real phenomenon; the deeper problem is that Democrats have stopped meaningfully contesting statewide elections in Indiana or West Virginia, leaving the best-case scenario in a third of states as a quietly reasonable senator who might be replaced by a sociopath who underperforms by 5 points and still wins. (4) On the Larry Bushart First Amendment case (sheriff jailed someone 37 days over a Facebook post, FIRE defended): the lack of progressive-media uptake isn't well-funded right-wing media, it's that progressive media is interested in D-on-D primaries that divide Democrats, not cross-party cases that unite them.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is Yglesias's popularist flank running at full strength. The piece reads cleaner than usual because it uses the mailbag genre to weave the popularist argument (the David Shor / Yglesias doctrine that Democrats should optimize for median-voter material preferences over college-educated-staff preferences) through five distinct cases — Platner/Maine, Bennet/2020, Lindsey Graham/responsible Republicanism, the Bushart First Amendment case, the DNC autopsy — and the resulting through-line is more persuasive than any single argumentative essay would have been. The lead-paragraph NYT/Siena 52-25 datapoint is a strong anchor: if rank-and-file Democrats want the party to move center by a 2-to-1 margin, and the entire elite intra-party conversation is about whether to move left on Israel, there is a real gap between party-as-coalition and party-as-elite-discourse that's worth naming.

The strongest piece of analysis is the diagnosis of why left-populism's economic-substitution thesis keeps failing: it's not that progressive activists are stupid or naive, it's that they themselves weight cultural issues over economic ones, so they cannot conceive that voters might too. That's a more generous and more analytically sharp reading than "they're out of touch." It also explains a pattern Yglesias has documented across many pieces — progressives treating cultural-issue moderation as a moral failure (gay marriage, transgender sports, abortion lateness) while treating economic moderation as a strategic choice — without requiring any bad-faith attribution. The piece's argument that economic-populism-as-cover-for-cultural-radicalism is structurally unstable because voters don't actually accept the substitution is a stronger version of the popularist case than Shor's polling-based version, because it's grounded in revealed political behavior rather than in survey-instrument design.

The "Platner doesn't need to solve the Trump-voter problem because Maine is blue" framing is a useful corrective to a lot of bad meta-narrative around the race. But it cuts harder than Yglesias quite makes explicit. If Platner's electoral problem is "Susan Collins's incumbency + avoid factionalism," and he's running on a cultural-moderate-rejection platform anyway, the implication is that Platner's framing of his own race is wrong — he's running like a Trump-state candidate when he needs to be running like a blue-state candidate. That's a sharper critique than "his theory of national-template-building doesn't work"; it's "his strategy for his own race may not work." Yglesias gestures at this but doesn't quite land it.

The Michael Bennet counterfactual is a Yglesias-genre move and worth flagging: he uses a mailbag answer to consolidate his ongoing 2020-primary post-mortem (Bennet had better energy policy, better focused CTC framing, better K-12 takes, no age issue, similar electability profile) into a "we would not have these problems if Democrats had looked one position down the famous-straight-white-man list" argument. This is consistent with his earlier writing on Biden-was-the-wrong-pick and worth noting as part of his sustained critique of Democratic electability-by-laundry-list-criteria thinking.

The Lindsey Graham / responsible-Republican answer contains the piece's most underrated structural observation: "Democrats have just stopped meaningfully contesting statewide elections in Indiana or West Virginia," and the resulting equilibrium is that a third of states produce either quietly-reasonable Republicans (whose presence is structurally complicit in MAGA consolidation but at the margin restraining) or sociopaths who underperform by 5 points and still win. The Bill Cassidy / Louisiana arithmetic ("40% Dems + 15% Cassidy moderates + 45% MAGA fanatics = potential anti-MAGA coalition, which Democrats are not within light years of trying to build") is the cleanest single-paragraph statement of the contestability-of-red-states case the project has captured. Worth pulling.

The First Amendment / Larry Bushart case answer is where Yglesias makes the case for symmetric civil-liberties enforcement against the progressive-media incentive structure. The point that progressive media is interested in D-on-D primaries that divide Democrats (Sirota interviewing Platner to get attacks on abundance; Rabb's Philadelphia primary win) rather than in cross-party cases that unite Democrats (the Bushart case) is a structural observation about progressive media's funding model and audience that holds up under scrutiny. It also continues a Yglesias pattern of treating the progressive-NGO-media complex as the operative obstacle to popularist coalition-building, more than the right itself.

The piece's weakness is internal to the popularist framework: it does not engage the structural counter-argument that popularism's electoral track record over the past three cycles is itself mixed (Harris ran on a fairly popularist platform and lost; the Yglesias-favored candidates in primaries have a mixed record). The implicit response is "popularism wasn't really tried" or "the cultural-issue tail wagged the dog despite the popularist branding," but those rejoinders need to be stated and defended, not assumed. If popularism's case rests on "Democrats need to hew to the median voter on culture," and they did and still lost, the popularist needs to argue either that they didn't actually hew or that they would have lost worse otherwise. Yglesias gestures at the former throughout 2024-26; the explicit version of the argument is overdue.

↩ Back to TOC

I’m sick of “affordability”

Matthew Yglesias · 2026-05-26 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The post-2024 Democratic embrace of "affordability" as a unifying frame; specifically, the Hewlett Foundation's Economy and Society grant program (which funded the American Prospect, Revolving Door Project, and American Compass on the explicit thesis that pre-2020 policy was over-focused on growth); Lina Khan's neo-Brandeisian antitrust agenda; environmental-movement degrowth politics; Piketty's "Capital in the 21st Century" and Occupy Wall Street's inequality framing; and Ruben Gallego's Lulu Garcia-Navarro interview on "I'm going to make sure you get your troquita" (truck).

Yglesias concedes voters genuinely care about inflation per se (older mentors warned him this would be vindicated, and it has been) and that internet-era news negativity has made governing harder, then pivots to his actual complaint: Democrats' adoption of "affordability" as their unifying frame is a category-error evasion. Affordability of stuff, he argues, is just economic growth and rising incomes — that's what makes more things affordable. But the major Dem-aligned factions are explicitly committed to the opposite of growth-maximization: Lina Khan's antitrust school argues prices and output were overrated as goals; environmentalists hold that some output should be sacrificed for nature; the Piketty/Occupy/Sanders inequality politics says distribution matters more than median growth; and the Hewlett-funded "Economy and Society" infrastructure was built explicitly to move policy away from growth. Using "affordability" lets all these factions claim alignment with voter concern about cost-of-living without admitting the underlying disagreements with growth-maximization. Yglesias quotes Gallego's "I'm going to make sure you get your troquita" pitch to Latino men as the kind of specific, materialist, growth-coded language Democrats keep retreating from into abstractions like "economic stability" — same evasive function as "affordability." Closes by asking whether the people who built the Biden-Warren synthesis have actually changed their minds about growth, or whether the affordability rebrand will produce another round of price-control policy that leaves people with less stuff.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is core-Yglesias territory and the argument lands harder than the listed-as-1750-words length suggests. The genuinely useful analytical move is treating "affordability" as a factional code-switch — a word that papers over real disagreements among the people deploying it. That's not a marketing complaint; it's a claim about how progressive coalitions accumulate incompatible commitments by labeling them all the same thing. Lina Khan's neo-Brandeisian antitrust is, on its own terms, a deliberate move away from the consumer-price/output focus that made pre-Bork antitrust the inflation-fighting tool it was; environmentalism, on its own terms, requires accepting some loss of material abundance; Piketty's egalitarianism requires accepting some growth tradeoff. Each of these is internally coherent. Calling all of them "affordability" pretends they're aligned with median-voter cost-of-living concern when they're each, in different directions, in tension with it. Yglesias is right that the unifying frame obscures rather than resolves the factional dispute.

That said, the piece is doing what its own opening paragraphs say it shouldn't — it is partly complaining about the marketing. The genuinely substantive critique (Khan, environmentalists, Piketty all hold positions incompatible with affordability-as-growth) is buried in the middle, and could be sharpened. The strong form would be: name the trade-off you think you're making and defend it on its own merits; don't cosplay growth-coalition rhetoric while pursuing distributional or environmental policy. That's a real ask. The weaker form — "stop saying affordability, say growth instead" — is closer to what the piece actually says, and is more like Yglesias's standard popularist instinct ("the David Shor / Yglesias doctrine that Democrats should hew to median-voter views, which on cost-of-living means growth-coded language"). The popularist read of the piece is correct on its own terms but vulnerable to the rejoinder that "affordability" might be the word that lets a coalition with conflicting growth-instincts actually deliver some growth-adjacent policy. Yglesias would have to argue that the linguistic ambiguity will get cashed out in degrowth/antitrust/price-control policy rather than supply-side abundance policy — and the piece's final paragraph gestures at this ("a big new push for price controls that will ultimately leave people with less stuff") but doesn't establish it.

The Gallego troquita anecdote is the rhetorical highlight and worth flagging. It's a piece of evidence Yglesias has been carrying for two roundup-weeks now (Yglesias's running threads on popularist messaging vs. abstract policy language are unmistakable), and the framing here — "the big-ass truck is a potent synecdoche because most educated liberals do not personally aspire to big-ass-truck ownership, so it's a reminder that an agenda of broad material prosperity means everyone gets to get what they want, not just what the Yale Law School faculty wants them to want" — is the cleanest single-sentence statement of popularism (the David Shor / Yglesias doctrine that Democrats should optimize for median-voter material preferences over college-educated-staff preferences) the project has captured. Worth pulling as a quote even if the rest of the piece is read as a thinner argument.

One genuine weakness: Yglesias doesn't engage the strongest pro-affordability case, which is that "growth" is a word that Democrats have historically lost on (because voters associate it with corporate profits and Republican tax cuts) while "affordability" tests well as a voter-facing frame for the same underlying policies. If the supply-side abundance agenda Yglesias wants gets delivered under affordability branding, the rebranding is doing useful coalition work, not evasive work. The piece treats "affordability" as definitionally evasive without canvassing the political-coalition argument for why a strategic rebrand might be warranted. That's the missing rejoinder Yglesias should have pre-empted.

↩ Back to TOC

Time to freak out about the national debt

Matthew Yglesias · 2026-05-27 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Last week's spike in long-term US Treasury yields to post-2008 highs; CBO's most recent rate-projection error of 0.55 percentage points (implying $2T in additional 10-year spending); the bond-market policymaking dynamic that has paralyzed Keir Starmer's UK government; Bernie Sanders's 2010 talking filibuster against the Obama-McConnell tax deal; and Yglesias's own past Obama-era "don't worry about it" deficit position.

Yglesias documents the new fiscal arithmetic: long-term Treasury yields are at their highest since the Great Recession; rates are 0.55pp above CBO projections (which translates to $2T more in 10-year spending for no extra services); interest payments now exceed Medicaid and the Pentagon and are projected to overtake Medicare; private-sector borrowing costs follow Treasury costs upward, suppressing mortgages and construction. The proximate cause of the rate spike was the Iran War's inflationary impulse, but the deeper cause is that rich-country governments borrowed heavily during Covid and have done nothing to consolidate. The political problem: nobody — Reform UK, French populist right, the US populist left and right alike — wants to spend political capital on the arithmetic. Yglesias then makes his actual partisan argument: deficit politics historically helps Democrats because it highlights their popular ideas (tax the rich, defend Social Security/Medicare) against unpopular GOP priorities (defending tax cuts for the wealthy). The Dem flip to "don't worry about deficits" he reads as a stealth ideological makeover — the party premising itself on Nordic-scale welfare-state ambition rather than the Clinton-Obama "broadly shared prosperity" premise. He opens by explicitly conceding he was on the wrong side of the 2010-11 deficit-doves debate ("the macroeconomic situation changed fundamentally in 2021-22"), then argues that the contemporary fiscal situation is structurally different from the Obama era and demands a return to Clinton/Obama-era fiscal-discipline framing. Closes by noting that even easy wins (curbing Medicare Advantage overpayments) are being passed up.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is Yglesias publicly executing a position-flip in real time, and the piece is more interesting as that than as a fiscal-policy argument per se. He was the chief intellectual proponent of the "don't worry about deficits, the macroeconomic conditions justify higher spending" Obama-era position; he now says the position was right then and wrong now ("the macroeconomic situation changed fundamentally in 2021-22"). The honest flip is unusual enough in opinion writing to be worth noting; most writers either pretend they were always right or quietly drop the old position. Yglesias names the change and ties it to a specific year and macro condition, which is the correct way to do it. Worth flagging that this is a sharpening rather than a contradiction of his prior abundance-liberal commitments — his earlier deficit-doveism was conditional on low inflation and low rates; he's now arguing those conditions have ended, so the prior conclusion should update.

The substantive argument's strongest part is the institutional/political case: Clinton-era and Obama-era Democrats did benefit from deficit politics because "the spending problem is a Social Security problem" frame puts the GOP on the wrong side of an extremely popular policy. The 2026 GOP, defending the 2017 Trump tax cuts and adding new tax cuts, is even more vulnerable to this frame than the 2010 GOP was. The piece's claim that the Democratic flip on deficit-politics-as-loser reflects a "stealth ideological makeover" toward Nordic-scale welfare-state goals is the analytical core — it names a real factional shift inside the party (the Biden-Warren synthesis era) and identifies an opportunity cost of that shift in messaging terms.

What the piece elides is the harder argument about how deficit reduction would actually be accomplished given the Yglesias prior on abundance + state capacity + immigration. Most of his policy program (more housing, more energy, more high-skilled immigration) is genuinely growth-enhancing and therefore deficit-reducing in the long run, but doesn't show up in CBO scoring windows. The "balance the budget" question is therefore not a one-piece-of-legislation question but a "shift growth trajectory over 10-20 years" question. The piece doesn't quite engage that gap. The "easy wins" he names (Medicare Advantage cuts, restoring IRS enforcement) are real but small relative to the $2T projection error.

Compare to Smith's "America is heading for a debtpocalypse" piece on the same week — both are flagging the same structural concern (post-Covid rich-country debt + interest-rate spike + political incapacity to consolidate), but Smith reads it as fiscal crisis approaching and Yglesias reads it as partisan opportunity for Dems. That's a Yglesias-genre move: the macro reality is real, but the operative point is "here is how Democrats should message it." For a reader who wants the underlying macro analysis, Smith's piece is the more direct fiscal-crisis read; Yglesias's piece is more useful for the political strategy of who-owns-the-deficit-issue in 2026-28.

One quiet weakness: Yglesias treats Starmer's bond-market-driven fiscal paralysis as a warning sign for the US, but doesn't address the alternative reading from his own broader project — that bond markets may be overrating fiscal-deficit risk in the US case because of dollar reserve status, and the Liz-Truss-style "bond vigilantes" pattern doesn't straightforwardly transfer. The case for fiscal discipline as Dem messaging is stronger than the case that markets are about to discipline the US the way they disciplined Truss's UK. Yglesias is making both arguments at once and doesn't separate them cleanly.

↩ Back to TOC

Union enthusiasts should support abundance

Matthew Yglesias · 2026-05-28 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Kate Andrias and Alexander Hertel-Fernandez's April 2026 Roosevelt Institute paper arguing that abundance-oriented advocates should reorient to cater to labor union interests; Yglesias's prior conference exchange with the authors; California's high-speed rail cost overruns; Josh Shapiro's fast I-95 rebuild as an abundance-with-union-labor model.

Yglesias rejects the Roosevelt Institute framing — that abundance advocates should adapt to labor — and argues for the inverse: unions should adopt the abundance agenda because abundance is already concretely better for labor than the green-coalition default. The piece's pivot move is to catalog existing union-vs-green conflicts: the International Brotherhood of Electrical Workers backs data centers; the United Mine Workers haven't endorsed a Democratic presidential candidate since 2008 over mining policy; UAW criticized Biden's EV push; building trades unions backed Keystone XL; Steelworkers represent oil/gas workers. The unifying observation is that private-sector unions often align with bosses for "a bigger pie" rather than fighting bosses for "a bigger slice," and progressive NGOs are correct not to follow them rightward on prisons or oil — but they should follow them on nuclear, large-firm policy, and YIMBY housing. On California YIMBY/labor history: early bills passed with union riders that took such large bites out of construction surplus that nothing got built; subsequent bills give less away and have created union-YIMBY friction, but the choice is in labor's hands — actual new construction is the only way to create new union jobs. On California High-Speed Rail: featherbedding isn't the cost driver (Yglesias's empirical concession); rather, labor's tendency to cheerlead for any project without caring about project-management quality leaves them stuck defending boondoggles that prevent the next 10 rail projects from being built. On public-sector unions: pension health depends on city growth, which depends on housing — narrow occupation-specific carve-outs (housing set-asides for teachers) are short-sighted compared to the YIMBY case. The piece closes with Josh Shapiro's I-95 rebuild as the model: waive regulations, use union labor, the abundance side of the Dem party doesn't criticize you. Labor-aligned intellectuals who actually care about pensions and right-to-work-law reversal should make the case for abundance because abundance is already good for labor.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is Yglesias on the Yglesias-Bagley axis, but more specifically inside the abundance-liberal vs. neo-Brandeisian-coalition-politics dispute that has been live across his 2025-26 writing. The piece's framing is exactly the move Nicholas Bagley made in the abundance-vs.-Blue-State-Delusion UnHerd/Atlantic exchange: the operative critique of progressive coalition politics is not "you're wrong about values" but "your coalition arithmetic doesn't actually deliver the things you say you want." Yglesias applies that move to labor: the Roosevelt Institute paper's framing assumes labor has a coherent set of preferences that abundance should accommodate, but the actual evidence (IBEW + data centers, UMW + mining-friendly Democrats, UAW + EV skepticism, building trades + Keystone) is that private-sector labor's policy commitments are mostly orthogonal to the green-NGO-progressive coalition unions are nominally part of.

The strongest analytical move in the piece is the meta-observation about union-NGO asymmetry: NGOs do not actually follow unions to the right on any of the issues where unions' "bigger pie" politics aligns them with industry (longer prison sentences for prison-guard union jobs, more coal plants for mineworker jobs, etc.). That's correct and Yglesias frames it as fine — the asymmetry should run the other way, with unions following abundance leftward on housing/energy/transport. But this is also a place where Yglesias's argument is slightly less than he claims. The reason NGOs don't follow unions on prisons or coal is that prisons and coal are bad on the merits regardless of union employment. The reason unions might not follow abundance is symmetric — they may judge that small-business-favoring antitrust or anti-data-center politics is good on the merits regardless of jobs. The piece treats the labor-NGO asymmetry as evidence that labor should update toward abundance, but the underlying logic is just "follow the merits, ignore the jobs frame," which cuts both ways.

The California high-speed rail concession is genuinely worth noting: Yglesias explicitly walks back the "labor featherbedding causes American infrastructure cost disease" claim that he and other abundance writers have flirted with, in favor of the better-evidenced "project management and planning failures cause cost disease" claim (the operative source here is Eno's transit-construction-cost research and the Transit Costs Project at NYU Marron Institute, though Yglesias doesn't name them). This is the right empirical update; the labor-as-cost-driver claim doesn't survive contact with comparative international data. The strongest version of the piece would have made this the lead — abundance writers giving up the "blame the unions" framing they sometimes deploy creates the conditions for the alliance Yglesias wants. Burying the concession in the high-speed-rail subsection underplays its rhetorical weight.

What the piece misses is the structural reason private-sector unions in the US have shrunk: it's not because they aligned wrong with greens, it's because the Wagner Act / NLRB framework was built for a 1930s industrial economy and has been steadily hollowed out by sectoral shifts, court rulings, and right-to-work expansion. The Andrias-Hertel-Fernandez Roosevelt Institute argument Yglesias is responding to is built on a sector-by-sector theory of labor revival (sectoral bargaining, public-sector unions, sectoral works councils) that does not depend on abundance vs. green coalition arithmetic. Yglesias does not engage the actual policy proposals in their paper — he engages the framing question (who should cater to whom). That's a Yglesias-genre move and it lands in a debate context but leaves the more substantive labor-policy disagreement unaddressed. A reader interested in whether sectoral bargaining is the right path forward wouldn't get an answer here.

The Shapiro I-95 example is the kind of evidence the piece needs more of and one of the cleanest abundance-with-union-labor revealed-preference cases in recent Dem politics; the Beshear-Sierra-Club observation is similarly sharp. The piece's argumentative weakness is in the gap between these specific examples and the general "unions should adopt the abundance agenda" claim — between "here are three cases where the abundance side delivered union jobs" and "labor's institutional theory of itself should change."

↩ Back to TOC

The Democrats Sabotaged Their Own Autopsy

Sam Kahn · 2026-05-26 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The forced release (after CNN obtained it) of Paul Rivera's 192-page DNC autopsy of the 2024 election loss, accompanied by Chair Ken Martin's Substack disavowal ("I am not proud of this product"), DNC redline annotations, and a wave of commentary (Michelle Goldberg in the New York Times, Chris Cillizza, Rich Lowry, Noah Rothman, Evan Barker) calling the report a disaster.

Kahn — associate editor at Persuasion (Yascha Mounk's center-left anti-illiberalism publication), substack writer of Castalia, and editor of The Republic of Letters — takes the contrarian position against essentially every other commentator on this report. His claim is that the report, while not breathtaking, is exactly what an after-action report from a Democratic consultant should be: technical, forward-looking, focused on the 10-year strategic plan Martin asked for rather than gossip about the Biden cover-up or the Harris campaign. Rivera makes common-sense recommendations: close the male/rural-voter gap; fight even in unwinnable places to limit losses; messaging should be year-round (the Trump-backing Turning Point USA as model); adapt to new media (Democratic digital ads are just shortened TV ads); lean into negativity. The errors the DNC cites (Mark Robinson's vote share off by ~5 points, "John Corzine" misspelling, "Matt Brevin" instead of Bevin) are real but trivial. The DNC's pedantic annotations ("In 2022, Joe Biden was president," "all three of those states voted for Trump in 2016") read as bad-faith messenger-shooting. Kahn's diagnostic frame is Catch-22's ex-P.F.C. Wintergreen, the mail clerk who rejected battle orders for stylistic faults — the DNC is, he argues, run by ex-P.F.C. Wintergreens.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is a contrarian Persuasion piece in a cluster where most of the week's coverage takes the opposite position. The pieces this week (per oneliners) include Goldberg's NYT view that the autopsy is fiasco-level evidence of party mediocrity and Ken Martin should be replaced; Rich Lowry's National Review piece that the autopsy is incompetent and confirms party rot; Noah Rothman's NR piece indicting the autopsy's omission of Gaza as a self-aggrandizing power-play by the anti-Israel activist left; Evan Barker's Free Press piece reading the leaked autopsy as confirmation that Harris lost because the party refused identity-politics retreat. Kahn's incremental contribution against this consensus is the (genuinely useful) point that the autopsy is a technical electioneering document graded against the wrong rubric — most of the criticism faults Rivera for not writing the dramatic insider tell-all autopsy people wanted, when his actual mandate was to draft a 10-year strategic operations plan for state parties. The reading is sympathetic but not unprincipled: Kahn is willing to specify what he thinks the report does well (the year-round messaging case, the "early money is like yeast" line, the dial-down on identity politics) and where it falls short (immigration barely discussed; no engagement with the group-think that produced the Biden mental-acuity cover-up).

The strongest part of the piece is the DNC-annotations diagnosis. Kahn produces specific examples of annotations that are pedantic in ways no functioning communications operation would tolerate — informing the reader that "In 2022, Joe Biden was president" as if Rivera couldn't possibly have known, or "all three of those states voted for Trump in 2016" to dispute a sentence about decades-long Blue Wall voting patterns. The ex-P.F.C. Wintergreen frame from Catch-22 (Joseph Heller's WWII satire about an Army mail clerk who unilaterally rejects generals' orders for literary infelicities) is genuinely good: it captures both the technical accuracy of the criticism and the comically off-mission deployment of it. The diagnostic claim — that the DNC's reflex to discredit its own commissioned product, rather than absorb the report and move on to the next news cycle, is "weird and insular" in a way that "electoral politics is extroversion" cannot tolerate — is the piece's most analytically distinctive contribution to the cluster.

The weakness is what Kahn doesn't engage. The "where Kahn adds vs reproduces" test for this cluster: Goldberg's argument is that party mediocrity is the deeper problem and a more competent leadership would have shipped the report; Lowry's is that the report is genuinely bad; Rothman's is that the Gaza omission is itself the indictment of an activist-left power play. Kahn essentially concedes Goldberg's mediocrity argument (the DNC's handling IS mediocrity) but disputes Lowry's report-is-bad claim, which is the dispute that actually matters for the substance. Where Kahn could have added the most value would have been an explicit head-to-head with at least one of the other readings; instead he sweeps them all together as "the media" and assumes their position is the same. Rothman's specific point about Gaza, for instance, deserves engagement (Kahn briefly mentions immigration as understudied but not Gaza), and the absence of any treatment of whether the omission was strategic or oversight is a gap.

For the project carry-forward this is the within-cluster dispute piece — the synthesis composer should pair Kahn with Goldberg and one of the right-side pieces (Rothman, Lowry, or Barker) and treat the dispute over the report's quality itself as the methodologically interesting question. The dispute is also a small instance of a broader cross-orientation convergence: writers from Mounk's center-left, Rich Lowry's establishment-right, and Bari Weiss's center-right Free Press all see the DNC as critically dysfunctional, but disagree on whether the autopsy was the manifestation of the dysfunction or just a victim of it. Kahn's distinctive value in that cluster is the methodological one (read the report against its actual mandate, not the one people wanted).

Democrats Can't Decide Whether the Problem Is Fraud or the People Exposing It

Jonas Du · 2026-05-27 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: YouTuber Nick Shirley's December video alleging widespread Medicaid fraud in Minnesota's Somali-run daycares, follow-up videos alleging $170M+ in California hospice/daycare fraud, and the partisan fight over how Democratic governors are responding.

Du catalogs a "loud denial / quiet action" pattern across three blue states. In Minnesota, Governor Tim Walz publicly called Shirley a "delusional conspiracy theorist" and accused Trump of "white supremacy" while his Department of Human Services was simultaneously terminating 18,000+ inactive Medicaid providers, designating services as "high-risk," and revalidating 5,500+ providers to avoid losing $2B in federal funds; AG Keith Ellison denounced Republicans for a "viral panic" while running an investigation of 200+ healthcare providers. In California, Newsom's press office mocked Shirley with an AI-generated image (depicting him as a ghoulish figure stalking daycares) while AG Rob Bonta filed charges against 14 hospice providers for $267M in fraudulent billing using stolen identities. The state assembly passed AB-2624 (the "Stop Nick Shirley Act," introduced by Bonta's wife) restricting disclosures about immigrant-serving businesses. In Maine, Governor Mills dismissed CMS director Mehmet Oz as a "TV doctor" while the state auditor admitted Medicaid monitoring was inadequate and quietly paused four high-fraud Medicaid categories. Du closes on a marginally hopeful note: the 24 Democratic AGs declined Vance's anti-fraud task force meeting but said they'd attend a future one with notice.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

Du has assembled a genuinely strong indictment-by-juxtaposition: every example pairs a Democratic official's public denunciation with a specific same-period administrative action that concedes the underlying problem. The juxtapositions are sourced and dated, not gestured at. The asymmetric-warfare framing — Republicans aggressive, Democrats torn between denial and quiet engagement — is the right read of the pattern, and the piece does the rare work of acknowledging the embarrassing-for-its-side data point that the 2024 GAO estimate puts the upper bound of federal fraud at $521B (less than a third of the deficit), which undercuts the Miller-Vance "balance the budget by eliminating fraud" line that the piece is ostensibly supporting.

The structural weakness is that the piece collapses a real distinction between fraud-prosecution-as-policy and fraud-as-political-weapon. Federal raids on Somali-American daycares conducted in coordination with high-volume right-wing content creators are different in kind from a state AG's office working through a normal fraud-prosecution pipeline, even if both end in indictments. Du's preferred frame — "Democrats are doing the same thing they denounce" — obscures that the Democratic-state actions Du lists are mostly standard agency-level fraud enforcement (terminating inactive Medicaid IDs, revalidating providers, halting applications in high-risk categories), while the Republican-federal actions are explicitly political: Stephen Miller framing fraud enforcement as a budget-balancing tool, the task force inviting only GOP AGs to its initial meeting, the rhetorical pairing with immigration raids. Both can be true: Democrats are downplaying real fraud problems for political reasons, AND the Republican enforcement push is also being conducted as political theater with selective targeting. Du's piece doesn't quite hold both halves of this.

The piece is also doing some sleight-of-hand with Shirley. Calling him "YouTuber Nick Shirley" without further description treats him as a neutral whistleblower; Shirley is a content creator whose style and selection criteria are themselves part of the story, and his Minnesota Somali-daycare videos drew accurate fraud allegations alongside other content that was contested. The piece would be more credible if it distinguished between the fraud allegations that have been independently confirmed by federal raids and indictments (which is a lot) and the broader narrative frame Shirley's videos were operating in (which is more contested). Treating Shirley as simply "the guy who found the fraud" understates how the fraud-discovery and the racial framing arrive together as a package — which is exactly what makes Democratic responses so politically fraught.

The Mia/Rob Bonta detail (the AB-2624 author is the AG's wife) is real and worth noting, but the piece deploys it as a gotcha without thinking about what it actually shows: a Democratic state has both an AG aggressively prosecuting hospice fraud AND a state assemblymember from the same household trying to constrain future content-creator-driven exposés. That's not "denial and quiet action" by the same actor — that's two different policy positions held by two different elected Democrats. Real, but smaller than Du is implying.

↩ Back to TOC

America needs liberal nationalism back

Noahpinion · 2026-05-29 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The Balaji Srinivasan vs. "Roman Helmet Guy" (RHG) Twitter debate over whether expatriated tech-rich Americans owe loyalty to the country that made them rich.

Smith reconstructs the exchange — RHG accusing Balaji of ingratitude for relocating to Singapore, Balaji countering that "the American people" no longer exists as a unified object of loyalty because Red and Blue America are too polarized — and uses it to triangulate his own position. He grants Balaji's point that mobile capital and ideals matter and that ethno-nationalism is unworkable: any attempt to define a "Heritage American" core by race, religion, or generation runs into electoral non-starter problems, and YouGov polling shows Americans of both parties define national identity by citizenship, Constitution-belief, law-abidance, and voting. But he grants RHG's point that a country whose rich exit at the first sign of trouble cannot tax, defend itself, or build public goods, and that taxpayer-funded research (genomics, in Balaji's case) creates real debts. Smith's synthesis is FDR-era liberal nationalism — civic religion built on pluralism, immigration-as-foundational, interfaith and interracial outreach (Federal Writers Project, "Don't Be a Sucker" film, Native American autonomy, Black cultural programs), and an insistence that Americans of disparate backgrounds form "a single unified whole" capable of provisioning shared public goods. He concedes FDR's record on Japanese internment and segregation compromise but contextualizes them as the era's ceiling rather than its trajectory. The piece closes with the claim that both the contemporary activist left ("no culture except multiculturalism") and right (Heritage Americans) have abandoned this ideology, which is why American policy is "insane right now."

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is Smith doing exactly what he opens the piece announcing he must now do — moving from policy technocrat to ideology-vendor. The synthesis is genuinely Smith's natural position: center-left abundance liberalism plus FDR-iconography plus the YIMBY-adjacent argument that public-good provision requires felt solidarity. The piece's strongest move is the immanent critique of RHG's "Heritage American" frame: the demand for an ethno-cultural core cannot survive contact with American electoral arithmetic, because every exclusionary line (WASPs only? Catholics? Jews? Asians?) is both arbitrary and electorally fatal. That argument has teeth and Smith deploys it efficiently.

The piece's structural weakness is that it treats Balaji and RHG as the only two right-wing options and then positions liberal nationalism as a third way — but the contemporary right-wing nationalism that actually matters politically (J.D. Vance, Christopher Rufo, the post-liberal-Catholic intellectuals) does not look much like Roman Helmet Guy's pseudonymous Twitter posting. By picking a vulgar pseudonymous interlocutor, Smith softballs his opposition. A liberal-nationalist defense against, say, Patrick Deneen's Why Liberalism Failed or Sohrab Ahmari on common-good constitutionalism would have to engage the claim that procedural liberalism is itself the corrosive force preventing the shared-fate solidarity Smith wants. RHG is a much easier target.

Second, the FDR canonization does work the historical record can't quite support. Smith concedes Japanese internment in a single subordinate clause and characterizes FDR's segregationist compromises as the era's constraint rather than as a substantive feature of liberal nationalism as actually practiced. But the New Deal's "single unified whole" was forged through CCC and WPA programs that were racially segregated by design, Social Security carve-outs (agricultural and domestic workers) that excluded most Black workers, and an immigration system (the 1924 Johnson-Reed Act) that the Roosevelt administration did not dismantle. The Federal Writers Project did employ Black writers; it did not contest the structural exclusions. "Liberal nationalism worked" is a defensible claim about midcentury white civic identity; whether it scales to a multi-racial polity without those constraints is the question Smith elides by treating the FDR template as universally applicable.

Third — and this is where Smith's center-left technocratic instincts show — the operative mechanism for renewed liberal nationalism is left vague. He says it requires "national unity," "shared civic religion," and rejection of the activist class on both sides; he does not say what concrete policies, institutions, or political coalitions would rebuild it. The historical FDR liberal nationalism was forged by depression, war mobilization, and a four-term presidency with massive congressional majorities. What contemporary equivalent does Smith have in mind? Without an answer, the piece reads as "the country I want would be nice to have," which is exactly the kind of vibes-not-policy mode Smith opens the piece criticizing.

What's genuinely useful here, and worth keeping separate from the FDR mythology, is the diagnostic frame: the simultaneous structural impossibility of ethnic nationalism in a multi-ethnic polity and the impossibility of rootless globalism for any polity that needs to fund public goods. Both halves of that scissor are right. The third-way label may be wrong, but the cornering is real.

↩ Back to TOC

Are you tired of the Trump era yet?

Noahpinion · 2026-05-25 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: USCIS's late-May rule eliminating Adjustment of Status for high-skilled visa workers; New York Times and CNN reporting on US intelligence assessments that Iran has rebuilt ~70% of its missile stockpile and 90% of its underground facilities during the ceasefire; Rolling Stone's reporting on the $1.776B "Anti-Weaponization" settlement and Trump's 3,600 stock trades in Q1; Robert Kagan's piece on what defeat in the Gulf would look like.

Smith opens by reasserting his "contingent Democrat" stance — he supports Democrats because the GOP is worse, not unconditionally — then catalogs three discrete administration disasters from the past few weeks that each would have ended any prior presidency. First, USCIS's rule eliminating Adjustment of Status (the standard pathway by which most green-card recipients except investors transition from temporary visas) would expel most high-skilled foreign workers from the US, devastating an AI industry where immigrants founded the biggest companies and dominate top-50 AI research; even the "tech right" was blindsided, and the policy was apparently pushed by anti-immigration USCIS staffers marinating in extremist online discourse. Second, the Iran War: Trump launched it with no immediate casus belli, has now lost it in the operational sense (Iran retains 70% of its prewar missiles, has restarted drone production, controls the Strait of Hormuz, and has been bumping US gasoline prices and inflation), while US weapons stocks are dangerously depleted with no rapid replenishment; Smith quotes Robert Kagan that defeat means the Gulf states accommodating Iran and the US standing exposed as a "paper tiger." Third, Rolling Stone's documentation of unprecedented presidential corruption: 3,600 Q1 stock trades on Trump's portfolio (suspiciously timed to administration actions on Nvidia/Intel/Palantir/Boeing), a $10B lawsuit Trump filed against his own government settled by his former personal lawyer for $1.776B distributed to political allies, and a "FOREVER BARRED" prosecution-immunity clause for the Trump family. Smith closes that this is "the most absurdly terrible presidential administration America has ever had" and notes the slow but persistent decline in Trump's approval. Note: this is genre-typical Smith — high-energy summary of admin disasters with a strong moral frame; not breaking news, but the consolidation has value.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is Smith in straight-up Democratic-partisan mode, not his more characteristic abundance-liberal / heterodox-tech-right-critic mode. The opening paragraph announces the genre — he reasserts his "I support Dems contingently, not tribally" credentials, then drops the contingency entirely for the rest of the piece. That's defensible given his read of the facts; it's also a register the roundup should note because it forecloses some of Smith's usual analytical angles. He doesn't, for instance, ask why the tech right's actual policy influence in the second term is so much weaker than Marc Andreessen and David Sacks were promising in 2024, or what that says about the durability of the tech-MAGA alliance Smith spent 2024 critiquing.

The piece's strongest section is the Adjustment of Status one, where Smith does what he does best: anchor an arcane immigration-policy change in concrete economic stakes (almost all green cards except investor visas come through this pathway; the AI industry's foreign-born researcher pipeline runs through it; the founders of nearly all top AI companies were immigrants), with EIG and MacroPolo data backing each link. The Gil Verdon vignette — Trump-booster Canadian semiconductor founder suddenly realizing the policy applies to him — is the kind of revealed-preference moment that does more work than analysis. The "policy is made by Trump or by neo-Nazis on X" framing is sharper than Smith usually deploys, and it lands.

The Iran section leans heavily on a single Robert Kagan column for the geopolitical-implications argument; that's a load-bearing source for what is otherwise an inference (Iran retains its weapons, ergo American power is broken). The intel-assessment numbers Smith cites are genuinely striking, but the inferential step from "Iran retains 70% of missiles" to "American power in the Gulf is broken" is mediated almost entirely by Kagan. A more careful version would acknowledge the alternative reading — that the Strait of Hormuz disruption is partial, that Iran's rebuilding requires Russian and Chinese support that may or may not persist, and that "lost the war" depends on how one defines war aims. Kagan is doing work that should be Smith's, and Smith's foreign-policy instincts have historically been less reliable than his econ-policy ones (cf. his early Ukraine triumphalism).

The corruption section is where the piece's persuasive form matters more than its content. The Rolling Stone numbers are damning, and Smith presents them numerically. But the analytic move he doesn't make — and that would have strengthened the piece — is connecting these disparate scandals to the institutional mechanisms that should have caught them. The $1.776B settlement was approved by an Acting AG who was Trump's personal lawyer; the 3,600 stock trades happened with full SEC visibility; the prosecution-immunity clause was signed by DOJ. These are not just "Trump is corrupt" facts but "the institutional checks designed to prevent presidential self-dealing have collapsed" facts, and Smith's analytical framework (centered on institutions, not personalities) should have been brought to bear. Instead the piece reads as outrage cataloging, which is what Smith opens by saying the DDOS strategy is designed to elicit.

The closing line about the "small solace" of Trump's slowly declining approval is the genre's tell: this is a piece written for Democratic readers who need to be reassured that the long arc bends toward sanity, not analysis aimed at undecided readers or even at Smith's own tech-right interlocutors. Compare the immigration-policy section, which actually engages the tech right on its own terms (Verdon's revealed preference, the AI industry's stake), to the corruption section, which assumes the reader already shares the moral premise. The piece could have been two tighter pieces; consolidated, it reads as catharsis more than analysis.

↩ Back to TOC

A party of one isn't a healthy party

Silver Bulletin · 2026-05-28 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Tuesday's Texas Republican Senate primary runoff in which state Attorney General Ken Paxton defeated incumbent Senator John Cornyn 64-36 — a "halftime box score" margin Silver attributes almost entirely to Trump's endorsement of Paxton the week prior. Silver's secondary frame: ongoing Democratic-Party crisis discourse (DNC dysfunction, Abundance-style governance critiques of blue states, the Biden-fitness post-mortem) that has dominated heterodox political commentary, which Silver wants to invert by applying the same diagnostic rigor to the Republican side.

Silver constructs an 11-point checklist for "party healthfulness" (electoral competitiveness, constructive governance, organizational architecture, advancing member interests, representing voter interests, brand image and agenda-setting, human capital, effective cartel discipline, securing long-term legal position, rational adaptation to defeat, compatibility with constitutional democracy). He then grades three entities separately on each: Democrats, the Trump-GOP, and the "GOP ex-Trump." The numerical verdict: Democrats average a 2.30 GPA (C+), Trump a 1.89 (D+), and the "GOP ex-Trump" a 1.25 — "almost entirely a failure, except for the courts and sometimes effective state-level governance." Silver acknowledges he is "relatively optimistic about American democracy's future" and is "not a both-sides relativist," then proceeds to write a column whose conclusion is structurally both-sides-ish (Democrats are a flawed-but-functional party; the GOP-ex-Trump is a rotted husk; Trump himself is an effective cartel-builder operating outside democratic norms). The Cornyn data point is presented as endpoint, not anomaly: "there's even less capacity for resistance to Trump from the rotted husk of what used to be called the Republican 'establishment' than might have been assumed." The framework is original; the grades are admittedly subjective; Silver invites the comments-section debate.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

Silver is in his political-science mode here, not his prediction-markets mode. The 11-point rubric is genuinely useful — it disaggregates party "strength" (cartel discipline, electoral wins) from party "health" (representativeness, democratic-norm compliance, adaptive learning), which is a distinction the discourse routinely collapses. The decision to grade three separate entities (Democrats, Trump-GOP, GOP-ex-Trump) is the column's analytic core, because it lets him reach the unintuitive conclusion that the worst-performing party in May 2026 is not Democrats and not Trump but the residual institutional GOP. Cornyn — 24-year incumbent, former Republican whip, voted with Trump 99% of the time per his own website — losing by 28 points to Paxton is the empirical hook; the framework lets Silver argue this is not a one-off Texas oddity but a measurable structural property of the Republican coalition.

Where the analysis is weakest: the "GOP-ex-Trump" entity is partly an artifact of the slicing. By treating Trump as a separable agent who operates within but apart from the Republican Party, Silver gets clean grades — Trump is an A in cartel discipline; the GOP-ex-Trump is an F in everything except court-capture. But the operational reality the Texas runoff documents is that the GOP-ex-Trump does not exist as an independent agent with which Trump shares power. Paxton's win was not a contest between Trump-GOP and GOP-ex-Trump; it was the GOP voting as Trump asked, which is the only thing the GOP is currently configured to do. The "1.25 GPA" framing implies the residual party is a separable entity making bad decisions; the actually-observed behavior is that the residual party has dissolved into a coordination layer for Trump-endorsements. That distinction matters for predictive purposes (Silver's home turf): if you ask "what happens to the GOP post-Trump?" the answer depends on whether the GOP-ex-Trump is a dormant institution awaiting reactivation or a memory of a coalition that no longer has the personnel or infrastructure to reconstitute itself.

The piece pairs naturally with the Democratic-side autopsy cluster the roundup keeps generating (Persuasion-Kahn, NR-Dougherty, multiple writers on the DNC's strategic exhaustion). Silver supplies the analogous diagnosis for the GOP: where Democratic discourse asks "how did the party of competent governance lose touch with the median voter?", Silver asks "how did the party that controls Congress, the executive, and the courts become the unhealthiest party on every dimension that does not reduce to short-run power?" Both autopsies share a structural assumption that party-health is downstream of party-internal mechanisms (talent recruitment, agenda-setting, candidate-selection); both elide the possibility that the underlying coalition realignment makes the old measurements partially obsolete. Silver names this concern in passing ("you can get into a whole What's the Matter with Kansas? debate about whether Trumpism is really serving the interests of the core MAGA constituent") but does not pursue it.

For Crosscurrents this is a useful diagnostic register — Silver's empirical-political-science voice applied to a question (Republican-Party institutional health) that the Democratic-autopsy discourse has mostly avoided. The mirror-image symmetry is the analytic move worth keeping: if heterodox commentary is going to treat the DNC crisis as a structural problem worth diagnosing, the analogous Republican-side structural problem deserves equivalent attention rather than being absorbed into Trump-as-singular-figure framing.

↩ Back to TOC

Is Trump trying to turn Texas blue?

Eli McKown-Dawson and Nate Silver · 2026-05-27 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Trump's late-runoff endorsement of Texas AG Ken Paxton over incumbent Senator John Cornyn (May 19, 2026) and the resulting general-election outlook against Democratic nominee James Talarico — with an update note that Paxton won the runoff by 28 points on May 26.

The piece argues Trump made what is probably his costliest endorsement blunder of the cycle by (1) waiting until the last week of the runoff to endorse, (2) choosing scandal-plagued Paxton over Cornyn (the better general-election candidate), and (3) doing it at his all-time approval-rating low (-19.3 net per Silver Bulletin's tracker, the worst for any president at this point in a nonconsecutive term). The framing question — "would Nancy Pelosi in a Freaky Friday body-swap have handled this any differently?" — captures the central claim. The mechanics: Texas projects R+4 with generic candidates given a Democratic +7 generic-ballot lead nationally, but the authors' historical research finds scandals move races by ~5 points, which would put a Paxton-Talarico contest at D+1 or tie. They reinforce this with the costliness point: the Texas Senate primary was the most expensive on record (~$20M on the runoff alone), money that Republicans could have spent defending Maine, Iowa, Florida, Alaska, and Ohio if Trump had endorsed early enough to avoid the runoff. Paxton's electoral history is mediocre (3-point AG win in 2018, much worse than Cornyn's 15-point average margins). The countervailing factor for Paxton: Talarico is a Texas state representative whose past "God is nonbinary" comment (made as a Presbyterian seminarian) gives Republicans a religious-voter attack line, and Texas hasn't elected a Democratic senator since Lloyd Bentsen in 1988. Bottom line: prediction markets showing a Paxton-Talarico toss-up are "working off reasonable assumptions," and if Republicans lose the Senate 49-51, "they'll have the man in the White House to blame."

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is Silver Bulletin doing exactly what it does best: applying a model-disciplined framework (generic ballot + state partisanship + scandal premium + candidate-quality measurements) to a high-stakes specific race, with appropriate hedging about model uncertainty this far from Election Day. The analytical contribution is the quantification of the scandal premium — "roughly in the 5-point range" — which is the kind of empirical anchor that most political-junkie commentary lacks. When Silver/McKown-Dawson say "prediction markets showing a Paxton-Talarico toss-up are probably working off reasonable assumptions," that's a substantive claim being made by people who built and continue to refine the model and are willing to be wrong in front of subscribers. Worth more than most pundit takes on the same race.

The piece's strongest move is the donor-allocation argument. The political-press default narrative is "Trump dominated, ousted Cornyn, won the primary fight." Silver/McKown-Dawson's reframe is that the right comparison is not "did Paxton win the primary" but "what did the GOP forgo by spending $20M+ on a Texas Senate primary instead of defending Maine/Iowa/Florida/Alaska/Ohio." That's a genuinely sharper way to score the move. If their generic-ballot reading is right (Democrats +7 nationally, several other states bluer than Texas), the resource-allocation cost is real even if Paxton ultimately holds Texas. This is the kind of observation that doesn't surface in horse-race coverage because reporters score Trump endorsements as binary win/loss rather than as opportunity-cost decisions.

Where the piece is weaker is on the "questionable taste in candidates" frame. The Herschel Walker parallel is correct as a pattern (Trump favors loyalty over electoral viability in his endorsements), but it understates that Trump's endorsement is doing what Trump wants it to do, which is consolidate party control by ejecting "disloyal" Republicans. The piece notes this — "Trump's endorsements do have a winning track record, but only in primaries" — but doesn't sit with the implication: if Trump's revealed preference is for partisan purification over general-election win-probability maximization, then "Trump endorsed the wrong guy" is a less coherent critique than "Trump's endorsement function isn't the one Silver Bulletin assumes." Trump may be perfectly willing to lose a Senate seat to keep Cornyn out of the chamber. That doesn't change the empirical analysis but does sharpen the strategic framing.

The Talarico vulnerabilities the authors flag are also doing more analytical work than they explicitly acknowledge. The "God is nonbinary" line from 2021 in a state-rep race in Austin is exactly the kind of statement that gets weaponized in a general election against a Democratic Senate nominee in Texas, and the Walz/football-coach analogy is precisely the right reference class — biographical credentials that don't survive contact with the messaging environment of a competitive Senate race. If the scandal premium for Paxton is ~5 points and the Talarico-vulnerability discount is also material, the actual net might be tighter than the piece's headline framing implies. Silver/McKown-Dawson are honest about this — they say "a reversion to R +1 might be a more realistic baseline" — but the headline-and-hook structure pushes harder on the "Trump turning Texas blue" framing than the body text supports.

The update note about Paxton winning the runoff by 28 points is the most important meta-data in the piece. A 28-point margin against an incumbent senator who voted with Trump "99% of the time" (per Cornyn's own campaign site) is genuinely remarkable, and the authors' read — Trump's stranglehold on the GOP base is total — is correct. The interesting downstream question for synthesis is whether that stranglehold extends to general-election persuasion or only to primary turnout. The piece's argument hinges on the answer being "primary-only."

↩ Back to TOC

Trump Cares About Controlling the Republican Party, Not Winning the Midterms

Ezra Klein · 2026-05-29 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Trump's recent string of intra-party endorsement and primary decisions — his last-week endorsement of scandal-plagued Texas AG Ken Paxton over incumbent Senator John Cornyn, his help defeating Louisiana Senator Bill Cassidy (who voted to convict in the first impeachment), his successful primary of Kentucky's Thomas Massie (the House Republican who helped release the Epstein files), his attacks on Brian Fitzpatrick (one of the few House Republicans in a Harris-won district), and the $1.8 billion "Anti-Weaponization Fund" appearing designed to pay out to Jan. 6 convicts. Plus Trump's "not even a little bit" answer when asked whether Americans' cost-of-living concerns motivated his Iran posture. Klein engages Republican strategist Liam Donovan (president at Targeted Victory, former Cornyn and NRSC staffer) for an interlocutor's read.

Klein's central thesis is interpretive-strategic: Trump's behavior since approval-rating decline is incompatible with a president trying to win midterms (no centrist pivot, no protection of vulnerable Republicans, active intervention against safe Republican seats) but coherent with a president prioritizing control of the Republican Party itself as his long-term power base and post-presidency protection. The evidence chain is the Paxton-Cornyn endorsement (risking a Texas Senate seat Republicans should hold easily), the Massie and Cassidy primaries (the latter in a state Democrats cannot win), and the Fitzpatrick attack (against the rare Republican who actually represents a swing district). The strategic logic: a Democratic Congress gives Trump an enemy to fight and frees him from legislative work he dislikes, while a Republican Congress with the spine to investigate or abandon him is the actual threat — so making opposition-from-the-right politically suicidal serves his interests even when it costs his party seats. The piece's secondary frame is Trump's continued power as the binding constraint on the post-Trump GOP: Donovan agrees the Carlson-Owens-Massie-Greene "YouTube Republican" wing has real momentum among young Republicans on Israel/Iran/Gaza, but argues none of it can dislodge Trump while he's alive — making the post-Trump fracture (Vance vs. Carlson, Fox News generation vs. YouTube generation) the actual 2028+ question. Donovan partially pushes back: Trump's 2024 operation has actually been better at picking competitive-state candidates than 2022 was, making Paxton-style purges the exceptions rather than the macro story. Klein's hypothesis predicts Trump would prefer a Democratic Congress to a Republican Congress that crosses him; the cycle's developing evidence is increasingly consistent with that prediction.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is Klein operating in his strongest mode — the long interpretive-strategic frame applied to a pattern of recent decisions that mainstream coverage treats as discrete events. The hypothesis is testable in the way Klein flags upfront, which is what makes it more than a vibe: if Trump cared about winning the Senate, the marginal expensive purge (Cornyn) is the one decision a strategic president would not make at his all-time approval low. The piece converges directly with this week's Silver's "A party of one isn't a healthy party" (the GOP-ex-Trump grades 1.25 on Silver's 11-dimension party-strength rubric, with the Paxton 64-36 rout as endpoint) and with McKown-Dawson and Silver's "Is Trump trying to turn Texas blue?" (mechanics: Paxton's scandals plus the national D+7 generic-ballot lead put Paxton-Talarico at D+1; the ~$20M runoff is money Republicans can't spend defending Maine, Iowa, Florida, Alaska, Ohio). Three roster pieces this week reach the same diagnosis through different routes — Silver via party-health rubric, McKown-Dawson via electoral mechanics, Klein via Trump-strategic-motivation reverse-engineering. The convergence is the week's strongest cross-writer pattern in Theme 04.

The strongest counterargument Klein engages only obliquely is the impulsiveness reading: Trump is not strategic, the Paxton endorsement was a personal-affinity choice in a context where Paxton was "his guy too," and Klein's strategic-control framework is post-hoc rationalization of behavior that is actually whim. Donovan flirts with this read ("he just kind of mashed buttons" in 2022) before backing off. The strongest version of the impulsiveness counter would note that the Cassidy primary (a Louisiana seat Democrats cannot win) is overdetermined by personal revenge for the impeachment vote without needing any party-control framework, and that the Massie primary was pushed largely by AIPAC over the Israel/Epstein-files fights rather than directly by Trump. Klein's framework absorbs these — Trump pursues control opportunistically through whatever motive happens to be available — but absorbing all evidence is itself a sign the framework may be unfalsifiable. The cleaner test Klein himself names is the operational claim: if his framework predicts Trump would prefer a Democratic Congress to a Republican Congress that crosses him, the cycle should produce additional pre-midterm decisions where Trump trades Republican seats for intra-party discipline. The Paxton-Cornyn decision is exactly this; the question is whether a second or third such decision arrives between now and November.

The piece also pairs with Theme 03's Lake profile of Elbridge Colby on the same "personnel war" mechanism from a different angle. Lake documents how the prioritizer-doctrine Pentagon strategist — meant to be the institutional voice of restraint inside Trump's second term — has been caged by the Wiles-Rubio memo, held-up protégé nominations, and House Armed Services Chair Mike Rogers's accusations that Colby usurped White House authority. The Klein-Donovan conversation generalizes the Lake finding: Trump's project across both the legislative branch (Paxton, Cassidy, Massie, Fitzpatrick) and the executive (Colby's institutional containment, Gabbard's DNI exit) is the same one — building a Republican coalition incapable of internal opposition to him, at whatever cost to the party's electoral prospects or institutional capacity. The convergence across Klein, Silver, McKown-Dawson, and Lake is what gives the week's Theme 04 its structural shape: not "Democrats vs. Republicans" but "Trump vs. the Republican Party as a coalitional entity," with Democrats as the contingent beneficiary.

Donovan's "YouTube Republicans vs. Fox News Republicans" framing — the Carlson-Owens-Massie-Greene generational schism on Israel/Iran/Gaza, with Vance as the only figure attempting synthesis — is the piece's other durable contribution. It pairs with last week's Compact piece from Daniel McCarthy ("Massie's dead-end libertarianism") arguing the liberty-Republican wing squandered two chances to embed inside Trump's coalition and chose permanent oppositionism instead. The Donovan read is more sympathetic — Carlson and Massie are not strategically suicidal, they're building post-Trump audience infrastructure that becomes operative when Trump exits — but the structural diagnosis is the same: nothing about the current GOP holds together absent Trump-the-person, and the 2028 fracture is overdetermined regardless of who wins the Vance-Rubio succession question. The reason this matters for the project's ongoing carry-forward on leader-or-faction-dependency-in-both-parties is that Klein-Donovan supplies the within-party-faction texture (Fox/YouTube split, attention-economy-vs-electoral-economy divergence, Vance as the only would-be synthesizer) that Silver's rubric-level diagnosis treats as a single low grade.

↩ Back to TOC

Industrial depth and the abundance question — housing, energy, and what "build" means

Damning (and Damming) the Outflow of Industry

Oren Cass · 2026-05-29 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent's May 29 Reagan Library address ("While America Slept"), plus the House Agriculture Committee chairman G.T. Thompson's H-2A expansion bill, plus a Wall Street Journal report on labor's share of national income falling to 51% (the lowest since 1947).

Cass — founder of American Compass, the post-fusionist think tank whose Commonplace magazine has spent years building the intellectual case against Reagan-era free-trade orthodoxy — frames Bessent's speech as the seminal Republican repudiation of that orthodoxy. Bessent's argument is that economic security IS national security; that GDP composition matters more than its level; that "the invisible hand" cannot be trusted to correct strategic vulnerabilities.
