Cass amplifies Bessent's "reservoir" metaphor for industrial capacity (engineers, welders, tool-and-die makers, logistics) as a more vivid restatement of the "industrial depth" framing he and Mark DiPlacido had used in their "Big Stick Economics" essay. He then bolts on three secondary arguments: that Chairman Thompson's H-2A expansion is "mudsill" cheap-labor policy that suppresses both wages AND the robotic-harvester innovation that would replace it; that Europe is finally moving toward Chinese-import tariffs (citing FT columnist Soumaya Keynes's "knife fight with a Scrabble manual" line); and that Samsung's profit-sharing deal with its union (10.5% of operating profit, ~$400K average bonus) is a model "pre-distribution" answer to the AI boom's record corporate-profit/wage-share divergence.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is a weekly Cass round-up post — the Commonplace house format — built around a single anchor event (Bessent's speech) plus three secondary themes that recur in Cass's editorial line: anti-cheap-labor immigration policy, EU-China trade-war alignment, and worker share of AI-boom gains. The piece's main argumentative work is framing the Bessent speech as a movement-conservative milestone rather than as ordinary Trump-administration messaging. Cass cites Bessent quoting Reagan only on the "we don't hide from our mistakes" line to invert the Reagan legacy — a careful rhetorical move that lets a Republican Treasury Secretary at a Reagan Forum repudiate Reagan-era economic policy without explicitly attacking Reagan himself. Cass is the audience this speech is engineered for: an American Compass figure who has spent years arguing exactly this case from outside the administration is being told from the inside that the war is won. The triumphalism is understandable but worth scrutinizing — Bessent saying it once at the Reagan Library is not the same as it having displaced free-trade economics inside the GOP coalition.

The empirical foundation is the weakest part. Cass's industrial-depth-as-sovereignty argument depends on a strong causal claim that manufacturing offshoring is what hollowed out American capacity, but the piece never engages with the well-known competing explanations: automation absorbing the bulk of manufacturing-employment decline; the services share of the economy rising as economies mature; and demand shifts away from goods. The "reservoir of practical capability" metaphor is rhetorically powerful but doesn't itself settle whether the reservoir was drained by trade policy specifically. Likewise the H-2A section relies on a strong induced-innovation claim — cheap immigrant labor blocks robotic harvesters from being commercialized — that is plausible but not demonstrated. The Bennett-Lumber-style induced-mechanization that Kishi's parallel piece invokes (cotton picker arriving once sharecropping ended) is a real pattern, but the magnitude and timing in contemporary agriculture is genuinely unknown.

The Samsung profit-share section is the most substantively interesting. The 10.5%-of-operating-profit deal is a real datum, and Cass's framing — "pre-distribution" at the point of production rather than after-the-fact transfer taxes — is the most coherent worker-share-of-AI-boom proposal currently on offer from the post-liberal right. It implicitly concedes what most American-right opposition to unions has obscured: that capturing the AI windfall requires the coordination mechanism that unions provide. Whether Cass and American Compass will pursue this concession to its logical conclusion (an actual labor-policy agenda) or treat it as illustrative-only is the test. For the project carry-forward, this piece reinforces the year-running thread on Cass's protectionism-as-sovereignty argument; the new development is the formal Treasury-Secretary endorsement, which raises the question of whether the framework now has policy machinery behind it or is still rhetorical.

↩ Back to TOC

Monopoly Round-Up: Private Equity Blocked from Buying Homes. Mostly.

Matt Stoller · 2026-05-25 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The U.S. House passing the 21st Century ROAD to Housing Act 396-13 on May 20, with a Section 1001 ban on large institutional investor purchases of single-family homes — the most significant federal housing legislation in decades.

Stoller walks through the political mechanics of how a Warren-Trump alliance produced a near-unanimous House vote (396-13) banning corporate ownership of most existing single-family homes. The setup: Trump's 2024 voters got mad about high housing costs, he couldn't move the Fed on rates, so in January he issued an executive order targeting "Wall Street treating America's neighborhoods like a trading floor" and called the big homebuilders an OPEC-like cartel sitting on "2 Million empty lots." This synced with a Tim Scott (R) / Elizabeth Warren (D) Banking Committee bill that grew to include a 350-home-per-investor cap; Senate passed it 89-10 in March. Stoller details the empirical case (FTC findings against Invitation Homes, a Blackstone spinoff, for "juicing this hog" through deceiving renters; Fed economists finding institutional buyers raise rents 60% more than average on acquisition) and explains the House fight: Republican French Hill (Financial Services chair) and Maxine Waters (87-year-old Democratic ranking member, who Stoller argues "doesn't want to give up power") teamed up to strip the ownership ban, backed by Hakeem Jeffries and the "Abundance" movement. The compromise: ban applies to existing housing stock but not new "Build to Rent" construction. Stoller's larger frame: this is "Carter-like" — a populist legislative win by a fish-out-of-water president whose party isn't actually populist, marking the beginning of the end of the post-Reagan everything-as-an-asset-class regime.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

Stoller is doing what he does well: tracing the specific institutional path of an unusual legislative outcome and naming the actors who blocked or enabled it. The piece's strongest contribution is the Waters portrait — most progressive commentary has been treating the House Democratic leadership's failure to embrace this populist housing bill as either incomprehensible or a generic "Wall Street capture" story; Stoller diagnoses it as a generational/power-retention issue (Waters, 87, who used to be a tenant-protection icon, allying with French Hill to protect institutional ownership of single-family rentals, with Jeffries backing her). Whether or not the specific motive-attribution is correct, the structural observation that octogenarian Democratic committee leaders are blocking pieces of the populist housing agenda their younger members would pass is empirically right and underreported.

The Carter parallel is also genuinely interesting — Stoller is arguing that Trump, like Carter, is doing populist economic policy that his own party hates and his successor will get credit for, the way Reagan got credit for Carter's airline/banking/trucking deregulation. This is a real historical pattern (transformative second-term legacies often belong to the next-administration "successor" who consolidates them), and Stoller's claim that the post-Reagan everything-as-asset-class regime is starting to crack is a real prediction with predictive content: if it's correct, we should see more populist-economic bills passing with cross-aisle support over the next 2-4 years, and the next Democratic administration will preserve and extend them rather than reverting to a Wall-Street-friendly default. Worth tagging for carry-forward.

The weakness is the Build-to-Rent carve-out, which Stoller flags but then minimizes. If institutional capital is "now where institutional capital is focused" (his words) on Build-to-Rent, then a ban on buying existing homes that exempts new-construction-for-rent is a ban that redirects rather than blocks. The Wall Street capital that wanted to be a corporate landlord is still going to be a corporate landlord; it just has to build the housing first instead of buying it. Stoller waves at this ("It will be left to future lawmakers to find ways of letting renters buy out their Build to Rent homes") but doesn't fully reckon with how much the carve-out gives back. The "remarkable accomplishment" framing depends on the existing housing stock being more politically and economically salient than the future housing stock — true for the next election cycle, but possibly not for the longer-run housing-as-asset-class story Stoller's own analysis predicts.

The Abundance-movement aside ("Wall Street-friendly liberals and libertarians") is rhetorically sharper than it is fair. The Abundance position is that supply restrictions (chiefly zoning) drive prices; Stoller's counter-evidence is "despite the removal of many such limitations in states like California, there hasn't been a spurt of homebuilding." This understates how minimal California's removals have actually been and how recent — and Stoller is in any case making a category error in setting up Abundance against the anti-monopoly position, since both can be partially right (zoning matters AND institutional concentration matters). The framing serves the piece's narrative but doesn't engage the actual Abundance argument.

↩ Back to TOC

The Conspiracy to Maintain High Gas Prices in California Is About Propping Up a Flailing Oil Industry

Matt Stoller · 2026-05-29 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Chevron's WSJ-amplified narrative blaming California's gas prices on environmental over-regulation, and Gavin Newsom's brewing 2028-shaped fight with the oil incumbents.

Stoller dismantles the Chevron critique by walking through the numbers. Environmental rules account for only 7% of California's $5.26/gallon price; state and local taxes another 14%; the bulk of the gap with the rest of the US is refiner and distributor margin (~30%). Since a 2015 fire at the Torrance Refinery, California's price premium over the national average jumped from $0.54 to $1.23 per gallon — the so-called "mystery gasoline surcharge" identified by California's Division of Petroleum Market Oversight (DPMO) at $59B in cumulative cost. Stoller attributes it to two structural facts: the top-four refiners control 90% of California capacity (Chevron alone at 33%), and 50% of retail sales flow through vertically integrated branded stations with exclusive supply contracts — versus just 9% nationally. He flags as the cleanest tell that the Western States Petroleum Association lobbies for keeping California's specialty CARBOB (California Reformulated Gasoline Blendstock for Oxygenate Blending) blend, since the specialty-blend requirement is what blocks new entrants from undercutting incumbent refiners. The piece closes by tying the local fight to a national story about whether the US stays locked into a fossil-fuel tech stack as renewables outcompete it elsewhere.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is Stoller in his strongest mode: forensic monopoly analysis with a clean narrative payoff. The crack-the-cartel reading hangs on three claims that are individually checkable — refiner concentration shares, the post-2015 price gap, and the DPMO's $59B cumulative-surcharge estimate — and Stoller cites the state agency (DPMO) and embeds charts rather than asserting bare. The vertical-integration argument (50% branded sales in CA vs. 9% nationally; 31¢ branded premium in CA vs. 6¢ nationally; Chevron specifically at 60-80¢) is the most novel contribution and is where the piece does work the WSJ-frame story doesn't. The Western States Petroleum Association detail — oil lobby asking for more environmental regulation on production — is the rhetorical kill shot and is the kind of forcing-function evidence that makes the cartel thesis hard to dismiss as ideology.

The weaker moves are at the edges. Stoller asserts California refiners "tell investors 24 cents/gallon and the state 72 cents/gallon" as a credibility-puncturing detail but the citation is implicit (a chart); a reader can't independently audit the two-sets-of-books claim without chasing the underlying CEC filing. Similarly, the Chevron-branded "60-80 cent" premium gets stated without a source link. These are load-bearing for the indictment, and the piece would survive scrutiny better if they were sourced inline. The closing pivot — that California is leading the country in the energy-stack transition and is "deliberately derided" — is more rhetorical than the body of the piece earns; the connection between in-state retail oligopoly behavior and national fossil-fuel-lock-in is asserted rather than demonstrated.

The deeper structural argument is the most interesting and the least developed: that incumbents win not by lobbying against regulation but by ensuring the regulatory architecture creates moats only they can clear. CARBOB is the perfect case — environmentally good, competitively exclusionary, defended by the lobby — and the proposed waiver via the California Air Resources Board (CARB, the state's clean-air regulator) is a non-legislative lever Newsom has not pulled. The note that Chevron is backing Newsom's likely successor Xavier Becerra is a one-line shiv that does real political-economy work and would reward fuller treatment in a follow-up.

↩ Back to TOC

Where Are the Economies of Scale in Homebuilding?

Brian Potter · 2026-05-28 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: a multi-month Construction Physics series on construction productivity (productivity metrics, costs rarely falling, prefab cost-savings often not materializing) — Potter now asks why economies of scale, the most obvious efficiency mechanism, are so weak in homebuilding.

Potter assembles three lines of evidence that US homebuilding exhibits surprisingly weak economies of scale. First, industry concentration is low: a 2022 Harvard Joint Center for Housing Studies (JCHS) study found 65,000+ US homebuilding firms with the top 100 holding under 50% of the market and the top 4 just 18% — vs. 90% in aircraft manufacturing and 58% in autos. Second, gross margins don't track scale: Lennar (80,000+ homes in 2025) has nearly identical margins to United Homes Group (1,192 homes). Third, in manufactured housing — which eliminates the obvious obstacles by using national HUD code, factory production, and stable crews — production is still spread across 100-300-person, ~200,000-square-foot factories making a few hundred homes each, with no relationship between firm size and gross margin. Potter then offers a theory: economies of scale work by closing the gap between raw-input costs and finished-product costs (he invokes Elon Musk's "idiot index" — the ratio of final-product cost to raw-material cost). For oil refining the ratio is ~1.2-1.3; for a BMW i3 the ratio is ~1.8; for a US single-family home, the hard-cost breakdown of ~50% labor and 50% materials means the ratio is already ~2 — about the same as a high-volume manufactured car. There's simply not enough "transformation" between lumber/drywall/concrete and a finished home for scale economies to compress further. The piece closes by noting that manufactured-home producers like Cavco and Legacy Housing maintain multiple factories within 75-120 miles of each other (well inside the ~350-mile cost-effective shipping radius), suggesting scale economies are too weak to justify consolidation even where transportation isn't a binding constraint. Potter previews future essays on three possible strategies: high-ratio sectors like nuclear, backward vertical integration into materials, and finding ways to use fewer or cheaper raw materials.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is Potter at the top of his form — a long-form Atoms-not-Bits explainer that takes a counterintuitive empirical finding (no economies of scale in a multi-hundred-thousand-unit-per-year industry) and earns it by walking through three independent evidence streams before reaching for a theoretical framework. The framework itself — the "idiot index" as a diagnostic for where scale economies can and can't work — is genuinely useful and is the kind of conceptual import that Construction Physics is uniquely positioned to make. (Musk's "idiot index" is the ratio of final-product cost to the cost of its raw materials; a high index suggests room for efficiency improvements, a low index suggests not. Potter modifies Musk's definition slightly to include purchased components.) Naming the ratio explicitly lets Potter pose a hypothesis that's falsifiable — if you found a high-ratio sector of construction (Potter nominates nuclear) and still saw weak scale economies, his theory would need revision.

The empirical evidence is well-marshaled and the comparisons are well-chosen. The Lennar vs. United Homes Group margin comparison is the kind of clean cross-firm test the homebuilding industry rarely gets. The manufactured-housing section is the most analytically interesting because it's the cleanest natural experiment: by removing the standard culprits (local building codes, jobsite variability, crew churn), Potter shows that even after you control for those, the scale economies still don't appear. The Cavco-and-Legacy-multiple-factories-close-together fact is the smoking-gun argument: if scale paid off, firms would consolidate.

The Ned Eichler quotes from the 1980s — that even US Home producing 15,000 units/year had not achieved economies of scale — function as a forty-year stress test of the thesis. That history matters because it forecloses one possible objection (that the industry just hasn't tried hard enough yet at scale). Levitt and Sons built thousands of houses in Levittowns using a reverse assembly line and the unit cost wasn't materially lower than smaller builders'.

What's slightly less developed is the institutional/regulatory dimension. Potter notes that Lennar and D.R. Horton "effectively subcontract all of their actual homebuilding to local contractors" — a finding that, if pursued, suggests the industry's organizational form is itself a response to the same constraint. The largest "builders" are not actually building; they're land assemblers and design coordinators wrapping a network of small subcontractors. This is consistent with Potter's input/output thesis but adds a labor-market layer (the persistence of small local trade contractors who can quote a price hard to underprice) that the framework doesn't fully capture. The three concluding strategies (high-ratio sectors, backward integration, fewer/cheaper materials) preview the next pieces and read as a serious research agenda rather than an obligatory close. This is the kind of piece that earns Potter's "Atoms-not-Bits" promotion to the regular Crosscurrents roster.

↩ Back to TOC

When the Train Comes Back to Town

Brad Pearce · 2026-05-28 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The September 2026 first-revenue run of the Washington, Idaho, & Montana Railway (WI&M) — a 1900s lumber short-line in eastern Washington and northern Idaho that was abandoned in 2018 and revived through a 2020-2026 effort by Jason Hill and Bennett Lumber Products Inc.

Pearce, a Palouse, Washington resident reporting from his own town (Commonplace's place-and-industry beat), tells the story of how the WI&M came back. The line ran from Palouse east into the Idaho forests, serving the lumber industry that originally founded these towns (including Potlatch, Idaho — once a company town of the Potlatch Lumber Company, home to the world's largest white pine sawmill, now a struggling bedroom community whose high-school mascot is still the Loggers). Truck shipping and federally-funded damming of the Columbia and Snake rivers gradually drained the line's business; Watco, the regional rail operator, abandoned the unprofitable 18-mile Idaho segment in 2018. The revival required Jason Hill (a railroad hobbyist who got the skills on a Mount Rainier tourist line), a sale of the Idaho track to family-owned Bennett Lumber (280 employees), $millions in restoration including 11,000 new railroad ties at $70-100 each, and a leased 1956 EMD GP9 diesel locomotive (#1838, ~$115K used vs ~$2M for a new engine that would last less long due to electrical-component obsolescence). The line now runs 5-10 cars each Saturday and Pearce closes by arguing that only a family business — not an institutional-investor-owned firm — could justify the long-horizon, community-element-inclusive economics this project required.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is Pearce's place-and-industry beat for Commonplace — first-person reporting from a small town that has been on the receiving end of the industrial-decline story American Compass and Commonplace exist to push back against. The piece works as journalism: real interviews (Hill the operator, Bryson Bennett the company VP, Kim Rundle the bar owner), concrete numbers (11,000 ties at $70-100, $115K used engine, 19 empty cars on the first eastbound run, 7 cars and ~21 trucks-worth of lumber on the first revenue run, 5-10 cars/Saturday now), and specific industry detail (newer engines built in the 1990s already obsolete because their electrical components have been discontinued — a notable concrete data-point cutting against newer-is-better assumptions). The lumber-from-the-Bennett-yard to Colorado/New Mexico/Oklahoma/Texas routing is specific and verifiable in a way most "rural America revival" stories aren't.

The policy and ideological frame is thinner. Pearce gestures briefly at two arguments — that trains are unfairly perceived as socialism while government-funded roads are perceived as freedom, and that institutional-investor ownership would have killed the project — but doesn't develop either with the rigor a more analytical Commonplace piece (e.g., Cass on H-2A) would deploy. The "family-business patient-capital" claim is real but smaller than the rhetoric implies: Bennett Lumber bought the track because rail shipping is genuinely cheaper for some of its customers per board-foot, not as charity. The deal pencils. Whether private-equity or REIT ownership of Bennett would have killed the deal is plausible but stipulated, not demonstrated. The closing turn — contrasting "real things" (lumber) with "vacuous or outright fake" things (AI, sports betting, internet pornography, data centers) — is Commonplace house-style anti-financialization rhetoric, signposted clearly enough that readers will read past it to the actual reporting.

The piece's real contribution to the project carry-forward is as a concrete instance of the induced-modernization-meets-patient-capital case Cass argues for in the abstract. Where Cass's "Damning the Outflow" piece this week invokes Bessent's "reservoir" metaphor without quite showing how the reservoir gets refilled, Pearce's piece is the ground-level mechanism: a hobbyist, a family-owned mill, a 70-year-old locomotive, $millions in restoration spread over three years. The aggregate political-economy story Commonplace tells lives or dies on whether enough Bennett-Lumber-like firms exist to support enough Jason-Hill-like restorations to add up to the "industrial commons" Cass invokes — and it's worth asking whether this case is a sample of one or a leading edge.

A modest note on the genre constraint: Commonplace publishes this kind of "trains came back to my town" piece partly because the rest of the political discourse won't, and partly because it confirms the magazine's editorial line. Both can be true. The reporting deserves to be read as reporting, not just confirmation.

↩ Back to TOC

AI as utility — the tool-not-totem case

Use AI This Election

Scott Alexander · 2026-05-26 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The June 2026 California primary ballot, and the longstanding voter problem of how to make informed choices on down-ballot races (community college board, parcel-tax measures, state superintendent) where party labels don't apply and traditional sources (party-line, newspaper endorsements, NGO endorsements) are weak signals.

Alexander prompted Claude with a self-description ("centrist liberal abundance YIMBY whose favorite political writers are Kelsey Piper, Matt Yglesias, and Ezra Klein") plus a request for candidate bios, policies, endorsements, key distinctions, and a final recommendation per race. The two excerpts he reproduces — California Superintendent of Public Instruction, and Measure A (Peralta Community College District parcel-tax reauthorization) — show Claude doing several things well: (1) framing the office's actual scope (state superintendent has limited authority; districts control most budget and curriculum); (2) building an explicit ideological filter ("the Kelsey Piper view") and ranking each candidate against it; (3) calling out the structurally interesting candidate (Muratsuchi as the only one who actually legislated structured-literacy funding); (4) on Measure A, providing both the affirmative case (community college cost-effectiveness, reauthorization rather than new tax, $48/parcel is small, no formal opposition) and concrete complications a careful voter would want to know about (the 2018–2023 accreditation crisis, the 2021 Alameda grand jury finding on broken board culture, the donor-vendor conflict where the campaign's largest single donor is a $678K-contract flooring vendor). Alexander self-reports a 10-race informal test: 5 A grades (matched his eventual vote), 3 B (his second-choice in a large field), 2 C (he disagreed but saw the reasoning), 0 F. Failure modes he identifies: Claude over-weighted his single-issue prompt cue (abundance/YIMBY), under-weighted strategic considerations (e.g., voting fringe candidates on races that don't matter), and over-corrected on party affiliation (inferring he'd lean Democratic from "liberal" and downweighting Republicans correspondingly). When asked to ignore his preferences and recommend who Claude thought best, three of four divergent picks Alexander preferred to Claude's original recommendation. He closes with a speculative-futurism note: AI-assisted political deliberation is one of his standing positive cases for how the post-AGI future could go well despite dictatorship/inequality/surveillance risks.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is Alexander at his most utilitarian-rationalist: an applied trip report on whether contemporary LLMs are useful for a specific, common, high-stakes cognitive task (informed voting on down-ballot races), with a roughly-quantified self-evaluation and explicit failure-mode analysis. The piece's strongest feature is the concrete excerpts. The Superintendent of Public Instruction sample is unusually well-constructed Claude output — it does the thing most voter guides don't, which is build an explicit ideological framework from the user's stated commitments and rank candidates against it rather than producing the standard "here are their positions, you decide" mush. The Peralta Measure A excerpt is even more interesting because Claude flagged a specific transparency concern (a flooring vendor with $678K in current district contracts being the campaign's largest single donor) that would not appear in any standard voter guide — that's the kind of context-specific signal that distinguishes useful AI research from regurgitated talking points.

The most interesting analytical observation Alexander makes, almost in passing, is that Claude's recommendations were better when asked to ignore his stated preferences. Three of four "what would you recommend if you didn't know me" divergent picks Alexander preferred to Claude's original ones. Alexander suggests this is because the unrestricted prompt let Claude make more "emotional" judgments; a less charitable read is that the original prompt encouraged Claude to cargo-cult its way to the candidate most rhetorically aligned with Kelsey Piper's stated positions rather than the candidate most likely to be good at the actual job. This is a real epistemic asymmetry — users prompt for ideological match because that's what feels legible, but the better question is often "who is likeliest to be competent in this office's actual scope of action," which doesn't require knowing the user's politics at all. Alexander gets near this but doesn't quite name it.

The piece's broader claim — that AI-assisted deliberation is a load-bearing positive case for post-AGI futures — is doing a lot of work and warrants more scrutiny than Alexander provides. The strongest version is something like: down-ballot races are an information-access problem (most voters can't spend an hour per race, so they fall back on party-line, name recognition, or noise), and AI lowers the cost of personalized research by 1–2 orders of magnitude, which structurally improves democratic information-processing without depending on any specific elite institution. That's a real benefit and Alexander's anecdotal A/B/C results are consistent with it. The weaker version of the claim — that AI political advisors are a meaningful counterweight to AGI-era concentration of power — is much harder to defend, because the same systems that produce useful voter research are owned by a small handful of companies whose training data, RLHF preferences, and refusal patterns are themselves political artifacts. Alexander doesn't engage with the obvious objection (what happens to AI-assisted voting when the model is trained to subtly favor candidates aligned with the lab's commercial or regulatory interests?) but it's a serious one. His own example data shows Claude has a default lean — Alexander notes Claude "leaned away from Republican candidates" after inferring his political affiliation, which suggests the model's defaults are doing meaningful work that users without Alexander's metacognitive awareness wouldn't notice.

A smaller observation: the piece is unusual for Alexander in that it's almost entirely practical — no speculative thought experiments, no historical analogies, no concept-mining for what a category secretly is. He's testing a workflow and reporting the results. Given how much of his recent output has been long-form interpretive essays (the Frankfurt School review the same week, for instance), it's worth noting that he can also write tight applied pieces when the topic warrants. The piece will be widely shared by readers looking for a permission slip to actually use Claude this way; whether that's good or bad depends on whether the median user has Alexander's metacognitive discipline about prompt-effects on output, which most don't.

↩ Back to TOC

Your future job will be to keep AI on task

Noahpinion · 2026-05-27 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Alex Imas's "Ghosts of Electricity" Substack post arguing humans will be valuable for their humanness alone, plus a cluster of AI-economic-displacement debates (Ryan Greenblatt on misalignment, METR's reward-hacking measurements, Kiran Garimella on AI detectors).

Smith rejects Imas's "humans-as-prestige-good" thesis (people are happily paying for Waymos over Uber drivers) and counter-proposes that the durable human job will be alignment maintenance: keeping increasingly agentic AI systems pointed at human goals. He develops the case in three moves. First, verification and alignment are the same thing — once AI output is technically competent, what's left to check is whether it matches what the user/company actually wanted, which is irreducibly a human judgment. The "workslop" phenomenon documented by BetterUp Labs and Stanford Social Media Lab (low-effort AI output that shifts work downstream to the receiver) shows verification-labor becoming more scarce and therefore more valuable, with middle managers (the Bill Lumbergh figures from Office Space) as the unexpected winners. Second, AI cannot reliably verify other AI: he cites Xu et al.'s resume experiment showing 67-82% self-preference bias, Adam Kucharski's fake-data interpretation experiment, hallucinated citations rising in biomedical papers, and Ryan Greenblatt's catalog of AI reward-hacking and overselling, all to argue that "who guards the guardians" recurses. Third, even hypothetical superintelligent AI would develop its own goals via reward-hacking and would operate at a time-scale (minutes/hours) misaligned with human time-scales (days/months), requiring human "Tiger Mom" supervision. The closing analogy: humans are their utility functions; wanting things is the most fundamental thing we do; the Monkey's-Paw problem ensures wishing-correctly will remain hard cognitive work.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is Smith doing what he does best — taking a live AI-displacement debate and pulling a plausible economic mechanism out of it. The piece's central conceptual move (verification and alignment are the same problem viewed at different scales) is genuinely useful and not commonly stated this cleanly. The Lumbergh-as-future-of-work inversion of the Office Space trope is a sharp rhetorical anchor; the "Tiger Mom poking her head in" image lands the alignment-as-supervision point in a way technical-alignment writing usually fails to.

The substantive case has two weak joints, however. First, the empirical evidence Smith stacks is suggestive but it doesn't quite establish his economic claim. Workslop is a real phenomenon; whether it produces durable rents for middle managers, or whether AI verification tools catch up and commoditize verification too, is exactly the question Smith waves at without answering. He concedes mid-piece that current AI fraud-detection (Pangram et al.) may not work for long, but then assumes human verification will remain valuable because "AI biases recur." That's a story, not an argument: it assumes that the failure modes of AI-checking-AI are persistent rather than temporary, but the persistence claim is what needs defending. Reward-hacking might be a solved problem in three model generations; the Xu et al. self-preference bias might be eliminable with better training; Greenblatt's frustrations might be 2026-specific artifacts. Smith's case essentially requires that some class of alignment problems remains unsolvable by AI alone, indefinitely. He gestures at this with the "AI will have its own goals" claim, but that's a speculative premise doing load-bearing work the empirical examples can't quite support.

Second, the piece elides the wage-distribution question that should follow from its own logic. If verification labor becomes the scarce input, and the median worker is now producing commoditized AI-assisted slop while a small class of managers/verifiers captures rents, that's a description of increased inequality and labor-market hollowing, not a reassuring "humans will still have jobs" story. Smith notes in passing that "salaries for middle management should rise relative to the salaries of the people pressing the slop button," but doesn't follow the implication — the slop-button-pressers may not have wages worth comparing to anything in the new equilibrium. The piece is framed as a comforting answer to the "what will humans do" question, but the answer it delivers ("a smaller class of humans will do supervisory work") is closer to the dystopian readings it's positioning against. This is a place where Smith's center-left abundance-liberal priors smooth over the distributional implications his own framework generates.

Third — and this is a Smith pattern worth flagging — the piece does the thing where it presents one mechanism (alignment-as-job) as the answer when the more honest framing would be "here is one plausible niche; there are also others (regulatory complement, in-person services, last-mile customization), and none of them individually solves the displacement problem." The "the job of the future" framing is a Substack-essay convention but it overstates the singular case. The Hume epigraph ("reason is the slave of the passions") is doing more work than the piece earns: it gestures at a deep philosophical claim about why humans-as-utility-function is irreducible, but the actual economic argument relies on much weaker claims about AI's current limits.

What survives criticism is the verification/alignment unification and the recognition that the operative scarce input in an AI-saturated economy is knowing what you want and confirming you got it. That's a useful contribution to the AI-labor discourse, and worth carrying forward independent of the specifics about middle-management wages.

↩ Back to TOC

Doc in a Box

Alex Tabarrok · 2026-05-28 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The first published review of Doctronic's AI-prescription-refill pilot in Utah.

In 72% of cases the AI recommended a refill; on those, at least one of two reviewing physicians agreed with the AI in 97% of cases. In the remaining 28% the AI escalated to a human telehealth appointment rather than approve; reviewing physicians judged 69% of those escalations appropriate and 31% overly cautious. Tabarrok notes overcaution is the right failure mode for a new system. He closes with a Doctronic-founder quote arguing that compute costs drop ~10x every five years while demand for care keeps rising, so AI consultations costing a few dollars today will cost pennies — turning the structural physician-shortage problem into an engineering problem with a known solution path.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

Frontal Tabarrok healthcare-delivery post, in his long-running beat that supply-side fixes (NP scope-of-practice expansion, telehealth, FDA reciprocity, retail-clinic deregulation) matter more than the demand-side healthcare debates that dominate U.S. political attention. The Doctronic numbers are encouraging — a 97% physician-agreement rate on auto-approvals would be excellent for a human physician, let alone a software system — but the bar is doing a lot of work in this framing.

Two cautions worth surfacing. First, the 97% agreement is on the 72% of cases the AI was confident enough to auto-approve, which by construction is the easier subset; the harder question is whether the 28% escalation set captures all the genuinely hard cases or whether some get misrouted into the auto-approve bucket and lock in errors. Second, "physician agreed" is not the same as "correct" — both could be wrong in the same direction (over-prescribing benzodiazepines, missing contraindications), and the audit can't catch that.

The founder-quote framing — "engineering problems have solutions" — is the load-bearing claim, and it's where Tabarrok and his critics will disagree most sharply. The optimistic reading is exactly right for refills, where the underlying medicine is settled and the bottleneck is throughput. The pessimistic reading is that the parts of primary care that actually cause health-system bottlenecks (diagnostic uncertainty, multi-comorbidity management, behavioral-health integration) are not well-modeled as "throw cheaper compute at it" problems. Tabarrok's piece is right on its own narrow claim about refills; the generalization to "AI solves the primary-care shortage" is bigger than the evidence base in this post supports.

↩ Back to TOC

AI in gdp

Tyler Cowen · 2026-05-28 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: A new paper by Anton Korinek and Patrick McKelvey, via Samir Varma.

Cowen excerpts three short claims: (1) quality-adjusted AI output grew over 2,000% per year in 2024 and 2025, driven by algorithmic progress (largest), hardware-efficiency gains, and data-center capacity; (2) nominal AI gross domestic product (GDP) hit roughly $250 billion in 2025, growing ~2,600% in quality-adjusted real terms; (3) national accounts weren't built to track this, and statistics agencies should build AI satellite accounts now.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

Frontal post. The headline number — 2,000%+ annual quality-adjusted growth — is doing all the work, and it hinges on the quality adjustment, which is where the measurement battle actually lives. A model that's "10x better" by some benchmark counts as 10x more output in the quality-adjusted framework; the choice of benchmark (Massive Multitask Language Understanding (MMLU)? code-eval? compute-equivalent?) is load-bearing and contested. The $250B nominal figure is more grounded but small — about 0.85% of U.S. GDP — and most of it is AI-firm revenue, not consumer-surplus generation.

The "build satellite accounts now" prescription is the right call procedurally, regardless of whether the specific growth number holds up. The 2000s software boom is the cautionary parallel: economists spent a decade arguing about whether productivity statistics were missing software's contribution, and by the time the methodology was settled, the question had moved on. Doing the methodology work upfront is cheap insurance.

↩ Back to TOC

The Compact cultural cluster — yuppies, post-literacy, and the post-liberal cultural register

How Yuppies Changed America

Helen Andrews · 2026-05-29 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Dylan Gottlieb's new book Yuppies: The Bankers, Lawyers, Joggers, and Gourmands Who Conquered New York (Harvard University Press, 2026), which Gottlieb (a Bentley historian) describes as "the first social history of financialization."

Andrews accepts most of Gottlieb's empirical chronicle — the doubling of NYC corporate lawyers 1978-1986, Harvard MBAs migrating from manufacturing to Wall Street, single women buying a third of US condos by 1980, the Manhattan birthrate collapse, Skadden Arps's innovations in preemptive bankruptcy (Continental Airlines, Manville asbestos), non-refundable retainers as "takeover insurance," and the financialization-driven inner-city arson wave (the 1968 Urban Property Protection and Reinsurance Act forced insurers to cover slum buildings, the new investment-driven business model made fast payouts cheaper than investigations, fifty-six died from fires in Hoboken alone 1978-1983). She breaks with him on the interpretive frame: where Gottlieb (and Joe Flom, the Skadden architect whose oral history Gottlieb relies on) treats the displaced WASP elite as racist, sexist, and lazy, Andrews insists the old order was norm-enforcing rather than incompetent — Paul Cravath was an Ohio abolitionist's son who attended Oberlin with black classmates, Cravath the firm had a strict anti-nepotism rule, hired Helen Buttenweiser in the 1930s and made Edward Benjamin a Jewish partner in 1958. Her concluding move generalizes from the WASP case: "exclusion is how moral codes are enforced," and the Clinton-Gottlieb hope for a diverse-and-collegial elite has had four decades to materialize without doing so.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is Andrews working her established Boomer-decline beat — her 2021 book Boomers indicted the generation for trading social cohesion for personal liberation — and Gottlieb's book is unusually well-suited to her frame because it gives her the financialization-as-moral-revolution synthesis she's been building toward without forcing her to do the archival work herself. The review is competent reception-criticism: she accepts the empirical history, contests the moral verdict, and uses the disagreement to advance a substantive thesis about norm-enforcement.

The strongest move is the Paul Cravath rehabilitation. The standard Skadden-vs-Cravath story (popularized in Lincoln Caplan's Skadden and reproduced in business-school case studies) treats Cravath as the genteel old guard that got outhustled by Jewish-immigrant strivers; Andrews's counter — that Cravath was a small-town Ohioan whose abolitionist father co-founded Fisk University and who himself attended Oberlin at a time it enrolled more black students than any other predominantly white college — is verifiable biographical fact that punctures the WASP-as-aristocrat caricature in a single paragraph. The hiring record (Buttenweiser 1930s, Beshar 1971 partner, Benjamin 1958 Jewish partner) makes the discrimination case look meaningfully overdetermined: the old firm was diversifying on a timeline broadly comparable to its rivals while maintaining the partnership-track norms (anti-nepotism, no rainmaker partnerships) the new firms abandoned.

The weakest move is the closing maneuver — "exclusion is how moral codes are enforced" — which does real work in the review but conceals its scope. Read tightly, it's a claim about voluntary professional associations: a law firm with norms needs the right to deny partnership to people who violate them. Read loosely, it's a claim about ethnic and racial exclusion as norm-enforcement, which is what most readers will hear given the WASP/Jewish framing of the surrounding argument. Andrews doesn't disambiguate, and the elision lets the piece carry a stronger anti-diversity conclusion than the actual argument supports. The Cravath case she just made cuts against the loose reading — Cravath enforced norms while expanding its hiring pool — but she doesn't draw the obvious lesson, that norm-enforcement and inclusivity were historically compatible in the old order and the financialization revolution destroyed both at once. That's the more interesting version of the argument and Andrews leaves it underdeveloped.

The Hillary Clinton 1989 ABA-speech anecdote is a genuinely useful pull and shows the piece doing what good book reviews do: surfacing a sourced detail (Clinton bemoaning lawyer-profitability at the expense of family life as chair of the ABA Commission on Women in the Profession) that complicates the standard partisan-narrative read of who was sounding alarms about financialization in real time. Whether Compact readers will absorb this as evidence that the diagnosis crossed party lines, or as ammunition for the "liberalism has had four decades and failed" conclusion, depends on the reader's priors more than the text.

↩ Back to TOC

From Human Bondage to Open Borders

Daniel Kishi · 2026-05-27 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Rep. Jasmine Crockett's (D-TX) April speech at a Waterbury, Connecticut Baptist church, in which she defended permissive immigration policy on the grounds that "we done picking cotton" — plus the wider bipartisan defense of cheap-foreign-labor agriculture (Pelosi 2022, Bush 2006, Lindsey Graham 2013, the current Rep. G.T. Thompson H-2A expansion bill).

Kishi — a Commonplace staff writer on the labor-and-immigration beat — traces the "jobs Americans won't do" argument back to its formal articulation in slaveholding South Carolina senator James Henry Hammond's March 1858 Senate "Cotton is King" speech. Hammond's "mudsill theory" rests on three premises: certain work is beneath the dignity of citizens, society nonetheless requires someone to do it, and the answer is a subordinate class whose legal status forecloses refusal. Substitute "immigrant" for "slave," Kishi argues, and the modern version is structurally identical — not abolition of the mudsill but its outsourcing. He shows the structure in three contemporary cases: (a) the existing illegal-immigrant agricultural workforce, whose unenforceable labor rights are the source of its docility; (b) the H-2A program's worker-tied-to-single-sponsor design, which builds dependency by law; and (c) long-haul trucking, where the American Trucking Association's lobbied-for English-proficiency relaxation, self-certified training, and non-domiciled CDL licensing converted a middle-class trade into a mudsill job. He invokes Lincoln's September 1859 Wisconsin Agricultural Society address as the direct rebuttal: the "free labor" doctrine that labor is prior to capital, that workers can leave because they have skills and legal standing, and that the path to prosperity runs through education and "capital deepening" rather than coerced labor. The historical proof case: when the captive labor force was gone (Great Migration), the mechanical cotton picker finally arrived.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is a structural-history piece that does what good Commonplace writing does well: takes a contemporary policy debate (H-2A expansion, illegal-immigration enforcement) and reframes it by reaching for the 19th-century American precedent that the post-fusionist right has been claiming for itself. The Hammond-as-mudsill-theorist reading is genuinely useful as analytic frame — it captures something the standard "jobs Americans won't do" debate keeps missing, which is that the phrase contains a moral premise (this work is beneath the dignity of citizens) the people deploying it would not concede if pressed. That Crockett, a progressive Black congresswoman, deployed it almost identically to George W. Bush ("jobs Americans won't do") is the piece's sharpest evidentiary contribution. The cross-party convergence is real and worth surfacing.

The analogy doing the load-bearing work is "slavery → modern illegal-immigrant agriculture" (with H-2A and trucking as intermediate cases), and it requires stress-testing. The strongest part: the structural premise — a class with foreclosed exit, justified by the claim that the work is fixed and only the workforce is negotiable — does carry across the cases. The weakest part: the moral and material gap between chattel slavery and contemporary low-wage immigrant labor is enormous, and the rhetorical move (Hammond's apology for slavery = Crockett's defense of immigration policy) elides differences of degree that matter. Kishi half-acknowledges this when he concedes that Hammond's portrait of slavery was "a self-serving lie" but says the structural claim about Northern wage labor survives. The careful reader should be tracking whether the analogy is illuminating or whether it's importing moral weight from slavery to win an immigration argument. Kishi mostly stays on the right side of that line because he is explicit about which premise he's borrowing.

The induced-mechanization claim — that the mechanical cotton picker arrived only when the captive labor force was gone — is the empirical claim the whole essay rides on. It is partly true (the timing roughly fits) but the historiography on the cotton picker is more contested than Kishi acknowledges: the technology had been under development for decades, and the Great Migration is one of several factors that affected its adoption rate. The stronger general claim — that wherever cheap captive labor is available, labor-saving capital investment is discouraged — is the well-known induced-innovation hypothesis (Hicks-Habakkuk), and Kishi could have invoked it explicitly to give the argument more bite. As it stands, the historical example is illustrative rather than dispositive.

For the project carry-forward, this piece is the long-form companion to Cass's "Damning the Outflow" round-up: same author-stable, same magazine, same anti-cheap-labor case, but Kishi's version is the moral-philosophical foundation and Cass's is the policy round-up. The two should be read together. The Hammond/Lincoln framing is also a deliberate Commonplace move on writer-of-the-American-tradition territory the political left has historically claimed — it argues, in effect, that Lincoln's free-labor tradition belongs to the post-fusionist right now, and that progressives have left themselves rhetorically defenseless by leaning into the mudsill premise. Whether that claim sticks depends on whether the right's policy machinery actually delivers higher wages and better conditions for the workers in question, or merely tightens immigration enforcement without the productivity-and-investment agenda that would let Americans take the jobs.

↩ Back to TOC

Title IX's Collision Course with Reality

Scott Yenor · 2026-05-28 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The May 2026 House v. NCAA settlement implementation — which establishes revenue-sharing salary caps and roster limits for college athletes — interacting with the proportionate-parity Title IX safe harbor that has been the operative standard since the 1996 Cohen v. Brown ruling and the Obama-era Department of Education OCR Dear Colleague letters.

Yenor's mechanism: under the safe harbor, the percentage of female athletes must match the percentage of female undergraduates (now 59% nationally) or schools risk lawsuits from female athletes whose sports get cut. The NIL settlement's roster caps force schools to shrink women's rowing, soccer, basketball, etc., which will move most major programs further from parity (he walks through Alabama, Florida, LSU, Texas A&M numbers); the only way back to safe harbor is adding novel women's sports like Nebraska's planned 2028 flag-football team, even though men dominate recreational flag football. He uses Brown's 1990s budget-cut lawsuit (Judge Hugh Bownes ruled empirical evidence of lower female sports interest "cannot justify providing fewer athletic opportunities") and a 2025 Concordia-Irvine federal preliminary injunction to argue that the standard locks schools into a one-way ratchet. The proposed remedy: Trump-administration regulatory action to allow interest-based compliance (a Bush-era option that lost out) and possibly exempt revenue-producing male sports from the totals. Caveat for downstream readers: the institutional mechanism is well-described, but the central empirical claim that women's sports interest is meaningfully lower than men's rests on a single 2004 Steven Rhoads book rather than current participation data and treats current outcomes as a measure of latent preference.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

Yenor is writing in the Heritage Foundation's policy-essay register — he chairs Heritage's American Citizenship Initiative and his work in this venue typically combines accurate procedural-history accounts with conservative-coalitional framing — and the strongest part of this piece is the institutional mechanism. The House v. NCAA settlement, finalized in 2025 and beginning to take effect for the 2025-26 athletic year, did impose roster caps as a cost-control measure (in exchange for athletes getting NIL revenue-sharing); the Title IX safe-harbor proportionality standard (one prong of the OCR's "three-part test" from the 1979 Policy Interpretation, institutionalized by Cohen v. Brown and Obama-era Dear Colleague guidance) does push schools toward maintaining female-athlete percentages at or above female-undergraduate percentages. The collision Yenor describes — shrinking women's rosters while needing to grow them — is a real regulatory contradiction and his specific examples (Alabama women's rowing going from 115 to 68, Concordia-Irvine's August 2025 preliminary injunction) are checkable and broadly accurate.

The weakness sits at the load-bearing empirical claim. Yenor's argument that women's varsity flag football is absurd because "men and boys make up the lion's share of flag football players in all recreational leagues" rests on the contention that competitive sports interest is meaningfully lower for women — for which his only direct citation is Steven Rhoads's Taking Sex Differences Seriously (2004), a book that itself relies on older participation-rate data from a period before women's recreational and competitive sports participation had grown substantially. Two decades of post-Title IX outcomes — soccer being the most participated youth sport for girls, women's basketball viewership records, the WNBA's recent expansion — make the "enduring biological interest gap" claim much more contestable than Yenor implies. The honest version of his argument is that the gap exists (it does) but not that current participation gaps reliably measure latent interest, since participation is itself shaped by opportunity structures the proportionality rule was designed to expand. That's a defensible position; Yenor's stronger framing ("when Title IX meets nature") collapses the empirical question.

The proposed remedy — let schools comply via interest surveys plus revenue-sport exemptions — is more interesting than the diagnosis. The Bush-era OCR did promulgate exactly this option (the 2005 "model survey" guidance) and it was rescinded by the Obama OCR in 2010 as too easily gamed by schools depressing measured interest. Yenor doesn't engage with why the survey approach was abandoned, which leaves the policy half of his essay weaker than the descriptive half. For a Crosscurrents reader: the piece is genuinely useful for the NIL/Title IX mechanism (which is not being widely covered in the sports press from this angle), and worth discounting on the "interest gap is natural" claim. It pairs interestingly with Yost on tradwives in the same issue — both pieces are working a "feminism overreaches against demographic reality" thesis, but Yost is doing literary criticism while Yenor is doing administrative-law analysis, and the law analysis is the better-supported half.

↩ Back to TOC

The Cheap Tradsploitation of 'Yesteryear'

Julia Yost · 2026-05-27 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Caro Claire Burke's debut novel Yesteryear (2026), a bestselling time-travel satire in which a 21st-century tradwife influencer (Natalie Heller Mills, a "good Christian woman" with prairie dresses and a hobby farm in Idaho) wakes up in 1855 on the same farm with her husband transformed into a violent patriarch; the novel's positive reception in The Guardian and the New York Times.

Yost's structural charge has three layers. First, the satire fails on its own terms because Burke doesn't know her subject: physical motherhood is sketched in clichés (a tear-during-childbirth scene contrasted with no mention of pelvic-floor weakness across multiple births), Natalie's religious identity is incoherent across denominations (Amish to Catholic, low-church to Sign-of-the-Cross-reciting), and the prose itself is bad enough to undermine the protagonist's claimed Harvard-honed cleverness ("a house absent of stuff," "shoring up energy," a nanny described as "unblinking, like a cat blinking"). Second, the violence inflicted on Natalie in 1855 is consistent with revenge fantasy rather than social critique — wounds matter when the plot wants them to matter, and don't when it doesn't. Third, the novel's "the future is female" peroration is actually retrospective: progressivism is correlated with tight reproductive control and environmental pessimism, while high fertility now correlates with confidence about the future, so the genuine demographic argument runs the other way. Yost folds in the Granta prose-scandal (where bad prose was published as good in a prominent literary venue) as evidence that political alignment, not literary quality, is what the tastemakers are buying. The literary criticism is sharp on its own terms; the closing demographic claim (that high-fertility religious communities will straightforwardly inherit the future) elides the well-documented secularization-of-the-children pattern Yost herself notes Burke depicting in the novel.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

Yost is the First Things senior editor whose 2024 NYT op-ed on Sydney Sweeney as "American Mind on legs" became one of the cleaner instances of right-Catholic-magazine writers reading mass-culture artifacts as legible signals of the demographic-cultural shift they expect. This piece works the same beat with sharper teeth, and the literary-criticism layer is largely persuasive on its own terms — the Burke-can't-write evidence she marshals is concrete enough to land (the cat-blinking-like-a-cat sentence is genuinely embarrassing), and the religious-incoherence point is fair: Burke conceding to the NYT that "a specific thread of Christianity would really just be more distracting" is the kind of admission that does collapse the satire's claim to being about anything specific in the world.

The structural weakness in Yost's argument is the slide from literary criticism to demographic prediction. The "fertile will inherit the earth" peroration is doing two distinct things at once that the essay treats as one: it's an empirical claim about differential fertility rates (true — Mormon, Orthodox Jewish, and high-religiosity Christian populations do reproduce faster than secular-progressive populations, though the magnitudes are smaller than the Steve Sailer / Eric Kaufmann literature suggests, and second-generation defection rates are substantial) and a normative claim that this constitutes "the future." The second move is where the argument starts borrowing strength from the first. Differential fertility does shift age-cohort composition but does not necessarily reproduce the parents' worldviews — Yost's own footnote that Natalie's daughters in the novel "convert from patriarchal to liberal Christianity" is exactly the secularization-of-the-children pattern that has historically attended every wave of high-fertility religious communities in the modern West.

The piece's most interesting move is the diagnosis of progressive backwards-looking-ness. The claim that the contemporary progressive imagination is suffused with environmental and demographic pessimism — climate doomerism, "we shouldn't have kids because of the planet" — is a real cultural pattern that deserves more attention than it usually gets from the venues that share Yost's coordinates. There's a defensible argument here that the political-coalitional effects of which side believes the future is worth having children for will compound over time, even if the simple genetic-inheritance version of the "fertile will inherit" thesis is too crude. Yost gestures at this in the closing line about urban professional women looking at "a photo of a big family on a windswept hill" and wanting that, but doesn't separate it from the differential-fertility argument it's actually distinct from.

For a Crosscurrents reader: the literary criticism is sharp and the cultural diagnosis is partly right; the demographic argument as deployed is the weakest layer and most of what makes the piece feel like First Things-flavored triumphalism rather than just book reviewing. Pairs with Yenor's Title IX piece in the same issue (Compact's running "feminism overreaches against demographic reality" beat) and with the Andrews-on-Yuppies review, which works a similar reaction-against-Boomer-individualism frame from a more historical angle.

↩ Back to TOC

Post-Literacy Raised the Stakes of the Odyssey Debate

John Byron Kuhner · 2026-05-26 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The months-long promotional campaign for Christopher Nolan's adaptation of Homer's Odyssey (starring Matt Damon as Odysseus, Anne Hathaway as Penelope, and Kenyan-born Lupita Nyong'o as Helen of Troy) and the unusual volume of cultural-conservative criticism it has drawn over casting, costuming, modernized dialogue, and Nolan's citation of Emily Wilson's bestselling 2017 Odyssey translation as an inspiration.

Kuhner's argument is structural: Hollywood has always taken liberties with source texts (Karl Tunberg writing the Ben-Hur screenplay without having read the book; Orson Welles casting Eartha Kitt as Helen in 1950; Denzel Washington as Don Pedro and Macbeth in Branagh/Coen Shakespeare adaptations), and the criticism currently aimed at Nolan would have been understood as missing the point until recently. What changed is not the films but the audience: the schools no longer transmit the classics, so the film adaptation is now plausibly the only version a generation will encounter. He cites Nyong'o (Yale Drama School MFA in the humanities) telling Elle "I really had no idea what the Odyssey was" before the role. The cultural stakes are therefore higher than they were when literary classics were buttressed by mandatory ninth-grade reading and quizzes. He extrapolates to a possible new norm — that filmmakers may need to treat adaptations as "propaideutic," encouraging engagement with the source rather than supplanting it, and that "the new byword for adaptations may need to be reverence."

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

Kuhner is an interesting voice for this argument because he occupies an unusual coordinate cell. He's a classicist by training (former editor of In Medias Res, the Paideia Institute's literary journal; longtime advocate for Latin pedagogy) who now runs an independent bookstore in J. D. Vance's Rust Belt Ohio, which means his "post-literacy" claim is not just a complaint from a metropolitan culture critic but an account drawn from actual book retail. The Tolkien anecdote — customers buying nice editions of The Lord of the Rings as decoration while preferring Peter Jackson's films, calling on Christopher Tolkien to back up his hatred of the Jackson adaptations — is the kind of texture that does load-bearing argumentative work in a piece like this.

The piece's strongest move is decoupling the legitimate concern (postliterate audiences) from the standard right-coded grievance (the casting of a black woman as Helen, allegedly anachronistic costumes, Emily Wilson's translation being too modern). Kuhner makes a point of noting that the casting and visual-anachronism complaints would have been considered missing the point a generation ago — Welles cast Kitt, Washington has played Spanish princes and Scottish kings without injury to Shakespeare — and that the real shift is on the receiving end, not the producing end. This is a more honest framing than the version that circulates in the discourse he's intervening in, where "Homer is being destroyed" gets conflated with "Hollywood made a casting choice I dislike."

The weakness is the prescriptive conclusion. "Reverence" as the byword for future adaptations would, if literally adopted, kill exactly the kind of adaptation Kuhner clearly admires — Luhrmann's Romeo + Juliet, the Coen Macbeth, the Welles Time Runs — all of which were freewheeling in ways the modern critics he describes would now flag. There's an unresolved tension between the descriptive claim ("the audience has changed") and the normative claim ("filmmakers should adopt reverence"), and the resolution would be that filmmakers should adopt reverence only because the schools have failed, which is a circular argument for a permanent diminishment of artistic license driven by educational collapse. The cleaner version of the argument is that the schools, not the filmmakers, are where the response should go. Kuhner gestures at this but doesn't follow through; the piece ends with a recommendation aimed at the wrong actor.

For Crosscurrents purposes: the piece is useful primarily for the postliteracy diagnosis (the Nyong'o quote is a genuinely arresting datum, and the Tolkien-bookstore observation is firsthand), and worth treating skeptically on the "reverence" prescription. Pairs naturally with the Compact "what does the secular world believe about ends" frame Shullenberger develops in the Pope Leo piece — both are diagnosing a vacuum where transmission used to happen — and with the long-running Crosscurrents thread on the disintegration of shared cultural infrastructure.

↩ Back to TOC

Be Excellent, Not Efficient

Eboo Patel · 2026-05-28 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The commencement-address genre, repurposed (per Patel's note at the top) as a Persuasion essay. The implicit foil is AI-driven inevitability ("the wave you're told to ride"), and the explicit cultural touchpoints are Mike Nichols's The Graduate ("there's a great future in plastics") and Alice Waters's founding of Chez Panisse as a counter to 1960s processed-food culture.

Patel — founder of Interfaith America (the Chicago-based interfaith-cooperation NGO he built from Obama-era faith-outreach work) and contributing writer at Persuasion — converts his May 16 Denison University commencement address into a three-point liberal-arts argument. (1) You are not obligated to ride the wave that presents itself as inevitable; liberal-arts judgment is the muscle that lets you decline. (2) Connect social change to the life you want to live, in the David-Brooks "some people find a better way to live and other people follow" mode — Alice Waters wasn't angry at fast food; she loved meals shared slowly with friends. (3) Choose excellence over volume; ihsan (Arabic "sacred excellence," the highest spiritual state in Islam) is the frame, and the Sydney-makes-Natalie-an-omelet scene from FX/Hulu's The Bear (season 2 penultimate episode) is the load-bearing illustration. Patel closes with Seneca: "While we live, while we are among human beings, let us cultivate our humanity."

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is a commencement-address-turned-essay, and it deserves to be read on its genre terms. Commencement addresses sit at the intersection of personal philosophy, professional self-presentation, and stage-managed inspiration; the rhetorical job is to send 22-year-olds into the world feeling that the four years had a coherent purpose. Patel does the job competently — Alice Waters, ihsan, Seneca, Thich Nhat Hanh, David Brooks, The Bear — but the structural argument under the rhetoric is light. There is a real claim ("liberal-arts judgment is the human capacity that AI cannot substitute for"), but the essay never engages the obvious counter-claim, which is that the AI labor-market disruption is precisely about whether liberal-arts judgment retains any economic anchor when most knowledge-work is being commoditized. The Denison-graduate-class audience for this is a small, well-positioned cohort whose worry about AI is largely status-positional rather than existential, and Patel's "you get to decide what matters" framing implicitly addresses that cohort rather than the much larger cohort whose options are narrowing.

The piece's three-part structure is the commencement-genre standard (one well-loved anecdote → one personal life-claim → one closing universalist invocation), and the parts hold together as a speech in a way they don't quite hold together as an essay. The Alice-Waters-as-prototype-of-Brooksian-social-change move is real and worth thinking about — Waters founded Chez Panisse in 1971; the now-ubiquitous "farm-to-table," "organic," "local" food vocabulary is genuinely her legacy; and she did it from inside a liberal-arts trajectory rather than against one — but the implicit suggestion that AI-era graduates can replicate this is the part that most needs argument rather than assertion. Waters's success required not just her vision but a Bay Area food-supply ecology, restaurant economics that made Chez Panisse viable as a small-scale business, and the cultural capital that liberal-arts judgment confers in 1970s San Francisco. None of those preconditions are stable in 2026.

Ihsan as the closer is the essay's most distinctive move. Patel, as the founder of Interfaith America and an Obama-era faith advisor, has spent his career arguing that religious traditions are practical resources for civic life rather than private confessional matters; bringing ihsan (a real concept in Islamic theology, denoting the highest spiritual state of doing one's work as if one sees God) into a Denison commencement is a small but specific instance of that case. The pairing with The Bear's Sydney-makes-an-omelet scene is well-chosen: both Patel and the show are arguing that craft excellence is intrinsically other-directed and that this is what makes it irreducible to optimization. The argument has more force than the surrounding commencement-bromide framing might suggest.

The piece is best read as a Patel-on-Patel rather than as a structural intervention. It tells you what Patel believes (interfaith civic life, liberal-arts judgment, slow excellence, Brooksian social-change-as-modeling). It does not tell you much that you didn't already know about AI, work, or college. For the project carry-forward, this is a low-substance entry in the AI-and-human-dignity cluster (much weaker on the analytic side than the Persico Pope-Leo piece or even the Cass call for worker-share-of-AI-windfall pre-distribution), and it lives or dies on whether the reader finds Patel's tone genuinely earnest or commencement-engineered. Both readings are available.

Follow Persuasion on X, Instagram, LinkedIn, and YouTube to keep up with our latest articles, podcasts, and events, as well as updates from excellent writers across our network.

And, to receive pieces like this in your inbox and support our work, subscribe below:

The global-system layer — multipolar mechanics from Brussels to Lagos to Beijing

Why Europe keeps losing

** Wolfgang Munchau ** · 2026-05-25 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The EU's recent forced retreats on China policy — Brussels suspending and then ratifying the EU-US trade deal under German car-industry pressure, then lifting sanctions against Yangzhou Yangjie Electronic Technology (a Chinese semiconductor company) after German carmakers warned of supply-chain chaos — and the broader pattern of EU cheerleaders demanding retaliation the EU cannot deliver.

Munchau uses the metaphor of the EU as a confused football team with too many players, badly-coached spectators (UK and Economist-style commentators), and a tendency to be cheered into fights it shouldn't pick. The substantive case for European anger is real: Chinese exports are booming while domestic consumption is repressed, the yuan-dollar peg has made Chinese goods even cheaper against the euro, China's trade surplus with the EU rose 26% year-on-year January-April 2026, and Chinese tariff-evasion of US tariffs has diverted exports into Europe. But the EU's lever set is empty for one reason: dependency. The European car industry employs a reported 13.2 million people (2022 data) and is structurally dependent on Chinese semiconductors, batteries, rare-earth magnets (needed for windshield wipers as much as F-35s), and solar panels. The 2000s-2010s supply-chain-optimization choices made this dependency irreversible at any short horizon. The "Anti-Coercion Instrument" the EU could theoretically deploy goes unused not from political weakness but because Merz and Meloni would block it — and they have no choice given car-industry employment exposure. The fix Munchau proposes: stop fighting trade wars the EU can't win, adopt strategic patience, and chart a binding ten-year plan to reduce supply-chain dependence in critical materials (citing Japan's successful post-2010 rare-earth diversification after Beijing's East China Sea export ban as the model the 2024 Critical Raw Materials Act has so far failed to implement bindingly).

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

Munchau's distinctive contribution is the honest mechanistic diagnostic of European weakness — internal-failure rather than external-pressure-dominant. Compared to last week's Maitra (Hungarian Conservative, 5/24) reading of the empty European chair at the Beijing summit as the G2-arrival moment European thinkers have long feared, Munchau is on a tighter analytical layer: he names the specific dependency vector (German car industry semiconductor + battery + rare-earth exposure) and the specific failed institutional response (Critical Raw Materials Act's non-binding 2024 targets). Compared to Constantini's Commonplace piece last week on Germany's incapacity to execute its rearmament pledges (economically, demographically, politically), Munchau extends the diagnosis from defense to trade — same underlying pattern (German economic structure fundamentally constrains continental decision-making) applied to a different domain. Compared to Sternberg's WSJ "Europeans find out how poor they are" reads from prior weeks, Munchau is offering not just a diagnosis but a positive program (the ten-year decoupling plan) that the Sternberg-Anglosphere-right critique generally doesn't.

The piece's main analytical strength is the honesty about Europe's own mercantilism: "This is essentially a barfight between two mercantilists. The EU has been playing the same game" — naming that the 2010s eurozone-crisis devaluation-and-surplus strategy is structurally identical to what the EU now accuses China of doing, and that "the EU's lament has a certain 'I stole it first' quality." This is the corrective the Anglo-Atlanticist coverage of EU-China relations rarely supplies. Where the piece is weakest is in the football metaphor's recurring use: the analogy substitutes for analysis at key moments (the "offside trap gone wrong" closing image is evocative but vague about what specific institutional move it maps to). And the prescription — a binding ten-year supply-chain decoupling plan — assumes a level of EU-internal coordination Munchau's own diagnosis suggests doesn't exist. If Merz and Meloni block the Anti-Coercion Instrument because of car-industry exposure, why would they not also block (or render non-binding) a ten-year decoupling plan that would impose adjustment costs on the same constituency? Munchau's answer is implicit: the decoupling plan would unfold over horizons long enough that individual political principals' incentives change, while a trade-retaliation instrument hits immediately. Whether this is a real distinction or rhetorical sleight depends on whether the binding mechanisms can be designed to survive predictable political reversals.

Connecting to project standing themes: Munchau's piece extends the european-center-exhaustion carry-forward (opened 5/9) by adding a structural-economic-dependency layer underneath the electoral-politics layer. The synthesis composer should note that Munchau's "China is winning, just as Trump won his own trade war" frame converges with this week's Polk (WOTR) finding that US tariff-and-oil-shock fragility constrains American power similarly — both economic blocs are running into the same kind of supply-chain-dependency wall, with different specific compositions. The relevant carry-forward connection is bypass-network-as-post-american-architecture from the Iran cluster: the same partner-to-partner supply-chain re-architecting Tabatabai diagnosed in Gulf states would be required for the EU to escape the dependency Munchau describes, but the EU lacks the coalition coherence (Merz/Meloni veto reality) to do it.

↩ Back to TOC

Chartbook 450: Modern growth surges. Or, why China's economic development is unique in human history.

Adam Tooze · 2026-05-28 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Branko Milanović's The Great Global Transformation (just out), which offers a population-weighted growth-point comparison of China, Japan, and US economic takeoffs, and a recurring Tooze question about how best to quantify the scale of China's economic transformation.

Tooze takes the intuitive claim that China's growth surge is the largest in world history and runs it through three measures. First, coal consumption: human history splits into three energy regimes (pre-1750 somatic; 1750-1990s classic industrialization spreading from NW Europe out; and the 2000s when Chinese coal use shot vertically to 4x the US peak). Second, Milanović's population-weighted growth-rate calculation: China's 1.23 billion average population growing at 8.1% compounded annually for 44 years produces 38 billion "people/income units," 20x Japan's 1953-1991 1.9 billion units and ~290x the US's 1865-1914 0.13 billion. Third, absolute GDP added at 2011 prices: $25.3 trillion for China vs. $3.5 trillion for Japan and $738 billion for the US — meaning China's increment was 7.2x Japan's and 34x the US's. Tooze then deflates one piece of the story: as a share of global GDP growth in their respective periods, the US's late-19th-century surge accounted for ~30% of world growth while China's contribution was ~25% — somewhat smaller in relative terms, because the global economy China developed within was so much larger. The combined judgment: in scale and velocity, nothing in recorded economic history compares with China's last forty years. This continues Tooze's running "Chinamaxxing" thread from Chartbook 446 and the China-shock-coming Chartbook 445.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:
