This is one of Tooze's most useful "measure the thing properly" posts — short, intuitive, and analytically clean. The three-measure framework (coal, population-weighted growth points, absolute GDP) is a model of how to confront a claim everyone agrees with ("China's growth is unprecedented") with three independent quantitative tests rather than restating it. The Milanović population-weighted-growth method is the most rhetorically powerful and the least familiar to most readers: it captures both how many people moved and how fast they moved, in a way per-capita GDP comparisons can't.

The piece's analytical honesty is its strongest feature. Tooze does not stop at the 20x and 290x numbers; he goes on to show that on a share-of-global-growth basis, China's contribution is actually slightly smaller than Gilded Age America's — and explains why (a larger and faster-growing global denominator). That's the kind of move that distinguishes serious economic-history writing from cheerleading. A weaker writer would have buried the share-of-global-growth comparison. Tooze leads with it.

The implicit policy and analytical stakes — which Tooze leaves under the surface — are what makes this Chartbook worth carrying forward. If China's growth is uniquely large in absolute and population-weighted terms, then frameworks built around "convergence" or "catch-up" derived from Japan or Korea may systematically understate what China's continued development implies for global energy, commodity, and emissions trajectories. The opening pivot to "Chinamaxxing" via coal is the giveaway: Tooze is writing about this because the climate consequences of Chinese industrialization are mismeasured by frameworks that index off Western industrialization. The post does not extend that argument explicitly — that may be the next Chartbook — but the structure of the piece makes the implication unmistakable.

One mild structural note: the absolute-GDP comparisons rely on Maddison's historical GDP estimates, which Tooze acknowledges ("if we trust the Maddison data"). For 1865-1914 US figures these are genuinely contested; the takeaway that China's increment was 34x the US's would shrink or grow under different historical-GDP methodologies. The conclusion is robust to this — the order of magnitude is right under any plausible series — but readers should not treat the precise multiples as decimals-of-truth.

↩ Back to TOC

The Dangote Refinery and the End of Africa’s Engineered Dependency

Nouridin Melo · 2026-05-27 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Late-2025 Bloomberg reporting that the Dangote Refinery (commissioned in Lekki, Lagos) is now loading 45 million litres of petrol and 25 million litres of diesel daily — exceeding Nigeria's domestic demand — combined with the late-2025 commercial-dispute resolution between Dangote and the Nigerian National Petroleum Company (NNPC), and early-2026 refinery shipments of 456,000 tonnes of refined products to Côte d'Ivoire, Cameroon, Tanzania, Ghana, and Togo.

Melo's central claim is that the Dangote Refinery's significance is not the facility itself ($20B cost, world's largest single-train refinery at 650,000 barrels per day, taller-than-Big-Ben crude distillation column) but what it dismantles: the architecture by which Africa was designed to be a crude-exporter and refined-fuel-importer regardless of being the continent's largest oil producer. The mechanism's reach is now empirically visible — Nigeria's fuel imports fell 54% Q1 2024 to Q1 2025 ($2.6B to $1.2B per Dangote Group data); Africa as a whole still refines only 30% of its crude domestically, losing $25B/year to imports, and Dangote is the single most significant intervention into that equation. The build-out was contested: NNPC under the naira-for-crude agreement was committed to deliver 385,000 barrels/day minimum but consistently failed (Vice President of Dangote Industries Devakumar Edwin to Reuters in November 2024), with Crude Oil Refinery Owners Association of Nigeria (CORAN) and Nairametrics reporting deliberate undersupply to preserve the import model. The state opened the door through specific decisions: Tinubu's first act on May 29 2023 was to abolish the 1970s-era fuel subsidy regime; in February 2024 he signed three executive orders for oil-and-gas investment incentives; in October 2025 the government approved a 15% import duty on refined petroleum products. Naira depreciation drove the fuel import bill to a record ₦15.42 trillion in 2024 even as dollar-denominated prices held steady. Melo's broader claim — what Western intermediaries lose is "orchestration" (financing, technical standards, insurance markets) rather than ownership — is paired with a sharp anti-decarbonization-imposition closing argument: Africa is being told to forgo the carbon-intensive industrialization the Global North has already done, arriving "with striking convenience, just as African capital begins to complete the chain."

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

Melo's piece fills two of the project's standing function-lens gaps simultaneously: Atoms-not-Bits (concrete material-industrial reporting on a $20B physical facility producing 70 million litres of refined fuel daily) and Non-Anglophone Epistemic World (an African-Arguments piece read through African economic-policy logic rather than imported Western frames). Compared to the project's other recent African-Arguments pieces — Muto (5/17) on Azawad sovereignty and the 2026 Mali crisis through 2012, Michalon-Mogaka (5/17) on France-Kenya middle-bloc multilateralism, Bencherif (5/24) on AI-and-African-repression, Appiah-Mensah and Ilo (5/9) on Sahel and Nigerian-elections — Melo's contribution is the most directly economic and the most directly testable. The factual claims (54% Q1 fuel-import decline, 456,000 tonnes shipped to five African states, 30% Africa-wide refining share, $25B/year import loss, 385,000 bpd NNPC commitment, 15% October 2025 import duty) are specific and citation-anchored to BusinessDay, Bloomberg, Nairametrics, The Cable, and Punch reporting; the synthesis composer should treat these as load-bearing rather than rhetorical.

The "end of engineered dependency" framing is the piece's contested move. Melo's strongest argument is the structural one: when value-chain steps Africa was historically denied (refining, standards-setting, regional distribution) can be done in-region with sufficient capital concentration, the "natural" position of Africa as raw-materials supplier is revealed as architecture rather than as inevitability. The weaker version of this argument — that the dependency itself "ends" — overstates the case. Several dependencies persist in modified form: (a) the refinery itself sources US WTI Midland crude when domestic supply falls short, revealing technological-and-supply dependency on global oil markets; (b) the financing was Nigerian (Aliko Dangote, Africa's wealthiest individual) but the engineering, equipment, and technology stack are largely imported; (c) the regional distribution depends on shipping and insurance infrastructure Melo acknowledges is still externally-controlled; (d) the political mechanism through which Tinubu's reforms were possible (IMF-aligned fuel-subsidy abolition, currency floating) operates inside the same global financial architecture the "engineered dependency" frame implicitly opposes. A more cautious frame would be: the output-side refining dependency has been broken; input-side dependencies (technology, finance, regional logistics) persist but in modified form. This is consistent with the Polk (WOTR, this week) finding on supply-chain fragility: dependencies don't go away, they get re-architected, and the question is who controls each remaining choke point.

The carry-forward connection is two-sided. On the China cluster: Melo's piece is the rare African Arguments article that does not foreground Chinese involvement — the Dangote story is a Nigerian-capital, Nigerian-state story, with Western refiners as the losing party. This is a useful corrective to the Anglo-Atlantic frame in which African industrialization is always either Chinese-driven or Chinese-threatened. On the bypass-network-as-post-American-architecture carry-forward: the Dangote story is a parallel architecture-of-bypass story, with the bypass being out from under European refining intermediation rather than out from under US dollar/security plumbing. The mechanism is similar (regional capital concentration + state-enabled industrial protection + price-of-input agreements that don't go through the historical hub), the actors are different. Worth tracking as Angola and East African pieces follow whether this is a one-Dangote story or a continent-scale pattern.

Melo's closing decarbonization argument is the piece's normatively strongest section and the most likely to be controversial outside the African-Arguments readership. The claim — that Western pressure to decarbonize before African industrialization completes is "a reassignment of who bears the cost of a problem they did little to create" — is the African-economic-development tradition's central retort to climate-cosmopolitan framing. The synthesis composer should note that this argument lands harder in a week when the Pope Leo XIV encyclical (Ahmari, this week) is framing universalism as the operative Western moral move — Melo's piece names exactly the kind of universalist framing African actors are inclined to refuse, and the tension is productive.

↩ Back to TOC

Sensitive Words and Censored Content Related to the Recent Sino-American Summit

Cindy Carter · 2026-05-25 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The back-to-back May 14-15 Sino-American summit (Trump-Xi in Beijing) and Sino-Russian summit (Putin-Xi), and the Chinese internet censorship apparatus's "soft censorship" (search results restricted to Chinese government/state-media outlets) on a wide range of summit-related Chinese-language search terms. Reporting from FT and Bloomberg later indicated a tense exchange between Xi and Trump over Japanese PM Sanae Takaichi's increased military spending, which Xi characterized as Japanese "remilitarisation."

CDT documents that despite the absence of substantive diplomatic breakthroughs at the Trump-Xi summit, much Chinese social-media commentary instead focused on meme-friendly details (Elon Musk's son's tiger-head bag and Mandarin study, the state-banquet menu served to the American delegation). The most notable censorship incident grew out of a cross-platform exchange: dissident account "Teacher Li Is Not Your Teacher" (@whyyoutouzhele) posted summit videos and photos on X, Musk replied in simplified Chinese ("My son is studying Mandarin") drawing over 13 million views, and Chinese state-aligned media (including First Financial Network/CBN) reported on Musk's tweet while omitting any reference to the Teacher Li account. Chinese netizens commented on the irony of Musk "circumventing the GFW [Great Firewall] to access a banned overseas site right under Xi's nose"; the original CBN report and Weibo trending topics were then completely blocked, and Teacher Li tweeted that "Xi Jinping and I are just two 'Voldemorts' separated by a wall." CDT Chinese editors archived two additional posts: one WeChat article (from "He Liuwei") arguing against the practice of using schoolchildren to greet visiting political leaders (including Trump), and one (from "The Sun Also Rises") on two recently deleted articles including one titled "Taking Stock of Trump's Visit to China," with the author puzzled about the censorship cause and castigating "professional complainers" who report posts to platforms. The compiled sensitive-word lists are organized by topic: U.S.-China summit logistics ("Trump arrives in Beijing," "Trump visits the Temple of Heaven," "state banquet + Trump"), high-level talks ("U.S.-China heads of state + talks," "Trump-Xi secret talks"), CCP positioning rhetoric ("Will the CCP really + peacefully coexist with the U.S.," "U.S.-China relations can only improve, not worsen," "Charting a Correct Path of Coexistence Between the Two Great Powers"), Taiwan ("The U.S. side must handle the Taiwan question with the utmost prudence," "Maintaining Cross-Strait Peace and Stability Is the Greatest Common Ground"), trade negotiations and Boeing orders, Iran trade ("Chinese enterprises + Iran"), Zion Church pastor Jin Mingri's detention, the Musk-son-Mandarin-tweet cluster, and the parallel Sino-Russian summit.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

The CDT compilation is the project's primary fill for the Non-Anglophone Epistemic World function gap on the Beijing-summit cluster. Its analytical value is twofold: first, the contents of the suppressed terms surface what the CCP itself reads as politically dangerous about its own summit performance; second, the fact of the suppression — at the volume and across the topical breadth documented — is itself evidence that the friendly-tone reading of the summit (which several Anglosphere outlets and the Atlantic-Foer "lame-duck superpower" frame absorb on different grounds) does not hold inside China's own information environment. The sensitive-word lists reveal that even pro-CCP messaging frames ("Stable U.S.-China relations are a boon to the world," "China welcomes the U.S. strengthening mutually beneficial cooperation," "U.S.-China Relations Build on the Past and Open Up the Future") have been search-restricted — the apparatus is treating its own preferred frames as load-bearing enough that uncontrolled circulation is itself a risk. This is the structural pattern that the prior 5/17 and 5/24 CDT pieces (foreign-press-corps attrition, Douyin tortoise meme suppression) have collectively documented across multiple summit-adjacent dimensions: the CCP regime is not behaving like a confident hegemon flush with diplomatic victory; it is behaving like an apparatus aware that the gap between its preferred summit narrative and what citizens might actually think requires continuous active suppression.

The specific sensitive-word clusters reward close reading. The Taiwan cluster ("The U.S. side must handle the Taiwan question with the utmost prudence," "Maintaining Cross-Strait Peace and Stability Is the Greatest Common Ground Between the U.S. and China") shows that Beijing is suppressing search results even on its own preferred Taiwan-framing language — likely because such phrases circulate alongside Trump's reported summit statements about Taiwanese independence (per BBC, Karen Kuo's "self-evident sovereignty" response; per CBS, Ambassador Yui's "el cuento chino" — Chinese tale — framing of Xi's summit narrative) which Beijing would prefer remain low-salience. The Iran-trade cluster ("Chinese enterprises + Iran") is the operative connection to this week's Polk (WOTR) finding on US economic-fragility-as-coercion-ceiling: Beijing's suppression of search terms linking Chinese businesses to Iran is consistent with the still-developing question of whether Beijing will absorb Iranian oil through Chinese refiners and how visible that absorption can be. The Boeing-order cluster reflects that the discussed Boeing deal was likely the highest-volume substantive summit deliverable Beijing wants surfaced through state-media channels rather than emerging organically. The Musk-son-Mandarin cluster (馬斯克 + 中文 + 兒子 etc.) is suppressed not for the Mandarin-language detail but because it surfaces the underlying fact that Musk had to circumvent the Great Firewall to interact with the Chinese dissident sphere — exactly the kind of regime-fragility-signaling that the CCP's narrative apparatus is designed to occlude. The Jin Mingri/Zion Church inclusion confirms that Frannie Block's Free Press reporting (5/17) on the October 2025 pastor roundup is not a dated story but an active suppression target for Chinese-internet discussion.

The cluster connection is to the project's china-power-axis-disagreement carry-forward (opened 5/17). CDT's compilation supplies a third-axis data point that neither the demographic measure (Douthat) nor the industrial-and-AI-deployment measure (Stoller, Chan, Smith, Foer, Schuman, Bordoff, Zhou) directly captures: the regime-confidence-from-inside measure. The breadth and granularity of summit-coverage suppression suggests a CCP apparatus that does not behave like the confident regime that the friendly-tone summit reading would imply. Combined with the prior CDT translations (foreign-press-corps attrition, tortoise-on-utility-pole Douyin meme, the engineered-urban-rural-misperception thread), the CDT layer continues to read Chinese regime stability as more contested from inside than the Anglosphere read assumes. The synthesis composer should note that the CDT compilation does not contradict the Foer "lame-duck superpower" frame — both can be true simultaneously: the US is treated as term-limited by external actors AND Xi's regime is internally suppressing its own preferred summit narrative because uncontrolled circulation risks surfacing regime-fragility signals. The Fukuyama (Persuasion, 5/24) third-axis Taiwan-timing-inversion frame engages the same multi-axis structure.

↩ Back to TOC

You The People....

Aurelien · 2026-05-27 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The current European political moment — German media panic over AfD (Alternative für Deutschland) polling gains and French elite warnings that any vote for the Rassemblement National (RN, the rebranded Front National) threatens Democracy itself.

Aurelien's structural argument is that "Democracy" is not a defined system but a value-judgment slogan whose meaning is set by whoever holds discursive power; like all such normatively-loaded Liberal terms, its dissenters become Agamben's Homo Sacer — outlaws outside legal protection against whom otherwise unacceptable measures (in this case, vote restriction) become legitimate. Ordinary people, he claims, don't experience their countries as "democracies" but as states that are either responsive or unresponsive to material concerns; abstract "End of Democracy" warnings therefore land flat. He traces this to the loss of pre-modern Western cosmology — a divinely-ordered universe where political legitimacy was given rather than constructed — and to the consequent inability of post-Enlightenment Liberalism to develop a coherent overarching political structure (gesturing to Alasdair MacIntyre's After Virtue on the parallel ethical collapse). The contemporary symptom is what Aurelien calls "the Party" — a professional western political class whose members move University→NGO→party adviser→politician with no outside experience, who agree on substance (open borders, free trade, financialization), differ violently only on symbolic culture-war issues, and have given away the economic tools previous governments used to address material problems. Unable to deliver, they have substituted vilification: any subject that might benefit "the extreme Right" (immigration, Political Islam, education, security) becomes undiscussable, and citizens who raise such concerns become the extreme Right themselves. Aurelien invokes R. R. Reno's Return of the Strong Gods to predict that the unmet demand for substance will inevitably benefit the very forces the Party most fears. Throughout, Aurelien names the PMC (Professional and Managerial Caste) as the social base of this politics and treats the situation as a Foucauldian legitimacy crisis — not "why do people rebel?" but "why have they stopped obeying?"

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

Aurelien — a pseudonymous former European diplomat writing long-form essays on institutional decay and state capacity — is in his most characteristic mode here: a totalizing framework essay that traces a contemporary political pathology (the panic over right-populist voting in Europe) to deep civilizational shifts (the collapse of pre-modern Western cosmology, the rise of post-Liberal proceduralism, the professionalization of the political class). The strongest analytical thread is the substantive one: that the modern western political class has given away the economic tools governments once used to address material concerns (capital controls, industrial policy, fiscal autonomy, immigration enforcement, public ownership), and therefore cannot deliver on the implicit social contract — so it must displace the resulting legitimacy deficit onto its critics. The PMC observation — that today's politicians move University→NGO→adviser→office with no outside life — is concrete enough to test against any French or German cabinet roster, and largely holds. The claim that "the Party" is united on what matters (trade, capital, borders) and divided only on symbolic issues is also empirically defensible across most western European center-left/center-right governments since the 1990s.

The piece is operating in interpretive-framework mode rather than empirical-claims mode, so the right test is coherence and explanatory power rather than data citations. Several of the analytical moves earn their keep: the Liberal-slogan analysis of "Democracy" (it means whatever current power-holders need it to mean, hence cannot be argued against on substance) is a real observation about how the term functions in elite discourse; the Homo Sacer analogy to vote restriction is rhetorically strong even if it stretches Agamben's actual concept beyond its origin (Agamben's Homo Sacer concerned the sovereign exception in extreme cases, not normal democratic discourse-policing); and the Foucauldian reframe from "why rebel?" to "why obey?" usefully reorients the legitimacy question. The MacIntyre analogy — modern political incoherence as the political version of After Virtue's ethical incoherence — is suggestive though underdeveloped (MacIntyre's solution was Aristotelian-Thomist tradition, which Aurelien gestures at as "Strong Gods" via Reno but doesn't actually endorse).

The piece has several characteristic Aurelien weaknesses. First, "the Party" as a unified actor is doing too much work — German Greens, French centrists, British Tories, and US Democrats face genuinely different electoral structures and policy constraints, and treating them as one professional class with one ideology elides large variation. Second, the dichotomy between substantive economic politics ("things that matter") and symbolic cultural politics ("things that don't") imports Aurelien's own priors: many voters genuinely care about the cultural issues he dismisses, and "what matters" is itself a political claim. Third, the piece reads pre-modern hierarchical cosmology as more coherent and stable than the historical record supports — religious-cosmological politics also produced massive instability (wars of religion, peasant revolts, dynastic crises) — and the post-Liberal solution Aurelien gestures toward (the Strong Gods, a return to substantive political community) is not actually specified, so the essay's prescription is purely negative.

The most interesting structural feature is what the piece does not say. Aurelien is too sophisticated to endorse the AfD or RN directly — he treats them as symptoms whose rise the Party has earned rather than as substantive alternatives. But the essay's logic implies that genuine substantive politics (as opposed to PMC performance) requires precisely the kind of cultural-economic populism those parties offer, and Aurelien doesn't engage with the obvious counter-objection: that the "substantive concerns" being suppressed (immigration restriction especially) are themselves contested empirical and moral questions, not self-evidently the things that matter. He treats the elite's reluctance to "discuss" these as suppression of obvious truths; a critic would say the elite has different empirical and moral views, badly defended but not fraudulent. The piece's persuasive force depends on the reader already sharing Aurelien's substantive prior that the populists are responding to real problems the Party refuses to address — which is a substantive political claim doing the work of an analytical one. Within that frame, the structural critique of PMC politics is sharp; outside it, the critique becomes a sophisticated articulation of one side of the actual political dispute.

↩ Back to TOC

Standalone Pieces

Title Author Publication Summary
Raj Chetty's Just-So Stories Freddie deBoer's Blog deBoer deBoer argues that Raj Chetty's celebrated "teacher value-added" findings rest on a circular construct, fail every replication and stability test, and collapsed in real-world deployment — yet still anchor a decade of anti-teacher policy.
Book Review: The Dialectical Imagination Scott Alexander ACT Alexander reads the Frankfurt School as crypto-Hegelian mystics whose "negative theology" approach to Communism — refusing to specify the utopia while endlessly criticizing the present — explains both their obscurity and their inadvertent paternity of modern progressive slacktivism.
Why Pose Thought Experiments About Fake Versions of History? Freddie deBoer's Blog deBoer deBoer argues that defending the 2010s campus-activism era by sanitizing what activists actually did — as in Scott Lemieux's recent post — is condescending pseudo-solidarity that betrays the left's stated commitments and forfeits real accountability.
A Showdown over Israel in Park Slope Suzy Weiss Free Press Weiss frames the Park Slope Food Co-op's BDS vote as the predictable terminus of a neighborhood-cultural type — rule-loving progressives whose moralism scales to symbolic gestures with zero geopolitical purchase.
There's No Escaping the Rot in This Justice Department Michelle Goldberg NYT Goldberg argues the Northern District of Illinois U.S. Attorney's Office has been hollowed into a vengeance-and-protest-criminalization arm, and that "keeping your head down" is no longer ethically tenable for career prosecutors there.
What Is the Current US Healthcare Situation? Matt Bruenig People's Policy Project (Bruenig) Bruenig walks node-by-node through the US healthcare payment system (households, governments, employers, insurers, providers) to argue that nearly every dysfunction — uninsurance, churn, regressive tax subsidies, FSA/HSA forfeitures, Medicare Advantage fraud, the 15-20% insurer take, provider rents — would be solved by a single public insurer.
Do more Europeans die of summer heat than Americans die of guns? Hannah Ritchie Sustainability by Numbers (Ritchie) Ritchie rebuilds the viral "European heat deaths > American gun deaths" comparison using methodologically consistent data and finds the basic shape holds — though per-capita reverses the order — while flagging the comparison itself as a status-quo-bias pairing rather than a real tradeoff.
How Much Has Shale Gas Saved U.S. Consumers? Tyler Cowen Marginal Revolution Cowen flags a Lucas Davis NBER paper estimating U.S. shale gas saved consumers $3.1-4.3 trillion between 2007 and 2025 ($164-227 billion annually) by holding domestic natural-gas prices below European and Japanese benchmarks.
Are we undermeasuring inflation for lower earners? Tyler Cowen Marginal Revolution Cowen flags a Sangani NBER paper finding that official statistics understate the gap between low- and high-income household inflation rates by 70-90 percent because of "cheapflation" — equal absolute price hikes hit low-price varieties as larger percentage changes.
How to Eat Well on the Cheap Scott Lincicome Capitolism (Lincicome) Lincicome departs from his usual trade-policy beat to offer a Gen-X cheapskate's how-to-eat-well-on-a-budget guide for Gen Z workers reportedly spending $28/day on DoorDash lunches, threading affordability data through a brown-bagging tutorial.
How to improve British procurement Tyler Cowen Marginal Revolution Cowen, via a story about licensed beavers replacing a planned reservoir near a London Tube station, suggests (without saying so directly) that British infrastructure procurement is so costly that wildlife outperforms it.
Those new service sector economics jobs? Tyler Cowen Marginal Revolution Cowen flags (deadpan, via his recurring "those new service sector jobs" tag) that Russia has just authorized central-bank staff to operate anti-drone defense systems without involving special forces.

Raj Chetty's Just-So Stories

Freddie deBoer's Blog · 2026-05-26 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Reader requests over many years for deBoer to address Harvard economist Raj Chetty's value-added modeling (VAM) research, especially the 2014 American Economic Review papers (cited by Obama in his 2012 State of the Union and by the Vergara v. California tenure decision) claiming that replacing a bottom-5% teacher with an average one would raise a single classroom's lifetime earnings by ~$250,000.

deBoer's central charge is that VAM begs the question: a teacher's "value-added" is defined as the residual variance in standardized test scores after controlling for prior achievement, then that residual is redefined as "teacher quality" — assuming the conclusion. He then stacks four empirical problems: (1) even VAM-friendly literature attributes only 1–14% of test-score variance to teachers (per the American Statistical Association's 2014 statement); (2) the metric is wildly unstable year-to-year (correlations of 0.18–0.64; Darling-Hammond found 25–45% of bottom-quintile teachers jumped to the top the next year); (3) the same teacher gets different ratings depending on which model specification is used, with disadvantaged-student teachers systematically penalized; (4) Jesse Rothstein's 2017 North Carolina replication found the famous teacher-switching quasi-experiment's identifying assumption fails, introducing 10–35% bias. Finally, Houston ISD's real-world VAM deployment ended in Houston Federation of Teachers v. HISD, where a federal court found a Fourteenth Amendment due-process violation because the algorithm was unauditable. deBoer concludes the construct is noise dressed as measurement, sustained by a media reality-distortion field around Chetty and the Obama-era ed-reform consensus.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Claude's Response:

This is deBoer doing what he does best: a sustained methodological takedown of a quantitative claim that has acquired establishment-consensus armor, executed in the anti-credentialist register he has been refining for a decade (he is the author of The Cult of Smart and a longtime education-policy critic of the Obama-Duncan-Rhee reform coalition). The piece is unusually clean for deBoer — he names the sources (Koedel/Mihaly/Rockoff 2015, Darling-Hammond, Rothstein 2017, the ASA 2014 statement, the Carnegie Knowledge Network's own 16% stable-variance estimate), distinguishes validity from bias problems, and lays the chain out in numbered structural arguments rather than rhetorical flourishes. The Houston due-process kicker is well-chosen — a black-box algorithm that even district officials couldn't audit is a damning concrete instance of the abstract critique, and it lands inside hostile ideological territory (a federal court, not a teacher's union, called it unconstitutional).

The strongest part of the argument is the question-begging point. The reframing of "any unexplained variance in test scores" as "teacher quality" is exactly the kind of definitional move that produces enormous-sounding effect sizes which then can't be reproduced — because what's being measured is residual statistical noise, not an underlying trait. deBoer is also right that the year-over-year instability is dispositive: a metric that classifies the same teacher as bottom-quintile one year and top-quintile the next is not measuring a stable property of the teacher. The 0.18 low-end correlation deBoer cites really is noise; the high-end 0.64 is still well below what reliability standards demand for high-stakes employment decisions, and deBoer's "same teacher, different class periods" anecdote (if real) would be devastating.

The piece's main analytical limitation is what it does not engage with. Chetty's defenders (Chetty/Friedman/Rockoff have published rebuttals to Rothstein) argue that bias of 10–35% still leaves substantial true teacher effects, and that the long-run earnings findings survive in attenuated form. deBoer notes the dispute but doesn't characterize the rebuttals or explain why he doesn't credit them, which is a missed opportunity given his methodological framing — the strongest version of the steel-man is that Chetty's effects are real but smaller than headline, not that they're zero. Similarly, deBoer's secondary claim — "there's no such thing as school quality" — is asserted rather than argued, and it does a lot of work in the piece's policy implications without being defended. Readers who don't already share his priors will treat that as the load-bearing claim and want evidence for it specifically.

The framing of Chetty as "neoliberal just-so story" is doing politically loaded work but is also doing analytical work: deBoer's broader point is that the policy demand (identify and fire bad teachers; reward good ones) preceded the research and the research was retrofitted to justify the policy. This is a fair structural critique of the ed-reform coalition's relationship to Chetty, even if it's harder on Chetty personally than the evidence quite warrants — Chetty's empirical work is at least serious, even if its policy uses have been bad-faith. deBoer's closing — "someone email this to Chetty… and Obama, Rhee, Moskowitz, Yglesias, Chait, Duncan" — is the project memory note about deBoer's anti-credentialist streak fully on display: the people who built careers on this research now refuse to engage critics, and the press has cooperated by not making them.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Book Review: The Dialectical Imagination

Scott Alexander · 2026-05-29 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Martin Jay's The Dialectical Imagination, the 1973 classic history of the Frankfurt School (Horkheimer, Adorno, Marcuse, Benjamin et al.) — read in light of the contested status of "Cultural Marxism" as both an alt-right conspiracy frame (per Wikipedia) and a serious thesis about the Frankfurt School's lineage into postmodernism and contemporary progressive culture.

Alexander's interpretive frame is that the Frankfurt School was responding to a specific intellectual crisis: by the 1930s, Marx's predicted Revolution conspicuously hadn't arrived (most populations seemed content with their local variety of "state capitalism" — New Deal America, Stalinist Russia, Fascist Italy), and the rise of Nazism suggested history was going off-rails toward funhouse-mirror inversions of Communism rather than toward it. The Frankfurters' move was to invert orthodox Marxism's base-superstructure relation: maybe culture was upstream of capitalism, not just downstream, so fixing the culture could un-jam History. But — and here's Alexander's central interpretive claim — they paired this with a "negative theology" of Communism modeled on Jewish Bilderverbot (the prohibition on graven images), Christian via negativa, and Hindu neti neti: Communism, like God, was too far beyond current conceptual categories to be specified positively, so the only legitimate intellectual practice was negative dialectics — criticizing the residual gaps that any existing synthesis fails to capture, hoping the cumulative critique would precipitate a Kuhnian paradigm shift in human consciousness. Alexander notes Walter Benjamin literally studied Kabbalah with Gershom Scholem, and reads the whole project as a kind of secularized Lurianic mysticism (healing a wounded God via concept-development) which is why specifying utopia was forbidden and why Lenin's actually-existing Communism looked, to the Frankfurters, like a cargo cult — gesturing at Communist-sounding things without doing the conceptual work first. This explains Adorno's notoriously obscurantist art criticism (the residual lives in eerie modernist art that disrupts comfortable paradigms; Disney movies and jazz reinforce them). Marcuse alone, Alexander argues, kept the project anchored to material political claims — that decuple productivity gains should have produced 10x leisure rather than 10x consumption, and that advertising-induced false needs explain the divergence. Alexander's verdict on the "Cultural Marxism" charge is split: the orthodox Frankfurters explicitly opposed instrumental political action (so the "long march through the institutions" frame is wrong as biography), but their "vibe" of critique-without-construction does plausibly descend into contemporary progressive slacktivism — protesting global warming while opposing any plan that would stop it, criticizing police without an alternative — and the postmodernist movement (Derrida's deconstruction et al.) is genuinely downstream of their negative dialectics in less careful hands. The closing line — "as for whether you should blame the Jews, I think it's 50-50" — is glib but operates as a Hegelian-thesis-antithesis joke about how the Gentile reception of Jewish mystical concepts went predictably wrong.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Claude's Response:

This is Alexander in one of his signature modes: taking a difficult Continental-philosophy tradition that most rationalists treat as either embarrassing pseudoscience or politically toxic, and finding analogies — Kuhnian paradigm shifts, cargo cults, primitive tribes trying to invent capitalism, Zen koans, Seeing Like a State's residual — that make the underlying intellectual moves legible to a quantitatively-trained reader. The cargo-cult analogy for Lenin is unusually generative: it captures something real about what the Frankfurters thought went wrong with actually-existing Communism (it implemented gestures at Communist forms without the conceptual transformation those forms presupposed), and it does so in a register the audience can hear. Alexander's mapping of "negative theology" (the via negativa / neti neti / Bilderverbot convergence across religious traditions) onto Frankfurt School refusal-to-specify is genuinely illuminating and explains a feature of their work — the apparent perversity of complaining endlessly about capitalism without describing the replacement — that most popular accounts treat as either cowardice or evasion.

The piece's strongest analytical move is the Marcuse-as-Rosetta-Stone reading. Alexander notes that Marcuse alone tried to keep Frankfurt thought tethered to material claims (the leisure-vs-consumption divergence, the manufactured-desires hypothesis), and uses this to triangulate what the rest of the school was doing differently. The implicit critique is sharp: if Marcuse can specify what un-jammed history would look like (10% work weeks, jobs people enjoy, the polymorphous-perversity tangent aside), the obscurantism of Adorno and Horkheimer isn't a necessary feature of the project — it's a stylistic and temperamental choice. The Stravinsky-as-fascism-correlate and jazz-as-castration passages Alexander excerpts are doing a lot of work here: they let the reader judge Adorno's actual prose rather than just the apologetic summary, and the verdict is unflattering. Alexander is careful to grant the strongest interpretation (modern art highlighting the residual in ways that erode comfortable paradigms) before noting that Adorno's actual practice rarely operated at that level of nuance.

The piece's main analytical limitation is that it engages the Frankfurt School primarily as a philosophical-religious phenomenon and underweights the political-historical one. The Frankfurters were Weimar-then-exile Jews watching liberalism collapse into fascism in real time; their pessimism about the masses, their suspicion of mass culture, and their refusal to specify utopia were not just temperamental quirks but responses to a specific historical trauma (mass German publics enthusiastically embracing Nazism — a "state capitalism" variant that included their own extermination). Alexander gestures at this in the closing Sachertorte-and-Mahler reverie about interwar Vienna, but mostly as biographical color rather than as the load-bearing context for the philosophy. The "Cultural Marxism" question matters partly because the alt-right uses it to argue these Jewish intellectuals deliberately conspired to subvert Western culture — Alexander engages the lighter version of that charge (did Frankfurt School ideas inadvertently produce contemporary progressive dysfunction?) but mostly waves at the heavier version with the joke ending, when the historical-trauma frame would actually be a stronger rebuttal: refugees from a regime that murdered their families wrote pessimistically about mass culture, which is not the same as deliberately injecting cultural poison into America.

The piece's relationship to Alexander's own rationalist project is more interesting than it first appears. Alexander spends much of the essay finding analogies that make Frankfurt mysticism legible, but his final move ("If you take away the belief that history can phase transition into a utopia utterly different from and superior to modern life, then trying to improve things normally through human reason and human effort seems like the way to go") is a clean restatement of the Effective Altruism / rationalist / abundance-liberalism position against millennialist politics. He treats his inability to take Frankfurt-style dialectical mysticism seriously as a fact about himself ("As a subject of late capitalism, I am so stuck in its concepts that I fail to understand that a better world is possible") that the Frankfurters would have predicted — but he also can't quite resist letting that prediction land as a confession of analytical defeat rather than a substantive rebuttal. The strongest version of the rationalist objection would be: even if the Frankfurters were right that current concepts are limited, "negative dialectics" provides no actual evidence that a phase transition is possible (as opposed to merely desired), and one century of waiting for the cargo cult to stop being a cargo cult is moderate evidence that no such transition is coming. Alexander hints at this in the slacktivism diagnosis but doesn't quite name it as the central charge — that the Frankfurters' epistemology specifically forbids the empirical test their own predictions require. That would be the analytical close the piece earns but doesn't deliver. Still, as a popular introduction that respects both its subject and its audience, this is unusually good and is likely to be the piece many rationalist-adjacent readers cite the next time the "Cultural Marxism" debate flares.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Why Pose Thought Experiments About Fake Versions of History?

Freddie deBoer's Blog · 2026-05-29 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: A Scott Lemieux post at Lawyers, Guns, and Money (surfaced via Matt Yglesias's mailbag) that contrasted a Perry County, Indiana sheriff who jailed Larry Bushart for 37 days over a constitutionally protected post-Kirk Facebook meme against "the Oberlin undergraduate who politely told a student reporter that it was kind of insulting to call a pulled pork sandwich a 'banh mi.'"

deBoer's first move is factual correction: the Oberlin episode was not a polite one-off remark but a campus-wide cultural-appropriation campaign attacking the cafeteria over "international" dishes prepared by non-co-ethnic staff, culminating in a 14-page list of demands that included firing specific faculty over their political views and being paid hourly for activism. The example was a poor pick on its own terms — banh mi (a Vietnamese dish made on French baguette) is itself a product of the cultural mixing that anti-appropriation rules forbid. deBoer then makes the structural charge: Lemieux can't honestly describe what Oberlin (or Amherst, Wesleyan, UCSB) activists actually demanded because doing so would contradict his ostensible commitments to academic freedom, tenure, and political diversity, so he replaces the record with a counterfactual mild version that he can defend. deBoer draws on his own organizing history (anti-Iraq War, grad-student labor, tenants' rights) to argue that real respect for activists means holding them accountable for tactical and moral failures rather than pretending they didn't happen — Oberlin's refusal to negotiate predictably handed the administration a costless "no." He grants that Lemieux is right about the conservative free-speech assault (jailing Bushart was a straightforward abuse, and Yglesias notes the major free-speech voices have criticized it), but rejects "at least we're not the Perry County sheriff" as a standard for the left and insists the censorious 2010s campus politics did real, lasting damage by alienating allies, corroding free-inquiry norms, and handing the right a durable grievance.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Claude's Response:

This is deBoer in characteristic mode — anti-credentialist, anti-tribal, and willing to take the costlier intra-left position rather than the easier cross-tribal one — but unusually well-targeted because his concrete factual correction does most of the analytical work before the moral argument arrives. Scott Lemieux writes at Lawyers, Guns, and Money (LGM), a center-left political blog that has spent much of the past decade defending the academic left against right-wing attacks; deBoer's claim is that this defensive posture has degraded LGM's relationship to factual accuracy about what campus activists actually did. The pulled-pork-banh-mi example is well-chosen precisely because Lemieux had to misrepresent it to use it — the actual Oberlin episode included demands that, if accurately summarized, would conflict with the academic-freedom commitments LGM has elsewhere defended. deBoer is making a hypocrisy-via-selective-omission charge, and his examples (Amherst, Wesleyan, UCSB) extend the pattern beyond any single incident.

The strongest analytical move is deBoer's reframing of "supportive" defense as condescension. The left-of-center convention treats critique of activists as a betrayal that gives ammunition to the right; deBoer argues the opposite — that activists are owed the same accountability as any other political actor, and that pretending their tactical disasters were just minor PR problems treats them as children rather than political agents. He earns the argument by citing his own organizing background; without that, the piece would read as outside contempt rather than insider correction. The structural point that he holds left activists to a higher standard than conservative officials seeking political vengeance — "MAGA is hypocritical and evil? You don't say." — is the load-bearing claim, and it follows from his prior commitments: if the left is the side with stated normative principles about free inquiry, it should be judged against those principles rather than against the right's worst behavior.

The piece's main limitation is that it doesn't engage seriously with Lemieux's strongest possible defense, which would be something like: "Yes, the Oberlin demands were dumb, but the policy stakes of conservative state crackdowns are now orders of magnitude larger than the policy stakes of campus PR disasters ever were, so spending political capital re-litigating 2015 is a misallocation." deBoer half-acknowledges this ("MAGA sheriffs behaved censoriously… entirely unsurprising") but doesn't address the proportionality question head-on, treating it as an avoidance move rather than a substantive disagreement. A reader sympathetic to Lemieux could grant deBoer's factual corrections and still think Lemieux's broader framing — that the campus stuff was small, the state stuff is large, focus accordingly — is the right priority order. deBoer's response is essentially that even tactically, the left can't build credibility for opposing right-wing censorship while sanitizing its own; but that's an instrumental claim and the piece doesn't actually demonstrate that the audiences it needs (potential allies turned off by 2010s activism) would in fact be reconverted by an honest reckoning rather than just keeping their existing distance.

The implicit through-line connects this to deBoer's broader project against what he sees as the left's habit of substituting performative solidarity for political effectiveness — the same critique he makes of activist groups, identity-politics framings, and "the discourse" generally. Whether one buys the Lemieux-specific charge or not, the underlying argument that genuine respect requires accountability is one of deBoer's most consistent positions, and this piece is a clean execution of it.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

A Showdown over Israel in Park Slope

Suzy Weiss · 2026-05-29 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The Park Slope Food Co-op's Tuesday online membership meeting, which voted to adopt a Boycott, Divestment, and Sanctions (BDS) resolution that will remove Israeli products (Bamba, tahini) from the cooperative grocery's shelves.

Weiss, a Park Slope resident and lapsed Co-op member, treats the vote as both unsurprising — the Co-op is "a sleeper cell for people who hate fascism, yet love rules" — and a useful occasion for a sociological set-piece about the bougie-progressive grocery scene. The reporting is thin: one named friend (a current Co-op member, identity withheld) supplies the article's only on-record dissent, complaining the resolution flattens "an extremely nuanced issue" and conflates rejecting Trump with declaring "America is so rotten that we should boycott items produced by its civilians." Weiss does not interview Co-op leadership, BDS-resolution proponents, the meeting's procedural participants, or any Israeli-product supplier; the "vote about how to vote about how to vote" detail is second-hand. The piece is bundled with three short items — a podcast pitch (Scooter Braun on music industry, AI songwriting, fame), three quick recommendations (Sasse-daughter essay on fatherhood, Rosenfield on the Belle Burden memoir-fraud, Tupy in WSJ defending billionaires, Kriss on AI prose-slop). The frame is comedic-personal rather than reportorial: this is Weiss's column-as-newsletter, not a documented account of a meaningful event.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Claude's Response:

This is a Free Press genre-piece, not original reporting in the FP-investigation mode. The headline promises a "showdown"; the article delivers a vibe-essay built on one anonymous friend's quote plus the writer's recollections of her own lapsed Co-op membership. Compared with FP's stronger reporting work (the Solomon investigation into Calla Walsh's IRGC recruitment, or the Colby profile this week), this piece is closer to a Weiss byline in her older Substack mode: stylized, personally voiced, narratively confident, evidentially thin. The "[m]y eyes glazed over because, frankly, this is not my monkey nor my circus" line is a tell — the writer is signaling that the substance of the meeting (procedure, vote-tally, the BDS resolution's actual text) doesn't matter because the verdict has already been rendered at the cultural level. That works as comic essay; it does not work as reporting on what the Park Slope Food Co-op (17,000 members, oldest cooperatively-run grocery in the country) just did.

The argumentative work the framing does, beyond the literal claims: it positions BDS as inherently absurd-by-genre — a thing rule-loving Brooklyn progressives do because they conflate consumption choices with politics, not a thing with any defensible (or attackable-on-the-merits) substantive content. The dissenting Co-op member's argument is reduced to one register — "lack of nuance" — when the actual debate inside the Co-op (and in the broader BDS discourse) sits on contested empirical and normative questions about the war, the distinction between state and civilians, and the efficacy of consumer boycotts as a tool. None of that surfaces. The piece's preferred close — Weiss as Whole-Foods libertarian, "every man is a member and every woman a queen, and where there are no rules but the rule of law" — performs a tonal move (anti-collective, anti-rule, pro-anonymous-commercial-transaction) that aligns with FP's house worldview but is presented as personal preference rather than ideological commitment.

For Crosscurrents purposes the piece is best read as documentation of FP's editorial-product range in a single newsletter (a personal column, a podcast plug, three recommendations spanning psychology, billionaires, AI prose) rather than as a load-bearing piece on a Park Slope BDS vote. The vote itself probably warrants better reporting than this; we don't get it here.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

There's No Escaping the Rot in This Justice Department

Michelle Goldberg · 2026-05-29 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The collapse of the Broadview Six prosecution in Chicago after Judge April Perry discovered prosecutorial misconduct in grand-jury proceedings, plus the news that the same U.S. Attorney's Office (Northern District of Illinois) has opened a criminal investigation related to E. Jean Carroll's defamation case against Trump.

Goldberg uses the once-elite Northern District of Illinois — Patrick Fitzgerald's former office, which prosecuted Al Qaeda and Scooter Libby — as a case study of Trump-era institutional decay at DOJ. She walks through the Broadview Six case: protesters indicted for felony conspiracy after a brief confrontation with an ICE vehicle; prosecutors taking three grand-jury sessions to get an indictment, allegedly dismissing jurors who voted no, "vouching" for their own evidence (a prohibited practice), and giving the judge redacted transcripts that hid the misconduct. All charges were dismissed; the lead prosecutor was reassigned and then let go. Goldberg pairs that with the new investigation of Reid Hoffman's nonprofit grant to E. Jean Carroll's law firm — a years-old, publicly-known payment Trump's own lawyers had already failed to make hay of — as evidence the office is being weaponized for retaliation. She closes with a "Lord of the Rings" frame from reporter Devlin Barrett: DOJ careerists tell themselves they can do good work "until the Eye of Sauron turns to you" — and Goldberg's punchline is that even before it does, you're still working for the orcs.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Claude's Response:

This is a tightly-built indictment column, and the central move is the institutional-pedigree contrast: the Northern District of Illinois isn't a backwater that fell to MAGA, it's the office that produced one of the most respected federal prosecutors of the post-9/11 era (Patrick Fitzgerald, lead prosecutor on the Scooter Libby perjury conviction and the embassy-bombings cases). Goldberg deploys that pedigree to set up the "career prosecutors are the problem now, not just the Trump appointees" beat — the assistant U.S. attorney accused of vouching to the grand jury, Sheri Mecklenburg, was a longtime careerist, not an appointee. That move matters because the standard liberal frame on Trump-DOJ ("just wait out the political appointees") collapses once careerists are doing the work, which is the column's real argument.

The Broadview Six narrative does heavy work. Goldberg withholds the most damaging detail — prosecutors allegedly dismissed jurors who voted against indictment, then went back for another grand-jury session — until after she's established the office's pedigree, which is the right rhetorical sequencing. The redacted-transcript detail (prosecutors gave the judge a version that covered up their own misconduct) is the kind of specific, falsifiable claim that gives the column its grip. Worth noting: she cites Andrew Weissmann (former Mueller deputy) and a defense attorney from the case, both of whom have standing professional interests in the framing — the column would be stronger if it engaged one Trump-friendly legal commentator's counter, but Goldberg is writing in an opinion lane where that's not the convention.

The E. Jean Carroll segment is the weaker leg. The argument depends on the Hoffman-grant being old news that Trump's own legal team already litigated and lost on — which is true and well-attested — but the column doesn't quite establish that the new criminal probe is necessarily retaliatory rather than (say) a misguided but good-faith follow-up on something the civil case surfaced. Goldberg writes "it seems fair to assume" the investigation is retaliatory; that's the column's epistemic floor, and a reader who doesn't already share the prior will notice the hedge. The Devlin Barrett quotes are the column's spine — Barrett has a forthcoming book on Trump-DOJ that Goldberg is implicitly previewing, which is worth flagging for readers who'll see the same anecdotes in other outlets over the next month.

The Sauron framing is doing real work — it's a way of telling careerist readers (which DOJ has a lot of) that the "I'm just doing my technical job" defense is morally untenable even before they get a Trump assignment. That's the most aggressive claim in the piece, and Goldberg leaves it as the closer rather than building toward it; whether that registers as forceful or as glib will depend on whether you think rank-and-file prosecutors have realistic exit options (resignation, private practice, state AG offices), which the column doesn't explore.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

What Is the Current US Healthcare Situation?

Matt Bruenig · 2026-05-26 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The Searchlight Institute and Center for American Progress white papers on healthcare reform that are launching the 2028 policy primary — Bruenig is laying primer-level groundwork for the left's response to these centrist proposals.

Bruenig sets up the US healthcare system as a flow diagram with five nodes (households, governments, employers, private insurers, providers) and walks systematically through how each layer creates waste, inequity, or bad outcomes. The headline frame: the US spends over 17% of GDP on healthcare versus peer nations' ~12%, but does NOT use more healthcare — the excess goes to administrative costs, drug-price rents, and provider rents. He then catalogs specific failures: 1 in 5 Americans experience uninsurance over a four-year window (1 in 4 non-elderly adults); insurance churn resets deductibles mid-year on every job change; the bottom income decile is dominated by people whose medical bills wiped out their income; the employer-sponsored insurance (ESI) tax exclusion subsidizes 41.9% of premiums for the highest earners but only 14.2% for the lowest; FSAs cost workers $4B/year in forfeitures gifted to employers; HSAs facilitate backdoor tax shelters (the "triple tax advantage") and Truemed-style fraud (a $3,200 Garmin watch with a Letter of Medical Necessity); Medicare Advantage and Medicaid Managed Care have been targeted for reimbursement fraud at essentially every major insurer; employer mandates rationally push low-wage employers to keep workers under 30 hours; private insurers keep 15-20% of every dollar vs. traditional Medicare's 2% and are essentially redundant third-party administrators since Obamacare stripped them of risk-pricing and benefit-design discretion; provider-side admin costs are 19% in the US vs. 12% in a single-payer system. The conclusion: a single public insurer with a high actuarial value would, in principle, solve nearly all of this; Medicare for Kids is the gradualist stepping point.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Claude's Response:

Bruenig is doing primer-grade policy explanation rather than argument; the piece is a foundational document for the left's 2028 healthcare debate rather than a polemic, and it succeeds on those terms. The structural move — walking node-by-node through the payment system rather than starting from a preferred solution and working backward — is rare in left healthcare writing and analytically clarifying. Several of the specific findings are genuinely underreported even among policy-attentive readers: the regressivity of the ESI tax exclusion (14.2% vs. 41.9% effective subsidy across the income distribution), the $4B/year FSA forfeiture flow from workers to employers, the Truemed Letter-of-Medical-Necessity fraud channel, the way the employer mandate's 30-hour threshold shapes scheduling decisions in low-wage sectors. These are operational facts about how the system actually works, not partisan talking points.

The weakness is that the conclusion mostly assumes its way past the hard part. The piece spends 4,000 words on the diagnostic and ~300 on the prescription. "A single public insurer with high actuarial value would, in principle, solve nearly all of this" is true in the same sense that "demolish the building and rebuild it" solves nearly all renovation problems — it doesn't engage with the political constraints, transition design, provider-supply effects (the 19% vs. 12% provider admin gap is a real efficiency but requires forcing roughly half of US private-practice doctors and hospitals into a single payment system), or the empirical question of whether single-payer systems actually achieve the cost savings their advocates project. Bruenig promises to "elaborate in subsequent pieces," so this is fair primer framing rather than a flaw of the piece on its own terms — but readers should understand they're reading a problem-statement, not an analysis of competing solutions.

The Obamacare-stripped-insurers-of-actual-insurance-function point (Section 5) is the piece's sharpest single observation, and one the centrist policy world has never really reckoned with: if the Medical Loss Ratio rule caps insurer overhead/profit at 15-20% of revenue, the rule mechanically rewards higher medical costs (more revenue, more allowed profit). That's a real perverse incentive embedded in the ACA architecture that the Searchlight/CAP papers Bruenig is responding to will need to address.

The Truemed example is doing more work than its prominence suggests. A $1,300 federal subsidy on a $3,200 Garmin watch for a high-earner is real but quantitatively small relative to the $250B+/year ESI tax-exclusion cost. Bruenig deploys the example as a rhetorical sharpening of the regressive-subsidy point; for the broader argument, the magnitudes don't carry as much weight as the framing implies.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Do more Europeans die of summer heat than Americans die of guns?

Hannah Ritchie · 2026-05-28 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: a viral social-media chart claiming European summer heat deaths outnumber American gun deaths, framed as an indictment of European heat policy paralleling US gun policy paralysis.

Ritchie identifies the methodological flaw in the viral chart: the European heat-death figure uses excess-deaths modeling (the standard minimum-mortality-temperature approach) while the US figure uses death-certificate-coded heat deaths, which dramatically undercounts. The chart also mixed European geographic definitions: EU-27 for guns vs. a broader 32-country definition for heat. Ritchie rebuilds the comparison consistently — using a 2026 Salerno et al. paper that applies the same excess-deaths methodology to the US (~6,500 annual heat deaths, 2000-2020 average, 25-84 age group), averaging European 2022-2024 heat deaths to ~60,500, and building gun-death figures (suicide, homicide, other) for both EU-27 and broader-Europe definitions. The headline conclusion barely changes: European heat deaths still substantially exceed European gun deaths and US gun deaths. But adjusting for population per 100,000 flips the order — US gun-death rates exceed European heat-death rates. Ritchie's larger point is meta-methodological: the gun/heat pairing is "a bit silly" because they're not substitutes; both regions tolerate high-mortality status-quo conditions in one domain they'd reject in the other.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Claude's Response:

This is Ritchie operating in her clearest mode — taking a viral chart that scored a quick partisan point, rebuilding it honestly, and resisting the temptation to either dunk on or salvage the original. The methodological diagnosis is correct and important: comparing modeled excess-mortality estimates against death-certificate coding is a classic apples-vs.-oranges error that systematically inflates whichever side is using the modeled approach. The 2026 Salerno paper she uses (with the standard minimum-mortality-temperature methodology covering 92% of US population) gives the cleanest comparable baseline available; her decision to flag that other US estimates range from ~3,000 (Chu et al. 2025) to ~18,000 (Zhao et al. 2021) is exactly the right kind of uncertainty disclosure — the conclusion is robust to method choice within the plausible range.

The per-capita reversal is the analytically most interesting finding and Ritchie treats it correctly: as relevant but not dispositive. Her resistance to picking a side ("Both places take the status quo as a given: a high-mortality situation in one domain that they'd never accept in another") is the right move, but it sells short the implicit critique that drove the viral chart's appeal in the first place. The original chart's emotional payload was that European leftists' anti-gun moralism toward Americans coexists with neglect of a larger heat-mortality problem at home. Ritchie's diagnosis — that gun and heat aren't substitutes — is true but doesn't engage the political economy of the asymmetry, which is what made the chart go viral. A stronger version of the piece would have asked why heat-protection investment in Europe (especially the Mediterranean south) lags so badly relative to US gun-control efforts, given that Europe spends much more on the welfare state generally.

The piece's structural strength is methodological hygiene. Ritchie writes out which adjustments she made and why, footnotes the comparison-base details (age-range mismatch, year-range mismatch), and is honest about residual non-comparability. This is the kind of writing the project values precisely for not engineering its comparison-base. Where she could push harder: the policy implication — that excess-heat mortality is the more easily addressable problem of the two (air conditioning, urban tree cover, cooling centers, building retrofits are all known levers; firearms policy is structurally locked) — is left implicit when it could carry the closing.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

How Much Has Shale Gas Saved U.S. Consumers?

Tyler Cowen · 2026-05-29 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: A new NBER working paper by Lucas W. Davis on the consumer welfare gains from the U.S. shale-gas revolution.

Cowen excerpts the abstract verbatim. Davis takes the U.S.-Europe and U.S.-Japan natural gas price gap as the counterfactual — what U.S. consumers would have paid absent shale — and totals the implied savings over 2007-2025 at $3.1-4.3 trillion. The benefits are concentrated during supply shocks (the war in Ukraine being the largest) and spread broadly across sectors and states. The implicit point: shale gas has been one of the largest consumer-welfare interventions of the century, larger than most policy programs that get more political attention.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Claude's Response:

Frontal post — Cowen is citing a number he likes. The methodology is the load-bearing assumption: the price gap between the U.S. and European/Japanese benchmarks is treated as entirely attributable to shale. This conflates two things. Some of the gap is shale supply driving U.S. prices down; some is structural — Europe and Japan are LNG importers with shipping, regas, and political-premium costs baked in that have nothing to do with whether the U.S. had a shale boom. A more conservative counterfactual (U.S. continuing as a modest LNG importer at world prices) would still imply large savings but probably not $4 trillion.

That said, even cut in half the number is real and large, and the "concentrated during the Ukraine shock" point is the most defensible part — U.S. industrial users genuinely did pay 3-5x less than European counterparts in 2022-2023, and that is hard to attribute to anything other than the domestic supply cushion shale built.

Worth flagging what's missing from a pure-consumer-savings framing: the externality side (methane leakage, water use, induced seismicity) doesn't appear in this accounting. Davis's paper is explicitly a consumer-savings calculation, not a net-welfare one. Cowen is honest about this — he's flagging the gross benefit, not claiming it's the net — but readers should not take "$4 trillion saved" as a settled cost-benefit verdict.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Are we undermeasuring inflation for lower earners?

Tyler Cowen · 2026-05-29 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: A new NBER working paper by Kunal Sangani on within-category inflation differences across price tiers.

Cowen quotes the abstract verbatim with no added commentary. Sangani's mechanism: when upstream input costs rise, varieties within a product category tend to see similar absolute price increases, which translates to larger percentage increases for the cheapest varieties. Low-income households buy disproportionately at the low end of each category, so they face systematically higher and more volatile inflation than the headline number suggests. The paper claims this channel parsimoniously explains observed inflation-inequality fluctuations in the Great Recession and the 2021-23 post-pandemic surge, and that official statistics — by averaging within categories — understate the gap by 70-90 percent.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Claude's Response:

This is a frontal, not Straussian, post — Cowen is flagging research he finds compelling and letting the abstract speak. The mechanism is intuitive: a $0.30 increase on a $1.50 store-brand item is 20% inflation; the same $0.30 on a $4.50 premium variety is 7%. If pass-through is absolute-dollar rather than percentage-of-price, the cheapest varieties take the largest hit.

The 70-90 percent figure is the eye-catching claim and deserves more scrutiny than the post offers. It's a specific quantitative result that depends on (1) how Sangani defines categories (granularity matters a lot — finer categories mean less within-category variation to capture), (2) how representative food-at-home scanner data is for the rest of the consumption basket, and (3) whether the absolute-dollar pass-through pattern Sangani documents in groceries generalizes to services, where it's much less obvious it should. Cowen's post doesn't address these — fair, given the format, but worth flagging that the headline number is provisional.

The piece is also a contribution to the broader affordability-and-inflation cluster running through this week's roundup (Lincicome on Gen Z grocery costs, Yglesias on the political durability of "affordability"). If Sangani is right, the political phenomenology of inflation — that low-income voters experienced 2021-23 as far worse than headline CPI implies — has a measurement basis, not just a perception bias.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

How to Eat Well on the Cheap

Scott Lincicome · 2026-05-28 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: A viral Kevin O'Leary clip mocking young workers "making $70 grand a year spending $28 for lunch," and the ensuing online generational debate over whether Gen Z's high food-away-from-home spending reflects scarcity or preference.

Lincicome refuses both sides of the boomer-zoomer fight and instead offers a hybrid analytical-practical piece. The analytical part: surveys show Gen Z adults do spend more on food away from home than any other generation (a lot more), but also have higher real incomes at the same age than previous cohorts (per AEI's Scott Winship and Cato's Jeremy Horpedahl), and more free time — meaning the high delivery spend is largely a preference, not a necessity. The arithmetic: $28/day on lunches over ~235 working days is $6,580/year, about 12% of take-home on a $70K salary, and almost $2,000 more than the USDA's "moderate" budget for ALL food for a single adult. Food away from home is also where pandemic-era inflation has been worst — grocery inflation has been canceled out by wage growth for the average American, but restaurant prices keep rising. The practical part is a brown-bag-and-meal-prep tutorial: invest ~$150 in a basic spice rack and multi-function cooker; choose your store (Lidl/Aldi/Walmart/Costco save $1,179-$1,571/year vs Harris Teeter or Albertsons on the USDA moderate budget); bulk-buy flexible staples (eggs, chicken thighs, ground beef, frozen veggies, canned beans, rice); avoid precut/prepackaged versions which cost more; pick generic store brands; keep emergency frozen meals on hand; do Sunday meal prep with a few signature recipes (grilled bone-in chicken breast, slow-cooker pork loin, baked salmon, one-pot Mexican soup, chili); make your own vinaigrettes. The closing line: "you'll save a boatload while doing it — maybe even enough for DoorDash."

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Claude's Response:

This is a self-aware genre exercise — Lincicome announces upfront that he's "departing from my usual fare (get it?)" and the piece is structured as a Gen-X-uncle service column for the intern-season audience. On its own terms it's well-executed: the recipe-and-store list is genuinely useful and the napkin math ($1,179/year by shopping Lidl instead of Harris Teeter; $6,580/year on $28 lunches) gives the prescription concrete bite. The most analytically interesting move is folding in the Winship/Horpedahl data on Gen Z having higher real incomes than previous cohorts at the same age — that's actually the underreported empirical finding, and it cuts against the populist "Gen Z is uniquely broke" narrative that the original online fight was partly trading in.

The piece's structural weakness is that it never quite commits to either of its two registers. As a Cato VP and longtime economics columnist, Lincicome could have done a more rigorous treatment of why food-away-from-home inflation is decoupling from grocery inflation (labor-intensity, middleman counts, delivery-platform rents, etc.) — he gestures at this in one paragraph but doesn't develop it. As a practical guide, the recipe list is fine but reads like generic Internet cooking advice rather than something distinctive. The piece feels like Lincicome wrote it because the topic was viral and he had personal stake in it (the "dirt-poor think tanker" callback), not because there was an economics argument he needed to make.

Two small notes worth flagging for downstream synthesis. First, the Chart(s) of the Week mention "Murders in the U.S. continue to decline" — that's the same nationwide trend the Lehman/Baltimore piece sits inside, and Lincicome is implicitly siding with the "national trend explains a lot" reading rather than the "GVRS + Bates explain Baltimore specifically" reading. Second, the "Worth Your Time" link list includes "MAGAnomics are still a headwind" and "Yes, the slush fund is unprecedented (and troubling)" — a tone-marker that Lincicome's lane (free-market right, Cato-aligned) is fundamentally critical of the Trump trade/fiscal agenda even when he's writing a service column.

For a Capitolism piece, the analytic load is unusually light — this is more like a Friday column than a weekday economics deep-dive. Score it down for typicality and up for cheerful execution.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

How to improve British procurement

Tyler Cowen · 2026-05-30 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: A Mike Doherty piece on conservationists in West London getting a government license to resettle five beavers in a former golf course near Greenford Tube station; the beavers dammed the creek, ended chronic flooding, and let the city scrap a planned reservoir and levee.

The post is a single anecdote followed by one rhetorical question: "Should you need a government license to resettle beavers?" The implicit argument — which MR's regular commentariat reads frontally — is that the British state's capital-procurement apparatus is so expensive and slow that an unpaid family of rodents outperformed it, and the binding constraint on cheaper substitutes is permitting rather than ecology or engineering. Title frames it as a procurement post; body never mentions procurement directly.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Claude's Response:

This is genuinely Straussian in the narrow sense Cowen's regulars know to decode: the literal text is a charming nature story, the operative claim ("UK infrastructure costs are so deranged that licensing beavers is the reform proposal") is left as inference. The closing question is the only signal that this is a policy post and not a feel-good link. The implicit reading is the right one — Cowen has been on the high-British-construction-cost beat for years (Lower Thames Crossing, HS2), and this fits the running argument that the UK's binding constraint is permitting throughput, not capital or expertise.

What the post does not do is stress-test the prescription. Beavers worked at Greenford because the topology happened to suit them (small urban creek, low-stakes flooding); generalizing to "delicense more nature-based flood mitigation" requires assuming the supply of suitable sites and trained conservationists is large, which is unargued. The piece is a vibe-shift contribution to the British-state-capacity discourse, not a worked-out reform — and is best read as such.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Those new service sector economics jobs?

Tyler Cowen · 2026-05-28 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: A news item reporting that Russia's lower house of parliament passed a law on Tuesday allowing the central bank and other financial institutions to arm their staff and operate unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) defense systems against Ukrainian drone attacks, without special-forces involvement.

The post is a single linked-headline observation under Cowen's running "new service sector jobs" rubric — the joke being that "central banker" now includes "drone defense operator." Three sentences total.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Claude's Response:

A one-paragraph link post; not much to analyze on the merits. The signal worth flagging is what the tag accumulates over time: Cowen has been logging Ukrainian-drone-induced changes to Russian institutional life via this rubric for several years now, and the central-bank entry is a notable step — it pushes drone defense from a military function into a job description that financial-sector employees in a wartime economy are now expected to perform. Read alongside the Persuasion "warfare revolution" piece in this same week's roundup, it's one more datapoint that the drone era is reorganizing civil-military boundaries in ways that haven't been priced into how Western institutions think about their own security posture.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Full Articles

The crime collapse and the death of the progressive prosecutor

Why Did the Murders Stop in Baltimore?

Charles Fain Lehman · 2026-05-28 · Original →

Why Did the Murders Stop in Baltimore?

CHILDREN JOG IN MIDDLE BRANCH PARK IN THE CHERRY HILL NEIGHBORHOOD OF BALTIMORE, MARYLAND. (PETE KIEHART FOR THE FREE PRESS)

The number of homicides fell to a 60-year low after city leaders finally decided to start taking violence seriously enough.
