By Charles Fain Lehman 05.28.26 U.S. Politics

Say "Baltimore," and people think of crime. Shows like The Wire and Homicide: Life on the Street depicted the city as gritty, gun-ridden, and plagued by gang violence. These fictional representations weren't far off what statistics show about the city's reality. Since the turn of the millennium, young black men have been three to four times more likely to die by homicide in Baltimore than in the nation as a whole.

And as violent as it already was, things got worse a decade ago. The 2015 riots, in response to the death of 25-year-old Freddie Gray in police custody, inaugurated a retreat in Baltimore policing and a multiyear surge in street violence. In 2020, amid a national increase in killings, the Charm City saw homicide rates about eight times higher than the national average.

Then, in late 2022, Baltimore's fortunes turned. Starting sometime late in the calendar year, murder rates started plummeting. The city reported 333 murders that year. Last year, after three years of steady decline, there were just 133 murders in the whole city—a more than 60 percent drop. That's the fewest murders Baltimore has seen since 1965.

KIN "TERMITE" BROWN-LANE POSES FOR A PORTRAIT IN HER HOME IN THE CHERRY HILL NEIGHBORHOOD OF BALTIMORE, MARYLAND. (PETE KIEHART FOR THE FREE PRESS)

"It was shootings every day, every night," said Kin Brown-Lane, a lifelong resident of Cherry Hill, one of the city's most dangerous neighborhoods, about the situation prior to 2022. "People didn't have their kids outside to play, because you just never knew what was going to jump off out here."

Brown-Lane runs Switching Lanes Adult & Youth Institute, a nonprofit that gets community kids working with seniors. Now "the kids can go back outside and play," Brown-Lane said, and Cherry Hill seniors are feeling the difference. "The most important people that you want to talk to when it comes to safety and feeling safe are the seniors," she said. "And when you talk to the seniors, they'll tell you the changes."

Cleoda Walker, an 80-year resident of Cherry Hill, said that life there has "shifted for the better." While seniors still feel nervous going out at night or to go shopping, most of them tell her that they "aren't going to let crime stop them," in large part because they feel like it is under control.

What has happened in Baltimore was supposed to be impossible. The Wire creator David Simon long said that Baltimore's crime would never fall until the U.S. ended its drug war. Academics have regularly turned to systemic racism as the explanation for Baltimore's crime problem.

Yet the levels of crime Baltimore now experiences were seen as inconceivable just a few years ago. And the numbers keep falling. In April, Baltimore had four homicides, the lowest total for any single month since at least 1970. So far this year, there were 38, compared with 51 in the same period last year. At the current rate, Baltimore would end 2026 with fewer than 100 homicides. There were 323 just four years ago.

How did we get from a city in which the question was how high can crime rise, to one where the question is how low can it go? The answer might be linked to the nationwide decline in murder, spurred by a restoration of policing as the excesses of the George Floyd years recede. But that begs the question of what cities across the country are doing right. And it skips over how Baltimore has bucked nationwide murder trends at times, with homicides in the city staying high as violence fell elsewhere in the 1990s and 2000s.

When you look closely and talk to the people on the front lines, some strong patterns emerge, with two competing stories coming into focus. One is the return of a program focused on deterring the small fraction of offenders in Baltimore who commit the large majority of violent crimes. The other is the election of a new tough-on-crime prosecutor, who replaced a scandal-plagued "progressive."

Which story you prefer has increasingly become a partisan matter, producing unnecessary political feuding. The bigger lesson is that intelligently targeting the most violent offenders, both through deterrence and incapacitation, can yield large, durable reductions in murder. Baltimore's success shows that its homicide problem was, in reality, just the result of its past leaders' failure to take violence seriously enough.

Statistically speaking, almost no one reading this article will be murdered. Out of the 3,072,666 deaths in the United States in 2024, 20,162 were homicides, Centers for Disease Control and Prevention data show. If you live outside of one of a handful of murder hot spots, your risk falls even lower. Even within these areas, you need the right ingredients for a high homicide rate: groups of young men with guns, a tendency to get into personal "beefs," and little social oversight.

When you mix that recipe together, though, things can get insanely dangerous. In parts of some American cities, research has shown, young men have a higher rate of homicide death than did U.S. soldiers serving in Iraq and Afghanistan. This hyperconcentration of violence has shown up in Washington, D.C.; Chicago; Oakland, California; and elsewhere. The networked nature of the violence is important. Killers and victims are often tied up in the same social networks, meaning that beef between group members can continually escalate in a cycle of retaliation.

Baltimore follows this pattern. In Baltimore's Western District, 72 percent of murders between 2015 and 2021 were attributable to a small number of men, mostly organized into gangs. The same analysis estimated that the area's gang members accounted for just 2 percent of the district's population but as much as 75 percent of its shootings and homicides.

That's why Brandon Scott, then a mayoral candidate, reached out in 2019 to Anthony Braga, a criminologist now at the University of Pennsylvania. In the 1990s, Braga helped develop a strategy for reducing gun violence in Boston. The city had the standard problem: About 61 "gangs" accounted for just 1 percent of Boston's young people but 60 percent of the homicides.

Armed with this knowledge, Braga and Harvard Kennedy School colleagues David Kennedy and Anne Piehl designed a strategy variously called Group Violence Intervention, focused deterrence, or "Operation Ceasefire."

They gathered up the frequent offenders, well-known to police, and had officers issue a clear message: Violence would no longer be tolerated. Community organizations offered help—with jobs, records, and so on. But the overwhelming message was that if these offenders offend again, the law will come down on them like a ton of bricks.

The strategy worked. Youth homicides fell 63 percent, while the number of "shots fired" incidents fell 32 percent.

Braga said that his team was wary of bringing the program to Baltimore. The city had tried ceasefire twice before. The implementation fell apart. Nevertheless, after Scott became mayor at the end of 2020, the Penn group helped his administration put together its Group Violence Reduction Strategy (GVRS). The program started in the Western District; it now operates across six of the city's nine police districts.

The approach involves a detailed investigation of every shooting that happens in the city. Every week, the Baltimore Police Department and its partners review the week's incidents. They make plans to arrest anyone who has committed a crime or violated parole. Occasionally, the program leads to tips that end in an arrest.

But mostly the focus is on deterring the cycle of escalation. GVRS has yielded about 600 arrests in its five years of operation. For every shooting, GVRS prescribes reaching out to known associates of the victim. A second weekly meeting brings together law enforcement officials with service organizations and "community moral voices"—respected neighborhood figures who can put pressure on a would-be shooter to stand down.

At one recent coordination meeting, about 20 people gathered around the table of the conference room at Baltimore's Doxa Ministries Church Without Walls. Under the direction of Reginald Williams from the Mayor's Office of Neighborhood Safety and Engagement, they talked through two new "referrals" associated with the victim of a recent shooting. One had a long criminal history and was on house arrest. Another, barely an adult, was himself a victim a few years earlier.

Both men will have their doors knocked on by several of the meeting's attendees. They will be offered services—job training, tattoo removal, relocation, whatever they need to get out of the "life." But they will also get a clear message, delivered verbally and in the form of a letter from Mayor Scott: Baltimore is watching them—and will come after them.

Brown-Lane is one of the community's moral voices who works with GVRS. She described speaking to the brother of a young man who had been shot in a shopping center.

"His brother also was in the street, so literally nobody could get to him," she said. "So I went to him, and because of the respect level, he talked to me. He's in [an intervention program], he's doing well. He wanted to be moved, he wanted new, he wanted different—and he really wanted it, and he got it."

Brown-Lane reported that her neighborhood has gone for long stretches without a shooting or homicide. She thinks GVRS is to thank for it. "You can take the high road and do what you need to do, or you can go back out in the street, and there's consequences to going back out in the streets," she said.

News coverage sometimes emphasizes the social-services aspect of Baltimore's program. Braga said that misses the point. "It is not a soft approach to crime," he said. "The folks who are driving the violence are held accountable for their past violent behavior and any continuing violence that they are committing."

Something else has changed in Baltimore: its top prosecutor.

In 2015, Baltimore elected Marilyn Mosby as its state's attorney. She was the nation's youngest big-city prosecutor, part of the wave of "progressive prosecutors" that swept into office. Backed by left-leaning activists, these prosecutors sought to use their discretion to unilaterally decriminalize a host of petty offenses in the name of ending mass incarceration.

Mosby almost immediately got a chance to prove her bona fides. Shortly after her election, Freddie Gray died after a "rough ride" in a police van following a misdemeanor arrest for possession of a knife. In a predominantly black city already primed by the Ferguson, Missouri, protest movement, Gray's death lit the powder keg. The ensuing protests and riots drove a spike in violence in the city that arguably did not abate until 2023. Mosby stepped in, not to put an end to the disorder but to lead a failed attempt to prosecute the officers involved in Gray's death.

In 2021, her office stopped prosecuting drug possession, prostitution, trespassing, and "other low-level offenses" entirely. Unsurprisingly, the ensuing surge in crime made her unpopular with voters. So, too, did her indictment for perjury and making false statements on mortgage applications. In 2022, Mosby lost reelection. (She spent a year in home confinement after being convicted of mortgage fraud and perjury, though the fraud conviction was overturned.)

Mosby's replacement was Ivan Bates, a former defense attorney who had represented one of Gray's alleged killers. On the campaign trail, Bates took Mosby to task for her corruption and the city's crime problems, promising to focus on gun violence and on building bridges between the community and law enforcement. Since taking office, Bates has made prosecuting violent offenders his top priority.

BALTIMORE CITY STATE'S ATTORNEY IVAN BATES HOSTS AN EVENT HONORING MEMBERS OF THE COMMUNITY AT HIS OFFICES ON MAY 14, 2026, IN BALTIMORE, MARYLAND. (PETE KIEHART FOR THE FREE PRESS)

What if the new state's attorney is what's really driving violence down, not the mayor's strategy?

Some residents give Bates a significant share of the credit. Arch McKown has been involved in community public-safety efforts for between 15 and 20 years in two Baltimore neighborhoods: Patterson Park and Butcher's Hill. He said that things have gotten "a lot more quiet lately."

"Marilyn Mosby, she was of a very progressive mindset in terms of how she was prosecuting, and she sowed quite a bit of dysfunction in the agency," McKown said. Under Bates, McKown has seen more collaboration with the police, more willingness to process felons in possession of a gun, and a more credible threat of punishment.

Bates gives some credit to GVRS. Asked what drove the decline in homicide in Baltimore, his first word was "collaboration." And he agreed that "GVRS has done some good things." But, Bates added, "you cannot have GVRS be successful if you do not have successful prosecution."

Bates said that his office has identified about 6,000 frequent, violent offenders and put between 3,000 and 3,500 of them in prison. The cooperation of federal law enforcement has helped take a number of these offenders off the streets.

"IF YOU'RE SEEING YOUR FRIENDS ALL GOING TO PRISON, YOU'RE GOING TO GO: 'WHAT? I DON'T WANT TO GO TO PRISON,' " BATES TOLD THE FREE PRESS. (PETE KIEHART FOR THE FREE PRESS)

And while GVRS helps, Bates said, it's not the proverbial magic bullet. "The previous state's attorney, when there was GVRS, didn't prosecute. We had 342 murders," Bates said. Data from his office suggests that less than 4 percent of all cases closed in 2025 were referred from GVRS—implying that the program just isn't yielding many lockups.

That accords with GVRS's own total of about 600 arrests over five years. But that doesn't fully encompass the value of the program. Its goal is to deter criminal offenders, stopping them from offending in the first place.

GVRS also can't work without a tough prosecutor. GVRS provides the information to authorities, the warnings to potential offenders, and the support to leave violence behind. Prosecution is the alternative. And it's only when prosecutors get serious that the alternative can start to work.

"If you're seeing your friends all going to prison, you're going to go: 'What? I don't want to go to prison,' " Bates said. "Now all of a sudden, that job or that program someone's offered you before that you didn't want to talk about—now it looks pretty appealing."

The ambiguity behind Baltimore's homicide drop has given much of the discussion of crime an unfortunately partisan valence. Some right-of-center news outlets, eager to downplay the program that Scott has often made sound like a social-services solution to crime, have emphasized the election of Bates. Left-wing coverage often characterizes focused deterrence as a "public health" intervention.

A BIRD PERCHES ON A FENCE IN THE CHERRY HILL NEIGHBORHOOD OF BALTIMORE, MARYLAND. (PETE KIEHART FOR THE FREE PRESS)

In a sense, it is impossible to know which made the difference. But trying to credit one or the other obscures how crucially they work together. GVRS keeps violence from escalating, reducing opportunities for offending in the first place. At the same time, locking up thousands of violent repeat offenders will incapacitate them. But GVRS's deterrence can't work if the threat of incarceration isn't credible. A good prosecutor is the linchpin to a successful deterrent strategy.

For decades, Baltimore allowed violence to flourish. People died, were injured, or were scared off from the city entirely. Over the past five years, the city's government has come around to a simple conclusion: Stop tolerating violent people. When it did, the city got safer.

McKown, the public safety advocate in Patterson Park and Butcher's Hill, said there is reason for continued optimism: "Baltimore and a number of other cities have shown that violence is not some fixed condition that people just have to accept."

Baltimore shows other cities struggling with violence that murder is a problem that we know how to solve: Lock up offenders and deter further escalation. The only question is: Do more leaders have the will to do it?

↩ Back to article summary · ↪ See Claude's response

Why are Murders Down in Baltimore?

Alex Tabarrok · 2026-05-29 · Original →

In 2015 I wrote Baltimore Arrests are Down and Crime is Way Up and, as I predicted, Baltimore tipped into an high crime equilibrium. After the Freddie Gray riots, arrests declined and crime shot up but crime stayed high even after arrests rebounded. In my view, the surge fed on itself: higher crime strained police resources, and that strain—in and of itself—reduced the probability of punishment, sustaining the high-crime equilibrium, as in my crime wave paper.

Yet, beginning around 2022 crime in Baltimore—most especially murders—began to fall.

In April, Baltimore had four homicides, the lowest total for any single month since at least 1970. So far this year, there were 38, compared with 51 in the same period last year. At the current rate, Baltimore would end 2026 with fewer than 100 homicides. There were 323 just four years ago.

How did we get from a city in which the question was how high can crime rise, to one where the question is how low can it go? The answer might be linked to the nationwide decline in murder, spurred by a restoration of policing as the excesses of the George Floyd years recede. But that raises the question of what cities across the country are doing right.

So what caused the decline? We can’t be entirely sure as national trends confound but Charles Fain Lehman has a good piece in the FP arguing plausibly that the answer boils down to carrots, sticks and the non-random nature of murder. Begin with the latter. A significant subset of murders are highly predictable. A gang member gets gunned down today. Next week, you can expect retaliation. Moreover, you know who is going to do the murder even more than you know who is going to be murdered. Namely, a close associate—a fellow gang or family member—will be the one to do the killing. Sometimes pre-Cog is not so hard.

So with this in mind, Baltimore, under a new mayor and tough on crime prosecutor, began to intervene in the murder cycle before it happened, i.e. a focused deterrence program based on Boston’s Operation Ceasefire.

The approach involves a detailed investigation of every shooting that happens in the city. Every week, the Baltimore Police Department and its partners review the week’s incidents….For every shooting, GVRS prescribes reaching out to known associates of the victim.

…At one recent coordination meeting, about 20 people gathered around the table of the conference room at Baltimore’s Doxa Ministries Church Without Walls. Under the direction of Reginald Williams from the Mayor’s Office of Neighborhood Safety and Engagement, they talked through two new “referrals” associated with the victim of a recent shooting. One had a long criminal history and was on house arrest. Another, barely an adult, was himself a victim a few years earlier.

Both men will have their doors knocked on by several of the meeting’s attendees. They will be offered services—job training, tattoo removal, relocation, whatever they need to get out of the “life.” But they will also get a clear message, delivered verbally and in the form of a letter from Mayor Scott: Baltimore is watching them—and will come after them.

Carrots, sticks, and a little Pre-Cog. Together they appear to be working.

The post Why are Murders Down in Baltimore? appeared first on Marginal REVOLUTION.

↩ Back to article summary · ↪ See Claude's response

The amazing — somewhat mysterious — crime drop

Matthew Yglesias · 2026-05-28 · Original →

Jeff Asher thinks that 2026 is on track to have America’s lowest homicide rate on record after multiple years of striking declines spanning the Biden and Trump administrations. That’s an amazing and under-remarked trend, especially given how much attention was paid to the sharp rise in crime that happened in 2020 and 2021.

But what does it mean?

I think it is broadly clear that the spike was a reaction to the George Floyd protests and that the subsequent decline reflects a return to more vigorous levels of police activity.

Jerusalem Demsas is more skeptical, suggesting that the breadth and extent of the crime drop is too big to have a simple policy explanation.

We also disagree to an extent on how to characterize the dynamic I’m talking about. If the police reduce their activity levels in response to widespread public protests and crime goes up as a result, is that the fault of the protesters or the cops? In her view, this is a kind of wildcat strike to undermine democratic control of law enforcement. In my view, it’s mostly a more banal management problem: The mostly liberal people who live in liberal cities are not clamoring to go work as police officers, leaving mayors to supervise a group of personnel whose work is indispensable and who are very hard to replace.

But we’re in agreement that there was something perverse about the coalition dynamics that channeled outrage at police misconduct into funding cuts (which are congenial to the more progressive civilian public-sector unions) rather than into collective bargaining issues (which are not). And we try to consider ideas to increase and diversify the flows of human capital into policing, which might make all of these questions easier to deal with.

All that and much more on this week’s episode of The Argument.

Watch or listen wherever you get your podcasts.

New episodes post every Thursday.

For an ad-free version and the full transcript, upgrade to paid.

TRANSCRIPT BELOW:

Matthew Yglesias: Hello, I’m Matthew Yglesias.

Jerusalem Demsas: I’m Jerusalem Demsas.

Matthew Yglesias: You’re watching or listening to the argument. This is a show where two friends argue about politics, policy, and everything in between. This week, Jerusalem’s got a take.

Jerusalem Demsas: I feel like it’s less of a take and more of a “I feel confused.” I think that, as you and many other people have pointed out for many, many years, it’s clear that increasing the number of police… There are many actions like that that mayors etc. can do to reduce crime. But then, when I look at the past few years under COVID, and you see the decline in murders and property crime and everything else, it just does not seem plausibly that related to any action anyone took.

Matthew Yglesias: OK. So, I guess [to] clarify this, right, a lot of the time, when we’re doing empirical research in policy, right, we’re looking at sort of micro research, and there’s all these studies, and they look at deployments of police officers and they look at funding levels and things like this, and they try to show that, within any given neighborhood, if there’s more police there, you’ll get less crime — that it doesn’t just push the crime around. Also, that if a city has more policing, you tend to get less crime.

But you’re talking about macro, national crime trends. So let’s, just so that people know, right, because when crime goes up it gets a lot of coverage; when it goes down, it doesn’t as much. So, what’s been happening?

Jerusalem Demsas: Yeah. So, the Council on [Criminal] Justice put out their annual report. And, I mean, the bottom line is that it seems like 2025 is going to be the lowest homicide year on record, and that means going back to 1900. For numbers on that, that’s about four per 100,000 residents — homicides.

Beyond that, it marks the largest single-year percentage drop in homicide rate on record. And there’s this great Substack by this guy, John Roman, who’s a criminologist, and one thing that he does in there that’s interesting is it’s not just a return to the mean. And we can put the graph up that he creates on the screen. But you can see that crime declined even more than you would expect if COVID hadn’t happened. And we’d just been on the same trajectory.

Matthew Yglesias: Right, so, you know, there’s been this big, macro-scale fall in crime. And I do think it is true that this is not as clearly studied as these micro-scale things, because, in part, it’s harder to know how you would do that, right? Like, mechanically.

I’ve been a little bit annoyed by this discourse.

Jerusalem Demsas: Is this a discourse that’s happening a lot? Where are you seeing this?

Matthew Yglesias: I have seen this discourse. I have seen it from, I don’t know. There’s people out there who’ve been talking about this, saying, like, “Ah, crime’s gone down so much,” basically to say, “What are you guys even talking about?” You know, people who got really amped up about crime and policing.

So, I mean, I do think an important piece of context for the crime decline in 2024 and 2025 is that we had an enormous increase in crime in 2020 and 2021. Now, it’s true that we are not just seeing reversion to where it was in 2019, but that also — if you step back further, right, looking at the really big picture of crime — crime starts falling nationally in the 1990s and it’s falling quite steeply. Then...

And people at the time were not 100% sure why these macro-scale crime trends are controversial. And then, there was a recession in the early 21st century. And so, something that a lot of people speculated at that time was that the crime decline was driven by the very strong economy of the 1990s, and we were likely to see a reversion.

And that didn’t happen. Crime continued falling, albeit at a slower rate. And then there was a really big recession in 2007, 2008, 2009, and the rate at which crime was declining was slower in that period than in the 1990s, but it kept going down, right?

And so, then, at that point, I don’t think that solved anything data wise, but people sort of stopped talking about the economy and employment and I think got kind of complacent about crime as an issue. It really faded out of the national political dial.

Jerusalem Demsas: Yeah, because, as you said, people really only pay attention politically when it’s going up.

Matthew Yglesias: Right, and then, well, I mean, it’s complicated. But it’s like we had decline for so long, right, that people stopped paying attention, there was… A bipartisan movement toward criminal justice reform started, you know, in part because it seems like a good idea, in part because conservatives — certain conservatives and certainly the Koch family — got interested in… Like, “Can we cut state spending on prisons?” became a big thing.

And then, after Michael Brown died, that criminal justice reform sort of exploded, right, as a big, first-tier kind of topic, and…

Jerusalem Demsas: Which, I think, notably, I think I underestimated at the time how much people’s willingness to engage in criminal justice reform or police reform conversations was predicated on the fact that crime was pretty low, particularly in relation to recent memory for a lot of these politicians.

Matthew Yglesias: Yeah. And, well, so, OK, now we’re just, like, getting into the part where, you know, I feel bitter and angry about people but there was a significant increase in crime in the Saint Louis area following those protests. And James Comey said that he believed there was a “Ferguson Effect,” driving crime up. He got a lot of criticism from the left.

The late Richard Rosenfeld, who I know, he published some work indicating that the Ferguson Effect was not real, and that got a lot of attention in the media. Then, about a year later, he published a follow up indicating that he thought he was wrong and the Ferguson effect was quite real. That got a lot less attention in the press.

We got a similar situation in Baltimore after the death of Freddie Gray, where, again, you know, very significant increase in crime localized to Baltimore...

Jerusalem Demsas: I think it’s worth being specific about the mechanism here, a bit, though, because…

Matthew Yglesias: Well, I want to back up before we talk about the mechanism.

Jerusalem Demsas: Well, I’m gonna let you be cranky but I think that, when we talk about the Ferguson Effect, right, the real… What we see repeatedly is that, after a police protest, you see, in those areas that saw the protest, an increase in the crime rate. And people say, “OK, what’s going on here?”

And, I mean, I think the most plausible read of the data is that police retreat from engaging in policing activities, either because they feel chastened by the failure of one of their own, they feel frustrated that the public is blaming them for these systemic problems, they feel like it’s not safe for them because, “Oh, people are so angry. Maybe they’re going to turn normal interactions at a traffic stop into hostile ones.”

Whether you want to read this as very reasonable from the police to, like, “These are just police officers not doing their jobs even as they are paid to do their jobs,” I think that is clearly what’s going on here, but I think that’s important to say. Like, it’s not just…

I think sometimes people talk about this thing without having read — not you — but I hear this all the time, where people hear “The Ferguson Effect” and they seem to have some idea that protests are creating disorderly mess and it’s creating… Like, that’s not what’s going on.

Matthew Yglesias: Probably not. But, I mean, I guess I would say, you know, sometimes we have research findings that are clear, even when exact causal pathways are not that clear, and I do think that over the course of the 20-teens, right, we were in an interesting place, discourse-wise, because it’s like, crime had not gone up in a way that derailed criminal justice reform, but the decline in crime basically stopped during this period because different things were happening in different places, and empirical evidence was mounting up. It’s a…

There’s a roll in fryer paper that looks pretty systematically at this, and, you know, in many ways, good news for criminal justice reform. Like, this paper indicates that when the Department of Justice proactively does pattern or practice civil rights investigations into local police departments, it, on average, is beneficial, it makes the department work better. But that when there is some specific thing that happens that goes viral and there are big protests, that crime goes up in the city and that the intensity of police activity declines.

So, as somebody who followed those topics a little bit. You know, by the time that George Floyd died, I was just aware that this was an issue. You know what I mean? That, for whatever reason, right, you just would know from the basis of these papers that if we had large-scale nationwide anti-police protests in response to these events, the likely impact would be a large nationwide increase in crime.

Jerusalem Demsas: In the short run, yes.

Matthew Yglesias: Yes. And it seemed to me at the time that a lot of people were proceeding without this piece of information and that the climate of the moment was not well-suited to discussing it and to helping people understand the dilemmas that were involved in this — particularly because, as you say, most of the progress that had been made on reform was downstream of low crime, right?

Jerusalem Demsas: Well, we’ve gotten a little far afield of our original topic, but I do want to spend a little bit of time here because I think it’s important context setting. I think, to be fair to people who were choosing to protest, whether they were aware of this information or not. I’m not in the heads of, you know, all the activists or whatever pushing this. But, whether they were aware of the likely short-term impacts or not, the protest movement in 2020, I think is still underrated as one of the most large-scale, cross-ideological public protest movements of all time.

I mean, you saw there’s a study that was done at Hopkins, and we’ll put it in the show notes. But essentially what it showed is that the demographics of the people who protested in 2020 were more representative of the public than the voting public in the presidential election in 2020. And they were more representative on lots of things like income and race, and I don’t remember all the details of it.

But, at that moment, like Mitt Romney was protesting. You know what I mean? Like, it was like this was like a movement where there was a real national feeling of “We can actually get something done here.” And that…

Things are difficult. Politics is hard. Policy is even harder. And it did not result in all of the policy changes we would want to see. But I think people actually underrate the fact that lots of things changed as a result of that. Not only did public opinion pretty durably change about the reasonability of certain kinds of police actions, like chokehold, but many states passed — I think more than half of states passed — legislation showing, either banning chokehold or doing some sort of police brutality...

So, I don’t want to be too negative about the decision to have large-scale nonviolent protests, and I think that’s an important point to put out in the world.

Matthew Yglesias: OK. Well, I’m just trying to set the stage for my reactions to all of this, right, which is: I just think it was clear — on the basis of the preexisting empirical evidence that was not being discussed in a clear and fair-minded way in liberal-leaning and mainstream media circles — that if you had large-scale protests, there would be a significant increase in crime.

We did have large-scale protests. There was a significant increase in crime, but literally thousands of people, primarily African American, died as a result of that.

Fortunately, crime has come down since then, it’s gone down to below the prior baseline level. But when I see, when I hear, like, “Oh, it’s hard to say what happened,” or the kind of continued euphemisation that the crime increase happened “during COVID,” I mean, which is true, that is when it happened. But one of the few things we really can do international event studies on, right, is like, COVID happened everywhere and you did not see a systematic increase in murder rates across Western countries.

Murder did go up. The U.S. is not the only country with an increase in murder, although the amplitude was much, much smaller. But it also went down in a bunch of other countries.

Jerusalem Demsas: OK. Well, actually, I think here, I’m not sure I even... Yes, you are obviously correct that things were more deadly in the United States, things are often more deadly in the United States, often because of guns and other factors of culture and policy. But I do not think — do you believe?

Sorry. I wanna make sure I’m getting you correct. Do you think that the crime increase during 2020 to 2022 was largely a function of the protests?

Matthew Yglesias: Yeah.

Jerusalem Demsas: I do not believe that.

Matthew Yglesias: OK, but this was...

Jerusalem Demsas: You don’t think the staying at home, disruption to local services, the fact that police officers were worried about having transmission, so it changed… Like, there was concerns about transmission, so they were given instructions not to do traffic stops like they were normally doing. There were all these things that shifted. People were like...

There’s all these really interesting pieces about how many of the people who are likely to engage in shootouts often tend to actually be co-located in similar neighborhoods, but, for the course of their day, they’re often not in the same neighborhood because they’re at work or they’re at school or whatever. But COVID happens and a shutdown happens. And now, all day, every day, all the people who are likely to shoot each other are in the same place physically.

And so then, like...

Matthew Yglesias: But you’ve got...

Jerusalem Demsas: I just want to be clear. You don’t think all of those things had a large part to play?

Matthew Yglesias: I mean, I’m sure that different things matter in different kinds of ways, but again, we see, you know, during COVID, 17% decline in homicides in Denmark, right? A 12% decline in homicides in the United Kingdom, 10% in Spain, 9% in Italy.

Now, in the U.S., it goes up. It also goes up in Germany by almost the same amount. It goes up in Portugal by a modest amount. But the COVID kind of conditions existed everywhere, right? What’s unique to the United States is that we had this big debate around policing and that we had, you know a...

Jerusalem Demsas: Did they have that in Germany?

Matthew Yglesias: What’s that?

Jerusalem Demsas: I mean, did they have that in Germany protests?

Matthew Yglesias: I mean, not really. They didn’t have anti-police.

Jerusalem Demsas: But that’s my point.

Matthew Yglesias: Oh, sure. Listen, I’m not trying to say that the only thing that drives crime statistics is.

Jerusalem Demsas: But you think it was, like, the major cause in the United States?

Matthew Yglesias: Yes. And that then, as a result of the spike in crime, you know, we had this is like… It’s frustrating, because there was no law that was called like the Bring Our Cops Back Act of 2022, in which all the mainstream libs kind of were like, “You know what? I still hate the police. But what I’m mad about police now is that I kind of see them on their phones, in squad cars, and I want them to come out and work hard and do their jobs.”

And once everyone agreed that what they want is for the police to come out and work hard and do their jobs, they went and did it, and crime went down a lot. And it seems to me that that is the predominant factor on the way up and on the way down, is the intensity of police effort driven by the signals that were sent to them by political leaders and influencers in society, and their kind of implicit understanding of how they would be judged if they acted proactively in a difficult job.

Jerusalem Demsas: I actually do agree with you that police have shifted their view about whether they are disliked largely by society. And that vibe has shifted a lot of behavior. Like, if you look at San Francisco, there was like a nadir where they weren’t even doing traffic stops, like at fucking all, like they just weren’t doing it anymore.

Like, I don’t know what the fuck they were doing all day, but they weren’t doing one of the biggest parts of their job. And now, they are doing more parts of their job. And I think that that’s true, but that actually cuts against a lot of the intuitions I previously had in different ways. Like, one is that the individual actions of any given mayor or governor or whatever are almost irrelevant, right?

It wasn’t because, you know, like Dan Lurie in San Francisco seems like a guy who is very pragmatic, very reasonable in many ways, uh, was obviously elected in large part as a result of the disorder of the 2020 COVID period — to say euphemistically — and, you know, I don’t know, like he says he’s done a bunch of stuff. But when you look at other cities that did not do anything like what Lurie did, like Detroit, Philadelphia, Saint Louis, in cities run by progressives, by conservatives, you saw very similar crime declines going on.

And I mean, some of the bigger ones, it feels like San Francisco is just at a worse baseline to begin with. But, you know, he did actually take a bunch of steps, like hiring a bunch of new police officers, trying to reduce response times and encourage neighborhood patrols, etc. So, like, I think that one thing that this does is that, like, OK, the police are not taking their cues from whether the local community really hates them or not.

They’re taking these cues from, like, what is the national vibe around police.

Matthew Yglesias: No, so I mean, one thing that I think you see here that is annoying to empiricists is that there is like a type-token kind of issue here, right? Where, in certain cities, things happen that were read as signals of the changed mood, right? So it’s not true that, if you look cross-sectionally, that every progressive prosecutor has led to really high crime.

It is true that in the specific case of San Francisco, that the recall of Jason Boudin and his replacement by a pro-law enforcement Brooke Jenkins seems like it was an inflection point, you know, going back to before Lurie took office. And then Lurie did some things in addition. But, in a lot of ways, I think just winning the election at all was like the biggest Lurie signal. It was like, this wasn’t just a fluke that Brooke Jenkins is in. Now we have a mayor who backed that recall and blah, blah, blah, blah.

And, like, I don’t live in San Francisco. I have never met a San Francisco police officer. But in Washington, D.C., I know that what became the token was this Secure D.C. Act that Brooke Pinto introduced that the mayor eventually ended up signing, and that, you know, it has a lot of different moving parts, and Councilmember Pinto doesn’t like it when I characterize it as “legalizing chokeholds,” but it made some change to the restraint policy.

Anyway, what’s interesting, again, is if you look at the D.C. crime trends, the inflection point isn’t when the law is signed, it’s when the mayor does her press conference to say that she wants this bill, right? So it’s not like the police need it exactly. And I think you could probably find lots of studies showing that the particular restraint rules that had been put in by the reformers are, like, fine. And that it’s not important to controlling crime, that they be allowed to do whatever in exactly this circumstance.

But the understanding that developed, right, was that that law was like the anti-policing climate, right? And there was a case of a particular officer who, he was chasing a kid on a bicycle, who he had reason to believe was part of this violent drug crew. And he wanted to stop the kid because I think he — I think he felt pretty certain that he was going to catch him with, like, drugs and a gun in his backpack. And then this was going to be part of building a case.

But the official policy was that you’re not supposed to do a chase unless there’s an imminent risk to life. This kid ends up getting hit by a car while he’s fleeing from a police officer, and the cops went to prison over that. And that, you know, was not well- received by the cities.

Jerusalem Demsas: Did he have a gun?

Matthew Yglesias: Yeah, I believe he did. I mean, it’s not, it wasn’t like... But it was against policy. You know what I mean? Like, there’s no.

Anyway, he wound up getting pardoned by President Trump. And, you know, it’s a whole, it’s a very complex sort of thing. But in the climate of the era, right, the view was like, there’s nothing easier than to get a bunch of civil servants to not work hard. You know what I mean? Like, in general, right? If the political leadership of any entity says to any group of people — they don’t have to be police, they could be librarians.

Jerusalem Demsas: They don’t even have to be civic. It could just be like private sector workers.

Matthew Yglesias: Sure. Right, right, right. Yes. I mean, like.

Jerusalem Demsas: You’re saying human beings, yeah.

Matthew Yglesias: It’s like, if your boss is like, “My number one concern is that I don’t want you to go the extra mile. Like, I might send you to prison for you, like, acting in good faith to try to do this, but, like, eh.” You know, then, like you get a reduction in effort, right?

And, like, it would be interesting for the cause of social science if, like, Mayor Mamdani had not backed off any of his 2020-era statements, because then maybe we would see, like, can a high-profile mayor engineer a localized increase in crime amidst a national vibe shift? But, like, he went along with the vibe shift, right? And retained Eric Adams’ tough-on-crime police commissioner, said he was sorry, he, like, hung out with some Muslim cops.

Jerusalem Demsas: I mean, I would say I think two things. One is that even the fact that I think that we then both agree that this national vibe shift is like, I mean… There’s an indigeneity problem. Like, is it actually the specific thing that’s happening locally? Is it the fact that there’s a national trend? Who’s driving it? Is it just that the forces of reform have largely been spent and defeated by not being able to get their policies passed, and also the election of Donald Trump? Like, there’s so many things, it’s difficult to understand, what’s actually at the root cause of shifting these attitudes.

But I think the more important question here is like: We live in a democracy, and in a democracy, we elect people like mayors. And some cities don’t have mayoral control of police, but in some way, shape, or form, the police are meant to be democratically accountable to either the mayor, the city council, the people in some way.

Instead, we have a situation where if the vibes are mean to police, they stop doing the job they were paid to do. And then us, as citizens, are expected to be like, “We need to all be really nice to police.” And I want to be clear here, I’m not saying that it’s good if people… Like, if someone’s actively threatening cops, if people are actively doing things that are especially beyond the pale to individuals who are unrelated police officers to the killings of various American residents, like, that’s not good behavior.

Most of it is constitutionally protected behavior. Most of it is behavior that is within the realm of reasonableness if you’re trying to get reforms done. And the singular thing that all of this, everything we’ve discussed in this conversation, everything that’s happened since 2020, has led me to, is just like police union reform is the single most important issue if you are someone who cares about both the crime problem and also the problem of police brutality.

Because a mayor should be able to get into office, and whether or not they’ve been, you know, “I think cops are bad” or “I think cops are good,” it should be like you have a new CEO and it should be like, “If that guy is mad at you, you will lose your job in some way.” And instead we have this situation where, not only can police unions bargain on wages and benefits or whatever, they’re like bargaining on shit like, “We have this one guy who’s like the guy who’s killed like 12 people and keeps getting civilian complaints against him and, like, no one really wants him in this and no one can fire him, and also now the whole apparatus is in defense of him, both socially and morally, because they’re just like, well, that’s our guy, and like, everyone stays here forever. So what are you supposed to do about it?

Matthew Yglesias: Yeah, I mean, a few things on this, right? Like, one is, I mean, to an extent,I agree. Something that happens in highly unionized industries, you know, is sometimes there’s a strike, right? But sometimes, this particularly happens in airline industry, is a labor union will hold what they call a “work to rule strike,” right? And so, in a “work to rule strike,” it’s not an official strike because the strike can only be called under certain circumstances. But you tell everybody “Read the text of the contract. Read the text of the regulations and, like, do the minimum, right?

Like, if instead of showing up and being like, “Broadly speaking, my job is to get this airplane safely from point A to point B in a timely manner and, like, get people their sodas,” right, it’s like, “What am I legally required to do without getting fired?” And you can, like, really fuck up an enterprise by working to rule, you know, if people do it en masse. And it seems like that’s more or less, right, what was occurring, and to what extent that happened through a formal union mechanism or not, I think, is interesting to look at.

There’s definitely research indicating that collective bargaining, particularly over work rules for police officers, has negative impacts on cities, and the same for other categories of public sector workers. I think an interesting and telling part of the dysfunctionality of modern American progressive politics is that when, you know, there was a…

People didn’t just want to protest after Floyd’s death, they wanted to do something, right? And so, reforms to collective bargaining seemed to me like something that would be politically challenging to enact, but high benefit if you could enact it. However, you can also see why other state and local labor organizations would not be enthusiastic about that, including ones who the progressive movement is much more responsive to than police.

So, what we got instead was the idea of funding cuts to police agencies, which again, you can see why if you’re a non-police municipal labor union, you’re really excited about cutting police funding because that’s more funding for you. Whereas collective bargaining reform obviously opens the door to questions about, you know, how we’re dealing with other kinds of services.

And normally it’s the other way around right? Like when Scott Walker wanted to significantly curb collective bargaining in Wisconsin, the whole state union movement was like, “We gotta get, like, cops and firefighters out in front of this.” And then Walker was like, “I’ll just exempt the cops and fire fighters.” And then the unions were fucked. So yeah, that would be a better approach.

That being said, I don’t think the issue, really, of Democratic control does come down to the hiring and firing rules, because the practical problem that every mayor who I have spoken to about this raises with me is that they are all Democrats, and they all are mayors of cities where like all the people in the city are Democrats, or the vast majority of them, even the people in the close-by suburbs now tend to be Democrats.

And, like, those people don’t really want to be police officers. And the police officers who they have are like all Republicans, mostly living either in like distant exurbs or, if you have local residency rules, these like weird cop neighborhoods form, you know, where the police officers all live, and they’re quite happy to just quit and go become cops in Idaho, you know, or someplace else that’s more congenial. And there’s no obvious way to replace officers who you lose, right?

Jerusalem Demsas: Well, obviously, I think this is a question of it really depends who you are, right? Like, places like San Francisco, like D.C., like... Well, I don’t want to speak specifically. I think big cities largely can pay more for obvious reasons than these rural places and so can attract police officers. And that doesn’t solve the national shortage problem, but that can solve, often, the very cities that drive most of this conversation and, on a numbers level, drive a lot of the crime.

But then, I will also say to you, it’s not... I agree, it’s not just the hiring and firing and, you know, if a new reformer, progressive mayor got into a city and had complete control over hiring and firing, maybe you get a lot of quits in Baltimore, and they all moved to Baltimore County where they actually want to work or whatever.

But I think that the question is also just, like, the head of an organization being conceived of as the mayor changes a lot about how that thing is run. Not just hiring and firing. Like, what’s important? What are you getting rewarded for? What are the KPIs that this mayor cares about, you know?

Matthew Yglesias: Yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah. No, I mean, I’m just saying, I think this is downstream, fundamentally, though, of recruiting dynamics. Just, like, you never, you… There could never be a world in which, right, you have a big city mayor and her viewpoint is, you know…

Jerusalem Demsas: Very woke.

Matthew Yglesias: Each year, I have such-and-such budget for my new academy class, and I’m getting 2x the number of applications that I am funded to even fill. And some of those applications are bad, but plenty of them are good.

And so, if it was easier for me to fire my worst-performing police officers, I would expand the size of the academy class, because rookies are cheaper than veterans, you know? And, like, da da da da da, but I can’t because of the union rules, right? And that would be very structurally similar to a lot of education reform conversations that we were having 15 years ago. But nobody is actually in that situation, right?

Instead, almost all of these big cities are suffering attrition, just because of this, like — I don’t even want to say boomer. They’re probably Gen X at this point — but cops are aging out. They don’t — people don’t really want senior citizens working as police officers, so the retirement age tends to be pretty low for these kinds of things. People age out, and they’re struggling, like in D.C., they have put money into new police officer hiring. But the academy classes aren’t full, right? So there’s less interest in, like...

Jerusalem Demsas: But I think it also gets back to the question of, like, it’s a very circular problem. Like, people don’t want to work as policemen. Policing gets worse. The people who are there can’t be fired. The police departments are worse. Nobody wants to work in police departments.

And it’s sort of, like, where in there do we want someone to intervene? Obviously, you’d want multiple points of entry, theoretically…

Matthew Yglesias: So here’s my plan…

Jerusalem Demsas: But I just think, really, if the goal is “We think that people who are Democrats, they don’t even have to be progressive, but even just left-of-center individuals, to think of policing as a potential job for them,” when they are in their cities, they need to feel like being part of the police isn’t being part of this institution that protects horrible people that murder fellow residents of my city. And that seems to be the logical place, to really put in a lot of work.

Matthew Yglesias: Uh, sure.

Jerusalem Demsas: Also, of course, you could just raise… raising salaries is something that…

Matthew Yglesias: I mean, people do that, but it’s like the salary’s sort of ratchet, right?

Jerusalem Demsas: And, also, AI will make this possible, right?

Matthew Yglesias: It’s not so good. Well, maybe. I mean...

Jerusalem Demsas: The white collar work will be gone. People will be cops instead.

Matthew Yglesias: Oh, I was thinking RoboCop.

Jerusalem Demsas: Oh.

Matthew Yglesias: Is the solution. No, I mean, I don’t know, like, I feel like there’s a lot of sort of frustrating… you know, the thing where it could be a duck or it could be a rabbit, you know, depending on how you look at it. And I just, I feel like there’s a lot of that around here. And I guess me, the rabbit that I see, is that, like, affective with a negativity toward police and policing from the left is something that went from being quite marginal in the 1990s — I mean, I remember I went to an Agnostic Front show and, you know, “New York Police State” — but that was not, like…

Jerusalem Demsas: We should play the song.

Matthew Yglesias: …a vibe for mainstream left-of-center politicians at that time. And then...

Jerusalem Demsas: I mean, I feel like Rodney King caused a lot of people to say a lot of stuff about how it was bad that someone was murdered.

Matthew Yglesias: Yes, but, still, the Fraternal Order of Police, right, endorses Bill Clinton in 1996; endorses Al Gore, 2000; endorses John Kerry, 2004; endorses Barack Obama, 2008, right? So, yeah, Rodney King, that was bad, but the shift toward police...

Jerusalem Demsas: Mainstream Democrats are anti-police.

Matthew Yglesias: Right. And that police would see Democrats… you know… is pretty new. And I think it has not been… I don’t know what to say. Like, it hasn’t been useful in generating the outcomes that the people behind that vibe shift want.

You know, I think that progressives would genuinely be better off if they tried to — whatever they think, on some level, right — just exert a little bit more emotional self-discipline and self-control about this and project police positivity, both because, short term, high levels of police effort are good, and they save a lot of lives — crime being low is actually a better environment for structural reform of the criminal justice system — but, also, what you really need to do is create a situation in which there’s more people who want to be big city cops, you know.

And we can talk about how to make that happen. But I do think that the simplest way to make that happen is for left-of-center people to just directly say to America’s youth that a good thing to do with your life, that will be valorizing… you know, like, in the way, right, I think most soldiers are probably Republicans, as far as I understand it. But there’s a broad consensus, right, that, like, sign up for the Army, sign up for the Navy, whatever, like, good for you. And that’s why Democrats are always super eager to recruit veterans to run for office and so on and so forth, because, you know, we all agree, right? It’s like “we support the troops.”

Jerusalem Demsas: But I think I want to say two things. One of the things is that, exactly implicit in the Army example, I think, actually, a lot of what’s going on here is education polarization. That a lot of police are becoming anti-Democrat as a function of broader trends around people who are non-college-educated becoming Republicans.

Matthew Yglesias: Yeah, definitely.

Jerusalem Demsas: And that I think that, likely, most of that would have happened with or without the anti-police affect that exists nationally. But I think, more importantly, there’s a lot of power that your story puts in the hands of progressive activists specifically, which, I mean, I don’t know how broadly...

Matthew Yglesias: Yeah. I’m one of the only people — Unlike progressives, I believe that what progressive activists do is really consequential.

Jerusalem Demsas: I, tabling what you just said, I think that, unless you’re so capacious in your definition of progressive activists as to mean, quite literally, anyone who engages in progressive politics on social media…

I think that anti-police sentiment driven by the shootings of people like, the killings of people like George Floyd were very authentically a reaction to the video spreading all over the internet, like, you saw this vibe shift happen — I mean, among Republicans. I mean, you saw anti-police attitudes shifting, or positive affect declining significantly amongst Republicans, amongst independents...

I mean, I talked about this earlier, about how cross-sectional the protest movement was, but I just think that, as a piece of advice, I find it actually not that useful to say, like, “Oh, progressive activists should generate more positive police activity.” Like, when something like that happens, the question is how, both A) Can you, understand the democratic will of the people at that moment, which wasn’t defund the police. The democratic will of the moment was not that. Like, people wanted police reform; they wanted that kind of brutality not to exist anymore. They wanted that kind of dehumanization of people not to be so evident in the death of a person, but like… So, that was number one.

But, secondly, I do think that there was an anti-police, an authentic anti-police attitude, that was spreading throughout the country with or without, I don’t know, like the NAACP — I don’t know who you’re talking about, like [who] are these activists that are doing this.

Matthew Yglesias: So, I guess, here’s an interesting question: Why do you think there haven’t been any noteworthy, you know, Michael Brown-Freddie Gray-style protests in the past three, four years?

Jerusalem Demsas: There have been no murders the whole time.

Matthew Yglesias: I mean…

Jerusalem Demsas: No, I mean…

Matthew Yglesias: No, no, no, because, I mean, I think that this is, in some ways, one of the under-discussed adjacent questions to, like, what explains the macro crime?

Like, did we solve police brutality? That would be great, right? Because if that’s the answer, it seems like we should be talking about that more.

Jerusalem Demsas: I will say…

Matthew Yglesias: Or did people find a way to not care?

Jerusalem Demsas: I mean, I have not looked into this, so I’m not going to have the most cogent answer here. I think that there’s a couple of things: One is like what predicts… I mean, even amongst the time period where, like, George Floyd was murdered, obviously that was an extremely visceral video, like, the idea that we would have a close up video of someone just dying and, over the course of minutes while saying, “I can’t breathe,” like, that’s not a normal thing. That specific fact pattern was…

Matthew Yglesias: Yeah, it was very unusual.

Jerusalem Demsas: No, it was extreme. And it was such a… I mean, that was so horrible that I think that of course it was going to spread. I think many of the times that someone’s shot, there’s not civilian footage of that happening. There’s not, like… it happens in kind of a confusingly contested way, maybe it’s like in the dark, or someone’s shot in the back, and there are all these questions about, like… or someone’s shot in the front or like… There’s all these questions, like, did they have a reasonable fear of their life? Like, there’s just so many things that are different.

I would have to look into it, like, maybe there are some, like, George Floyd-esque murders that have happened in the last couple years.

Matthew Yglesias: Well, Floyd is maybe extreme, but like, part of it — there was an era, right? I mean, the Floyd video...

Jerusalem Demsas: But even during that era, it’s not like every single killing sparked that kind of reaction.

Matthew Yglesias: No, but there were more. I mean, whatever was...

Jerusalem Demsas: Like Freddie Gray. It’s also a very specific fact pattern. I mean, it’s like this young person who’s, like, in a van. I mean, it’s just like...

Matthew Yglesias: But I think the Kenosha, Wisconsin, one where, I forget the name, right, was an example of how the bar for this kind of thing goes up and down, right? And you got pretty significant protests at one point in time for something that, in that case, I think was pretty clearly like a justified police action.

Jerusalem Demsas: Jacob Blake?

Matthew Yglesias: Yeah.

Jerusalem Demsas: I don’t remember the fact pattern there.

Matthew Yglesias: OK. But, you know, it’s just, like, these things… They’re both extraordinary events where the actual facts drive a change in activity. And then there are also these kind of trends and fads and what it is people pay attention to and what it is they are concerned about.

Jerusalem Demsas: I don’t know, this sort of thing happens with a lot of progressive activity where people are, like, “Well, you stopped talking about it as much now, so, clearly, what was actually going on in the time period.” I’m like, “I think it’s not crazy that under Trump, people have now shifted their focus to higher-order political concerns.

Matthew Yglesias: Sure. But Trump was president the first time. I just,

Jerusalem Demsas: I mean, I think Trump 2 is worse. Like, I don’t know.

Matthew Yglesias: But maybe the answer is that police conduct became dramatically better, and now we all love police now, and they should be congratulated for their greatness... I just think it is interesting what does and does not get paid attention to in this discourse. I just want to make my final pitch for a policy idea here that I keep waiting for an elected official to pick up, but like, you know what Teach for America [TFA] was or is?

Yeah, we need that: Like Police for America. We need, like, a little thing, it’s like “We’re gonna get the best and the brightest, and they’re gonna go be beat cops in Chicago or Baltimore or whatever. And then, you know, some of them will be law enforcement careers, but as with TFA… Like, TFA wound up being a huge breeding ground for people who became bigger-picture education policy people, like state-level school administrators, things like that.

Both get people into education, but also gave them credibility.

Jerusalem Demsas: Exposure.

Matthew Yglesias: Not just exposure, but credibility. Because, you know, I think in all of these things, it’s kind of easy for me to sit here and be like, “What these public sector unions don’t understand…” But then, you know, other people are like, “You’ve never taught in a classroom. You don’t know what the fuck you’re talking about, right?”

And we don’t have a core of people who, like, do criminal justice policy, who have credibility with police officers to say, like, “I have walked a beat, I have slapped cuffs on a guy, like, I have had to try to maintain my cool as somebody yells at me in a heated…”

Jerusalem Demsas: Yeah, I think Detect for America is a good idea. I just think that if you’re going to get actual college-educated people to sign up for that, it’s going to require first making it not seem like a massive betrayal of every value...

Matthew Yglesias: No, but the white collar jobs are going away, so they’re going to need it.

Jerusalem Demsas: Yeah, we’ll do this another time. We have different takes on AI job loss, but I think it’s time for Peer Review.

Matthew Yglesias: OK, let’s do it. Alright. Since we’re talking crime today, I wanted to pull out a classic — an oldie but a goodie.

Jerusalem Demsas: A banger.

Matthew Yglesias: One of my favorites from 2008, this is a Gordon Dahl and Stefano DellaVigna: “Does Movie Violence Increase Violent Crime?”

So, I don’t know if you know this, because…

Jerusalem Demsas: I love this paper Matt.

Matthew Yglesias: No, but this happened when you were younger. But, like, there used to be all this discourse...

Jerusalem Demsas: No, I know this.

Matthew Yglesias: …about violence in the entertainment industry, and it was, like, causing crime.

Jerusalem Demsas: I had a little brother.

Matthew Yglesias: OK, this is a big, like, Bob Dole issue, and, like, Bill Clinton triangulated on it and stuff. So, this paper explores, essentially, the opposite hypothesis and finds that when violent movies come out, young men who love violence are in the movie theater, you know…

Jerusalem Demsas: So they’re not committing crimes.

Matthew Yglesias: Yes, and they’re not committing crimes. That it’s like, you know, they say that a 1 million increase in the audience for violent movies reduces violent crime by 1.1 to 1.3%. And they look at this a few different ways...

Jerusalem Demsas: This is 52,000 weekend assaults prevented.

Matthew Yglesias: Yeah.

Jerusalem Demsas: 52,000! That’s an insane number.

Matthew Yglesias: Sure! But it’s like, I was thinking about this more recently because we’ve been having these controversies in D.C. about these “teen takeover” things that are happening, and, you know, the tough-on-crime view is like, “We gotta have curfews and punish these people more.”

And then what you’re supposed to say as the high-minded lib alternative, is that the government needs to be creating these alternative community centers, you know, and other things for people to do. But it’s like: Nobody wants to go to like...

Jerusalem Demsas: They do go. Those are like packed, but not the violent kids.

Matthew Yglesias: Right, right, right, right, right. Versus, like, if John Wick 5 comes out, people are going to go to the fucking movies, like...

Jerusalem Demsas: So we need the D.C. government playing, like, hardcore screamo. I actually think what’s interesting about this is that, like, I think the violent movie thing is actually doing a little bit too much here. I think it’s actually just movies that young men like, because when you go look in the appendix at what movies we’re talking about, some of these are violent, I would say, consider them even violent now, like I find Passion of the Christ, maybe it’s because I’m Christian, I find that a very violent movie.

Matthew Yglesias: Sure.

Jerusalem Demsas: But, like, it’s other ones that are counted here are like Jurassic Park 3. Scary Movie. I guess it’s violent, but it’s not, you know, we’re not talking about a horrible thing to watch. Harry Potter 3 is considered mildly violent. Spider-Man is considered mildly violent. Star Wars 2 is considered mildly violent. Rush Hour 2, Austin Powers 2, Titanic, Twister…

So it’s like, at some level, and again, they do go into this in the paper a bit about, like, you know, movies that were popular that young men didn’t like, like Runaway Bride, obviously don’t have this kind of effect.

Matthew Yglesias: Yeah. That’s why I wanted to actually back up by talking about the violent movies discourse, because I think it’s a funny headline that they’ve given the paper, but, like, what it’s actually showing is that movies that young men are into reduces crime because people sit…

Jerusalem Demsas: No, what it’s actually showing is that young men, yet again, are the ones committing crimes.

Matthew Yglesias: Sure. But, I mean, you know if lots and lots of people were saying that violent movies are increasing crime, then sort of pointing out that, well, violent movies appeal to a young, male audience, and you’re basically diverting crime by getting them into the movie theater, is a good take.

Jerusalem Demsas: I mean, it’s a hilarious troll.

Matthew Yglesias: But if we want to, like, understand society, the point is that having stuff that young men like to do, particularly... But it’s genuinely like, right, because also, at least traditionally, movie theaters, you know, were kind of strictly “policed” on their own terms, you know, and so you sort of had to choose. It’s like, maybe your first-choice idea would be like, go see The Matrix and be rowdy, but they wouldn’t let you do that, right?

You could, like, not see the movie and go be rowdy on the street somewhere. Or you could sit fairly politely in the movie theater, like shut up and watch a cool movie, and so cool movies, you know, got people off the street.

Jerusalem Demsas: The real problem is that these researchers don’t consider the fact of all the crimes happening within theaters. Like, people watching their phones while it’s going on or talking to their neighbors really loudly. What about those crimes?

Matthew Yglesias: No, no, no, I mean, there’s been a big decline in theater behavior. I mean, another thing — you can tell this is an old paper because they have a thing where they’re looking at DVD and VHS rentals — They also mention offhandedly that you can’t buy booze in a movie theater, and so, like, people drink less when they go to the movies. That is not the case anymore as you’ve been trying…

Jerusalem Demsas: Is that normal? I think most theaters probably still don’t sell alcohol. I think that’s still a rare thing, right? Like, Alamo Drafthouse does it. But is that normal?

Matthew Yglesias: Well, and Landmark.

Jerusalem Demsas: But all Landmark?

Matthew Yglesias: Yeah. All landmarks have booze.

Jerusalem Demsas: OK.

Matthew Yglesias: It’s just, it’s become more widespread. I mean, as the industry has gotten weaker, you know, cities have become more permissive with those licenses...

Jerusalem Demsas: I think what’s also interesting about this study, though, is that, like it — kind of abstracting away, right, like obviously, it’s kind of a hilarious troll to be like, “You guys are saying that violent movies are making all these people more violent. But, actually, when we lock up all these violent people in the movie theater, they don’t commit crimes,” that’s like very, very funny and good scholarship, like, happy you did it.

But it’s also just, like, I mean, one way it cut against my expectations is that I would imagine that co-locating all the violent people in the movie theaters would probably lead to maybe more crimes. But the second thing I thought about...

Matthew Yglesias: They’re mostly not violent.

Jerusalem Demsas: I agree. But the the other thing I thought about was just, like, obviously there’s still this broader question about what is it that… like ideas in cinema, ideas in art, in video games, etc., do to people, which I think this paper doesn’t really speak to.

Like, even if that weekend, right, like, he doesn’t, he can’t talk about broad crime trends over the course of a year or two years or whatever. It’s just like, in the general period of time around this movie, you see the decline in violence, and you don’t see a commensurate increase in the immediate short-term future.

But we don’t know, like, did Harry Potter 3, you know, breaking Sirius Black out from his duly-appointed prison sentence, or at least whatever was going to happen to him... Maybe that increased attitudes on breaking criminals out of jail.

Matthew Yglesias: Maybe. No, so there’s actually a different paper by Jason Lindo, Isaac Swenson, and Glen Waddell, which is looking at The Ultimate Fighter TV show, and they purport to show...

Jerusalem Demsas: I don’t know what that is. What is that show?

Matthew Yglesias: It was like an early UFC or mixed martial arts thing. And so, they purport to show that early exposure to this show for, like, young, young boys had a persistent decrease in crime, a...

Jerusalem Demsas: A persistent decrease in crime? Why?

Matthew Yglesias: Yes, decrease, decrease.

Jerusalem Demsas: What’s happening?

Matthew Yglesias: Why?

Jerusalem Demsas: Yeah. What’s the mechanism?

Matthew Yglesias: Either they just become lifelong fans of the show... No, because they, like...

Jerusalem Demsas: They redirect their rage towards wanting to become UFC fighters.

Matthew Yglesias: Yeah. They learned about, like, a sport you could do if you want to beat people up. No, I don’t know.

I think there is a lesson here, somewhere, in this space…

Jerusalem Demsas: Is there?

Matthew Yglesias: That it’s... No, but I mean, it’s like, so, non-carceral approaches to crime, which are good, and which I wanted to bring up because the other thing about the COVID up and down is that we sometimes get this whole discourse about root causes of crime and how we should really be like addressing them. But that definitely didn’t happen — unlike the timeline of those macro swings.

Like, we neither solved poverty nor made it dramatically worse during this period of time. But the root cause-y things that seem more efficacious are the ones that kind of roll downhill with a certain propensity toward violence, if you know what I mean.

Jerusalem Demsas: I do. Honestly, though, I wouldn’t even call this paper a paper that is pushing us towards non-carceral solutions. And obviously I’m being a little bit tongue in cheek here, but like I actually think this is broadly situated in the incapacitation literature, right?

Matthew Yglesias: Yes. You are causing people to voluntarily opt into a high-surveillance environment.

Jerusalem Demsas: But not just voluntarily. One of the big findings of, I mean, I think the COVID period in general, is the “incapacitating children” in school has a lot of benefits for society.

Like, there’s just an actually really depressing paper about how places with school — I covered it when I was at The Atlantic, and I’m forgetting the exact thing here, so we’ll put it in the show notes if I fuck up this exact framing. But, basically, it was that parents who did not have access to their kids going to school, because school closures were longer, were more likely to drink more and to get SSRI prescriptions because, like, school is like daycare and having to be around your kids is such a costly thing that people are just self-medicating with alcohol during the pandemic.

And, obviously, I’m sure these parents largely love their kids...

Matthew Yglesias: As a parent…

Jerusalem Demsas: Butthe stresses of daycare during COVID were beyond the norm. But I found this finding to be one of the most depressing things I’ve ever seen in my life.

Matthew Yglesias: The thing is…

Jerusalem Demsas: Matt is censoring himself.

Matthew Yglesias: No, I’m just, I’m a parent. You know, my son was five… It’s not that spending time with your child is so stressful, so much as that managing, trying to manage, remote school while also doing other things… You know, that’s a lot.

Jerusalem Demsas: That’s a more fair reading.

Matthew Yglesias: There’s also other evidence indicating that parents had better mental health outcomes during the pandemic because, like, it was stressful to deal with, but also, constructive agency-oriented activity, right? Rather than just sitting home alone, being bored and being like, “Seems bad, there’s a pandemic,” you know, you had like immediate things that you had to take care of and do, so it’s like...

Jerusalem Demsas: But I’ll say it’s...

Matthew Yglesias: It’s not always good to be in a low-stress situation, I guess is what I would say. Like, nobody likes to be stressed out constantly. But, you know, I think one of the most plausible COVID crime links, right, is you had all these bored teenagers, right? Like, there wasn’t any violent movies to go see. They weren’t in high school. There wasn’t, like, a school football practice to attend, right? And so it’s like, you know, when people have less stuff to do, some of what they do is crime.

Jerusalem Demsas: Yeah, this is one of the big theories of what, you know, this is what I was pointing out, Matt, that it’s not all the Ferguson effect; that a large part of it was exactly what you’re saying right now. So I’m glad we’ve come back to Jerusalem’s position right here at the end of the podcast.

Matthew Yglesias: Well, in sum, we agree about everything. I think.

Jerusalem Demsas: Welcome to The Argument.

Matthew Yglesias: I feel like we disagreed about a lot.

Jerusalem Demsas: We did.

Matthew Yglesias: But I guess we did agreeably.

Jerusalem Demsas: Fantastic. Well, if you guys enjoyed it, please, like, subscribe, tell your friends about the show, and we’ll be back next week.

↩ Back to article summary · ↪ See Claude's response

The Best News in America

Ross Douthat · 2026-05-30 · Original →

I lived in Baltimore for a brief time just before the Great Recession, when the city seemed like a great experiment in gentrifying can-do — the wealth spillover from Washington, D.C., funding youthful forays in exposed-brick living, top-to-bottom renovations of long-abandoned townhouses, tapas restaurants and gastro pubs springing up just blocks from zones of dereliction. (Ever since, I've had a weird nostalgic response to the middling rom-com "He's Just Not That Into You," which was filmed against this backdrop.)

One night a friend reported an inner revelation at a lavish happy hour near the Inner Harbor. This can't last, he told us. He was right.

First the financial crisis cut the flow of money. Then, after Freddy Gray died in police custody in 2015, Baltimore experienced what the rest of the country experienced in 2020 — a surge in protest and rioting, a police retreat, and a murder spike that made the city's pre-existing problems bad enough to make "The Wire" seem like a good-government documentary. By the time the national homicide rate neared a pandemic-era high point in 2020, Baltimore's murder rate was about eight times the American average.

Today, though, the city is a different leading indicator: In the last few years it's become a case study in how remarkably and rapidly crime rates can fall. In 2008, the end of what I remember as Baltimore's halcyon days, there were 234 murders in the city. By 2019, there were 348. In 2025, there were just 133.

The national trend is similar and striking. There are many ways in which America in 2026 is not experiencing the "golden age" that our president promised upon his re-accession to the White House.
