The Compact cultural cluster — yuppies, post-literacy, and the post-liberal cultural register

How Yuppies Changed America

Helen Andrews · 2026-05-29 · Original →

How Yuppies Changed America Helen Andrews May 29, 2026

Yuppies: The Bankers, Lawyers, Joggers, and Gourmands Who Conquered New York By Dylan Gottlieb Harvard University Press, 352 pages

Dylan Gottlieb, professor of history at Bentley University, describes Yuppies as “the first social history of financialization.” It lives up to the billing. The book is more than a nostalgia tour through the years of Perrier and disco. It chronicles a social and a moral revolution from which America is still struggling to emerge.

The financial sector went from 4.8 percent of GDP in 1980 to 5.9 percent in 1990. Behind those numbers was a social earthquake. In New York, the number of corporate lawyers doubled between 1978 and 1986. Manufacturing had been the top destination for Harvard MBAs as late as 1975. Wall Street soon overtook it, partly because it paid better, partly because it offered a chance to take part in an attractive urban lifestyle. There were 4 million yuppies in America in 1985, defined as people aged twenty-five to thirty-nine living in cities and earning more than $40,000 ($122,000 today) per year at a professional or managerial job. A market research firm estimated a further 26 million “psychographic yuppies” who saw their wealthier peers as tastemakers.

The theme of the new era was individualism. Take the example of jogging. Obviously running is the most solitary of sports. Gottlieb points out that it was also the most educated: In the 1983 New York Marathon, 90 percent of participants had college degrees and 45 percent had been to graduate school. Running is not a rich man’s sport the way polo or sailing is. It needs no expensive equipment. The reason it appealed almost exclusively to the educated and affluent was its ethos of self-improvement.

Self-improvement defined even the yuppies’ pleasures. Food was not just to enjoy but to be knowledgeable about. Gourmet grocery stores popped up to serve the new demand for kalamata olives and French chèvre—Dean & DeLuca in 1977, Whole Foods in 1980. The Silver Palate at Columbus Avenue and seventy-third Street specialized in pre-made meals, perfect for women who came home from the office too late to cook.

Women had always been present on Wall Street as secretaries, but now they took their place as investment bankers and law firm associates. The number of female MBA students went from 2,700 in 1971 to 23,000 in 1980. These women were not looking to marry a rich husband and move to the suburbs. They wanted to enjoy the affluent single lifestyle as long as possible. Due in part to laws against credit discrimination passed in the 1970s that made it easier for women to buy property, they were able to. One-third of the condominiums sold in the US in 1980 were bought by single women.

The results were predictable. “By 1980, the birthrate in Manhattan fell to less than half of the 1950 figure,” writes Gottlieb. Goldman Sachs asked female interviewees if they would be “willing to have abortions to stay on the fast track.” Individualism left no room for the family. In the bad old days, a woman’s place in the home was justified not primarily on the grounds of her incapacity but as a matter of division of labor. A married couple needs both to earn income and to raise children, tasks accomplished better by one person than by two people splitting the work. But a moral worldview that only sees individuals has no way to understand something as simple as these efficiencies.

“Running is the most solitary of sports.” “Cravath was intensely meritocratic.”

Financialization, like feminism, changed America in ways we are all still living with. The yuppies themselves were concentrated in New York and a few other megacities, but the decisions they made rippled out into the entire economy. Gottlieb is good on the ways sweeping cultural shifts often came down to the personal considerations of a few thousand people. Ivy League graduates started flocking to jobs on Wall Street in part because New York seemed like a more fun place to spend their twenties than Detroit or Rochester.

“At cocktail parties lovely ladies would corner me and ask my opinion of the market but, alas, when they learned I was a bond man, they drifted away,” one Salomon Brothers analyst said. Bonds were an “unglamorous sideshow,” explains Gottlieb, until Michael Milken and his imitators made them the most exciting branch of finance. Bond traders maximizing their earnings and social cachet had unfortunate effects for the rest of America, however, as workers were laid off and managers became more short-sighted in order to fend off corporate raiders.

Explanations for why financialization occurred tend to focus on policy changes: lax antitrust enforcement under Ronald Reagan; the end of fixed brokerage commissions in 1975; the end of Regulation Q in 1980; the Securities and Exchange Commission’s Rule 415 allowing shelf registration of corporate debt in 1982; the deregulatory Garn–St. Germain Act of 1982. More broadly, the inflation kicked off by massive government spending in the 1960s forced middle-class savers to invest their money rather than letting it sit in the bank, which gave Wall Street a lot more to play around with.

These changes were important, but financialization was also a shift in morals. Before 1980, there were certain things Wall Street regarded as simply not done; after 1980, no one cared. The new Wall Street was less insular, less WASPy, and younger. “Easily 50 percent of Wall Street is 35 or under,” a recruiter said in 1987. Suddenly, morally questionable maneuvers that an old white-shoe law firm would have refused to execute for its clients became the in-house specialties of upstart firms like Skadden Arps.

Gottlieb offers several examples of the unscrupulous things the new generation dared to do. Skadden Arps advised its clients on how to “preemptively file for bankruptcy while keeping their current management team in place.” Continental Airlines did so in 1983 in order to void its union contracts, “even though it had many millions in assets and cash on hand.” Another Skadden client, Manville Corporation, declared bankruptcy “to shelter itself from future mass tort claims related to its production of asbestos.”

Non-refundable retainers were another Skadden innovation. Companies would pay tens or hundreds of thousands each year simply for the right to call on the firm’s services. “Generously, retainers might have been described as ‘takeover insurance,’” Gottlieb writes. Companies wanted the best takeover firm on their side—and, more importantly, unavailable to their adversaries. “To some, they looked more like a protection racket.”

The demographic changes on Wall Street in the 1970s and ’80s are generally seen in retrospect as a good thing. The old guard simply got outhustled. It was dumb and lazy, and the new generation was clever and energetic. The threat of hostile takeovers forced companies to focus more on their stock prices, and this imposed some much needed discipline on a corporate sector that had grown flabby.

Insular elites losing their position to dynamic outsiders is the oldest tale in the book, but usually it occurs because the insular elites commit some blunder. In what did the WASP elite’s failure lie? The old Wall Street presided over the peak decades of American power. They left some profits on the table, but they chose to do so. They had a set of norms that they lived by, and their community was small enough—all the Wall Street law firms together employed about 3,000 attorneys in the early 1970s—that these norms could be enforced.

When Jack Welch took over General Electric in 1981, the company was profitable. He just thought it could be more profitable if it focused on maximizing shareholder value. Sandy Weill, later CEO of Citigroup, had one of his first coups facilitating the acquisition of Reliance Insurance, a sleepy Philadelphia fire and casualty company. Reliance was not badly managed. It simply had a fat balance sheet with more assets than it needed to cover its policies, in keeping with the conservative practices of the insurance industry at the time. The whizzes on Wall Street wanted to take these assets and put them to work.

This trend soon spread throughout the insurance industry, and by the mid-1970s most insurance companies were making more from their investments than from their core business. “Contrary to popular belief, few insurers make money from insurance,” The Nation explained in 1986. Underwriting losses piled up into the billions, but insurers did not mind as long as their investment profits more than made up for it. This shift in the business model led directly to another yuppie-era phenomenon that Gottlieb describes: the arson wave.

The arson wave that hit the Bronx and other slum areas in the 1970s had two main causes. The first was the Urban Property Protection and Reinsurance Act of 1968, which banned discrimination on the basis of race or location, forcing property insurers to cover inner-city buildings they had previously rejected as uninsurable. The second was the industry’s new financialized business model. Insurance companies grew careless. If a company had to cover a slumlord’s buildings, then it was better to cut him a quick check when the buildings burned down rather than waste money on litigation and investigation, since the path to profits now was not so much avoiding losses as taking in a high volume of premiums in order to play the market.

The human cost of this arson wave was heartbreaking. Fifty-six people died from fires in Hoboken alone between 1978 and 1983. Seven thousand New Yorkers suffered fire-related injuries in 1975. Apartment dwellers slept with their shoes on and their suitcases packed in case they needed to run out of the building during the night. The new breed of insurers “view fire losses as a business cost and just pass it on to the consumer,” the Bronx district attorney said in 1977. Perhaps the old-fashioned way of insurance based on conservative underwriting practices and modest profits had something to be said for it.

Obviously, the real answer to the question of what the WASPs did wrong is that they were racist and sexist. They excluded people. Joe Flom, the attorney who revolutionized Skadden Arps, was memorialized by a colleague this way: “They used to say, ‘Joe destroyed the gentlemanly practice of law,’ When he heard that, he said, ‘That’s fine, that’s terrific.’” Before he came along, “WASPs practiced with WASPs, the Jews practiced with the Jews, the Catholics practiced with the Catholics, and black people and women couldn’t get jobs. . . If I’ve destroyed that, I’ve done a great job.’”

Gottlieb buys into this caricature of the old guard, but surely we should not let Joe Flom and his peers serve as our final authority on the class they displaced, whom they had a clear interest in painting in as bad a light as possible. The law firm that represented the opposite of Skadden was Cravath Swaine. Gottlieb quotes a famous speech given by Paul Cravath at Harvard in 1920. In law, “brilliant intellectual powers are not essential,” he said. “Too great cleverness, too facile fluency, if not leavened by a sound sense of proportion, are quite as likely to impede success as to promote it.” The best clients “want as their counsel a man who is primarily honest, safe, sound, and steady.”

If you assumed on the basis of this quotation that Cravath was a silver-spoon mediocrity who coasted through Yale with gentleman’s Cs, you would be wrong. Paul Cravath was a small-town boy from Erie County, Ohio. His father, an abolitionist and Congregationalist minister, co-founded the historically black college Fisk University, and as a teenager Paul accompanied the Fisk Jubilee Singers on their European tour. He attended Oberlin College at a time when it enrolled more black students than any other predominantly white college.

Gottlieb implies that Cravath Swaine cared more about breeding than merit. “When hiring, associates’ raw talent mattered less than their character—a quality always understood in terms of class and background,” he writes. Actually, Cravath was intensely meritocratic. It had a strict rule against nepotism, which excluded the sons, nephews, and in-laws of partners even when they were qualified. Cravath himself insisted that no one would ever be made partner simply on the basis of the business he brought in with his connections. Legal acumen alone would be the standard.

Cravath was one of the first white-shoe firms to hire women. Helen Buttenweiser was hired there in the 1930s, and Christine Beshar became the firm’s first female partner in 1971. Edward S. Benjamin became its first Jewish partner in 1958. Gottlieb writes as if the 1980s was the first time Wall Street had experienced ethnic conflict, but the German-Jewish families of “Our Crowd” had faced similar exclusions, and they eventually achieved professional (if not always social) acceptance from even the stodgiest WASPs. The two sides, after much initial misunderstanding and some mutual antagonism, learned to play by the same rules. The difference in the 1980s was that this did not occur.

Louis Auchincloss’s Diary of a Yuppie (1986) is a novel that many people must have picked up in used bookstores expecting something in the vein of Bret Easton Ellis or Jay McInerney, only to find a staid tale of a corporate lawyer in the middle of a hostile takeover. But for Auchincloss, as for Gottlieb, financialization was what the yuppie moment was all about. The protagonist of the novel clashes with his boss, who hates the vulgar jargon of leveraged buyouts, “bear hugs,” “shark repellent,” and “golden parachutes.” The older lawyer says, “Does it never strike you, Bob, that these labels may be the true moral indicia of what we are doing?”

Bob tells his boss that he doesn’t have “the remotest understanding of the moral climate in which we live today. It’s all a game, but a game with strict rules. You have to stay meticulously within the law; the least misstep, if caught, involves a penalty. But there is no particular moral opprobrium in incurring a penalty, any more than there is being offside in football.”

The moral decline of the legal profession was noticed by someone else around the same time: Hillary Rodham Clinton. As chair of the American Bar Association’s Commission on Women in the Profession, she gave a speech at Columbia Law School in 1989 bemoaning the focus on profitability above all else, at the expense of lawyers’ family lives. Her hope, Gottlieb writes, was that “firms could salvage the collegiality of the old white-shoe world without duplicating its gender and racial exclusions.”

But exclusion is how moral codes are enforced. Even exclusion based purely on values with no element of prejudice—which, of course, is rarely how it works in the real world—is offensive to modern sensibilities. Gottlieb highlights the way the winners of financialization have used their admirable record on diversity to distract from all the other moral standards on which their record was not so good, such as inequality. Like Hillary Clinton, he seems to desire a best-of-both-worlds synthesis where elite professions have both maximum inclusiveness and a robust moral code. Liberalism has had four decades to come up with such a synthesis. America has not seen it yet.

Helen Andrews is the author of Boomers: The Men and Women Who Promised Freedom and Delivered Disaster.

From Human Bondage to Open Borders

Daniel Kishi · 2026-05-27 · Original →

Speaking last spring at the 125th anniversary celebration of Grace Baptist Church in Waterbury, Connecticut, Democratic congresswoman Jasmine Crockett offered its historically black congregation a criticism of President Donald Trump’s immigration policy. Americans, she explained, need immigrants because U.S. citizens no longer want to do agricultural work. “The fact is ain’t none of y’all trying to go and farm right now,” Crockett said. “We done picking cotton. We are. You can’t pay us enough to find a plantation.”

Taken at face value, her argument is simple: the United States requires a class of laborers to perform work that Americans will not do, and employers must look beyond our borders to recruit them. She presented this as a progressive insight, but in fact it is one of the oldest arguments in American political economy. One of its most prominent proponents was not a champion of the oppressed but a slaveholding senator from South Carolina named James Henry Hammond.

In March 1858, Hammond took to the Senate floor and delivered what became known as the “Cotton is King” speech, in which he advanced his “mudsill theory” of social hierarchy. A mudsill is the lowest beam of a structure, the timber set directly on the ground that bears the weight of everything above it. “In all social systems,” he declared, “there must be a class to do the menial duties, to perform the drudgery of life. That is, a class requiring but a low order of intellect and but little skill. Its requisites are vigor, docility, fidelity.” This class, he continued, “constitutes the very mudsill of society and of political government; and you might as well attempt to build a house in the air as to build either the one or the other, except on this mudsill.”

The South, Hammond argued, was fortunate to have found a race “adapted to that purpose to her hand.” But his real provocation was directed northward. The mudsill, Hammond insisted, was universal; the North simply refused to acknowledge its own. “Your whole hireling class of manual laborers and ‘operatives,’ as you call them, are essentially slaves,” he told his Northern colleagues. “The difference between us is that our slaves are hired for life and well compensated; there is no starvation, no begging, no want of employment among our people.” His portrait of slavery was, of course, a self-serving lie. But the provocation about the North—that its economy also depended on workers without real alternatives—is the structural claim that survives, even though the conflation of wage labor with slavery does not.

New to Commonplace*? Subscribe below to get the magazine in your inbox.*

Hammond’s speech drew a direct rebuttal from Abraham Lincoln, then a member of Congress, to which I will return later. What matters here is the structure of Hammond’s reasoning. His argument was not that hard labor exists—a condition of human life since the fall from the Garden of Eden—but that a higher class must assign it to a permanent underclass. The “mudsill theory” rests on three premises: that certain work is beneath the dignity of citizens, that a civilized society nonetheless requires someone to do it, and that the solution is to source a subordinate class whose legal status forecloses the possibility of refusal. That structure persists to the present day.

Substitute “immigrant” for “slave,” and a modern mudsill theory echoes Hammond’s in its core premise. It does not argue that agricultural work should be improved, mechanized, or made attractive to American workers with better wages and conditions. It argues that Americans are “done” with it, permanently, and that the nation must therefore allow for the importation of a labor force willing to do what its citizens will not. In other words, it does not call for the abolition of the mudsill, but for its outsourcing.

Crockett’s candor should not be mistaken for originality. The belief she stated so plainly has been the quiet consensus in both political parties, held by progressives who frame immigration enforcement as cruelty and by the business lobbies that fight that enforcement because they profit from cheap and compliant labor. In September 2022, then-Speaker of the House Nancy Pelosi said that “we need them to pick the crops down here.” In April 2006, President George W. Bush, pushing the Senate to pass immigration reform, argued the country must “recognize that there are people here working hard for jobs Americans won’t do.” Senator Lindsey Graham, defending the immigrant meatpacking workforce in a February 2013 hearing, told his colleagues that employers in South Carolina could “advertise all day long every day of the week” without finding domestic workers.

The phrase “jobs Americans won’t do” is the mudsill theory translated into the language of labor economics: the work cannot be transformed; the only question is where to find people desperate enough to do it. To accept that premise is to assert that such work is beneath the dignity of citizens by nature, and that someone else’s dignity is a lesser concern.

America’s agricultural labor market gives this logic institutional form. For decades, lax enforcement of our immigration law has supplied farms with a workforce of illegal immigrants who cannot complain about wages or report unsafe conditions, because any assertion of their labor and employment rights risks deportation. This is the logical end of the mudsill: a policy of vulnerability, maintained by employers who profit from it and a government willing to look the other way. The worker with no legal standing in the country he feeds is the mudsill itself.

Where public policy has formalized this arrangement, the structure is no better. The H-2A Temporary Agricultural Workers program ties workers to a single sponsoring employer. For all its wage and housing requirements on paper, the program’s basic design deters exit: leaving a job means losing lawful status unless a worker can quickly secure another sponsor. A worker’s presence in the country thus depends on continued compliance with whatever his sponsor assigns. This is a legally constructed dependency that creates the “docility” Hammond prized.

While America’s farms are the most visible example, the same logic runs through many segments of the low-wage economy. Construction firms and restaurant kitchens are staffed, in many places, by workers whose employers count on their silence as much as their labor.

Long-haul trucking shows something worse. The mudsill there was not inherited but built. Driving a truck was once a path to the middle class, a job that could support a family without a college degree. For years, the American Trucking Association insisted the industry nonetheless faced a permanent driver shortage, and used that claim to lobby Washington to dismantle barriers to entry. As the logistics executive Craig Fuller has documented, the industry won relaxed English-proficiency enforcement, self-certified training, and non-domiciled licenses for foreign nationals.

And yet, the shortage was always a mirage: the lobbying created a capacity glut that drove down rates for American truck drivers, pushed law-abiding carriers toward insolvency, and put inexperienced and vulnerable labor on American highways, imperiling public safety. A respectable trade was made into a mudsill job, one its own workforce could no longer climb.

The most revealing case is one that involves no hard labor at all. Tending the very young and the very old demands no great physical strength. It is not “drudgery” in the sense Hammond meant. Rather, it is the most basic form of human reciprocity—the work parents do for children, and children, in time, for parents. But once that work is moved out of the home and into the market, it becomes a paid task, often a low-paid one, to be assigned to whoever can be found to perform it. The mudsill cannot operate on labor that families do for one another out of love and obligation. It requires that the labor first be commodified, then handed off to a paid, and now often imported, class. That case strips the mudsill theory bare. The mudsill was never about the difficulty of the work, but about which work can be offloaded, and who can be made to do it.

Lincoln understood the stakes. In September 1859, just over a year before his fellow citizens elected him their president, Lincoln traveled to Milwaukee to deliver an address to the Wisconsin Agricultural Society in which he rejected the mudsill theory. Lincoln began by restating Hammond’s logic. The mudsill theorists, he said, “naturally conclude that all laborers are necessarily either hired laborers, or slaves. They further assume that whoever is once a hired laborer, is fatally fixed in that condition for life.” This, Lincoln argued, was wrong as a matter of fact and dangerous as a matter of principle.

Against it, Lincoln posed the vision of “free labor.” “Labor,” he declared, “is prior to, and independent of, capital. In fact, capital is the fruit of labor, and could never have existed if labor had not first existed.” Work was not a caste but a condition—one the worker could leave because he had the skills and the legal standing to choose otherwise. As Lincoln described it, “The prudent, penniless beginner in the world, labors for wages awhile, saves a surplus with which to buy tools or land, for himself; then labors on his own account another while, and at length hires another new beginner to help him.” This was “free labor—the just and generous, and prosperous system, which opens the way for all.”

Then Lincoln named what made the mudsill theory not merely wrong but destructive. Under that doctrine, he said, “a blind horse upon a treadmill, is a perfect illustration of what a laborer should be—all the better for being blind, that he could not tread out of place, or kick understandingly.” The mudsill theory assumed “that labor and education are incompatible” and that the laboring class must never acquire the agency, knowledge, and leverage to demand something better. Lincoln’s answer was the opposite. The path to prosperity ran through education, innovation, and what he called “thorough work,” rather than the exploitation of a captive labor force. He was describing, in the language of 1859, what we would now call workforce training and capital deepening, the critical ingredients that make labor more productive and better paid.

The subsequent century vindicated his logic, if unevenly. American agriculture did not collapse when it lost access to its mudsill. The end of slavery produced severe disruption. Cotton exports plummeted during the Civil War, and the plantation system that had sustained them disintegrated. The slaveholders who had sunk the majority of their wealth into human property found themselves bankrupt, and the freedmen who had constituted their labor force now possessed the power to refuse, to negotiate, and to leave. Every premise of the plantation economy—that the work was too degrading for free men, that only a captive workforce could sustain it, that abolition would result in economic ruin—was tested at once.

And then reorganization came. The crop did not vanish: within roughly a decade, growers had reorganized the system that produced it, and the South began, in fits and starts, to diversify into the kind of manufacturing that slavery had suppressed. But where the region found new instruments of coercion—convict leasing, debt peonage, the crop-lien system—it replicated the mudsill, and the regions most dependent on coerced labor were slower to modernize. The pattern held into the next century. When the Great Migration finally drained the sharecropping labor force, the mechanical cotton picker arrived—not because the technology was new, but because the mudsill was gone. The lesson runs the length of history. Where public policy dismantled the mudsill, adaptation followed, and adaptation meant the technological and organizational modernization that coerced labor had made unnecessary.

The modern mudsill theory espoused by Crockett and others argues that the field still needs picking, that it will always need picking, and that the nation’s task is to find people willing to pick it. The irony is that cotton, the very crop Crockett names, is a textbook example of work Americans stopped doing by hand. The machines came when the captive labor force was gone. The premise—that work is fixed and only the workforce is negotiable—is not liberation theory but mudsill theory. Updated for a new century, Hammond’s theory survives intact.

The alternative is to reject the premise—to ensure that all jobs on offer in the U.S. labor market are those that Americans woulddo. Not every job that exists today must exist in its current form. As with cotton, not every task performed by hand must always be performed by hand. The question ought not be “who will pick the crops?” but rather “why are we still picking them by hand?” and “what combination of incentives and investment would allow us to stop?”

A nation that insists on maintaining a two-tiered labor class—whether through slavery, sharecropping, the tacit acceptance of illegal employment, the relocation of production to wherever labor is cheapest and least protected, or temporary visa programs that bind labor to a single employer—has made a political choice. It has chosen the mudsill. But it can choose otherwise. Lincoln’s answer, and the right one, is to build an economy that does not need one.

Leave a comment

Title IX's Collision Course with Reality

Scott Yenor · 2026-05-28 · Original →

Title IX’s Collision Course with Reality Scott Yenor May 28, 2026

Beginning in 2028, the University of Nebraska will sponsor women’s flag football as a varsity sport. The NCAA now officially recognizes women’s flag football as an Emerging Sport, with the promise of an NCAA championship in the near future if more schools adopt it. No men’s flag football programs exist in the NCAA, though men and boys make up the lion’s share of flag football players in all recreational leagues. The reason for this is simple: Title IX demands that schools have as many female athletes as the female proportion in the student body. If women are 59 percent of the student body (the national average), then 59 percent of the athletes must be women too if schools want to be in the “safe harbor” for Title IX compliance.

Nebraska was about 53 percent male in 2000, but is now split 50-50. This presents a problem. Roughly 53 percent of Husker athletes are men, according to its 2025 Equity in Athletics Disclosure Act (EADA) report. Its 155 football players stifle the creation of other male sports and oblige Nebraska to fund women’s sports like flag football, beach volleyball, bowling, rifle, soccer, swimming and diving (without male equivalents). Nebraska had fifty members of its female basketball team in 2025; the men’s team had only eighteen. Nebraska cut men’s swimming in 2001 under such Title IX pressure. It hasn’t added a men’s sport since 1993. Even with all these changes, Nebraska is not in Title IX’s “safe harbor.”

Matters are about to get worse. The Title IX regime is on a collision course with the new Name-Image-Likeness (NIL) regime. The NIL settlement, known as the House v. NCAA case, has established revenue sharing for college athletes. Schools that opt in can directly pay their athletes up to $20.5 million per year (increasing annually) while students also get scholarships and Name-Image-Likeness deals—uncapped side payments—from collectives and boosters, who are often friends of the school.

Under the settlement, athletes gave up scholarship limits and got NIL money and, in exchange, schools got a salary cap and roster limits to contain costs. Roster limits keep big schools from stockpiling talent, but they also create a Title IX minefield.

Most major colleges find a safe harbor by inflating women’s sports offerings and rosters. At the University of Alabama, for instance, 57 percent of the student body is female, but only 51 percent of the athletes are female. The athletic department offset men’s football 132 roster spots with women’s rowing (115 participants) and women’s basketball (30 players). Roster limits for female rowing are lowered to sixty-eight under the NIL settlement, while women’s basketball rosters are trimmed to fifteen. If Alabama keeps all its current sports and fills all the roster spots, its percentage of female athletes will dip from 51 percent to 46 percent.

Much the same is true across Division I. The University of Florida’s percentage of female athletes will drop from to 45 percent to 44 percent if the athletic department keeps all its current sports and fills all its rosters, though 55 percent of Gator students are female. Louisiana State University’s percentage of female athletes will go from 51 percent female to 48 percent female (57 percent of LSU students are female). Texas A&M’s will fall from 47 percent to 43 percent (47 percent of students are female). Nebraska will remain 47 percent female, but adding 20 women’s flag football players (the roster limits have not yet been set under the NIL settlement) will bring Nebraska closer to the safe harbor.

Until the NIL settlement, all of these schools demonstrated, as Title IX regulations demand, a “history and continuing practice of program expansion for the underrepresented sex.” There are still disparities, but the disparities are not getting worse. The roster limits will change that. The NIL settlement forces Florida to shrink its women’s soccer team or Alabama its rowing team, but Title IX prevents both actions. LSU will have to trim its women’s cross-country and volleyball teams, but this will create a Title IX situation. The same goes for the women’s equestrian and swimming and diving teams at Texas A&M.

Under these circumstances, big-time college programs will be forced to follow Nebraska’s lead and add women’s flag football to show “continuing practice of program expansion” for women. Even minor cuts trigger conditions for a Title IX lawsuit from female athletes who can no longer participate in college athletics. In fact, cutting women’s sports causes legal problems for schools that have not reached proportionate parity. And very few have.

The NIL agreement is already running afoul of Title IX-inspired lawsuits. As part of the settlement, the NCAA and Power conferences (like the SEC and Big Ten) will pay former athletes back damages for NIL opportunities forfeited under the old rules. More than 90 percent of the money is going to men’s football and basketball players, who, after all, are the main beneficiaries of NIL money. Female athletes sued, arguing that the marked sex difference in back pay is a discriminatory payment formula prohibited by Title IX. The Ninth Circuit has put disbursement of back-pay on hold. Women receive well under 20 percent of NIL money in 2025-2026.

“Matters are about to get worse.” “Under President Obama, high schools came under the same intense supervision.”

The proportionate parity standard has prevailed since the early 1990s. In his book The Transformation of Title IX, R. Shep Melnick recounts that Brown University, facing a budget deficit, demoted two men’s varsity teams (golf and water polo) and two women’s varsity teams (volleyball and gymnastics) to club status. Despite having one of the highest percentages of female athletes among major universities at the time, female gymnasts and volleyball players sued, since Brown had not yet achieved proportionate parity.

Brown argued that the school should have the regulatory relief to balance athletics and finances; that it could not fully accommodate female interests while ignoring male interest under the law; and that whatever differences between women sports and men sports derived from the fact that women, on average, showed less interest in competitive varsity sports than did men.

Federal courts rejected Brown’s defense. According to Judge Hugh Bownes (1996), even if empirical evidence showed women had less interest in sports, such evidence “cannot justify providing fewer athletic opportunities for women than for men.” Women had to occupy as many athletics spots as their proportion of the student body for schools to satisfy Title IX’s demands.

As Brown sought to comply, it tried to count unfilled roster slots on the volleyball team as part of its total. But courts held that roster spots have to be filled, or Brown is not really trying. Unfilled roster spots did not prove that schools were creating opportunities for women; institutions had to fill roster spots or face liability. As a result, Brown had to invest millions of dollars upgrading its facilities, hiring coaches, and improving its scheduling to create opportunities for women’s athletics. Women’s crew was added despite the budget crunch. Maybe someday flag football will come to Providence, to go with its current female-only sports of equestrian, fencing, field hockey, and sailing (Just over 50 percent of Brown’s athletes are female today, and 52 percent of undergraduates are female).

After Brown, the Department of Education’s OCR promulgated a Dear Colleague letter institutionalizing the “safe harbor” of proportionate parity. “Unfilled slots” on rosters would not count as giving “athletic opportunities.” By 2000, there were 300 more women’s teams than men’s teams competing in the NCAA.

Under President Obama, high schools came under the same intense supervision, with all of the complications of ensuring that the opportunities were equal. The Obama Administration’s OCR applied an expansive proportionate parity standard to high schools as well, challenging scheduling, facilities, and practice times.

A 2012 Seventh Circuit case about scheduling illustrates the breadth of Title IX oversight. The court found that when boys play under the Friday night lights, and girls play during the week, this violates Title IX. Scheduling games on weekdays “creates a cyclical effect,” the judge contended, which “stifles community support, prevents the development of a fan base, and discourages females from participating in a traditionally male dominated sport.” Girls must play on weekend nights just as often as boys. Schools learned that the safest (and often the cheapest) path to compliance was cutting men’s non-revenue programs rather than adding women’s teams or controlling costs in football and men’s basketball or sacrificing the gate to put girls under Friday night lights. My wife, an athletic director in Idaho, testifies to how Title IX obsessions hamstring schools and cost athletic departments money at the gate.

Proportionality remains the practical standard, as recent lawsuits show. Concordia-Irvine has a sex ratio among undergraduates at the national average—59 percent female. Females are only 51 percent of the athletes, however. Concordia has two sports—beach volleyball and stunt/cheer—without male equivalents. When a budget crunch hit in 2025, Concordia announced swimming and tennis for both men and women would be discontinued. Female athletes filed a class-action lawsuit against Concordia in August 2025, claiming that cutting women’s program without first securing proportional parity violated Title IX. The federal judge issued a preliminary injunction against cutting the women’s tennis and swimming teams (but not against cutting the men’s sports).

Division II and Division III schools are not covered under House v. NCAA settlement. For these schools, which are generally far from the proportionate parity that Title IX has come to demand, Title IX is on a collision course with the demographic cliff, impending enrollment declines, and the corresponding budget crises.

The demographic cliff—where the number of available students to enter college shrinks every year—will lower enrollments and stress college budgets. Like Brown and Concordia, schools must ensure that budget cutting complies with Title IX. It will not be easy. The gaps between the percentages of females in the undergraduate body and the percentages in athletics are much larger in the more cash-strapped lower divisions. Ferris State University, a Division II school in Michigan, does not have Alabama’s ability to use football revenue to subsidize a women’s rowing team. If anything, lower-division schools have more athletes than most Division I programs, since they use sports to entice students (mostly male students) to attend.

In successful lower-division athletic programs, the problem of Title IX compliance is quite stark. Gaps of twenty percentage points are quite common at Division II schools. The best Division II women’s volleyball schools, for instance, have parity gaps from a mere 4 percent (St. Cloud State) to a yawning 24 percent (St. Paul Concordia). Most Division III teams have, if anything, bigger gaps. Only male-dominated schools with an emphasis on engineering defy the trends on sex ratios.

Many Division II are cash-strapped schools with student bodies skewing heavily female. Many men come to many of these schools to continue the camaraderie and fraternity of sports. When budgets have to be cut, only men’s sports will be cut. A vicious cycle of fewer men leading to fewer students leading to fewer men’s sports will ensue. Ultimately, Title IX will exacerbate budget crises up and down America’s colleges.

The proportionate parity Title IX regime embodies our reigning civil rights orthodoxy. Every disparity between a supposedly underrepresented group (women, in this case) and men is said to be traceable to discrimination. What discrimination creates, state power must solve. This reigning orthodoxy guided the seemingly haphazard development of Title IX, from a mere anti-discrimination law to an engine for social engineering based on denial of sex.

In the beginning, regulators asked if cheerleading, exercise clubs, or competitive dance should count as athletic participation; the answer was no—some activities and sports just reinforce stereotypes of women as non-competitive. Only competitive varsity sports would count.

Later, in the 1990s, schools wanted to define “equal athletic opportunity” as meeting the seemingly different “interests” of men and women. But here too, the idea that men and women have different interest levels in sports was treated as a stereotype that Title IX must re-engineer or assume away. Proportional parity in competitive varsity sports is demanded, in high school and college.

Can schools accommodate different levels of public interest in male and female sports by giving men and women different schedules? No. Such differences are a vestige of outdated stereotypes. Is creating opportunity enough? No. Opportunity, to be real, must conjure up interest. Only real equality of interest proves equality of opportunity.

Such assumptions lead to women’s varsity flag football as a varsity sport at Nebraska or the growth of women’s bowling, sailing, fencing, and field hockey. Even though women make up well under a third of people who play these sports recreationally.

As political scientist Steven Rhoads wrote in his 2004 book Taking Sex Differences Seriously, “one of the greatest differences between the sexes is their level of interest in sports.” These differences are cross-cultural and enduring. But when Title IX meets nature, it calls nature a stereotype and insists that it be engineered away. When Title IX meets the budget as it did for Brown and will do for many Division II and III schools as the demographic cliff hits, the budget has to give way. Better to be bankrupt than fail the proportionality test.

Much the same is likely to happen when Title IX meets the NIL settlement. Roster limits for women’s sports will be ruled to violate Title IX.

The proportionate parity standard never went through the normal administrative procedures back in the 1990s. It was the product of the administrative-judicial “leapfrogging,” by which administrators and judges reinforce and radicalize a particular finding. Just as the Trump administration rescinded more than fifty years of disparate impact in contracting, it could eliminate the proportionate parity requirement from regulations. As a preliminary matter, schools could be allowed to comply with Title IX by providing opportunities consistent with demonstrated student interest. The last effort to make student interest rather than proportionate parity the standard happened under President George W. Bush. Schools could collect data on the proportion of students of each sex interested in varsity sports and provide opportunities roughly consistent with the results. This would allow schools the flexibility they need to attract students in times of declining enrollment. Additionally, the Trump administration may be able to explicitly exempt major revenue-producing male sports from counting toward a school's underlying totals.

The benighted view that all disparities between men and women are traceable to discrimination and stereotyping should be expunged from our laws. Women and men value competition differently, and our institutions should not have to engineer away that reality.

Scott Yenor is chair of the American Citizenship Initiative at the Heritage Foundation.

The Cheap Tradsploitation of 'Yesteryear'

Julia Yost · 2026-05-27 · Original →

The Cheap Tradsploitation of ‘Yesteryear’ Julia Yost May 27, 2026

One of the year’s bestselling books contains a pivotal sex scene in which a time-traveling tradwife finds her impotent husband transformed into a domineering patriarch. She is terrified and unwilling and, wouldn’t you know it, satisfied for the first time in her life. In Yesteryear, Caro Claire Burke presents antifeminist gender norms for our condemnation and titillation. You could say she pioneers the genre of tradsploitation. Our tradwife gets what she asked for, good and hard. She must pay for her betrayal of her sex, and of the future. The future, of course, is female.

The plot turns on a nifty conceit. Natalie Heller Mills is a tradwife influencer, a “good Christian woman” who runs a nostalgic hobby farm in Idaho with her husband, the scion of a political dynasty. The aesthetic is prairie dresses and sourdough starter. They have chickens and cows and scads of kids. A gigantic pantry hides the modern conveniences. Natalie’s whole life is a performance—for her TikTok, for herself. She despises her husband, resents her kids, and urges herself, “Don’t forget to smile!” One day she wakes up on the same farm but apparently in 1855, and the past she cosplayed gets real.

Yesteryear is being hailed as a sharp satire of the hypocrisies of tradwives, the manosphere, and Christian nationalism. But it’s hard to square the critics’ sense of cathartic critique with the staleness of the ironies. Did you know that tradwife influencers are also businesswomen? That social-media accounts can have producers behind the scenes? That wealthy families employ nannies? That the most redpilled men are the biggest losers? That sometimes, within a Christian marriage, the woman wears the pants?

Burke regards this last revelation as especially significant. In their twenty-first-century guises, Natalie is the stern ambitious spouse, her husband Caleb the nurturer. “I should’ve been born a man,” Natalie concludes in exasperation. The shock of her Lady Macbeth moment is lessened by the fact that Natalie isn’t a terribly persuasive portrait of a woman. The Guardian is right to object to the vagueness of her physical motherhood. The reader is told that breastfeeding is a drag, but “there is no latch, no letdown, no description of any kind.” Instead, there’s this:

Sleep, breastfeed. Breastfeed, sleep. Sleep, sleep, sleep. Breastfeed. Cry. Breastfeed. Sleep. Kill yourself.

The stereotyped nature of Natalie’s physical life is a problem for Yesteryear’s polemic, which is staked on the idea that trad women merely pretend to be fulfilled by motherhood: “It’s the long, golden string of insincerity,” Burke writes. “Your mother lies to you, and then you lie to your children, and then your children lie to their children, and then you are an old woman, looking back at your life, lying to yourself.”

The charge of conspiracy would be more compelling if Burke demonstrated some understanding of what it’s actually like to have kids, especially several kids. When the first child arrives, much is made of the fact that Natalie’s “softest and most private patch of skin [tears] a full inch.” This outrage is compounded when, naturally, she goes for a run and the stitches rip. It’s the kind of calamity a childless writer might seize on to evoke the process of (in Shulamith Firestone’s lively phrase) “shitting a pumpkin.” Mundane realities such as pelvic floor weakness, a far more serious curse upon mothers of multiple children, never enter Natalie’s list of physical complaints, even as her roster of children lengthens.

The descriptions of Natalie’s pain are more consistent with revenge fantasy than with social critique. In 1855, Natalie exists to get slapped around. Caleb the patriarch gives her a black eye and a blackout. While attempting to flee her abusive homestead, she steps in an animal trap and sustains a deep wound to an ankle. The wound is sewn up gruesomely by her young daughter. While the foot is still hanging on by a thread and she is hoping again to escape, Natalie walks far from the farmhouse with no support but a cane—and no consequences to her recovery or the stitches. Trauma to Natalie’s body is significant, except when it gets in the way of the plot. It is unclear how readers are to take Natalie’s body seriously when the author doesn’t.

Something else Burke doesn’t take seriously is Natalie’s religion. Natalie’s classmates at twenty-first-century Harvard casually call her Amish, Baptist, evangelical, Catholic—a running joke that makes them sound shallow and dismissive, even as it contains Burke’s admission that she has kept an important character trait out of focus. At her childhood church, grape juice was distributed at communion, and yet wine was served at the Christmas Eve church mixer. Her Romneyesque father-in-law launches an election campaign in Salt Lake City, serves alcohol at his son’s wedding, and drinks pinot grigio in his kitchen. In a moment of distress, the generally low-church Natalie recites the verbal formula for the Sign of the Cross. When she considers having an abortion, she imagines her family’s shock: “How modern of me.” Yes: The problem patriarchal Christians have with abortion, a practice they have condemned since Apostolic times, is its modernity.

“Natalie exists to get slapped around.” “The fertile will inherit the earth.”

Burke has explained to The New York Times that Natalie might be any kind of Christian, since “fundamentalism” exists in so many churches. But as written, Natalie is no kind of Christian at all, since the details of her faith are not reconcilable. Burke says the inconsistency is consistent with the lack of “intelligence … Natalie has about her own religion.” But what sense does that make? Natalie is intensely self-regarding as well as very “clever.” Her “good Christian woman” identity is central to her self-conception. Does she really have no clear idea what sort of Christian she is? “A specific thread of Christianity would really just be more distracting,” Burke says, as though making the specific signify beyond itself were not the task of an artist. The real consideration is surely coalitional: You can see in Natalie’s faith whatever Christian tradition you most despise, or all of them if your distaste is ecumenical.

About Natalie’s cleverness. The clearest proof of it is that she was called up by Harvard from the hinterlands—though a Harvard strangely located in Boston rather than Cambridge, and populated by designer-obsessed coeds who would be more at home in a state-school sorority. The prose that renders Natalie’s thoughts does not invite the attribution of cleverness. But she is subject to her creator’s limits. Burke can’t write, at least not in English. When Natalie rejects minimalism, she defines it as “a house absent of stuff.” She worries that her hopeful visions “sniffed of greed.” She lies in bed, “shoring up energy.” During that rapey sex scene, “the whites of [her] eyes travel around the room.” Elsewhere, a nanny is described as “looking up at us with a cool, unblinking stare, like a cat blinking lazily out from their sunlit perch.” Unblinking, like a cat blinking.

In the novel’s most widely quoted line, Natalie declares herself “the manic pixie American dream girl of this nation’s deepest, darkest fantasies.” Three clichés—“manic pixie dream girl,” “American dream,” “girl of our dreams”—are synthesized here. To what end? Look closely and you’ll find that Natalie is calling herself the American dream girl of America’s dreams.

Reviews in major publications contain much approbation of Burke’s “biting prose.” As the Granta scandal reminds us, our literary tastemakers either don’t know bad prose when they see it, or don’t have anything against it. The politics are what matter. This novel sniffs of fear. Its plot presumes that traditional styles of femininity lack contemporary validity, and yet the very fact that tradwives are on-trend refutes the notion that they represent anything so simple as a return to the past. Yesteryear and its overwarm reception bespeak a knowledge that progressivism is losing the future. In the book’s rather hysterical conclusion, Natalie’s grown daughters rhapsodize about “this future you prayed we would never get the chance to see”: “The people were so much nicer. The cities were so much cleaner.” (Been to New York lately?) The girls have converted from patriarchal to liberal Christianity, musing that “church might just be another word for people.” This is a retro rhetoric for a quaint future, a vision more Boomer than Zoomer.

Turning from fiction to social science, the math is obvious: The fertile will inherit the earth. To the extent that “fundamentalism” correlates with high fertility, the future is fundamentalist. More than that, the ethos of progressivism has become backward-looking. Tight control of reproduction, once an optimistic practice, now correlates with social and environmental pessimism. High fertility, a curse in 1855, is a choice in this era of IUDs and climate doomerism—a choice that presumes a future worth living in.

Confidence is charismatic. Fertility is a flex, and not just for homeschooling, homesteading Christians. In America’s cities, the numbers of children are dropping fast, due to suburbanization and falling birthrates; the only exceptions are the wealthiest neighborhoods. In those precincts, tradwife-level fertility rates are a form of conspicuous consumption, signalling a change in what the professional class thinks it means to have it all. Tradwives are a trend, and a threat, because women who have seen the future can look at a photo of a big family on a windswept hill and say, “I want that.”

Julia Yost is a senior editor of First Things.

Post-Literacy Raised the Stakes of the Odyssey Debate

John Byron Kuhner · 2026-05-26 · Original →

Post-Literacy Raised the Stakes of the Odyssey Debate John Byron Kuhner May 26, 2026

Hollywood has always taken a permissive approach to adaptations. When Sam Zimbalist, an MGM producer, wanted a new movie of Lew Wallace’s 1880 novel Ben-Hur, he summoned Karl Tunberg to his office. Tunberg had served as president of the Screen Writers Guild, and Zimbalist wanted him to do the script. Tunberg demurred, saying he knew nothing about Ben-Hur. He hadn’t even read it. Sam waved him off. “Don’t worry about that,” Sam barked. “It’s a classic! That means nobody’s read it! We can do what we want with it!”

The system generally worked, even if it meant that you couldn’t watch the movie in order to pass your test in English class. Boris Karloff’s monosyllabic monster wasn’t the Milton-analyzing literary whiz of Mary Shelley’s Frankenstein, but he was iconic all the same. The Philip K. Dick story about memory implantation featured no Martian syndicate selling air, no rebels, no vanished alien civilization, not even a single scene on Mars—but the 1990 Arnold Schwarzenegger vehicle Total Recall did it a sleazy kind of justice anyway. Producers weren’t selling faithful literary adaptations. They were selling the magic of Hollywood. There were some clunkers, but audiences bought in. If you loved Shakespeare, you were excited to try Baz Luhrmann’s Romeo + Juliet, with Leonardo DiCaprio and Claire Danes. It was freewheeling but you expected nothing else. If you loved the Iliad, you wanted to go see Troy, even if it was absurd to see Menelaus killed dishonorably by Hector a third of the way in. That was part of the fun: You had no idea what crazy thing the screenwriters would do with the story.

We are now several months into the promo for Christopher Nolan’s Odyssey. I think we can declare the old system dead. There are now substantial segments of the population who simply don’t believe Hollywood can do what it wants with an old story. We are now seeing liberties with demonstrable precedent being taken as threats to civilization itself. Nolan cast Lupita Nyong’o, a Kenyan woman, as Helen of Troy (not a major role in the book). In 1950, Orson Welles cast Eartha Kitt, an African-American singer—you’ve heard her version of “Santa Baby”—as Helen of Troy in a stage adaptation of Faust called Time Runs. No one accused Welles of trying to topple Western civilization. Denzel Washington played Don Pedro in Kenneth Branagh’s Much Ado About Nothing, and Macbeth in Joel Coen’s Macbeth. Washington is neither Spanish nor Scottish, but Shakespeare is not known to have been injured during either production.

The same pattern applies to historically inaccurate costumes, modernizing dialogue, or the film’s non-Mediterranean color palette. Not long ago this kind of criticism would have been seen as missing the point. Shakespeare’s Julius Caesar doesn’t fall apart because the conspirators are interrupted by the tolling of a clocktower’s bell. It’s anachronistic, yes—but it’s dramatic, and drama is the name of the game.

The controversy has even spread to Emily Wilson’s translation of the Odyssey, darling of the publishing world when it arrived in 2017 and still a bestseller. Nolan citing her work as an inspiration has subjected Wilson to a stream of criticism—criticism her book did not receive when it was first published. Why is this? Why do people care so much now?

It’s because screens are different. Screens are where the battle for the culture is taking place. When all Wilson had done was a translation of the Odyssey, there was no particular need to notice her. If you don’t like her translation, there are lots of others. But we are not fighting about a book anymore. In a postliterate age, the movie has a chance to be the version of the Odyssey for a generation—in a way that simply was not the case before. In earlier decades, the classics were buttressed by the schools. My ninth grade English class spent September reading the Odyssey. There were quizzes. There was a test. And then through the rest of the year we were asked to compare other books to the Odyssey.

Now the classics are buttressed by nothing. By the end of this summer, there is a good chance that there will be a substantial number of young Americans whose entire exposure to the Odyssey will be Christopher Nolan. And Emily Wilson will thereby become the mouthpiece of Homer. Lupita Nyong’o can serve as our example. She has a degree from Hampshire College. She also has a master’s degree from the vaunted Yale Drama School. This is what she said about getting the role of Helen: “I really had no idea what the Odyssey was,” she told Elle magazine. “I was like, ‘Oh, snap, I don’t know the first thing about this.’ So it was a crash course. I picked up the books and read them immediately. I have this film to thank for my Greek mythological education.” The possessor of a master’s degree from Yale—in the humanities—can have “no idea what the Odyssey was.”

This is why the stakes have become so much higher. It’s not Nolan’s Odyssey. It’s the Odyssey. It’s Homer. It’s all of Greek mythology. It’s Western civilization. The schools have failed, and people look to Hollywood to pick up the slack.

I see this in my bookshop. I sell nice copies of The Lord of the Rings to adults who describe themselves as huge Tolkien fans. They promise me that at some point they even intend to read the books. For these people, the Peter Jackson movies are Tolkien. They discuss which of the two Tolkien options they prefer: the cinematic versions or the extended versions. The books, meanwhile, make nice decorations. Personally, I hate the movies and don’t see how a lover of the books could enjoy them (and I can call on Christopher Tolkien to back me up), but most people I speak with can’t even sense how the books and movies differ. Their imaginations have been completely colonized. When they think Aragorn, they see the face of Viggo Mortensen.

Tolkien could have worse interpreters. And now, people fear the worst for Homer. Previous generations thought Hollywood adaptations were lighthearted follies; now critics are treating Nolan as a deadly enemy. There is a fear of movies which shows just how all-powerful the screen has become. We’ll see if the critics actually affect the success of the movie. Moviemaking is expensive, and depends on the collaboration of three groups of people: an elite with ideas and vision to tell a story; thousands of middle-class artisans whose skill creates the visual world the elite envision; and a supportive public whose love of spectacle provides the money for the whole enterprise. If the public rejects this film—by the director of the Batman trilogy, and starring Matt Damon and Anne Hathaway—the rules for the next historical epic may be quite different.

For there is undoubtedly an opportunity for those who want to use the arts in a propaideutic way—encouraging deeper engagement with original sources rather than riffing on them or supplanting them. Nolan may succeed in doing this despite his critics, who, let it be said, are making their claims with inadequate evidence. But he may not have even been aware that anyone expected him to be an educator instead of a filmmaker. Sam Zimbalist’s observation that no one reads the classics may be more true than ever, but his conclusion that this means they can be adapted freely is no longer obvious. No one has read the classics. For that precise reason, filmmakers may no longer be able to do what they want with them. They may need to treat their films not just as great stories which resonated with their own modern preoccupations, but as lessons in history and myth and culture. The new byword for adaptations may need to be reverence.

John Byron Kuhner is the owner of Bookmarx Books in Steubenville, Ohio.
