I agree. I hope to see this friend, and many others, in the aisles of Foodtown, where every man is a member and every woman a queen, and where there are no rules but the rule of law.

Scooter Braun’s Vision

For more than a decade, Scooter Braun was one of the most powerful people in music. When I spoke to him this week for the podcast (check it out here), I didn’t want to interview him because he knows the most famous people in the world, or because he is famous himself. Okay fine. I didn’t only want to talk to him about that. I wanted to see how he felt about new developments in the music industry, from AI-assisted songwriting to the clipping campaigns that have taken over social media and which are warping our sense of what’s popular—and real.

We talked about all the above, plus about fame, power, fandoms, and who controls culture in the digital age. At a moment when the music industry or the entertainment “machine”—the network of record labels, event companies, entertainment conglomerates, and streamers—has become a subject of skepticism and panic, I wanted to talk to the man who carved out a kingdom for himself within it. You can listen to our conversation here. Or watch it below:

Here’s What Else I’m Thinking About:

Anybody would be lucky to know the great Ben Sasse, but only a handful have the privilege of truly knowing him. This week, we published an essay from the former senator’s daughter Alex, who wrote all about the daily gifts he brought her, and how it made her who she is today. Unlike almost all 22-year-olds, she said, “My dad is the first person I text—whether about good news or bad, whether I’m overjoyed or disappointed, exhilarated or mind-numbingly bored.” It’s a beautiful reflection on fatherhood.

If there’s one book that has all the women in my life in a chokehold, it’s Belle Burden’s memoir Strangers. It’s a story of a woman whose husband betrayed her—he walked out of their shared Martha’s Vineyard home after it was revealed that he was having an affair. The other shoe: He’d gotten very rich during their marriage, and he had no intention of sharing his wealth with his ex, who’d grown up affluent but had given up her career to raise their three kids. It was the perfect, sympathetic tale, if only it were true. This week, a New Yorker investigation into Burden’s finances revealed that, in fact, she had quite the safety net and things weren’t quite what they seemed. But her fans didn’t bat an eye. My colleague Kat Rosenfield explains the psychology behind her cult following, and why Burden’s impression may just matter more than the truth.

Hating billionaires is all the rage these days, whether for colonizing Mars or the Met Gala, for flying private or complaining about taxes. The idea is that surely the rich and powerful only got their wealth by cheating. Marian Tupy offers a counterpoint in The Wall Street Journal, and it’s that maybe Jeff Bezos made his money because he deserved it. Read her piece and come back to it the next time you do all of your grocery shopping for the week in five minutes, from home.

AI may be incredible at synthesizing information, but, as Sam Kriss argued this week, it’s awful at writing. AI slop is everywhere lately, and our sentences are suffering for it. Reading it, Kriss says, has become “viscerally disgusting,” like “a cold shudder like someone’s poured jelly down the back of my neck.“ His piece is a welcome antidote to the terrible, horrible, no good, very bad AI words—so don’t ask ChatGPT to summarize it.

The Free Press earns a commission from any purchases made through all book links in this article.

The Free Press is for free thinkers. Send this to someone who values open debate and curiosity.

There's No Escaping the Rot in This Justice Department

Michelle Goldberg · 2026-05-29 · Original →

The U.S. Attorney's Office for the Northern District of Illinois used to be considered one of the best federal prosecutors' offices in the country, but the last week has shown that Donald Trump's administration is driving it off the rails.

First, an egregious attempt to prosecute protesters fell apart amid serious allegations of prosecutorial malfeasance. Then, this week, we learned that the office is investigating payments made to the law firm representing E. Jean Carroll, who successfully sued Donald Trump for millions after accusing him of sexual abuse. The former home of the legendary prosecutor Patrick Fitzgerald — known for going after both Al Qaeda and the Dick Cheney aide Scooter Libby — is devolving into just another squalid arm of Trump's vengeance campaign. Which raises the question: How long can decent people continue to work for such a corrupted institution?

In the face of Trump's attempts to turn the Department of Justice into his legal Praetorian Guard, many career employees seem to think they can keep their heads down and wait out their new masters. "We are the mole people now," one department employee told the New York Times reporter Devlin Barrett in his forthcoming book, "The Department of Revenge: How Trump Took Control of American Justice." But at a certain point, it may be impossible to serve both the cause of justice and an unjust government.

If Trump didn't manufacture scandals on such an industrial scale, the case that collapsed last week in Chicago would have been a huge story. It stemmed from a September protest outside an ICE detention center in suburban Broadview in which demonstrators briefly surrounded a government vehicle and allegedly damaged it. A month after the incident, six local politicians and activists who'd been part of the crowd, including a Democratic congressional candidate, Kat Abughazaleh, were indicted on charges including felony conspiracy, for which they faced years in prison.

The case of the Broadview Six, as their supporters call them, was a naked attempt to criminalize protest, and prosecutors needed three grand jury sessions just to get an indictment. The real scandal, however, was what went on inside the grand jury room.

Apparently, after initially failing to secure an indictment, prosecutors excused grand jurors who rejected the government's case before trying again. One career prosecutor, the assistant U.S. attorney Sheri Mecklenburg, was accused of "vouching," essentially leveraging her own credibility with the grand jurors to convince them to trust her evidence, a prohibited practice. Then, when Judge April Perry of Federal District Court asked to review grand jury transcripts, prosecutors provided her with redacted versions that covered up their apparent misconduct.

Last week, after Perry finally reviewed the unredacted transcripts, she said she was "incredibly shocked" by what she'd discovered, exclaiming, "I have never seen the types of prosecutorial behavior before a grand jury that I saw in those transcripts." All charges against the Broadview Six have been dismissed, and defense attorneys in the case are now seeking sanctions that would let their clients recover some of their legal fees. Mecklenburg had since been reassigned to work with Democrats on the Judiciary Committee; last week Senator Dick Durbin, a Democrat, announced she'd been let go.

In response to the debacle, Andrew Boutros, the Trump-appointed U.S. attorney for the Northern District, who himself was accused of having personal contact with the grand jury, has announced "sweeping reforms to internal grand jury practices and disclosures." His announcement brought to mind the hot dog meme from "I Think You Should Leave": "We're all trying to find the guy who did this."

One puzzling thing about the case is that Mecklenburg was a longtime career prosecutor, not a MAGA apparatchik. Yet she seems to have been willing to behave in ways that have astonished legal observers.

"You really do wonder about the ethical environment that these people are operating under, if this is what we're seeing," said Andrew Weissmann, the former deputy to Robert Mueller and author of the new book "Liar's Kingdom." Christopher Parente, a defense attorney for one of the Broadview Six, spent almost a decade as a prosecutor in the Northern District. Now, he said, "I'm worried that no one's ever going to trust an indictment that comes out of this office again."

The day after I spoke to Parente, news broke that Boutros's office has opened a criminal investigation related to E. Jean Carroll's case. The inquiry is reportedly looking into a nonprofit run by the Democratic billionaire Reid Hoffman, which made a grant to Carroll's lawyer's firm. "The investigation, which is expected to look at statements Carroll made during a deposition, could also morph into a criminal perjury probe against the 82-year-old columnist," reported The Washington Post.

Hoffman's payment has been public knowledge for years; The Times reported on it in 2023, and Trump's lawyers tried and failed to make an issue of it during his trial and appeal. It therefore seems fair to assume that the investigation into it is yet another attempt by the administration to go after Trump's enemies.

The White House's demands for political prosecutions "are putting career prosecutors into very, very difficult situations," Barrett told me, forcing people to constantly ask where their red line is. Some in the Justice Department, he said, make a "Lord of the Rings" analogy. "Most of the people there can still do their jobs well and do good, meaningful work in law enforcement until the Eye of Sauron turns to you," he said, meaning you get pulled into one of Trump's vendettas. The problem is that even before that happens, you're still working for the orcs.

↩ Back to article summary · ↪ See Claude's response · ↩ Back to standalones table

What Is the Current US Healthcare Situation?

Matt Bruenig · 2026-05-26 · Original →

The 2028 policy primary has kicked off. In the realm of healthcare, the Searchlight Institute and the Center for American Progress recently put out white papers proposing tweaks to the system. We will likely see many more of these proposals in the coming months, including from the left. To best understand these competing agendas, it is useful to take a step back and take account of what our current healthcare system actually looks like and what sort of problems it has. This is what I aim to do in this post.

  1. The System at a Glance

It is difficult to capture the US healthcare system in a single diagram, but the following illustration is a good place to start.

Money initially flows from households in the form of taxes, foregone wages, and premiums into a middle layer of governments, employers, and insurers. Money then bounces around within that middle layer until some portion of it eventually pops out the other end and makes its way to healthcare providers like doctors and hospitals. Money also flows directly from households to providers in the form of self-pay, copays, coinsurance, and deductibles, i.e. out-of-pocket expenditures.

This system is tremendously expensive. The United States spends over 17 percent of its GDP on healthcare while peer nations spend one-third less than that.

This higher price is not because the United States uses much more healthcare. According to the OECD data, which is a little bit old, healthcare utilization in the US does not, in aggregate, differ that much from peer nations.

What does differ is how much money the United States spends per unit of medical care. This excess spending leaks out in the form of needless administrative expenses, high drug prices, and provider rents.

  1. Households

One way to understand all of the problems with this system is to move from node to node in the diagram above, starting with households. In one way or another, households ultimately suffer from all of the consequences of the system, but in this section I will focus on a few of the direct harms.

2a. Uninsurance

Despite spending much more than peer nations on healthcare, the United States fails to even formally insure its entire population. Most estimates of the uninsurance rate either provide an annual uninsurance rate, which defines someone as uninsured only if they were uninsured for the entire calendar year, or a point-in-time uninsurance rate, which typically asks people if they are currently uninsured. The following graph uses the Survey of Income and Program Participation to show these two figures.

These annual and point-in-time figures can lead you to believe that uninsurance is not that common. But this is not the case. Using the Survey of Income and Program Participation (SIPP), we can follow individuals for four years to see what percent of them faced at least one month of uninsurance over that period. The below graph shows this figure for all ages (solid lines), for all ages except the elderly (dashed lines), and for all ages except the elderly and children (dotted lines).

In a typical four year period, around 1 in 5 people experience uninsurance at some point. Medicare and Medicaid keep uninsurance down for the elderly and children. Thus, for non-elderly adults, around 1 in 4 experience uninsurance.

2b. Insurance Churn

One of the basic design principles of the US healthcare system is year-long health plans. The idea is that individuals will generally enroll in a plan during an annual open-enrollment period and stick with it for the next year. In reality, insurance churn — the name given to moving in and out of different insurance types and plans — is very prevalent, even within a calendar year.

Outside of the elderly, healthcare in America is just a constant nightmare of insurance churn. Lose or change your job? Insurance churn. Get your hours cut to part-time? Insurance churn. Employer decides to change or eliminate their health benefits? Insurance churn. Grow your income beyond Medicaid or ACA subsidy cutoffs? Insurance churn. Turn 26 years old? Insurance churn. Graduate from college? Insurance churn. Primary policyholder dies? Insurance churn. Divorce? Insurance churn. Move to a different area? Insurance churn.

In addition to this churning being a huge hassle, it also collides with other basic design principles of the US healthcare system. Most significantly, it collides with the idea of annual deductibles and out-of-pocket maximums. Someone who is forced to switch insurance in the middle of the year sees their deductible and out-of-pocket maximum start over from zero even if they have already spent thousands of dollars towards those limits in that year.

2c. Healthcare Affordability

Even if you have stable insurance, that does not necessarily mean that healthcare is always affordable. Insurance plans typically come with cost-sharing rules that make it difficult for many to actually use the healthcare system.

Healthcare affordability problems can present as foregone treatment, medical debt, or large bills. One particularly alarming way to see the effect of large bills is to look at out-of-pocket medical spending across the SPM income distribution. SPM income, which is used to calculate the supplemental poverty measure, deducts out-of-pocket health spending from each family's income. In general, this sort of spending rises with income. But there is an exception to this: at the very bottom of the income distribution, out-of-pocket health spending is massive.

That spike on the left is being driven by people whose healthcare bills were so large that they completely wiped out their annual income. One upshot of this graph is that, within the supplemental poverty metric, the poorest people each year are overwhelmingly people who got hit with a catastrophic medical bill.

  1. Governments

Federal and state governments, especially the former, are responsible for structuring the whole system and so, in some sense, they are to blame for everything. However, in this section, I will focus on tax expenditures and the outsourcing of Medicaid and Medicare to private insurance companies.

3a. Tax Exclusion for Employer-Sponsored Health Insurance

Most know that the federal government finances Medicare and Medicaid. But less understood is the fact that the federal government also finances a large portion of employer-sponsored insurance (ESI) premiums as well. This financing is done through the tax code: ESI premiums are exempt from income tax, Medicare tax, and Social Security tax.

One consequence of this policy is that the federal subsidy for employer-sponsored insurance varies based on each tax unit's income. For the lowest earners, this federal subsidy covers only 14.2 percent of ESI premiums. For the highest earners, it covers 41.9 percent.

The same thing is true of the tax subsidies for Health Savings Accounts (HSAs) and Health Flexible Spending Accounts (FSAs) as well. For every $100 a low-earner places into one of those accounts, the federal government kicks back $14.20. For a very high-earner, the federal government kicks back $41.90.

3b. FSAs and HSAs

FSAs and HSAs, which allow workers to funnel "pretax money" into accounts that they can then use on certain medical expenditures, have additional problems beyond unequal federal subsidies.

FSA users are subject to strict use-it-or-lose-it rules that require them to forfeit any money they fail to use by the end of the plan year. This induces some FSA users to engage in wasteful medical spending near the end of their plan year purely to try to avoid forfeiture. On the other end of things, either due to a lack of need or forgetfulness, around half of all FSA users fail to use all of their money and thus face a forfeiture. These forfeitures, which are paid out to employers, cost workers around $4 billion every year. Thus, through the FSA program, workers gift about $4 billion of their own wages to their employers every year.

HSAs, which can only be used if an individual is enrolled in a high-deductible health plan (HDHP), are not subject to use-it-or-lose-it rules like FSAs are. HSAs are perpetual, like a 401k or an IRA. The perpetual existence of HSAs has led certain rich, savvy people to use them as backdoor tax-advantaged retirement accounts. Unlike regular retirement accounts, which get a tax advantage at contribution or distribution, HSAs enjoy a one-of-a-kind triple tax advantage: contributions, investment growth, and distributions are all untaxed. On the top end, then, HSAs are diluting the federal tax base in a way policymakers never anticipated or intended.

On the other end of things, the perpetual existence of HSAs has created a problem of orphaned accounts. Individuals often wind up with an HSA because it came along with an employer insurance option they selected. Their employer contributes small sums of money to the HSA each month, e.g. $50, but the employee never realizes they have an HSA or forgets about it, especially after they leave the company. Once an account is orphaned and contributions to it cease, whatever financial institution is responsible for the account will eventually drain the balance to $0 with maintenance fees. Thus, in the same way that FSAs facilitate the gifting of worker wages to employers, HSAs facilitate the gifting of worker wages to financial institutions.

In recent years, companies like Truemed have emerged to facilitate HSA and FSA fraud. Traditionally, HSA and FSA money could only be used to purchase certain medical items. Truemed has upended this by teaming up with companies that sell products that have some kind of marginal relationship to health and issuing Letters of Medical Necessity (LMN) to their customers so that they can pay for them with their HSAs or FSAs. For instance, Garmin sells this $3,200 watch and advertises that Truemed will issue an LMN for it. This would allow a very high-earner to essentially get a $1,300 discount on the watch, paid for by the federal government.

3c. Medicare Advantage and Medicaid Managed Care

Colloquially, "Medicare" and "Medicaid" are often used to refer to the traditional fee-for-service (FFS) plans run by federal and state governments. But Medicare and Medicaid have undergone substantial privatizations in recent years. No longer are these programs in which taxes flow through governments to medical providers. Nowadays, there is typically a private insurer sitting in between the government and the medical provider.

Adding a private insurer layer inside these programs is pointless and costly. Indeed, essentially every major health insurer (Kaiser, UnitedHealth, Cigna, Humana, Aetna, Anthem) that participates in Medicare Advantage has been targeted at one point or another for fraud, typically a kind of reimbursement fraud where they overstate the health risks of their customers to collect huge sums of money from the federal government.

  1. Employers

One could imagine a hypothetical corporatist welfare system in which workers and their dependents received insurance through collective funds run by employers while everyone else received insurance through the state. This would not be a great system, and no country — not even the most corporatist-friendly like Switzerland — does this. But one could at least imagine it.

Sometimes, it seems like this is what the US is going for. But rather than have employer insurance run through some kind of collective fund where all the employers contributed some kind of predictable amount scaled to their payroll, we instead make it the individual responsibility of each particular employer to set up health insurance. Big companies often do this through self-funded plans that they hire a third-party to administer while others do it through contracts with private insurers.

Immediately, some obvious problems emerge from this model. The first is that the average cost of insurance varies from $9,300 for a single plan to $27,000 for a family plan. For someone who works 2,000 hours per year, this is equal to about $4.65/hr for the single plan or $13.50/hr for a family plan. For someone working 30 hours a week, which is when the employer mandate to provide insurance kicks in, the single plan is $6/hr while the family plan is $17.30/hr. For low-wage employers, this sort of head tax would be immense, especially given that the federal subsidy for employer-provided insurance is relatively small for low-earners, as noted in section 3a above.

To avoid this hit, low-wage employers create schedules that ensure that their workers get less than 30 hours a week, thereby avoiding the employer mandate threshold. This is rational for employers, but it wreaks havoc on the lives of workers who cannot get full-time hours and leads to an underutilization of labor factors in the economy. Even employers that might want to give their workers full-time hours and absorb the cost of health insurance end up having to think twice about putting themselves at such a disadvantage relative to their competitors. Entire sectors of the US economy end up with labor models that are completely distorted around the employer insurance system.

Another problem with this model is that employers end up spending some of their time organizing welfare benefits, whether they are good at it or not. This is true of all employer-based welfare systems, including pensions, leave, child care flexible savings accounts, life insurance, and so on down the line. Creating independent welfare bureaucracies within each corporation is redundant and inefficient and pulls business managers away from actual production.

Workers also suffer from this employer-based insurance model. Employer health insurance directly and indirectly chews into worker wages. Potential raises get reduced or eliminated altogether to finance escalating health insurance costs. Employer-sponsored insurance increases job-switching frictions, which makes it harder for workers to raise their wage by taking advantage of outside options. ESI increases the economic penalties associated with getting fired or striking, which makes it harder for workers to raise their wage through organizing and collective bargaining.

  1. Private Insurance

Private insurers keep around 15 to 20 percent of every dollar that is paid to them. Traditional Medicare does the same thing private insurers do but only keeps around 2 percent. The gap between the two is pure waste. It serves no purpose. It adds no value. It is rent. It is a scam.

Private health insurers have always been an unnecessary menace in the system, but, after Obamacare, they became almost comically unnecessary.

In other sectors, insurers assess risk and charge premiums accordingly. Indeed, this is the general description of what the business of insurance actually is. However, Obamacare largely banned health insurers from doing this because it led to sky-high premiums for people with pre-existing conditions and certain other kinds of price discrimination, e.g. on the basis of gender.

In other sectors, insurers have a lot of leeway to determine what their products will actually cover. In theory, this leads to competition and consumer choice. Obamacare largely eliminated this flexibility as well by requiring all insurers to cover certain essential health benefits without lifetime or annual caps.

In other sectors, the amount that insurers charge for overhead and profit is not set by law but is theoretically determined by market competition. The Obamacare Medical Loss Ratio (MLR) rule effectively caps overhead and profit at 15 to 20 percent of revenue. This rule also reduces the incentive of the insurance sector as a whole to keep medical costs down: the higher the medical costs, the more profit insurers are allowed to book.

So American health insurers are largely not involved in assessing risk, establishing benefit packages, or even establishing their own prices. They are essentially third-party administrators of the Obamacare-regulated system. That is better than the alternative of having them actually act like insurers, but it calls into question why they should receive so much money for administrative work that the government can do at a fraction of the cost.

  1. Providers

Finally, we get to the providers. For the providers, we have two main problems: rents and administration.

The United States pays more for drugs than any other country in the world. This is partially a function of the US patent system but also separately a function of a policy decision to pay that much. Other countries, which also respect US drug patents, force drug companies to charge much lower prices whether through formal price controls or monopsonistic bargaining. The US does neither and the drug companies are happy to charge massive prices far in excess of what would be necessary to induce them to sell the drug (i.e. rents), thereby driving US healthcare costs up.

Other healthcare providers, like doctors and hospitals, do much the same, primarily by taking advantage of the privately insured. But these other healthcare providers also have uniquely high costs that are associated with our fractured multi-payer health insurance system. Specifically, provider-side administrative costs are 19 percent, which is more than 50 percent higher than they would be in a single-payer system (12 percent).

Cutting these provider rents and administrative costs would save Americans a tremendous amount of money every year.

Conclusion

The left's answer to this is, by now, pretty well known. By collapsing the middle layer into a single public insurer offering a single public health plan with a high actuarial value, virtually all of these problems could be solved.

A universal public plan would eliminate uninsurance and insurance churn. A high actuarial value would eliminate the affordability problems of high out-of-pocket cost-sharing. Employer-sponsored insurance, FSAs, and HSAs would be eliminated along with the unequal tax subsidies that help fund them. Private insurers, including those operating through the Medicare Advantage and Medicaid Managed Care systems, would be eliminated, which would directly recoup the excess administrative costs of private insurers and indirectly recoup the excess provider-side administrative costs that are caused by our multi-payer system. Employers would be liberated from the burden of providing health insurance as well as the employer mandate rules that push them to immiserate low-wage workers by underscheduling them. Provider-side rents would be neutralized by monopsonistic bargaining or price-setting.

To the extent that this sort of ideal is not currently achievable or deemed too disruptive, things short of this that make steps in this direction might also be worth considering. In the past, I have endorsed Medicare for Kids as a logical gradualist stepping point. In subsequent pieces, I will elaborate on that as well as the current proposals offered by more centrist policy outfits.

↩ Back to article summary · ↪ See Claude's response · ↩ Back to standalones table

Do more Europeans die of summer heat than Americans die of guns?

Hannah Ritchie · 2026-05-28 · Original →

The chart below has been doing the rounds on social media. The headline is really about how poorly Europe protects people from heat. But the flip is also true for America and guns. European policy protects people against guns, but not heat; the US, the opposite.

As I explain later, I think this comparison is a bit silly, but sympathise with the overall sentiment: Europe is not doing a good job of protecting people from heat. Things don’t have to be this bad; it’s a choice.

But, unfortunately, the chart has several issues.

The heat death numbers for Europe are modelled as “excess deaths” during summer months; this attempts to capture people dying from a range of health conditions — cardiovascular disease, stroke, and others — earlier than they would have in more optimal temperatures. This is a common way researchers measure heat deaths (or cold, for that matter). I’ve done a deep dive on these methods previously.

But the US number isn’t based on this type of modelling; it’s based on heat deaths recorded on death certificates. If you used death certificate figures for Europe, they’d be far lower.

It also uses European Union figures for gun deaths, but a broader definition of Europe for heat deaths.1

I thought I’d have a quick go at making a better version. These numbers are still not perfect, but hopefully a closer comparison.

Here are some of the changes:

  • I’ve used more comparable excess heat death figures for the US (details in the footnote), so we’re not relying on death certificate records.2 This is the annual average between 2000 and 2020 and for adults aged 25 to 84 (it’s not a perfect comparison, but the best I could do with available data).3
  • I’ve taken the average of 2022 to 2024 for Europe, so we’re not catching an “irregular” year and comparing it to a more average year in the US.4
  • Created one version that consistently uses EU-27 figures for both guns and heat.
  • A second version that uses the broader European definition in the heat deaths paper, sourced in the original chart: the 26 EU countries (minus Malta), plus the UK, Switzerland, Bosnia and Herzegovina, Montenegro, Norway, and Serbia (so 32 in total).5
  • Shown the breakdown of gun deaths by homicide, suicide, and other causes (which is small but includes unintentional injury and deaths by law enforcement).6

Here’s the comparison for countries in the European Union only.

And now using the broader “Europe” definition.

Does it change the conclusions much? Not really. But hopefully it provides a slightly more accurate comparison. And I think the breakdown of gun deaths provides more useful context.

What about per capita?

As always in a comment section, people asked: “What if we adjust for population?”

Here’s the same data adjusted for rates per 100,000 people in the total population7

It does actually change the order a bit. Gun deaths in the US are now slightly larger than European heat death rates. But to me, it doesn’t matter much for the conclusion.

As I mentioned, I found the initial framing of guns and heat as a contest to be a bit silly — they’re not substitutes. Europe is doing a bad job of protecting itself from life-threatening heat. That the US has disproportionately high rates of gun deaths compared to other rich countries is also bad. The two are not mutually exclusive.

But it does highlight something important about status quo bias. Both places take the status quo as a given: a high-mortality situation in one domain that they’d never accept in another.

Subscribe now

1

The definition of Europe, to most people, will seem a bit random: it's the EU-27 minus Malta, plus the UK, Switzerland, Bosnia and Herzegovina, Montenegro, Norway, and Serbia.

2

This data comes from a recent paper from Salerno et al. (2026), which uses the common minimum-mortality temperature (MMT) approach.

It estimated that between 2000 and 2020, there was an average of around 6,100 heat-related deaths in the US per year. This study covered 92% of the population, so I’ve rounded it to around 6,500 to account for 100% population coverage.

This is not a perfect comparison as it compares to a 2024-specific figure for Europe. But I couldn’t find 2024 data for the US using this methodology.

Some studies find lower and higher US estimates than this. Zhao et al. (2021) estimate a much higher figure of around 18,000. Chu et al. (2025) estimate lower figures of 3,000 to 4,000.

de Oliveira Salerno, P. R. V., Estrada-Mendizabal, R. J., Chen, Z., Dazard, J. E., Nasir, K., Requia, W., ... & Deo, S. V. (2026). Social Vulnerability and Mortality Attributable to Non-Optimal Temperature in the United States: A County-Level Ecological Analysis. Current Problems in Cardiology, 103260.

Zhao, Q., Guo, Y., Ye, T., Gasparrini, A., Tong, S., Overcenco, A., ... & Li, S. (2021). Global, regional, and national burden of mortality associated with non-optimal ambient temperatures from 2000 to 2019: a three-stage modelling study. The Lancet Planetary Health, 5(7), e415-e425.

Chu, L., Dubrow, R., & Chen, K. (2025). Heat-and cold-related mortality burden in the US from 2000 to 2020. JAMA Network Open, 8(11), e2542269.

3

Heat-related deaths tend to be concentrated in older individuals, so not having data for those aged 85+ may still slightly understate heat-related mortality in the US.

4

This doesn’t change the conclusion much: estimates were for 67,873in 2022, 50,798 in 2023, and 62,775 in 2024.

This gives an annual average of 60,500.

5

Janoš, T., Quijal-Zamorano, M., Shartova, N., Gallo, E., Méndez Turrubiates, R. F., Denisse Beltrán Barrón, N., ... & Ballester, J. (2025). Heat-related mortality in Europe during 2024 and health emergency forecasting to reduce preventable deaths. Nature Medicine, 31(12), 4065-4074.

6

European gun death figures are estimated by combining several publications. Eurostat does not publish a single aggregate figure for firearm deaths at the EU-27 level.

Firearm suicides are estimated at around 4,300/year from Eurostat total suicide figures and the Flemish Peace Institute factsheet’s estimate that 9% of EU suicides involve firearms.

Firearm homicides are estimated at around 800 per year from IHME Global Burden of Disease (2019) and Eurostat/WHO-based mortality data.

Other firearm deaths (accidents, undetermined, legal intervention) at around 450 per year.

For the broader “Europe” definition, numbers from the UK, Switzerland, Norway, Serbia, Bosnia and Herzegovina, and Montenegro are estimated from national sources.

The original source used data from the Flemish Peace Institute's factsheet Firearms and Deaths by Firearms in the EU (Duquet & Van Alstein, 2015), which is a bit older, and has slightly higher figures.

It is estimated that approximately 6,700 firearm deaths occur per year in the EU, comprising roughly 5,000 suicides, 1,000 homicides, and 700 other firearm deaths. This is an average based on 2000–2012 data; the more recent IHME Global Burden of Disease estimates suggest this figure has fallen a bit.

7

If you were to calculate rates based on specific age groups (for example, only those particularly vulnerable to heat deaths, or age groups with highest gun mortality rates) then you’d get different results.

How Much Has Shale Gas Saved U.S. Consumers?

Tyler Cowen · 2026-05-29 · Original →

It may seem like a distant memory now, but as of the mid-2000s, U.S. natural gas production had been flat for a decade, and the U.S. was importing liquefied natural gas (LNG), with plans to import much more. Then shale gas happened. Advances in hydraulic fracturing and horizontal drilling caused U.S. natural gas production to increase significantly, and the U.S. went from being a net importer of natural gas to being the world’s largest exporter. This paper calculates how much shale gas has saved U.S. natural gas consumers. Using price differences between the United States, Europe and Japan, we calculate that U.S. natural gas consumers have saved $3.1-$4.3 trillion between 2007 and 2025, equivalent to $164-$227 billion annually. Access to low-price U.S. natural gas has been particularly valuable during major supply shocks such as the war in Ukraine, and the benefits of shale gas have been experienced broadly across sectors and states.

That is from a new NBER working paper by Lucas W. Davis.

The post How Much Has Shale Gas Saved U.S. Consumers? appeared first on Marginal REVOLUTION.

↩ Back to article summary · ↪ See Claude's response · ↩ Back to standalones table

Are we undermeasuring inflation for lower earners?

Tyler Cowen · 2026-05-29 · Original →

We document a new source of fluctuations in inflation inequality. When the cost of upstream inputs rises, varieties within a product category tend to have similar absolute price increases. However, the same absolute price increase constitutes a larger percentage change for low-price products, resulting in excess inflation at the low end (“cheapflation”). Since low-income households tend to buy lower-priced varieties, the inflation rates they face are disproportionately sensitive to upstream costs. Using data on food-at-home purchases, we show that this mechanism generates cycles in inflation inequality and excessive volatility in inflation for low-income households relative to high-income households. This channel parsimoniously accounts for observed fluctuations in inflation inequality over time, including surges in cheapflation and inflation inequality during both the Great Recession and the 2021–2023 post-pandemic inflation. Official statistics mask these within-category differences in inflation and thus understate the differences in inflation experienced by low- and high-income households by 70–90 percent. We provide evidence that this mechanism applies to a range of consumption categories beyond food at home. The same mechanism also leads to systematic differences in inflation across cities and import price inflation across countries in response to nationwide and global cost shocks.

That is from a new NBER working paper by Kunal Sangani.

The post Are we undermeasuring inflation for lower earners? appeared first on Marginal REVOLUTION.

How to Eat Well on the Cheap

Scott Lincicome · 2026-05-28 · Original →

UNLOCKED DISPATCH MARKETS

How to Eat Well on the Cheap From someone who knows. Scott Lincicome / May 28, 2026

You're reading Dispatch Markets, a weekly newsletter on economics featuring Scott Lincicome, Kyla Scanlon, Karl Smith, and Marian Tupy. To access more Dispatch reporting and analysis, become a member today.

While you were enjoying your (rainy) holiday weekend, an online brouhaha erupted over a viral clip of Shark Tank's Kevin O'Leary grousing about young workers "making $70 grand a year spending $28 for lunch" instead of investing the money like they should. As Dispatch contributing writer Jeremiah Johnson astutely noted at The Argument, the online discussion quickly devolved into your standard generational battle. Oldsters mocked Gen Z workers for frittering away their nest eggs on DoorDashed avocado toast and bubble tea. The Youths—and their populist online champions—replied that The Boomers Just Don't Understand: $28 workday lunches are common, if not required today, because food is just really expensive and cooking decent, healthy meals is just too hard and time-consuming (while also being expensive).

As a proud member of Gen X, I have no interest in picking a side in yet another boomer/zoomer melee. Yet I'm also someone who loves to eat, is moderately health-conscious, spent much of his 20s as a dirt-poor think tanker and/or student, and thus has something more constructive to contribute to this common debate: extensive experience in living on a slender budget and eating pretty darn well. With intern season starting up soon in Washington, D.C., and elsewhere, it's a perfect time to share this wisdom with the underpaid souls that might need it. So, in a departure from my usual fare (get it?) here at The Dispatch, I'll spend today with some quick, easy guidelines for eating cheap, healthy meals on the go—if you or someone you know is inclined to do so but unsure how to do it.

Cheap Eats

An Aldi store on February 28, 2025, in San Diego. (Photo by Kevin Carter/Getty Images)

Gen Z's delivery preference is a unique (and expensive) choice.

Before we get to that, however, a few initial points are in order. First, as Johnson reviews, myriad surveys show that Gen Z adults do, in fact, spend more on food away from home than every other generation—a lot more:

Johnson also shows on Twitter that Gen Z's unique mealtime spending habits are generally not, as some have claimed, forced on them because they're too busy working to cook at home. In reality, surveys show that people in their mid-20s and 30s have plenty of free time but just use it in, ahem, different ways.

On the other hand, Gen Z workers also have more money to spend. As the American Enterprise Institute's Scott Winship reminds us, for example, zoomer households have higher incomes in their early years than previous generations did at the same age:

Drilling down to just median wages, as Cato Institute adjunct scholar Jeremy Horpedahl did last week, reveals a similar Gen Z premium:

Surveys also show that zoomers are building wealth (though it's admittedly early) and aren't financially miserable. At some level, therefore, many Gen Z mealtime spending habits are simply a matter of choice: Yes, they eat more delivery food than we oldsters do, but they also have more money to spend on such things and may simply prefer to devote their finite resources to doing something other than cooking. That choice might seem insane to a once-poor Gen Xer like me, but—unless the kids start demanding federal DoorDash subsidies or something—who am I to judge?

Nevertheless, last week's online debate and my own discussions revealed a good number of young people who are on a tight budget, seeking to save more money, and intimidated by home-based meal prep. Some, it seems, are also unaware of the substantial savings that brown-bagging can generate, so let's start with that. At $28 per day, for example, a young worker with a $70,000 annual salary, three weeks of vacation, and the regular U.S. holidays would spend roughly $6,580 each year (approximately 235 working days)—or roughly 12 percent of his take-home pay—on just workday lunches. For reference, the USDA estimates that a "thrifty" adult male in his 20s will spend a little under $3,800 this year on all food, while his above-median ("moderate") counterpart would spend around $4,750 for the same thing.

That's almost $2,000 less than our hypothetical zoomer's lunch tab. But on all food. For the entire year.

Even if $28 is extreme, the lesson overall remains the same, albeit at slightly lower daily dollar amounts: Young workers willing to cook at home and bring their lunch to work can probably save thousands of dollars each year as compared to their meal-outsourcing colleagues—money the former can use to invest in their retirement, pay down student loans, save for a down payment on a house, or just spend on fun things like a nice vacation.

Life's all about trade-offs, friends, and this is one of them.

Just as importantly, the savings young workers enjoy by avoiding daily food delivery will probably increase further in the future. As we've discussed, the grocery biz is an intensely competitive, low-margin industry, while food preparation and delivery are more labor-intensive and have more middlemen taking a cut. Thus, while all food is more expensive today than it was prior to the pandemic, separate USDA data show that the pain is more acute for food away from home—precisely where zoomers are (apparently) shopping. In fact, recent income gains have now canceled out pandemic-era grocery inflation for the average American, but the relative cost of eating out continues to rise:

Thus, for those interested in saving more and spending less, developing solid mealtime spending habits is a good place to start and will likely pay off even more in the future. So, let's talk a little about how to do it.

Some guidelines for eating well on the cheap.

For the purposes of our discussion today, I'll assume that our young professionals work standard 40-hour weeks and live in a house or apartment with the usual kitchen setup: oven/stove combo, refrigerator-freezer, microwave, and a few typical cooking wares. Obviously, not everyone fits this description, but most do—and most of what comes next can be adapted to other working/living arrangements (though a normal fridge-freezer is a necessity). To get the most out of your kitchen, I also recommend making a relatively small, upfront investment in a few additional basics that you'll use regularly: 1) a modest spice rack with a good assortment of basic spices like oregano and cumin; 2) large containers of salt, black pepper, garlic salt, red pepper flakes, chili powder, lemon juice, soy sauce, olive oil, and canola oil; 3) an assortment of reuseable food containers; and 4) a cheap, multifunction cooker (slow, pressure, etc.). Based on my quick skim of Amazon, you can get all of this for less than $150 total—i.e., the cost of six delivery lunches. Unlike the lunches, however, this spending will last you for months, greatly expand your recipe arsenal, and save you a ton over the longer term.

Another essential issue is where you shop because prices can vary dramatically across different stores—and not just at notoriously pricey specialty stores or bougie supermarkets like Whole Foods (aka "Whole Paycheck"). This price differential can mean big savings on groceries each week and, by extension, even greater savings versus eating delivery food all the time. Earlier this year, in fact, the folks at Consumer Reports compared a representative basket of dozens of everyday grocery items at national and regional supermarkets, warehouse clubs Costco and BJ's, and longstanding price leader Walmart (their baseline in the charts below). The results are striking: The difference between the highest- and lowest-priced mainstream grocer in each city examined was more than 33 percent—and the differential was even more significant when throwing in warehouse clubs and pricey specialty grocers.

Thus, by simply choosing to shop at a warehouse club or a cheaper store like Aldi or Walmart (or a regional comparable), you can save big bucks compared with buying the same things at national chains like Kroger or Safeway. (And don't even get me started on Whole Foods!)

A little napkin math using the national averages above shows how these savings (or costs) really add up each year. According to the USDA's latest figures, for example, a single adult on the "moderate" budget will spend around $85 per week on groceries. If they regularly spent that amount at Harris Teeter or Albertsons—perfectly nice supermarkets but nothing fancy—and then switched to Lidl, they'd save $1,179 each year. If they went to Costco, they'd save even more: $1,571 after paying for the $65 annual membership fee. (The awesome Costco fashion is just gravy.) Families, of course, would see even bigger annual savings because they spend more on food each week—savings not from buying cheaper stuff but from simply shopping at a cheaper place.

Of course, not everyone has a Costco or Lidl/Aldi nearby, and regional or dietary peculiarities might change your personal rankings a bit. The main point, however, remains: Almost everybody has cheaper options within a short commute of where they live (or even online, given Amazon's push into offering price-competitive groceries)—all they have to do is actually look. The optimal American grocery experience™ likely involves a combination of cheap/warehouse places, online shopping (I love my Amazon subscription for coffee), and some high-end or specialty stores for certain items or occasions. Regardless of the precise mix, however, the Consumer Reports data and decades of my lived cheapskate experience show that just taking a few minutes to consider where you shop will matter a lot—especially when you're young and poor.

Choosing what to buy matters too, and—without getting too deep into the recipe weeds—there are a few useful guidelines. First, always make a grocery list and never shop hungry, as doing the opposite will inevitably result in a cart full of things you don't need or even want—and stuff that's worse for you, too. (No, really, it's science.)

Second, make regular bulk purchases of flexible, low-cost staples that can be used in a variety of dishes and stored for weeks in your cupboard, fridge, or freezer for cooking later on. In my single youth, for example, I'd go to my nearby Shoppers Food Warehouse or less-close Costco a couple times a month to buy hilarious quantities of eggs, peanuts, chicken breasts, ground beef (80/20), bacon, butter, Dijon mustard, frozen broccoli and green beans, fresh spinach, canned black beans, rice or couscous, cheese, and a few other essentials that I knew I'd use in various meals over the next few weeks. (Dry pasta, of course, fits here too, but I didn't do that much because I was and remain a snooty carbophobe.) These regulars can then be supplemented with a rotation of perishable, seasonal, or on-sale items that can pair with your staples: bread, sliced ham (it's much more flexible than turkey), onions and garlic, vinegars, sauces, etc. And, finally, you can and should splurge on the occasional treat—whatever floats your particular boat—from your go-to stores or a pricier alternative.

Third, avoid prepackaged, precooked, or precut versions of things like chicken, fish, vegetables, salads, melon, potatoes, beans, rice, etc.—because they will always be more expensive than the same things in whole or uncut/cooked form. (They're often less healthy, too.) The other day, for example, I spent around $5 on a large whole watermelon that—with 5 minutes of easy knife work—made maybe 10 times as much fruit as I could get spending the same amount on pre-cut stuff. (And it tasted better.) I froze some of it for smoothies later and have eaten the rest over the past week or so.

Other fresh fruits and veggies—as well as things like dry peas/beans, whole fryer chickens, raw potatoes, lettuce heads, and "value packs" of fish and meat—fall into the same category: a few minutes of slicing, bagging, and storing will save a lot over time, and it'll typically taste better than whatever prepared version the supermarket (or its third-party vendors) made a day or more before you bought it. For even more savings, look for bone-in or "alternative" cuts of meat—chicken thighs, pork loin instead of pork tenderloin, tri tip or chuck roast, tilapia, etc.—that are less-known and just as tasty with a little additional cooking or know-how. (More on that in a sec.)

Palates and diets will vary, of course, but you'll usually spend more and get less when buying a box/bag of the thing you found in the middle of the supermarket that you could've also gotten in raw form around the store's perimeter. When venturing into the center aisles is needed, meanwhile, pick generic or store ("private label") brands, which usually taste the same at a cheaper price—a rule that also applies to things like cleaning supplies, over-the-counter drugs, and more. (Yes, some exceptions do apply.)

Finally, you should always have a few "emergency" snacks and meals for times that you can't cook—or just don't want to. Protein shakes and bars are a cheap, easy, and healthy way to get an instant, portable meal without hitting the office vending machine or drive-thru. If you have a few minutes and a microwave, meanwhile, frozen dinners have come a long way: Thanks to capitalism and immigrants, for example, you can now get a decent Indian or Mexican meal for $5 or less at Walmart. (My teenage daughter swears by the frozen breakfast sandwiches we get at Costco for a little more than a buck each.)

Eating on a budget doesn't mean you're suddenly a #TradWife. It just means you're attentive to where and when you save—and splurge.

The final stage is cooking and prep. In general, you can divide weekly cooking into two categories: advance and immediate. Back in the day, I'd do my big weekly shopping on a Saturday after lunch and then head home to bag and store most of the day's haul. The rest I'd cook Sunday evening for dinner and a few lunches for the coming workweek, cleaning up along the way. A few of my staples were 1) grilled chicken breasts (bone-in, skin-on) marinated in lemon juice, olive oil, garlic salt, basil or savory, Dijon, and red pepper flake (teriyaki or soy/honey is also good); 2) slow-cooker pork loin with garlic and a chipotle pepper or two (look for the small can); 3) baked "value pack" salmon cut into single servings, marinated in honey, soy, and garlic; 4) one-pot Mexican soup with olive oil, boneless chicken, onion, canned black beans, chili powder, cumin, garlic salt and chicken broth (from the dried bouillon cubes, of course); or 5) spicy chili with ground beef and canned kidney beans (and lots of cumin, chili powder, and cayenne—plus the obligatory can of beer). I'd combine these entrees with a frozen veggie and rice, potatoes, or couscous—all microwaved with just a little salt and butter—and would be all set for several good, inexpensive workweek lunches.

Times, of course, change—for the worse (ugh, beef prices) or better. New pressure cookers, for example, let you do tons of easy stuff—especially with dry beans and rice—that used to take old people like me several hours at higher cost. (Pressure cooker pot roast is particularly awesome.) There are also tons of websites and YouTube channels with loads of easy recipes. Find a handful you really like, buy the basic ingredients in bulk, and you'll be in great shape.

Immediate dinners all depend on your time, level of exhaustion, and what you have in the fridge. Sometimes, you just want to inhale something and veg out, so a sandwich, big chef salad, scrambled eggs (microwaved with milk, cheese, and ham!), or frozen dinner can do the trick—and everything you need should already be on-hand. If you have the energy, you can make something more elaborate—just make sure to double the portion so you have leftovers for the next day. Other essential tricks are to make your own salad dressings and pasta sauces, both of which are really easy. A great simple vinaigrette, for example, can be had by filling a jar with 1 part oil, 2 parts vinegar (red wine or balsamic), 1 part lemon juice, garlic salt, black pepper, honey, a squeeze of Dijon, and whatever dried herbs—rosemary, basil, oregano, etc.—you have in your spice rack or otherwise lying around (experiment!). Seal it, shake it up, and the result is cheaper, better, and healthier than the store-bought stuff—and it goes great with almost anything.

None of this takes more than a few minutes.

Summing it all up.

As noted at the outset, it's likely that many young workers today have the resources to eat out a lot and just prefer it to cooking at home, despite the significant savings. For those who don't have the money or just want to save more, on the other hand, it's still very much possible to eat quick, healthy meals on a budget—and the above rules will get you a long way there. Surely, not everything a beginner cook makes will be amazing—there will be mistakes. But if you can follow simple directions, even your initial efforts will (probably!) be edible, and you'll get faster and better over time, while surely discovering more complex and enjoyable meals along the way.

And you'll save a boatload while doing it—maybe even enough for DoorDash.

Markets FTW

A new preliminary study shows promise for a gene-editing treatment that could lower cholesterol levels with one dose. From the New York Times:

Gene therapies for rare diseases carry multimillion-dollar price tags. But Dr. Daniel Skovronsky, chief scientist at Eli Lilly, said that would not be the case if this treatment were eventually approved.

"That's not what we're going for here," he said. "We're going for a medicine that someday could be part of primary care."

Thanks, Big Pharma!

Chart(s) of the Week

Tariff refunds show, yet again, that American companies and consumers are paying Trump's tariffs:

Murders in the U.S. continue to decline:

More fluid labor markets mean stronger wage growth:

Scott Lincicome is an author of the Dispatch Markets newsletter, vice president of general economics and trade at the Cato Institute, and a visiting lecturer at Duke University Law School. He wrote the Capitolism newsletter at The Dispatch from 2020 through 2026.

How to improve British procurement

Tyler Cowen · 2026-05-30 · Original →

Until two years ago, West London’s Greenford Tube station used to flood whenever it rained heavily. The train tracks are aboveground, but the ticket office would often get inundated. Sandbags still line the corridor.

But in October 2023, a new family moved in nearby, determined to halt the water. The family members built their house from scratch with local wood and kept odd hours, sleeping all day and working only at dawn and dusk. They even put their young children to work.

The new neighbors were beavers.

In West London, conservationists got a government license to resettle a family of five beavers in a 20-acre urban park near the Greenford Tube station. It used to be a golf course, with a creek running through it. Within weeks, the beavers dammed up the creek, creating a pond that holds water and stops it from spilling into the city. They also diverted the creek’s flow into smaller tributaries, creating a wetland that better absorbs heavy rainfall — mitigating the risk of flooding downstream…

The beavers have also allowed the city to scrap expensive plans to dig a reservoir and levee.

Here is the full story, via Mike Doherty.  Should you need a government license to resettle beavers?

The post How to improve British procurement appeared first on Marginal REVOLUTION.

Those new service sector economics jobs?

Tyler Cowen · 2026-05-28 · Original →

Russia has passed a law authorizing its central bank and other financial institutions to repel drone attacks with their own defense systems, as the country struggles to defend against Ukrainian strikes.

The law, passed by Russia’s lower house of parliament on Tuesday, will allow staff at Russia’s central bank to be armed and to operate the systems used to down unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV, or drone) attacks without the involvement of special forces.

Here is the full story.

Index by Author

Author Title Publication Summary
Sohrab Ahmari Pope Leo’s unfashionable universalism UnHerd (publication) Ahmari reads Pope Leo XIV's debut encyclical Magnifica Humanitas as a defense of moral and political universalism — descended from Leo XIII's 1891 Rerum Novarum — that is "supremely unfashionable" because it embraces UN-style multilateralism just as the West's emancipatory universalisms are collapsing.
Scott Alexander Use AI This Election ACT Alexander reports that Claude, prompted with a few favorite-writer names and an ideological self-description, produced better down-ballot voting research than any voter guide he has seen — and frames AI-assisted political deliberation as a load-bearing positive case for post-AGI futures.
Scott Alexander Book Review: The Dialectical Imagination ACT Alexander reads the Frankfurt School as crypto-Hegelian mystics whose "negative theology" approach to Communism — refusing to specify the utopia while endlessly criticizing the present — explains both their obscurity and their inadvertent paternity of modern progressive slacktivism.
Helen Andrews How Yuppies Changed America Compact Andrews reviews Dylan Gottlieb's Yuppies as a social history of 1980s financialization and uses it to argue that the WASP-elite displacement was a moral regression, not a meritocratic upgrade.
Aurelien You The People.... Aurelien Aurelien argues that "Democracy" has become a content-free Liberal slogan masking a Western political class that cannot deliver substantive results and therefore substitutes vilification of voters ("the extreme Right") for governance.
Freddie deBoer's Blog Raj Chetty's Just-So Stories deBoer deBoer argues that Raj Chetty's celebrated "teacher value-added" findings rest on a circular construct, fail every replication and stability test, and collapsed in real-world deployment — yet still anchor a decade of anti-teacher policy.
Freddie deBoer's Blog Why Pose Thought Experiments About Fake Versions of History? deBoer deBoer argues that defending the 2010s campus-activism era by sanitizing what activists actually did — as in Scott Lemieux's recent post — is condescending pseudo-solidarity that betrays the left's stated commitments and forfeits real accountability.
Dan Boguslaw Congress's CIA Eyes AI Data Center Critics Ken Klippenstein Boguslaw obtains an April Capitol Police Intelligence Services Bureau "Intelligence Note" warning of public hostility to AI data centers — while admitting no actual threats to Congress exist.
Matt Bruenig What Is the Current US Healthcare Situation? People's Policy Project (Bruenig) Bruenig walks node-by-node through the US healthcare payment system (households, governments, employers, insurers, providers) to argue that nearly every dysfunction — uninsurance, churn, regressive tax subsidies, FSA/HSA forfeitures, Medicare Advantage fraud, the 15-20% insurer take, provider rents — would be solved by a single public insurer.
Silver Bulletin A party of one isn't a healthy party Nate Silver Silver argues that Ken Paxton's 64-36 Texas-runoff demolition of John Cornyn proves the GOP-ex-Trump is the unhealthiest major American party on an 11-dimension party-strength rubric.
Cindy Carter Sensitive Words and Censored Content Related to the Recent Sino-American Summit China Digital Times (discovery) CDT compiles dozens of soft-censored sensitive-word combinations around the May 14-15 Trump-Xi Beijing summit — covering trade, Taiwan, Iran, Boeing orders, Musk's Mandarin tweet, and the detention of Zion Church pastor Jin Mingri — showing CCP suppression operating even on summit coverage Beijing claimed as a victory.
Oren Cass Damning (and Damming) the Outflow of Industry Commonplace Cass uses Treasury Secretary Bessent's "While America Slept" speech to argue that conservative-economics has formally repudiated the comparative-advantage tradition and adopted industrial-depth-as-sovereignty as Republican Party orthodoxy.
Tyler Cowen AI in gdp Marginal Revolution Cowen flags a Korinek-McKelvey paper estimating quality-adjusted U.S. AI production grew over 2,000% annually in 2024-25 (~$250B nominal in 2025) and calling for AI-focused satellite national accounts before the measurement gap becomes a policy gap.
Tyler Cowen How Much Has Shale Gas Saved U.S. Consumers? Marginal Revolution Cowen flags a Lucas Davis NBER paper estimating U.S. shale gas saved consumers $3.1-4.3 trillion between 2007 and 2025 ($164-227 billion annually) by holding domestic natural-gas prices below European and Japanese benchmarks.
Tyler Cowen Are we undermeasuring inflation for lower earners? Marginal Revolution Cowen flags a Sangani NBER paper finding that official statistics understate the gap between low- and high-income household inflation rates by 70-90 percent because of "cheapflation" — equal absolute price hikes hit low-price varieties as larger percentage changes.
Tyler Cowen How to improve British procurement Marginal Revolution Cowen, via a story about licensed beavers replacing a planned reservoir near a London Tube station, suggests (without saying so directly) that British infrastructure procurement is so costly that wildlife outperforms it.
Tyler Cowen Those new service sector economics jobs? Marginal Revolution Cowen flags (deadpan, via his recurring "those new service sector jobs" tag) that Russia has just authorized central-bank staff to operate anti-drone defense systems without involving special forces.
Ross Douthat The Best News in America NYT Douthat argues the dramatic post-2020 collapse in U.S. murder rates — with Baltimore's plunge from 348 (2019) to 133 (2025) homicides as the case study — credits a tougher post-Woke prosecutor more than progressive interventions, while caveating that aging, surveillance, and indoor life would drive crime down under any policy regime.
Ross Douthat Pope Leo Isn't Standing Athwart the Singularity NYT Douthat reads Pope Leo's AI encyclical "Magnifica Humanitas" as ultimately committed to an "AI is a normal technology" frame — useful as a politicizing move but too quiet on the personhood-and-idolatry challenge that even Anthropic's own researchers are now openly raising.
Jonas Du Democrats Can't Decide Whether the Problem Is Fraud or the People Exposing It Free Press Du argues Democratic governors in Minnesota, California, and Maine publicly denounce Republican fraud investigations as racially charged while quietly executing the same purges and prosecutions themselves.
Francis Fukuyama Ukraine and Iran are Changing Warfare Persuasion Fukuyama argues drones have closed the strategic and close-air-support domains and are now closing the operational middle domain — meaning combined-arms armored maneuver warfare is over until someone builds a cheap anti-drone system.
Michelle Goldberg There's No Escaping the Rot in This Justice Department NYT Goldberg argues the Northern District of Illinois U.S. Attorney's Office has been hollowed into a vengeance-and-protest-criminalization arm, and that "keeping your head down" is no longer ethically tenable for career prosecutors there.
Sam Kahn The Democrats Sabotaged Their Own Autopsy Persuasion Kahn breaks from the week's consensus and argues that Paul Rivera's DNC autopsy was "perfectly fine" as a technical electioneering after-action report — and that Chair Ken Martin's "bitchy" annotations and public messenger-shooting are the actual story of party dysfunction.
Daniel Kishi From Human Bondage to Open Borders Commonplace Kishi argues that the bipartisan "jobs Americans won't do" frame is the structural heir of antebellum slaveholder James Henry Hammond's "mudsill theory" — and that the right response is to abolish the mudsill, not outsource it.
Ezra Klein Trump Cares About Controlling the Republican Party, Not Winning the Midterms NYT Klein theorizes that Trump is not trying to win the midterms but to consolidate control of the Republican Party — because what he fears is a GOP with the spine to abandon him as his fortunes fall.
Ken Klippenstein The Real Reason Tulsi Gabbard Resigned Ken Klippenstein Klippenstein argues Gabbard's DNI resignation is the latest expression of a position deliberately neutered in 2004 by Rumsfeld-Cheney maneuvering that Senate authors Collins and Lieberman accepted without a fight.
John Byron Kuhner Post-Literacy Raised the Stakes of the Odyssey Debate Compact Kuhner argues that the unprecedented backlash to Christopher Nolan's Odyssey adaptation reflects not new prudery but a postliterate reality in which Hollywood now sets the canonical version of texts no one reads.
Eli Lake Meet the Top War Skeptic Inside the Trump Pentagon Free Press The Free Press profiles Elbridge Colby as a "prioritizer" Pentagon strategist whose lifetime case against Middle East wars has been overrun by the very administration he was meant to restrain.
Charles Fain Lehman Why Did the Murders Stop in Baltimore? Free Press Lehman attributes Baltimore's 60% homicide drop since 2022 to the combined effect of focused-deterrence policing (GVRS) and a tough new prosecutor replacing progressive Marilyn Mosby.
Scott Lincicome How to Eat Well on the Cheap Capitolism (Lincicome) Lincicome departs from his usual trade-policy beat to offer a Gen-X cheapskate's how-to-eat-well-on-a-budget guide for Gen Z workers reportedly spending $28/day on DoorDash lunches, threading affordability data through a brown-bagging tutorial.
Michael Lind How the ‘American Way of War’ failed in Iran UnHerd (publication) Lind argues the Iran war is the latest expression of a persistent "American Way of War" pattern — tactical wins followed by strategic defeat — rooted in technology-substitution-for-troops and a president-empowered war-launching architecture Congress lost in the Truman era.
Nouridin Melo The Dangote Refinery and the End of Africa’s Engineered Dependency African Arguments (publication) Melo argues Nigeria's 650,000-bpd Dangote Refinery — by keeping crude-to-product conversion on African soil — has cut Nigeria's fuel import bill from $2.6B to $1.2B Q1 year-over-year and now exports refined products to five African states, breaking the colonial-era export-crude-import-refined architecture.
Wolfgang Munchau Why Europe keeps losing UnHerd (publication) Munchau argues the EU keeps losing trade fights with both Trump and Beijing not because of political weakness but because two decades of car-industry supply-chain choices have made retaliation economically impossible — and the only fix is a binding ten-year supply-chain decoupling plan.
Noahpinion America needs liberal nationalism back Noahpinion Smith argues FDR-style liberal nationalism — civic identity plus pluralism plus shared-fate institutions — is the only viable American ideology because right-wing blood-and-soil nationalism collapses on its own definitional incoherence and tech-libertarian globalism cannot fund public goods.
Noahpinion Are you tired of the Trump era yet? Noahpinion Smith argues the second Trump term's pace of disasters — anti-immigrant tech-worker expulsion, the losing Iran War, and unprecedented self-dealing corruption — is using the first-term DDOS rhetorical strategy as actual policy to overload public attention.
Noahpinion Your future job will be to keep AI on task Noahpinion Smith argues that as AI commoditizes technical output, the durable human comparative advantage shifts to alignment-and-verification work — the cognitively demanding job of knowing what we actually want and confirming AI delivered it.
Eboo Patel Be Excellent, Not Efficient Persuasion Patel adapts his 2026 Denison University commencement address into a three-point case for liberal-arts judgment against AI-era inevitability, social-change-as-life-lived (per David Brooks), and ihsan (Islamic "sacred excellence").
Brad Pearce When the Train Comes Back to Town Commonplace Pearce reports the family-owned revival of the WI&M short-line railroad in eastern Washington as evidence that small-town industrial America can be rebuilt where ownership is patient and long-horizon rather than institutional.
Tomer Persico What the Pope Is Telling Us Persuasion Persico reads Pope Leo XIV's Magnifica Humanitas encyclical as warning not against AI itself but against the secular-humanist premise that human dignity is grounded in intelligence — a premise that makes "speciesist" a coherent insult.
Andy Polk Glass Jaw? The New Economic Fragility Recasting American Power War on the Rocks (publication) Polk argues Trump's IEEPA-and-Section-122 broad tariffs have stripped the US economy of its built-in agility to absorb shocks, leaving the Iran-war oil-price spike to land on companies with no remaining buffer — and that this economic-fragility is now a hard ceiling on US national-security freedom of action.
Brian Potter Where Are the Economies of Scale in Homebuilding? Construction Physics (Potter) Potter argues homebuilding lacks the economies of scale that transform other industries because its output-to-input cost ratio is already ~2x — the same as a finished BMW — leaving almost no margin for scale-based efficiency to act on.
Hannah Ritchie Do more Europeans die of summer heat than Americans die of guns? Sustainability by Numbers (Ritchie) Ritchie rebuilds the viral "European heat deaths > American gun deaths" comparison using methodologically consistent data and finds the basic shape holds — though per-capita reverses the order — while flagging the comparison itself as a status-quo-bias pairing rather than a real tradeoff.
Geoff Shullenberger The Thinker Who Foresaw Pope Leo's Critique of AI Compact Shullenberger reads Pope Leo XIV’s new AI encyclical Magnifica Humanitas through Ivan Illich’s late critique of cybernetic systems, arguing that the Church now occupies argumentative ground secular AI skeptics have left vacant.
Geoff Shullenberger Reification and the Consciousness of the Tech Bro Compact Substack Shullenberger argues that the AI Singularity myth is best understood as Lukácsian commodity fetishism — a relation between people misrecognized as the autonomous agency of a thing.
Eli McKown-Dawson and Nate Silver Is Trump trying to turn Texas blue? Nate Silver McKown-Dawson and Silver argue Trump's late, last-minute endorsement of scandal-plagued Ken Paxton over John Cornyn in the Texas Senate runoff — at the moment of his weakest approval rating — opens a real Democratic path to a Talarico upset that could flip the Senate.
Matt Stoller Monopoly Round-Up: Private Equity Blocked from Buying Homes. Mostly. BIG (Stoller) Stoller credits a "shockingly weird Warren-Trump alliance" with passing the 21st Century ROAD to Housing Act, which bans large institutional investors from buying existing single-family homes — though Build-to-Rent gets a carve-out.
Matt Stoller The Conspiracy to Maintain High Gas Prices in California Is About Propping Up a Flailing Oil Industry BIG (Stoller) Stoller argues California's "mystery gasoline surcharge" — costing residents $59B since 2015 — is the product of a Chevron-led refining oligopoly and unusual vertical integration in retail, not the environmental regulations Chevron blames.
Alex Tabarrok Why are Murders Down in Baltimore? Marginal Revolution Tabarrok argues Baltimore's murder collapse (38 YTD vs. 323 four years ago) vindicates his "crime-wave equilibrium" model and that focused-deterrence (Boston Ceasefire-style intervention on predictable retaliatory shootings) is doing the lifting.
Alex Tabarrok Doc in a Box Marginal Revolution Tabarrok flags a Utah pilot showing AI-driven prescription refills agreed with physician judgment 97% of the time on auto-approved cases (72% of the volume) and escalated appropriately 69% of the time on the remainder, framing AI primary-care as turning a supply problem into an engineering problem.
Adam Tooze Chartbook 450: Modern growth surges. Or, why China's economic development is unique in human history. Chartbook (Tooze) Tooze quantifies China's 1978-2022 growth surge as 20x larger than Japan's 1953-1991 takeoff and ~290x larger than US 1865-1914 Gilded Age — across population-weighted growth, absolute GDP, and share of global growth.
Suzy Weiss A Showdown over Israel in Park Slope Free Press Weiss frames the Park Slope Food Co-op's BDS vote as the predictable terminus of a neighborhood-cultural type — rule-loving progressives whose moralism scales to symbolic gestures with zero geopolitical purchase.
Scott Yenor Title IX's Collision Course with Reality Compact Yenor argues that the post-NIL-settlement collision between college-sports roster caps and Title IX's proportionate-parity standard will force absurdities like varsity women's flag football at Nebraska while bankrupting smaller schools.
Matthew Yglesias The amazing — somewhat mysterious — crime drop Slow Boring Yglesias argues the 2024-25 record crime drop is primarily a return of police effort that retreated after the George Floyd protests (a Ferguson Effect at national scale), against Demsas's pushback that the macro-trend's breadth is too big for a single-mechanism explanation.
Matthew Yglesias The left-populist fallacy Slow Boring Yglesias argues left-populism (Graham Platner's Maine Senate campaign, Bernie-style "anti-oligarchy" framing) is fallacious because economic-cultural-issue substitution doesn't survive election results — voters weight cultural questions more than progressive activists assume they do, and an NYT/Siena poll shows rank-and-file Dems want the party to move center 52-25.
Matthew Yglesias I’m sick of “affordability” Slow Boring Yglesias argues "affordability" is empty Democratic messaging slop that lets degrowth-, antitrust-, and anti-inequality factions evade the unspoken implication of their own framing — namely that economic growth and rising incomes should be the central goal.
Matthew Yglesias Time to freak out about the national debt Slow Boring Yglesias argues the post-Covid spike in long-term US borrowing costs has reached the point where Democrats should re-embrace deficit politics, because deficit framing highlights popular Dem priorities (tax the rich, defend Social Security) against unpopular GOP ones.
Matthew Yglesias Union enthusiasts should support abundance Slow Boring Yglesias inverts Andrias-Hertel-Fernandez's "abundance should cater to unions" argument, contending instead that unions should adopt the abundance agenda because YIMBY-plus-state-capacity-plus-nuclear is concretely better for labor outcomes than the green/anti-growth coalition unions currently default to.
Julia Yost The Cheap Tradsploitation of 'Yesteryear' Compact Yost pans Caro Claire Burke's bestselling tradwife-satire novel Yesteryear as a literarily incompetent revenge fantasy whose overwarm reception betrays progressive panic that "the fertile will inherit the earth."

↩ Back to TOC
